Exam Answer Modes

Long Answer Skeleton

  1. Epidemiology, transmission, latency, reactivation
  2. Clinical syndromes (immunocompetent, immunocompromised, congenital)
  3. Diagnosis (PCR, antigenemia, serology, IgG avidity, histology)
  4. Treatment (valganciclovir, ganciclovir, foscarnet, cidofovir, maribavir)
  5. Resistance (UL97, UL54 mutations)
  6. Transplant management (prophylaxis vs pre-emptive, D/R stratification)
  7. Congenital CMV (diagnosis, treatment, sequelae)
  8. Pregnancy management (IgG avidity, fetal diagnosis, treatment)
  9. Resistance management

Short Note Skeleton

  • CMV = β-herpesvirus; latency in myeloid; reactivation in immunocompromised
  • D+/R− = highest risk; prophylaxis (valganciclovir) vs pre-emptive PCR
  • Syndrome: fever + viraemia + 2 of (leucopenia, thrombocytopenia, ALT↑, atypical lymphs)
  • 1st-line: valganciclovir 900mg BD → OD; ganciclovir IV 5mg/kg 12h
  • Resistant: UL97 → maribavir/foscarnet; UL54 → foscarnet/cidofovir
  • Transplant: D+/R− prophylaxis or pre-emptive; others pre-emptive
  • Congenital: urine PCR birth; symptomatic → ganciclovir/valganciclovir 6w
  • IgG avidity: low = recent primary (pregnancy)

Viva One-Liners

  • CMV syndrome: fever + viraemia + 2 of (leucopenia, thrombocytopenia, ALT↑, atypical lymphs)
  • 1st-line: valganciclovir 900mg BD → OD; ganciclovir IV 5mg/kg 12h
  • D+/R− = highest risk; prophylaxis valganciclovir 900mg OD ×3–6m or pre-emptive
  • Resistance: UL97 → maribavir/foscarnet; UL54 → foscarnet/cidofovir
  • Congenital: urine PCR at birth; symptomatic → ganciclovir/valganciclovir 6w
  • IgG avidity: low = recent primary (<3–4m); high = past
  • Prophylaxis delays only; pre-emptive still needed after stop

Ward-Case Discussion Points

  • Lung transplant D+/R− day 30: fever, hypoxia, CXR bilateral infiltrates → CMV PCR 50,000 → ganciclovir IV +/− IVIG
  • Renal transplant R+, day 45: fever, diarrhoea, CMV PCR 5,000 → valganciclovir BD ×2w
  • Pregnant 12w, primary CMV (IgG avidity low) → amniocentesis PCR → counsel re congenital risk
  • AIDS CD4 50, floaters, vision loss → CMV retinitis → ganciclovir IV + intravitreal foscarnet/ganciclovir

Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet

CMV: D+/R− = highest risk (prophylaxis valganciclovir or pre-emptive PCR). Syndrome: fever + viraemia + 2 of (leucopenia, thrombocytopenia, ALT↑, atypical lymphs). 1st-line: valganciclovir 900mg BD→OD; ganciclovir IV 5mg/kg 12h. Resistant: UL97→maribavir/foscarnet; UL54→foscarnet/cidofovir. Transplant: D+/R− prophylaxis valganciclovir 900mg OD ×3-6m or pre-emptive. Congenital: urine PCR birth; symptomatic → ganciclovir/valganciclovir 6w. IgG avidity low = recent primary. Prophylaxis delays only.

Summary

Cytomegalovirus (CMV) is a ubiquitous β-herpesvirus (HHV-5) establishing lifelong latency in myeloid progenitors; reactivation causes disease in immunocompromised hosts. Transplant risk stratification: D+/R− (donor positive/recipient negative) = highest risk (primary infection); peak 1–4 months; D+/R+ and D−/R+ = intermediate (reactivation/reinfection); D−/R− = low. Clinical syndromes: Immunocompetent — asymptomatic (90%) or mononucleosis-like; Immunocompromised — CMV Syndrome (fever + viraemia + ≥2: leucopenia, thrombocytopenia, ↑ALT, atypical lymphocytes) → Tissue-invasive disease (pneumonitis, colitis, retinitis, hepatitis, encephalitis, nephritis). Diagnosis: Quantitative PCR (blood) = gold standard; pp65 antigenemia (rapid); IgG avidity (low = recent primary <3–4m, high = past — critical in pregnancy); histology = owl's eye intranuclear inclusions. Treatment: 1st-line oral = Valganciclovir 900mg PO BD (induction) → 900mg OD (maintenance); 1st-line IV = Ganciclovir 5mg/kg IV 12h; CMV syndrome = valganciclovir 900mg BD ×2–3w; Tissue-invasive = ganciclovir IV 5mg/kg 12h ×3–6w; Resistant CMV: UL97 mutation (phosphotransferase) → Maribavir (UL97 inhibitor) or Foscarnet; UL54 mutation (DNA polymerase) → Foscarnet/Cidofovir; Maribavir = UL97 inhibitor (oral, no myelosuppression). Transplant strategy: D+/R− high-risk = prophylaxis (Valganciclovir 900mg OD ×3–6m) OR pre-emptive (weekly PCR ×3–6m, treat if >threshold); others = pre-emptive. Congenital CMV (most common congenital infection): urine/saliva PCR at birth = gold standard; symptomatic (10–15%) → Ganciclovir 6mg/kg IV 12h OR Valganciclovir 16mg/kg PO BD ×6 weeks (improves hearing/neurodevelopment); asymptomatic = surveillance for SNHL. Pregnancy: IgG avidity low = recent primary (<3–4m) → high vertical transmission risk (30–40%); amniocentesis PCR for fetal infection. Resistance: UL97 mutation → ganciclovir/valganciclovir resistance → Maribavir or Foscarnet; UL54 mutation → foscarnet/cidofovir resistance → Maribavir or Leflunomide adjunct.

MCQs (10)

  1. Highest risk CMV serostatus for transplant recipient:

A. D+/R+

B. D+/R−

C. D−/R+

D. D−/R−

E. All equal

  1. CMV Syndrome definition requires fever + viraemia plus ≥2 of:

A. Leucopenia, thrombocytopenia, ↑ALT, atypical lymphocytes

B. Fever, cough, dyspnoea, hypoxia

C. Diarrhoea, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting

D. Headache, photophobia, neck stiffness, confusion

E. Rash, arthralgia, myalgia, fatigue

  1. First-line oral treatment for CMV disease:

A. Ganciclovir IV

B. Valganciclovir 900mg PO BD (induction) → 900mg OD (maintenance)

C. Foscarnet IV

C. Cidofovir IV

E. Aciclovir PO

  1. UL97 mutation in CMV confers resistance to:

A. Foscarnet

B. Cidofovir

C. Ganciclovir/Valganciclovir

D. Maribavir

E. Letermovir

  1. Congenital CMV — gold standard diagnosis in neonate:

A. Cord blood IgM

B. Urine or saliva PCR (birth–3 weeks)

C. Maternal IgG avidity

D. Neonatal IgM

E. CSF PCR

  1. UL97 mutation confers resistance to which drug class?

A. Foscarnet

B. Ganciclovir/Valganciclovir

C. Maribavir

D. Cidofovir

E. Letermovir

  1. Transplant CMV strategy for D+/R− recipient:

A. No prophylaxis needed

B. Prophylaxis (valganciclovir) OR Pre-emptive PCR monitoring

C. Pre-emptive only

D. No monitoring needed

E. Vaccination

  1. Congenital CMV — treatment for symptomatic neonate:

A. Supportive only

B. Aciclovir PO ×6w

C. Ganciclovir 6mg/kg IV 12h OR Valganciclovir 16mg/kg PO BD ×6 weeks

D. Aciclovir IV ×6w

E. No treatment (self-limiting)

  1. IgG avidity low in pregnancy indicates:

A. Past infection

B. Recent primary infection (<3–4 months)

C. Reactivation

D. Immunity

E. No infection

  1. UL97 mutation confers resistance to:

A. Foscarnet

B. Ganciclovir/Valganciclovir

C. Maribavir

D. Cidofovir

E. Letermovir

  1. Maribavir mechanism of action:

A. DNA polymerase inhibitor

B. UL97 kinase inhibitor (prevents ganciclovir phosphorylation)

C. Viral entry inhibitor

C. Protein synthesis inhibitor

E. Integrase inhibitor

SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 40-year-old man, 2 months post-lung transplant (D+/R−), on valganciclovir prophylaxis, presents with fever, hypoxia, bilateral infiltrates. CMV PCR: 50,000 IU/mL. Best management?

A. Continue valganciclovir prophylaxis

B. Switch to IV ganciclovir 5mg/kg 12h ×3–6 weeks

C. Add foscarnet

D. Switch to maribavir

E. Stop prophylaxis, monitor

  1. A 30-year-old kidney transplant recipient (R+, D−) on day 45 develops fever, diarrhoea, CMV PCR 5,000 IU/mL. No pneumonitis. Management?

A. Continue prophylaxis

B. Valganciclovir 900mg PO BD ×2–3 weeks (pre-emptive therapy)

C. IV ganciclovir 5mg/kg 12h

D. Foscarnet

E. No treatment (asymptomatic viraemia)

  1. A 25-year-old pregnant woman at 12 weeks develops primary CMV (IgG avidity low, IgM+). Amniocentesis PCR positive. Fetal ultrasound normal. Counselling regarding congenital CMV?

A. Termination recommended

B. High transmission risk (30–40%), but >85% asymptomatic at birth; 10–15% SNHL; offer serial ultrasounds, serial PCR

C. Guaranteed severe microcephaly

D. Immediate termination

E. No risk (asymptomatic mother)

  1. A renal transplant patient on valganciclovir prophylaxis develops CMV viraemia (PCR 10,000). Genotypic resistance testing shows UL97 mutation. Best next step?

A. Increase valganciclovir dose

B. Switch to IV ganciclovir

C. Switch to maribavir 400mg PO BD

D. Switch to foscarnet

D. Add foscarnet to valganciclovir

  1. A 28-year-old pregnant woman at 20 weeks is found to have low IgG avidity for CMV. What does this indicate?

A. Past infection, no risk

B. Recent primary infection (<3–4 months), high vertical transmission risk

C. Reactivation, low risk

D. Immunity

E. False positive

  1. A renal transplant patient develops CMV retinitis. Best treatment?

A. Valganciclovir PO only

B. IV ganciclovir 5mg/kg 12h + intravitreal ganciclovir/foscarnet

C. Oral valganciclovir + intravitreal foscarnet

D. Foscarnet IV only

E. Cidofovir IV

  1. Pre-emptive therapy for CMV in transplant — trigger threshold:

A. Any positive PCR

B. PCR >1000–2000 IU/mL OR >1 log rise in 1 week

C. PCR >10,000 IU/mL

D. Only if symptoms develop

E. Only if antigenemia >10 cells

  1. Which antiviral is used for UL54-mutant CMV resistant to foscarnet?

A. Ganciclovir

B. Valganciclovir

C. Maribavir (if UL97 intact) or Leflunomide adjunct

D. Cidofovir

E. Letermovir

  1. Which statement about letemovir is TRUE?

A. Treats active CMV disease

B. Primary prophylaxis in HSCT (CMV prevention)

C. Treats resistant CMV (UL97)

D. Oral only

E. No drug interactions

Flashcards

  • Q: CMV highest risk transplant

A: D+/R−

  • Q: CMV syndrome

A: Fever + viraemia + 2 of (leucopenia, thrombocytopenia, ALT↑, atypical lymphs)

  • Q: 1st line oral

A: Valganciclovir 900mg BD→OD

  • Q: 1st line IV

A: Ganciclovir 5mg/kg 12h

  • Q: UL97 mutation

A: Ganciclovir resistance → Maribavir/Foscarnet

  • Q: UL54 mutation

A: Foscarnet/Cidofovir resistance → Maribavir/Leflunomide

  • Q: Congenital CMV dx

A: Urine/saliva PCR at birth

  • Q: Congenital CMV Rx

A: Ganciclovir/Valganciclovir 6w

  • Q: IgG avidity low

A: Recent primary infection (<3-4m)

  • Q: Transplant D+/R-

A: Prophylaxis valganciclovir OR pre-emptive PCR

  • Q: Maribavir mechanism

A: UL97 kinase inhibitor

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — D+/R− (donor seropositive, recipient seronegative) carries highest risk of primary CMV infection.
  2. A — CMV syndrome = fever + viraemia + ≥2 of: leucopenia, thrombocytopenia, ↑ALT, atypical lymphocytes.
  3. B — Valganciclovir 900mg PO BD (induction) → 900mg OD (maintenance) is 1st-line oral therapy.
  4. C — UL97 encodes viral phosphotransferase; mutations prevent ganciclovir phosphorylation → resistance to ganciclovir/valganciclovir.
  5. B — Urine or saliva PCR within 3 weeks of birth is gold standard for congenital CMV diagnosis.
  6. B — UL97 mutation = ganciclovir/valganciclovir resistance.
  7. B — D+/R− highest risk → prophylaxis (valganciclovir 900mg OD ×3–6m) OR pre-emptive PCR monitoring.
  8. C — Symptomatic congenital CMV: ganciclovir 6mg/kg IV 12h or valganciclovir 16mg/kg BD ×6 weeks (improves hearing/neurodevelopment).
  9. B — Low IgG avidity = recent primary infection (<3–4 months); high avidity = past infection.
  10. B — UL97 mutation = ganciclovir/valganciclovir resistance.
  11. B — Maribavir is a UL97 kinase inhibitor (prevents ganciclovir phosphorylation).

SBAs

  1. B — CMV pneumonitis (tissue-invasive) in transplant recipient on prophylaxis → escalate to IV ganciclovir 5mg/kg 12h ×3–6w.
  2. B — R+ recipient with pre-emptive indication (viraemia >threshold, no tissue-invasive) → valganciclovir PO BD ×2–3w.
  3. B — Primary CMV in pregnancy: 30–40% transmission risk; >85% asymptomatic; 10–15% SNHL; serial monitoring.
  4. C — UL97 mutation = ganciclovir resistance → maribavir 400mg BD (UL97 inhibitor).
  5. B — Low IgG avidity = recent primary infection (<3–4m); high vertical transmission risk (30–40%).
  6. B — CMV retinitis = IV ganciclovir + intravitreal ganciclovir/foscarnet (systemic + local).
  7. B — Pre-emptive trigger: PCR >1000–2000 IU/mL OR >1 log rise in 1 week.
  8. C — UL54 mutation → foscarnet/cidofovir resistance; maribavir (UL97 intact) active; leflunomide adjunct.
  9. B — Letermovir = primary prophylaxis in HSCT (CMV prevention); not for treatment.
  10. B — Chronic Q fever endocarditis: Phase I IgG ≥1:800; doxy + HCQ 18–24m.

Flashcards

  • Q: CMV highest risk

A: D+/R−

  • Q: CMV syndrome

A: Fever + viraemia + 2 of (leucopenia, thrombocytopenia, ALT↑, atypical lymphs)

  • Q: 1st line oral

A: Valganciclovir 900mg BD→OD

  • Q: 1st line IV

A: Ganciclovir 5mg/kg 12h

  • Q: UL97 mut

A: Ganciclovir R → Maribavir/Foscarnet

  • Q: UL54 mut

A: Foscarnet/Cidofovir R → Maribavir/Leflunomide

  • Q: Congenital dx

A: Urine/saliva PCR birth

  • Q: Congenital Rx

A: Ganciclovir/Valganciclovir 6w

  • Q: IgG avidity

A: Low = recent primary (<3-4m)

  • Q: D+/R- strategy

A: Prophylaxis valganciclovir OR pre-emptive

  • Q: Maribavir

A: UL97 kinase inhibitor

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — D+/R− (donor positive, recipient negative) is the highest risk combination for CMV disease post-transplant.
  2. A — CMV syndrome definition per IDSA/AST guidelines.
  3. B — Valganciclovir 900mg PO BD (induction) → 900mg OD (maintenance) is first-line oral therapy.
  4. C — UL97 encodes phosphotransferase; mutations prevent ganciclovir phosphorylation → resistance.
  5. B — Urine/saliva PCR at birth is gold standard for congenital CMV (sensitivity >95%).
  6. B — UL97 mutation = resistance to ganciclovir/valganciclovir.
  7. B — D+/R− highest risk: prophylaxis (valganciclovir 900mg OD ×3–6m) OR pre-emptive (weekly PCR ×3–6m).
  8. C — Symptomatic congenital CMV: ganciclovir 6mg/kg IV 12h or valganciclovir 16mg/kg BD ×6 weeks (improves hearing/neurodevelopment).
  9. B — Low IgG avidity = recent primary infection (<3–4 months); high = past infection.
  10. B — Maribavir inhibits UL97 kinase → prevents ganciclovir phosphorylation (unique mechanism).

SBAs

  1. B — CMV pneumonitis (tissue-invasive) = IV ganciclovir 5mg/kg 12h ×3–6 weeks; valganciclovir PO insufficient for pneumonitis.
  2. B — Pre-emptive therapy indication: viraemia >threshold, no tissue-invasive disease → valganciclovir PO BD ×2–3w.
  3. B — Primary CMV in pregnancy: transmission 30–40%, >85% asymptomatic, 10–15% SNHL; serial monitoring.
  4. C — UL97 mutation = ganciclovir resistance → switch to maribavir (UL97 inhibitor).
  5. B — Low IgG avidity = recent primary infection (<3–4 months); high vertical transmission risk.
  6. B — CMV retinitis: systemic IV ganciclovir + intravitreal ganciclovir/foscarnet (local + systemic).
  7. B — Pre-emptive trigger: PCR >1000–2000 IU/mL OR >1 log rise in 1 week (IDSA/AST guidelines).
  8. C — UL54 mutation = foscarnet/cidofovir resistance; maribavir (UL97 target intact) + leflunomide adjunct.
  9. B — Letermovir = primary prophylaxis in HSCT (not for treatment of active disease).
  10. B — Chronic Q fever endocarditis = Phase I IgG ≥1:800; doxycycline + hydroxychloroquine 18–24m.

Flashcards

  • Q: CMV highest risk

A: D+/R−

  • Q: CMV syndrome

A: Fever + viraemia + 2 of (leucopenia, thrombocytopenia, ALT↑, atypical lymphs)

  • Q: 1st line oral

A: Valganciclovir 900mg BD → OD

  • Q: 1st line IV

A: Ganciclovir 5mg/kg 12h

  • Q: UL97 mutation

A: Ganciclovir resistance → Maribavir/Foscarnet

  • Q: UL54 mutation

A: Foscarnet/Cidofovir resistance → Maribavir/Leflunomide

  • Q: Congenital CMV dx

A: Urine/saliva PCR at birth

  • Q: Congenital CMV Rx

A: Ganciclovir/Valganciclovir 6w

  • Q: IgG avidity

A: Low = recent primary (<3-4m)

  • Q: Transplant D+/R-

A: Prophylaxis valganciclovir OR pre-emptive

  • Q: Maribavir mechanism

A: UL97 kinase inhibitor (prevents ganciclovir phosphorylation)

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — D+/R− = highest risk for primary CMV infection post-transplant.
  2. A — CMV syndrome per consensus criteria.
  3. B — Valganciclovir 900mg PO BD (induction) → OD (maintenance) = 1st-line oral.
  4. C — UL97 mutation → ganciclovir resistance.
  5. B — Urine/saliva PCR at birth = gold standard congenital CMV diagnosis.
  6. B — UL97 mutation → ganciclovir/valganciclovir resistance.
  7. B — D+/R−: prophylaxis (valganciclovir 900mg OD ×3–6m) OR pre-emptive PCR.
  8. C — Symptomatic congenital CMV: ganciclovir/valganciclovir ×6w (hearing/neurodevelopment benefit).
  9. B — Low IgG avidity = recent primary (<3–4m); high = past infection.
  10. B — Maribavir = UL97 kinase inhibitor (prevents ganciclovir phosphorylation).

SBAs

  1. B — Tissue-invasive CMV (pneumonitis) = IV ganciclovir; prophylaxis failure → escalate to IV.
  2. B — Pre-emptive: viraemia >threshold, no tissue-invasive → valganciclovir PO.
  3. B — Primary CMV in pregnancy: 30–40% transmission, 10–15% SNHL, >85% asymptomatic.
  4. C — UL97 mutation → maribavir (UL97 inhibitor).
  5. B — Low avidity = recent primary; high vertical transmission risk.
  6. B — CMV retinitis = systemic IV + intravitreal (ganciclovir/foscarnet).
  7. B — Pre-emptive threshold: PCR >1000–2000 OR >1 log rise/week.
  8. C — UL54 mut → foscarnet/cidofovir R; maribavir (UL97 intact) + leflunomide.
  9. B — Letermovir = HSCT prophylaxis; not for active disease.
  10. B — Chronic Q fever endocarditis = Phase I IgG ≥1:800; doxy+HCQ 18–24m.

Endemic Mycoses (Dimorphic Fungi)

[!info] Davidson Ch 11 Alignment: Infectious Disease → Specific Organism Groups → Fungi → Endemic Mycoses (Dimorphic Fungi)
FCPS/MRCP Focus: Histoplasmosis, Coccidioidomycosis, Blastomycosis, Paracoccidioidomycosis, Talaromycosis, endemic geography, diagnosis, treatment

🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Identify Dimorphic Fungi: Mould at 25°C / Yeast at 37°C — Histoplasma, Coccidioides, Blastomyces, Paracoccidioides, Talaromyces (Penicillium)
  • [ ] Recognise Endemic Geography: Histoplasma (Ohio/Mississippi River Valleys), Coccidioides (SW USA, N Mexico), Blastomyces (Great Lakes, SE USA), Paracoccidioides (Latin America), Talaromyces (SE Asia)
  • [ ] Recognise Clinical Presentations: Acute/Subacute Pneumonia, Chronic Pulmonary, Disseminated (Skin, Bone, CNS, Adrenal), Immunocompromised
  • [ ] Diagnose: Culture (Gold Standard), Antigen Detection (Urine/Blood/CSF), Serology (CF, ID, EIA), PCR, Histopathology (Yeast in Macrophages)
  • [ ] Manage: Itraconazole (Mild-Mod), Amphotericin B (Severe), Lifelong Suppression (Immunosuppressed), Azoles (Itraconazole, Voriconazole, Posaconazole)

📖 General Features of Dimorphic Fungi

FeatureDetails
DimorphismMould at 25°C (Environment) → Yeast at 37°C (Host) — Thermal Dimorphism
Infection RouteInhalation of Conidia → Primary Pulmonary → Haematogenous Dissemination
Risk FactorsImmunocompromise (HIV/CD4<150, Transplant, TNF-α Inhibitors, Steroids), Occupational/Recreational Exposure (Caves, Soil, Construction)
EndemicityGeographically Restricted (See Table Below)
Incubation1-3 Weeks (Acute), Months-Years (Reactivation)
PathogenEndemic RegionVector/Source
Histoplasma capsulatumOhio/Mississippi River Valleys (USA), Central/South America, Africa, SE AsiaBird/Bat Guano (Caves, Chicken Coops, Old Buildings)
Coccidioides immitis/posadasiiSW USA (CA, AZ, NV, NM, TX), N MexicoSoil (Desert, Dust Storms, Construction, Archaeology)
Blastomyces dermatitidisGreat Lakes, SE USA, Ohio/Mississippi River Valleys, Canada (Ontario, Manitoba)Moist Soil, Decaying Wood (Riverbanks, Lakes, Excavation)
Paracoccidioides brasiliensis/lutziiLatin America (Brazil, Colombia, Venezuela, Argentina)Soil (Rural, Agricultural)
Talaromyces (Penicillium) marneffeiSE Asia (Thailand, Vietnam, Hong Kong, Taiwan, NE India)Bamboo Rats, Soil (Immunocompromised Hosts)
[!tip] Endemic Mycoses = Dimorphic Fungi (Mould 25°C → Yeast 37°C). Inhalation → Primary Pulmonary → Dissemination. Geography = Diagnosis Clue.

📖 Specific Endemic Mycoses

1. Histoplasmosis (Histoplasma capsulatum)

AspectDetails
Endemic AreasOhio/Mississippi River Valleys (USA), Central/South America, Africa, SE Asia
ExposureBird/Bat Guano (Caves, Chicken Coops, Old Buildings, Bridge Demolition)
Clinical FormsAcute Pulmonary (Self-Limited), Chronic Pulmonary (Cavitary, COPD-like), Disseminated (Immunocompromised — Adrenal, Skin, CNS, Mucosal), Mediastinal Fibrosis (Broncholithiasis, SVCO)
Acute PulmonaryFlu-like, Cough, Fever, Chest Pain, Hilar Lymphadenopathy, Self-Limited
Chronic PulmonaryCavitary Apollo Upper Lobes, Cough, Haemoptysis, Weight Loss, Mimics TB
DisseminatedHIV/CD4<150, Adrenal Insufficiency (Addison's), Oral/NG Ulcers, Hepatosplenomegaly, CNS

Diagnosis

TestSensitivity/SpecificityRole
Urine/Serum Histoplasma AntigenHigh Sens/Spec (90%+)Rapid, First-Line
Culture (Gold Standard)DefinitiveSlow (2-4 Weeks), BSL-3
Serology (CF, ID, EIA)Antibody DetectionSupportive
HistopathologyYeast in Macrophages (Small, Oval, Intracellular)Diagnostic (Biopsy)
PCRRapidEmerging

Treatment

Syndrome1st LineDuration
Acute Pulmonary (Mild)Observe (Self-Limited) / Itraconazole 200mg BD if Prolonged >1 Month6-12 Weeks
Chronic PulmonaryItraconazole 200mg BD12-24 Months
Disseminated / CNSLAmB 3-5mg/kg/day IV ×2w → Itraconazole 200mg BD12 Months (Lifelong if Immunosuppressed)
Mediastinal FibrosisNo Antifungal → Surgery/Stents if SVCO—

2. Coccidioidomycosis (Valley Fever)

FeatureDetails
AgentCoccidioides immitis (CA), C. posadasii (AZ, MX, Rest)
EndemicSW USA (CA, AZ, NV, NM, UT, TX), N Mexico
ExposureSoil Dust (Construction, Archaeology, Dust Storms, Military, Agriculture)
Clinical60% Asymptomatic; Acute Pneumonia (Valley Fever): Fever, Cough, Fatigue, Erythema Nodosum (Erythema Multiforme), Arthralgia ("Desert Rheumatism"), Chronic Cavitary, Disseminated (Meningitis, Skin, Bone, Joints)
Risk FactorsImmunocompromised, Pregnancy (3rd Trimester), African/Filipino Ancestry (↑ Dissemination)

Diagnosis & Treatment

TestUtility
Serology (CF, ID, EIA)IgM (Early), IgG (Late), CF Titer (Prognosis)
CultureDefinitive (BSL-3)
Antigen (Urine/Serum)Available
Syndrome1st LineDuration
Primary Pulmonary (Mild)Observe / Fluconazole 400mg OD3-6 Months
Chronic Pulmonary / DisseminatedFluconazole 400-800mg OD / Itraconazole 200mg BD12+ Months
MeningitisFluconazole 800-1200mg OD + Intrathecal Amphotericin (Refractory)Lifelong
[!warning] Coccidioidal Meningitis = Lifelong Suppression (Fluconazole 800-1200mg OD). Relapse Common if Stopped.

3. Blastomycosis

FeatureDetails
AgentBlastomyces dermatitidis
EndemicGreat Lakes, SE USA, Ohio/Mississippi River Valleys, Canada (ON, MB)
ExposureMoist Soil, Decaying Wood (Riverbanks, Lakes, Excavation, Construction)
ClinicalAcute Pneumonia (Flu-like), Chronic Pneumonia (Cavitary, Mimics TB), Disseminated (Skin: Verrucous Ulcers, Bone (Osteomyelitis), Prostatitis, CNS)
Skin LesionsVerrucous, Ulcerative, Crusted (Face, Extremities) — "Blastomycotic Dermatitis"

Diagnosis & Treatment

TestUtility
CultureGold Standard (Yeast at 37°C, Broad-Based Budding)
Antigen (Urine/Serum)High Sens/Spec (Cross-Reacts with Histoplasma)
KOH/HistologyBroad-Based Budding Yeast (8-15µm), Thick Wall
Syndrome1st LineDuration
Pulmonary (Mild/Moderate)Itraconazole 200mg BD6-12 Months
Severe / CNS / DisseminatedLAmB 3-5mg/kg/day ×2w → Itraconazole 200mg BD6-12 Months
CNSLAmB 5mg/kg/day ×4-6w → Fluconazole 800mg ODLifelong
[!warning] Blastomycosis = Broad-Based Budding Yeast (8-15µm) on KOH/Histology. Skin Lesions = Verrucous Ulcers. Prostatitis Common in Men.

4. Paracoccidioidomycosis

FeatureDetails
AgentParacoccidioides brasiliensis / P. lutzii
EndemicLatin America (Brazil, Colombia, Venezuela, Argentina, Ecuador)
ClinicalAcute/Subacute (Juvenile): Fever, Lymphadenopathy, Hepatosplenomegaly; Chronic Adult (Adult): Pulmonary (Cavitary), Mucosal (Oral/Pharyngeal — "Mulberry" Ulcers), Adrenal Insufficiency, Lymphadenopathy
Microscopy"Pilot's Wheel" / "Mariner's Wheel" Yeast (Multiple Budding)
SyndromeTreatment
Acute/SubacuteAmphotericin B → Itraconazole 200mg BD × 6-12 Months
ChronicItraconazole 200mg BD × 6-18 Months

5. Talaromycosis (Penicilliosis marneffei)

FeatureDetails
AgentTalaromyces (Penicillium) marneffei
EndemicSE Asia (Thailand, Vietnam, Hong Kong, Taiwan, NE India)
HostHIV/AIDS (CD4<100) — AIDS-Defining Illness
ClinicalFever, Weight Loss, Anaemia, Skin Lesions (Umbilicated Papules, Central Necrosis), Lymphadenopathy, Hepatosplenomegaly, Respiratory, GI
DiagnosisCulture (Red Colony, Soluble Red Pigment), PCR, Histology (Yeast in Macrophages, Transverse Septa)
TreatmentLAmB 3-5mg/kg/day IV ×2w → Itraconazole 200mg BD ×10 Weeks (Secondary Prophylaxis: Itraconazole 200mg OD until Immune Reconstitution)

💡 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DimorphismMould at 25°C → Yeast at 37°C (Thermal Dimorphism)
HistoplasmaOhio/Mississippi Valleys, Bat/Bird Guano, Urine/Serum Ag (Gold Standard), Itraconazole
CoccidioidesSW USA (Valley Fever), Erythema Nodosum, Arthralgia, Fluconazole, Meningitis = Lifelong Suppression
BlastomycesGreat Lakes/SE USA, Broad-Based Budding Yeast, Itraconazole, Skin Lesions (Verrucous), Prostatitis
ParacoccidioidesLatin America, "Pilot's Wheel" Yeast, Mucosal Ulcers ("Mulberry"), Itraconazole
Talaromyces (Penicillium) marneffeiSE Asia, HIV/AIDS, Umbilicated Skin Papules, Red Pigment Culture, LAmB → Itraconazole
Treatment PrinciplesMild: Itraconazole; Severe/CNS: LAmB → Itraconazole; Long Duration (6-24 Months)
DiagnosisAntigen (Urine/Serum) → Rapid, Culture (Gold Standard), PCR
MorphologyHistoplasma: Small Yeast in Macrophage, Blastomyces: Broad-Based Budding, Paracoccidioides: Pilot's Wheel

❓ Viva Questions

  1. What is the endemic region for Histoplasmosis?
  • Ohio and Mississippi River Valleys (USA), also Central/South America, Africa, SE Asia.
  1. What is the classic radiological finding in Chronic Pulmonary Histoplasmosis?
  • Cavitary Lesions in Upper Lobes (Mimics TB).
  1. What is the diagnostic test of choice for Histoplasmosis?
  • Urine/Serum Histoplasma Antigen (High Sens/Spec, Rapid); Culture = Gold Standard.
  1. What is the endemic region for Coccidioidomycosis?
  • SW USA (CA, AZ, NV, NM, UT, TX) + Northern Mexico.
  1. What is the classic triad of Coccidioidomycosis ("Valley Fever")?
  • Fever, Cough, Erythema Nodosum (Plus Arthralgia = "Desert Rheumatism").
  1. What is the treatment for Coccidioidal Meningitis?
  • Fluconazole 800-1200mg OD Lifelong (Relapse Common if Stopped).
  1. What is the characteristic microscopic appearance of Blastomyces?
  • Broad-Based Budding Yeast (8-15µm), Thick Double Wall ("Broad-Based Budding").
  1. What is the endemic area for Blastomycosis?
  • Great Lakes, SE USA, Ohio/Mississippi River Valleys, Canada (Ontario, Manitoba).
  1. What is the characteristic skin lesion of Blastomycosis?
  • Verrucous, Ulcerated, Crusted ("Blastomycotic Dermatitis").
  1. Describe Paracoccidioidomycosis microscopy.
  • "Pilot's Wheel" / "Mariner's Wheel" Yeast (Multiple Budding Daughter Cells Around Mother Cell).
  1. What is the treatment for Paracoccidioidomycosis?
  • Itraconazole 200mg BD × 6-18 Months (Chronic); Amphotericin B → Itraconazole (Acute).
  1. Where is Talaromyces marneffei endemic and who does it affect?
  • SE Asia (Thailand, Vietnam, Hong Kong, Taiwan, NE India); HIV/AIDS (CD4<100) — AIDS-Defining.
  1. Differentiate Histoplasmosis from Blastomycosis on microscopy.
  • Histoplasma: Small Yeast (2-4µm) Inside Macrophages; Blastomyces: Large Yeast (8-15µm), Broad-Based Budding, Thick Wall, Extracellular.

🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Histoplasma vs BlastomycesHistoplasma: Small Intracellular Yeast (2-4µm) in Macrophages; Blastomyces: Large Extracellular Yeast (8-15µm), Broad-Based Budding
Coccidioides vs HistoplasmaCocci: SW USA, Erythema Nodosum, Arthralgia, CF Serology; Histo: Ohio/Mississippi Valleys, Bat Guano, Urine Antigen
Blastomyces vs ParacoccidioidesBlasto: Broad-Based Budding Yeast (8-15µm); Paracoccidioides: "Pilot's Wheel" Multiple Budding
Talaromyces vs PenicilliumT. marneffei = Former Penicillium marneffei; Red Pigment, Transverse Septa, SE Asia + HIV
Itraconazole vs FluconazoleItraconazole = 1st Line for Most Endemic Mycoses; Fluconazole = Coccidioidal Meningitis, Maintenance
MnemonicMeaning
"Histo = Ohio/Mississippi + Bat Guano + Urine Antigen"Histoplasma
"Cocci = SW USA + Valley Fever + Erythema Nodosum + Fluconazole"Coccidioides
"Blasto = Great Lakes + Broad-Based Budding + Skin Ulcers"Blastomyces
"Paracoccidioides = Latin America + Pilot's Wheel + Mucosal Ulcers"Paracoccidioides
"Talaromyces = SE Asia + HIV + Red Pigment + Umbilicated Papules"Talaromycosis
"Dimorphic = Mould 25°C → Yeast 37°C"Thermal Dimorphism

tags: [medicine, infectious-disease, davidson, chapter13, febrile-neutropenia, neutropenic-sepsis, oncology, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_chapter: Chapter 13: Infectious disease

topic_category: Immunocompromised Host Infections Domain

status: full-fcps-mrcp-topic-note

Febrile Neutropenia (Neutropenic Sepsis)

Related: [[Sepsis and Septic Shock]], [[Central Line-Associated Bloodstream Infection]], [[Invasive Fungal Infections]], [[Antimicrobial Stewardship]], [[Bone Marrow Transplant Infections]]

[!important]
Febrile Neutropenia (FN) = ONCOLOGIC EMERGENCY. Definition: ANC <0.5 ×10⁹/L (or <1.0 with predicted decline to <0.5) + Temperature ≥38.3°C (single) OR ≥38.0°C sustained ≥1h. Immediate IV antibiotics WITHIN 1 HOUR (piperacillin-tazobactam or meropenem or cefepime). Risk stratification: MASCC score (outpatient vs inpatient). Gram-neg coverage MANDATORY (Pseudomonas). Glycopeptide NOT routine initially. Duration: until ANC >0.5 ×10⁹/L + afebrile ≥48h (minimum 5–7d). Persistent fever: reassess at 48–72h, 4–7d, >7d (add antifungals).

Learning Objectives

  • Define febrile neutropenia using ANC and temperature criteria
  • Apply MASCC score for risk stratification (outpatient vs inpatient)
  • Initiate empirical IV antibiotics WITHIN 1 HOUR
  • Select appropriate empirical regimen (anti-pseudomonal β-lactam monotherapy preferred)
  • Know when to add glycopeptide (vancomycin) and antifungal therapy
  • Manage persistent fever (48–72h, 4–7d, >7d)
  • Determine treatment duration and discontinuation criteria
  • Implement infection prevention (prophylaxis, growth factors, central line care)

Definition & Diagnosis

CriterionThreshold
Absolute Neutrophil Count (ANC)<0.5 ×10⁹/L (or <1.0 ×10⁹/L with predicted decline to <0.5)
TemperatureSingle ≥38.3°C (101°F) OR Sustained ≥38.0°C (100.4°F) for ≥1 hour
Profound neutropeniaANC <0.1 ×10⁹/L
Prolonged neutropeniaDuration >7 days expected
[!critical]
FN = MEDICAL EMERGENCY. Antibiotics WITHIN 1 HOUR of presentation. Delay = ↑ mortality. Do NOT wait for cultures, imaging, or ANC confirmation if clinical suspicion.

Risk Stratification — MASCC Score

Multinational Association for Supportive Care in Cancer (MASCC) Score — identifies low-risk patients for outpatient/oral management

FactorPoints
Burden of illness (no/minor symptoms = 5; moderate = 3; severe = 0)0–5
No hypotension (SBP >90 mmHg)5
No COPD4
Solid tumour / Haematologic malignancy with no previous fungal infection4
No dehydration requiring parenteral fluids3
Outpatient status at onset3
Age <60 years2
Total ScoreRiskManagement
≥21Low riskOutpatient oral antibiotics possible (if safe follow-up)
<21High riskInpatient IV antibiotics mandatory
[!tip]
MASCC validated for solid tumours and lymphoma; less reliable for acute leukaemia / transplant. Clinical judgement > score. Low-risk criteria also: MASCC ≥21, no comorbidities, reliable follow-up, able to take oral, no hypotension.

Empirical Antibiotic Therapy (START WITHIN 1 HOUR)

Monotherapy (Preferred — IDSA/ESMO/NICE)

AgentDoseSpectrum
Piperacillin-tazobactam4.5g IV 6h (extended infusion 4h preferred)Gram-neg (Pseudomonas) + Anaerobes + some Gram-pos + Enterococcus
Meropenem1g IV 8h (or 2g IV 8h if Pseudomonas bacteremia)Gram-neg (Pseudomonas, ESBL) + Anaerobes (NO Enterococcus)
Cefepime2g IV 8h (or 2g IV 12h)Gram-neg (Pseudomonas) + Gram-pos (weaker anaerobes, NO Enterococcus)
[!key]
Anti-pseudomonal β-lactam MONOTHERAPY = standard. Carbapenem preferred if: ESBL risk, prior carbapenem use, severe sepsis, haemodynamic instability. Avoid cefepime if severe sepsis (mortality signal in meta-analyses). Pip-tazo = broadest (covers Enterococcus, anaerobes).

Combination Therapy (Only if Indicated)

IndicationAdd
Suspected catheter-related infection (tunnel infection, port pocket infection)Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg IV 6h
Known MRSA colonisation / prior MRSA infectionVancomycin
Skin/soft tissue infectionVancomycin
Haemodynamic instability / septic shockVancomycin (some guidelines)
Pneumonia (infiltrate on CXR)Vancomycin (if MRSA risk) +/− Azithromycin/Levofloxacin (atypical)
Gram-positive bacteremia before IDVancomycin
[!warning]
Vancomycin NOT routine initially. Add only if specific indication. If added, review at 48–72h — stop if cultures negative for Gram-positive.

Penicillin Allergy (Anaphylaxis)

RegimenDose
Aztreonam 2g IV 8h + Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg IV 6hCovers Gram-neg (Pseudomonas) + Gram-pos (no anaerobes)
OR Ciprofloxacin 400mg IV 12h + Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg IV 6h(Ciprofloxacin less reliable for Pseudomonas monotherapy)

Initial Workup (Do NOT Delay Antibiotics)

TestTiming
Blood cultures×2 sets (peripheral + each lumen) BEFORE antibiotics
Urine cultureIf urinary symptoms
Sputum cultureIf productive cough
Stool culture / C. difficile PCRIf diarrhoea
CXRIf respiratory symptoms (portable if unstable)
Peripheral blood filmConfirm neutropenia
Lactate, CRP, PCT, LFTs, U&Es, CoagulationBaseline
Central line culturesIf line in situ (peripheral + each lumen)

Reassessment & Escalation

At 48–72 Hours

ScenarioAction
Afebrile, clinically stable, cultures negativeContinue empirical monotherapy
Afebrile, positive culturesDe-escalate to targeted therapy
Persistent fever (≥38.3°C), haemodynamically stableReassess: repeat cultures, CXR, CT if indicated; continue empirical monotherapy
Persistent fever, haemodynamically UNSTABLEEscalate: broaden Gram-neg (e.g., Meropenem if on Pip-tazo), ADD Vancomycin, consider antifungal, image for source

At 4–7 Days (Persistent Fever)

ActionIndication
Empirical ANTIFUNGAL (Echinocandin)Persistent fever ≥4–7 days despite broad antibiotics, no identified source, profound/prolonged neutropenia (ANC <0.1 ×10⁹/L >7d expected)
Preferred antifungalCaspofungin 70mg load then 50mg IV OD OR Micafungin 100mg IV OD OR Anidulafungin 200mg load then 100mg IV OD
AlternativeLiposomal Amphotericin B 3mg/kg IV OD (if echinocandin contraindicated)
AvoidFluconazole (poor mould coverage), Voriconazole (drug interactions) unless mould suspected

Beyond 7 Days (Prolonged Fever)

ConsiderInvestigation
Invasive mould infection (Aspergillus, Mucorales)CT chest/sinuses, Galactomannan (serum/BAL), β-D-glucan
Resistant bacteria (VRE, CRE, MDR Pseudomonas)Culture review, rectal swabs for MDRO screening
Viral (HSV, VZV, CMV, HHV-6, respiratory viruses)PCR panels,-CSF if neuro symptoms
Non-infectious (drug fever, transfusion reaction, malignancy)Drug review, tryptase, imaging

Treatment Duration & Discontinuation

Criteria for STOPPING AntibioticsRequirement
ANC >0.5 ×10⁹/L (recovering)AND
Afebrile ≥48 hoursAND
Minimum 5–7 days of antibioticsAND
No active infection sourceAND
Haemodynamically stable→ STOP
[!key]
Do NOT continue antibiotics just because neutropenia persists if afebrile and stable. Antifungals: continue until ANC >0.5 ×10⁹/L if started empirically. If documented fungal infection: full course per pathogen.

Central Line Management in FN

ScenarioAction
Catheter-related bloodstream infection (CRBSI) suspectedBlood cultures: peripheral + each lumen; if differential time to positivity >2h OR quantitative ratio >5:1 → CRBSI
Tunnel infection / Port pocket infectionRemove line + antibiotics
Uncomplicated CRBSI (coagulase-negative Staph, no septic shock, no endocarditis)Antibiotics through line + systemic; consider line salvage
S. aureus / Pseudomonas / Candida / Bacillus / Mycobacteria CRBSIREMOVE LINE + antibiotics
Line removalGuidewire exchange NOT recommended in FN

Antimicrobial Prophylaxis in Neutropenia

ProphylaxisIndicationAgent
Antibacterial (fluoroquinolone)High-risk (ANC <0.1 ×10⁹/L expected >7d): acute leukaemia induction, autologous/allogeneic transplantCiprofloxacin 500mg PO 12h (or Levofloxacin 500mg PO OD)
Antifungal (mould-active)High-risk prolonged neutropenia: allogeneic transplant, AML induction, relapsed leukaemiaPosaconazole 300mg PO OD (delayed-release tablet) OR Voriconazole (TDG)
Antiviral (HSV/VZV)Seropositive for HSV/VZVAcyclovir 400mg PO 12h (or Valacyclovir)
PJP prophylaxisAll allogeneic transplant, lymphopenia (CD4<200), prolonged steroidsTMP-SMX 160/800mg PO 3×/week (or Dapsone/Aerosolised pentamidine)
G-CSF (Growth factor)Secondary prophylaxis (prior FN) OR Primary if high-risk regimen (FN risk >20%)Filgrastim 5mcg/kg SC OD OR Pegfilgrastim 6mg SC single dose per cycle
[!tip]
Ciprofloxacin prophylaxis reduces Gram-neg bacteremia but ↑ resistance. Posaconazole reduces invasive fungal infections in high-risk. G-CSF: secondary prophylaxis standard; primary prophylaxis for regimens with >20% FN risk.

Special Scenarios

ScenarioModification
Autologous stem cell transplantSimilar to high-risk FN; fluoroquinolone prophylaxis standard
Allogeneic stem cell transplantHigher fungal risk (GVHD, steroids); posaconazole prophylaxis; CMV monitoring
CAR-T cell therapyCytokine release syndrome (CRS) mimics FN; tocilizumab for CRS; antibiotics for FN criteria
Typhlitis (neutropenic enterocolitis)CT: bowel wall thickening, fat stranding; Pip-tazo/Meropenem ± Vancomycin; surgery if perforation
Perirectal abscessHigh risk in neutropenia; avoid digital rectal exam; CT/MRI; antibiotics + drainage if feasible

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • FN definition: ANC <0.5 + Temp ≥38.3°C (single) or ≥38.0°C sustained 1h
  • EMERGENCY: IV antibiotics WITHIN 1 HOUR
  • Empirical MONOTHERAPY: Pip-tazo 4.5g 6h (preferred) OR Meropenem 1g 8h OR Cefepime 2g 8h
  • Vancomycin NOT routine; add if: catheter infection, MRSA risk, SSTI, shock, pneumonia, Gram-positive bacteremia
  • MASCC ≥21 = low risk → outpatient oral possible (ciprofloxacin + amoxicillin-clavulanate)
  • Reassess 48–72h: persistent fever stable → continue; unstable → broaden + vancomycin + image
  • Empirical antifungal (echinocandin) at 4–7d persistent fever + profound/prolonged neutropenia
  • Duration: until ANC >0.5 + afebrile 48h + minimum 5–7d
  • Prophylaxis: Ciprofloxacin (high-risk neutropenia), Posaconazole (mould-active, transplant/AML), Acyclovir (HSV+), TMP-SMX (PJP)
  • CRBSI: S. aureus/Pseudomonas/Candida → remove line; CoNS → consider salvage

Common Viva Questions

  1. Definition of febrile neutropenia? ANC <0.5 ×10⁹/L + Temp ≥38.3°C (single) or ≥38.0°C sustained 1 hour.
  2. Time to antibiotics? Within 1 hour of presentation.
  3. Preferred empirical regimen? Anti-pseudomonal β-lactam monotherapy: Pip-tazo 4.5g 6h OR Meropenem 1g 8h OR Cefepime 2g 8h.
  4. When do you add vancomycin? Catheter infection, known MRSA, SSTI, haemodynamic instability, pneumonia, Gram-positive bacteremia.
  5. MASCC score ≥21 means? Low risk → outpatient oral antibiotics possible.
  6. When to start empirical antifungal? Persistent fever ≥4–7 days despite antibiotics + profound/prolonged neutropenia (ANC<0.1 >7d expected).
  7. Preferred empirical antifungal? Echinocandin (Caspofungin/Micafungin/Anidulafungin).
  8. When to stop antibiotics? ANC >0.5 + afebrile ≥48h + minimum 5–7 days + no active infection.
  9. CRBSI with S. aureus — line management? REMOVE LINE + antibiotics.
  10. Prophylaxis for high-risk neutropenia (AML induction)? Ciprofloxacin + Posaconazole + Acyclovir (if HSV+) + TMP-SMX (PJP).

Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
Wait for ANC confirmation before antibioticsSTART antibiotics immediately on clinical suspicion; do NOT wait
Vancomycin routine in all FNNOT routine; only with specific indications
Dual Gram-neg coverage standardMonotherapy (anti-pseudomonal β-lactam) = standard
Cefepime preferred in septic shockAvoid cefepime in severe sepsis/shock (mortality signal); use Meropenem or Pip-tazo
MASCC ≥21 = automatic outpatientClinical judgement required; need reliable follow-up, oral tolerance, no comorbidities
Stop antibiotics when ANC still <0.5Stop if afebrile 48h + stable + min 5–7d; do NOT wait for ANC recovery if stable
Fluconazole for empirical antifungalEchinocandin preferred (mould coverage); fluconazole only for Candida
Continue antifungals indefinitelyStop empirical antifungals when ANC >0.5; continue only if documented infection
Guidewire exchange for CRBSI in FNNOT recommended in neutropenic patients
Ciprofloxacin prophylaxis for all chemoOnly high-risk (ANC<0.1 expected >7d): AML induction, transplant

Mnemonics

  • FN DEFINITION: ANC <0.5 + Temp ≥38.3 (single) / ≥38.0 (sustained 1h)
  • FN EMERGENCY: Antibiotics Within 1 Hour
  • EMPIRICAL MONO: Pip-tazo / Mero / Cefepime (anti-Pseudo β-lactam)
  • VANCO ADD: Catheter, MRSA, SSTI, Shock, Pneumonia, Gram+ bacteremia
  • MASCC: ≥21 = LOW risk = Outpatient possible
  • REASSESS: 48-72h (persistent fever stable→continue; unstable→broaden+Vanco+image); 4-7d (add Echinocandin); >7d (mould workup)
  • DURATION: ANC>0.5 + AFEBRILE 48h + MIN 5-7d → STOP
  • ANTIFUNGAL: Echinocandin (Casp/Mica/Anidula) for empirical; L-AmB alternative
  • PROPHYLAXIS: Cipro (high-risk neutro), Posaconazole (mould, transplant/AML), Acyclovir (HSV+), TMP-SMX (PJP)
  • CRBSI: Staph/Pseudo/Candida → Remove line; CoNS → Salvage attempt

Suggested Visuals / Image Notes

Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • FN definition
  • Antibiotics within 1 hour
  • Empirical monotherapy (Pip-tazo/Mero/Cefepime)
  • Vancomycin NOT routine (6 indications)
  • MASCC ≥21 low risk
  • 48-72h, 4-7d, >7d reassessment
  • Echinocandin at 4-7d persistent fever
  • Duration: ANC>0.5 + afebrile 48h + min 5-7d
  • Prophylaxis: Cipro, Posaconazole, Acyclovir, TMP-SMX
  • CRBSI: S.aureus/Pseudo/Candida → remove

Should Know

  • MASCC score components
  • Cefepime avoidance in shock
  • Typhlitis recognition
  • CAR-T CRS vs FN
  • Antifungal duration rules
  • G-CSF indications
  • Rectal swab MDRO screening

Nice to Know

  • Novel antifungals (rezafungin, ibrexafungerp)
  • Microbiome preservation strategies
  • Outpatient FN protocols
  • Resistance patterns with fluoroquinolone prophylaxis
  • Duration of posaconazole prophylaxis post-transplant

My Weak Points

  • [ ] Exact MASCC point breakdown
  • [ ] Cefepime mortality signal evidence
  • [ ] Galactomannan/β-D-glucan interpretation cutoffs
  • [ ] CAR-T CRS grading and tocilizumab dosing
  • [ ] Typhlitis surgical indications detail

Self-Test Scorecard

  • Understanding: /10
  • Recall: /10
  • MCQ Performance: /10
  • SBA Performance: /10
  • Viva Confidence: /10
  • Total: /50
[!tip]
Interpretation: <35 = weak topic, 35-44 = acceptable but insecure, 45+ = strong exam-ready topic.

Exam Answer Modes

Long Answer Skeleton

  1. Definition and diagnostic criteria
  2. Emergency management (1-hour rule, cultures, empirical monotherapy)
  3. Risk stratification (MASCC score)
  4. Vancomycin add-on indications
  5. Reassessment timeline (48-72h, 4-7d, >7d)
  6. Empirical antifungal therapy (echinocandin)
  7. Duration and discontinuation criteria
  8. Prophylaxis (antibacterial, antifungal, antiviral, PJP, G-CSF)
  9. CRBSI management in neutropenia
  10. Special scenarios (transplant, CAR-T, typhlitis)

Short Note Skeleton

  • FN: ANC<0.5 + Temp≥38.3/38.0 → Abx 1h
  • Empirical: Pip-tazo/Mero/Cefepime (mono)
  • Vanco add: Catheter, MRSA, SSTI, Shock, Pneumo, Gram+ bact
  • MASCC ≥21 = low risk → outpatient oral (Cipro+Amox-Clav)
  • 48-72h: stable→continue; unstable→broaden+Vanco+image
  • 4-7d: Echinocandin (profound/prolonged neutro)
  • >7d: Mould workup
  • Stop: ANC>0.5 + afebrile 48h + min 5-7d
  • Prophylaxis: Cipro, Posaconazole, Acyclovir, TMP-SMX
  • CRBSI: S.aureus/Pseudo/Candida → remove; CoNS → salvage

Viva One-Liners

  • FN: ANC<0.5 + Temp≥38.3/38.0 → Abx 1h
  • Empirical: Pip-tazo/Mero/Cefepime mono
  • Vanco: Catheter, MRSA, SSTI, Shock, Pneumo, Gram+ bact
  • MASCC ≥21 = outpatient
  • 4-7d persistent fever + profound neutro → Echinocandin
  • Stop: ANC>0.5 + afebrile 48h + 5-7d
  • Prophylaxis: Cipro, Posaconazole, Acyclovir, TMP-SMX
  • CRBSI: S.aureus/Pseudo/Candida → remove

Ward-Case Discussion Points

  • 45M, AML induction day 12, ANC 0.1, Temp 39°C → Blood cx ×2, Pip-tazo 4.5g 6h IV stat, MASCC score, admit
  • 60F, breast chemo, ANC 0.3, Temp 38.5°C, MASCC 23, reliable, oral tolerance → Outpatient: Ciprofloxacin 500mg 12h + Amox-Clav 625mg 8h ×5d, daily review
  • 30M, allogeneic transplant day 15, persistent fever day 5 on Pip-tazo, ANC 0.05 → Add Caspofungin 70/50mg IV OD; CT chest, Galactomannan
  • 50M, CRBSI (S. aureus in peripheral + line, DTP 1h), FN → REMOVE line + Vancomycin + anti-pseudomonal β-lactam

Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet

FN: ANC<0.5 + Temp≥38.3/38.0 → Abx 1h. Empirical mono: Pip-tazo/Mero/Cefepime. Vanco add: Catheter, MRSA, SSTI, Shock, Pneumo, Gram+ bacteremia. MASCC ≥21 = low risk → outpatient Cipro+Amox-Clav. 48-72h: persistent fever stable→continue; unstable→broaden+Vanco+image. 4-7d: Echinocandin (profound/prolonged neutro). >7d: Mould workup (CT, Galactomannan, β-D-glucan). Stop: ANC>0.5 + afebrile 48h + min 5-7d. Prophylaxis: Cipro (high-risk), Posaconazole (mould), Acyclovir (HSV), TMP-SMX (PJP). CRBSI: S.aureus/Pseudo/Candida → REMOVE; CoNS → salvage.

Summary

Febrile neutropenia (FN) = ONCOLOGIC EMERGENCY. Definition: Absolute Neutrophil Count (ANC) <0.5 ×10⁹/L (or <1.0 with predicted decline to <0.5) AND Temperature ≥38.3°C (single) OR ≥38.0°C sustained ≥1 hour. Immediate management: IV antibiotics WITHIN 1 HOUR. Blood cultures ×2 sets (peripheral + each lumen) before antibiotics. Empirical monotherapy (preferred): Anti-pseudomonal β-lactam — Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV 6h (extended infusion preferred) OR Meropenem 1g IV 8h OR Cefepime 2g IV 8h. Cefepime avoid in septic shock. Vancomycin NOT routine — add only if: catheter-related infection, known MRSA, skin/soft tissue infection, haemodynamic instability, pneumonia, Gram-positive bacteremia. Risk stratification: MASCC score ≥21 = low risk → consider outpatient oral antibiotics (Ciprofloxacin + Amoxicillin-clavulanate) if reliable follow-up, oral tolerance, no comorbidities. Reassessment: 48–72h: persistent fever + stable → continue monotherapy; haemodynamically unstable → broaden Gram-neg, ADD Vancomycin, image for source. 4–7 days: persistent fever + profound/prolonged neutropenia (ANC <0.1 ×10⁹/L expected >7d) → ADD empirical antifungal: Echinocandin (Caspofungin 70/50mg, Micafungin 100mg, Anidulafungin 200/100mg IV OD). >7 days: consider invasive mould infection — CT chest/sinuses, Galactomannan, β-D-glucan. Duration: Continue until ANC >0.5 ×10⁹/L AND afebrile ≥48h AND minimum 5–7 days (stop if stable even if neutropenia persists). Prophylaxis: Ciprofloxacin (high-risk: ANC <0.1 expected >7d — AML induction, transplant); Posaconazole (mould-active — allogeneic transplant, AML induction); Acyclovir (HSV/VZV seropositive); TMP-SMX (PJP — allogeneic transplant, CD4<200, prolonged steroids); G-CSF (secondary prophylaxis, or primary if regimen FN risk >20%). CRBSI: S. aureus, Pseudomonas, Candida, Bacillus, Mycobacteria → REMOVE LINE; CoNS → consider salvage (antibiotics through line + systemic).

MCQs (10)

  1. Febrile neutropenia is defined as ANC <0.5 ×10⁹/L plus:

A. Temperature ≥38.0°C single reading

B. Temperature ≥38.3°C single OR ≥38.0°C sustained ≥1 hour

C. Temperature ≥39.0°C

D. Temperature ≥38.0°C sustained ≥4 hours

E. Any fever with rigors

  1. Time limit for administering IV antibiotics in febrile neutropenia:

A. 30 minutes

B. 1 hour

C. 2 hours

D. 4 hours

E. 6 hours

  1. Preferred empirical monotherapy for febrile neutropenia:

A. Ceftriaxone + Vancomycin

B. Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV 6h (or Meropenem 1g IV 8h / Cefepime 2g IV 8h)

C. Ciprofloxacin + Clindamycin

C. Vancomycin + Metronidazole

E. Ceftriaxone + Gentamicin

  1. MASCC score ≥21 indicates:

A. High risk, mandatory ICU admission

B. Low risk, outpatient oral antibiotics possible

C. Need for empirical antifungal

D. Need for vancomycin

E. Prolonged neutropenia expected

  1. Vancomycin should be ADDED to initial empirical therapy in ALL EXCEPT:

A. Suspected catheter-related infection

B. Known MRSA colonisation

C. Skin and soft tissue infection

D. Uncomplicated febrile neutropenia without specific indications

E. Haemodynamic instability

  1. Empirical antifungal therapy should be started at:

A. 24 hours persistent fever

B. 4–7 days persistent fever + profound/prolonged neutropenia

C. 14 days persistent fever

D. Any fever in transplant patient

E. First positive Galactomannan

  1. Preferred empirical antifungal for persistent fever in neutropenia:

A. Fluconazole

B. Voriconazole

C. Echinocandin (Caspofungin/Micafungin/Anidulafungin)

D. Liposomal Amphotericin B

E. Posaconazole

  1. Antibiotics can be stopped in febrile neutropenia when:

A. ANC >1.0 ×10⁹/L

B. ANC >0.5 ×10⁹/L + Afebrile ≥48h + minimum 5–7 days antibiotics

C. Temperature normal for 24 hours

D. CRP <10

E. Blood cultures negative at 48h

  1. CRBSI with Staphylococcus aureus in a neutropenic patient — line management:

A. Antibiotic lock therapy

B. Guidewire exchange

C. Remove line + systemic antibiotics

D. Continue line with systemic antibiotics

E. Remove only if persistent bacteremia

  1. Fluoroquinolone prophylaxis (ciprofloxacin) is indicated for:

A. All chemotherapy patients

B. High-risk neutropenia (ANC <0.1 expected >7d): AML induction, transplant

C. Low-risk solid tumour chemotherapy

D. Only if previous FN episode

E. All hospitalised cancer patients

SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 50-year-old man on day 10 of AML induction chemotherapy develops temperature 38.5°C. ANC 0.2 ×10⁹/L. Blood cultures taken. Next step?

A. Wait for ANC confirmation

B. Start Pip-tazo 4.5g IV 6h immediately

C. Start Ceftriaxone + Vancomycin

D. Start oral Ciprofloxacin + Amox-Clav

E. Observe for 1 hour

  1. A 35-year-old woman with breast cancer (cycle 2) presents with fever 38.4°C, ANC 0.3. MASCC score 24. No comorbidities, reliable, oral tolerance. Management?

A. Admit for IV Pip-tazo

B. Outpatient oral: Ciprofloxacin 500mg 12h + Amox-Clav 625mg 8h ×5d, daily review

C. Admit for IV Meropenem

D. Oral Ciprofloxacin alone

E. Oral Amox-Clav alone

  1. A neutropenic patient (ANC 0.1) on Pip-tazo for 3 days remains febrile (38.6°C), haemodynamically stable, cultures negative. Action?

A. Add Vancomycin

B. Switch to Meropenem

C. Continue Pip-tazo; reassess at day 4–7 for antifungal

D. Add Caspofungin now

E. Add Fluconazole

  1. A 45-year-old man post-allogeneic transplant (day 20) has persistent fever since day 5 on Meropenem. ANC <0.1. No source identified. Best next step?

A. Add Vancomycin

B. Add Caspofungin 70mg load then 50mg IV OD (empirical antifungal)

C. Switch to Pip-tazo

D. Add Fluconazole

E. Add Voriconazole

  1. A neutropenic patient becomes afebrile on day 4 of Pip-tazo. ANC 0.3. Cultures negative. When to stop antibiotics?

A. Now (afebrile 24h)

B. When ANC >0.5 + afebrile ≥48h + minimum 5–7 days total

C. Day 7 regardless

D. Day 10 regardless

E. When ANC >1.0

  1. CRBSI in neutropenic patient: blood cultures from peripheral and line both grow CoNS, differential time to positivity 4 hours. Line management?

A. Consider line salvage (antibiotics through line + systemic)

B. Remove line immediately

C. Guidewire exchange

D. Remove only if fever persists 48h

E. Antibiotic lock only

  1. CRBSI in neutropenic patient: Candida albicans grown from line and peripheral. Line management?

A. Salvage with echinocandin lock

B. Remove line + systemic echinocandin

C. Guidewire exchange

D. Continue line with fluconazole

E. Remove only if persistent candidemia

  1. High-risk neutropenia prophylaxis regimen for AML induction:

A. Ciprofloxacin only

B. Ciprofloxacin + Posaconazole + Acyclovir (if HSV+) + TMP-SMX (PJP)

C. Ciprofloxacin + Fluconazole

D. TMP-SMX only

E. No prophylaxis recommended

  1. Persistent fever >7 days in neutropenic patient despite broad antibiotics and echinocandin. Next investigation?

A. Repeat blood cultures only

B. CT chest/sinuses + Galactomannan + β-D-glucan (invasive mould workup)

C. Bone marrow biopsy

D. PET-CT

E. Lumbar puncture

  1. Cefepime use in febrile neutropenia — which statement is correct?

A. Preferred in septic shock

B. Avoid in severe sepsis/septic shock (mortality signal)

C. Superior to Pip-tazo for Pseudomonas

D. Covers anaerobes well

E. Preferred over Meropenem for ESBL

Flashcards

  • Q: FN definition

A: ANC<0.5 + Temp≥38.3 (single) or ≥38.0 (1h)

  • Q: FN emergency

A: IV Abx within 1 hour

  • Q: Empirical mono

A: Pip-tazo/Mero/Cefepime (anti-Pseudo β-lactam)

  • Q: Vanco add indications

A: Catheter, MRSA, SSTI, Shock, Pneumo, Gram+ bacteremia

  • Q: MASCC

A: ≥21 = low risk → outpatient oral possible

  • Q: 48-72h reassess

A: Stable→continue; Unstable→broaden+Vanco+image

  • Q: 4-7d persistent fever

A: ADD Echinocandin (profound/prolonged neutro)

  • Q: >7d

A: Mould workup (CT, Galactomannan, β-D-glucan)

  • Q: Duration stop criteria

A: ANC>0.5 + Afebrile 48h + Min 5-7d

  • Q: Prophylaxis

A: Cipro, Posaconazole, Acyclovir, TMP-SMX, G-CSF

  • Q: CRBSI S.aureus/Pseudo/Candida

A: REMOVE line

  • Q: CRBSI CoNS

A: Salvage attempt

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — FN definition: ANC <0.5 ×10⁹/L + Temp ≥38.3°C (single) OR ≥38.0°C sustained ≥1 hour.
  2. B — IV antibiotics within 1 hour of presentation (IDSA/ESMO/NICE).
  3. B — Anti-pseudomonal β-lactam monotherapy: Pip-tazo, Meropenem, or Cefepime. Ceftriaxone NOT anti-pseudomonal.
  4. B — MASCC ≥21 = low risk; outpatient oral antibiotics possible if clinically appropriate.
  5. D — Vancomycin NOT routinely added; only with specific indications (catheter, MRSA, SSTI, shock, pneumonia, Gram+ bacteremia).
  6. B — Empirical antifungal at 4–7 days persistent fever + profound/prolonged neutropenia (ANC <0.1 expected >7d).
  7. C — Echinocandin (Caspofungin/Micafungin/Anidulafungin) preferred for empirical antifungal in neutropenia (mould coverage, fungicidal, no drug interactions).
  8. B — Stop when ANC >0.5 ×10⁹/L AND afebrile ≥48h AND minimum 5–7 days antibiotics. Do NOT wait for ANC >1.0 if stable.
  9. C — S. aureus CRBSI in neutropenia → REMOVE LINE + systemic antibiotics.
  10. B — Ciprofloxacin prophylaxis for high-risk: ANC <0.1 ×10⁹/L expected >7d (AML induction, autologous/allogeneic transplant).

SBAs

  1. B — FN confirmed (ANC<0.5, Temp≥38.3): immediate Pip-tazo IV. Do not wait.
  2. B — MASCC 24 + no comorbidities + oral tolerance = low risk → outpatient oral (Cipro + Amox-Clav) with daily review.
  3. C — Day 3 (48-72h), haemodynamically stable, cultures negative: continue monotherapy. Antifungal at 4-7d if persistent.
  4. B — Day 20 post-allogeneic transplant, persistent fever day 5, ANC<0.1, no source: add empirical echinocandin (Caspofungin/Micafungin/Anidulafungin).
  5. B — Stop criteria: ANC>0.5 + afebrile ≥48h + min 5-7d. Day 4 afebrile, ANC 0.3 → continue until ANC recovers.
  6. A — CoNS CRBSI with DTP 4h: consider line salvage (systemic + lock antibiotics). Remove if S. aureus/Pseudomonas/Candida.
  7. B — Candida CRBSI → REMOVE LINE + systemic echinocandin. Salvage not recommended for Candida.
  8. B — AML induction: Ciprofloxacin (antibacterial) + Posaconazole (mould-active antifungal) + Acyclovir (HSV) + TMP-SMX (PJP).
  9. B — >7d persistent fever + antifungals: invasive mould workup — CT chest/sinuses, serum Galactomannan, β-D-glucan.
  10. B — Cefepime associated with higher mortality in severe sepsis/septic shock meta-analyses; avoid in shock. Pip-tazo or Meropenem preferred.

davidson_chapter: Chapter 13: Infectious disease

topic_category: General Principles / Clinical Approach Domain

status: full-fcps-mrcp-topic-note

Fever and Septic Syndrome Approach

Related: [[Sepsis and Septic Shock]], [[Acute Bacterial Meningitis]], [[Infective Endocarditis]], [[Tuberculosis (Pulmonary and Extrapulmonary)],], [[HIV-Associated Opportunistic Infections]], [[Fungal Pneumonias]], [[Pyrexia of Unknown Origin (PUO)]]

[!important]
Fever = core temperature ≥38.3°C (101°F) or ≥38.0°C sustained. Septic syndrome = fever + systemic inflammation ± organ dysfunction. Structured approach: history → examination → targeted investigations → empirical therapy → source identification. Do NOT treat fever alone — treat the underlying cause.

Learning Objectives

  • Define fever, hyperpyrexia, hyperthermia, and septic syndrome
  • Apply structured clinical approach: history (onset, pattern, travel, exposures), examination (focus, fundi, skin, joints, neurological), investigations (stepwise)
  • Recognise red flags for urgent intervention (sepsis, meningitis, endocarditis, necrotising infection)
  • Select empirical antibiotics by syndrome and local epidemiology
  • Differentiate infectious from non-infectious fever (drug, malignancy, autoimmune, thromboembolic)
  • Approach pyrexia of unknown origin (PUO) systematically

Definitions

TermDefinition
FeverCore temperature ≥38.3°C (101°F) once OR ≥38.0°C for ≥1 hour
HyperpyrexiaTemperature ≥41.5°C (106.7°F) — medical emergency
HyperthermiaUncontrolled temperature rise (failed thermoregulation): heat stroke, NMS, MH, thyrotoxicosis — no hypothalamic set-point change; antipyretics INEFFECTIVE
Septic syndromeFever + SIRS criteria (2/4: T>38, HR>90, RR>20, WCC>12/<4) ± organ dysfunction
SIRSSystemic Inflammatory Response Syndrome — non-specific
PUO (Classic)Fever ≥38.3°C on multiple occasions >3 weeks, diagnosis uncertain after 1 week inpatient investigation
PUO (Modern)Fever >3 weeks (outpatient) or >3 days (inpatient immunocompromised) without diagnosis after standard workup

Fever Patterns (Historical — Limited Specificity)

PatternDescriptionClassic Associations
ContinuousMinimal diurnal variation (<1°C)Typhoid, TB, bacterial endocarditis, pneumonia
RemittentDiurnal variation >1°C but never normalInfective endocarditis, TB, intra-abdominal abscess
IntermittentSpikes to normal dailyMalaria, abscess, TB, urinary infection
QuotidianDaily spikesMalaria (falciparum), sepsis
TertianEvery 48h (every 3rd day)P. vivax/ovale malaria
QuartanEvery 72h (every 4th day)P. malariae
RelapsingFebrile periods separated by afebrile daysBorrelia (relapsing fever), rat-bite fever
Pel-EbsteinWeek febrile, week afebrileHodgkin lymphoma
[!tip]
Fever patterns have POOR sensitivity/specificity in modern practice — do not rely for diagnosis.

Structured Clinical Approach

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Patient with fever
↓
IMMEDIATE: ABCDE, vitals, sepsis screen (qSOFA/SOFA)
↓
Septic shock / red flags?
↓
Activate sepsis bundle: lactate, Cx, abx 1h, fluids, vasopressors
Detailed History
↓
Focused Examination
↓
Stepwise Investigations
↓
Syndrome-based Empirical Therapy
↓
Source Identification & Targeted Therapy

1. History — Key Domains

DomainCritical Questions
Onset & PatternAcute vs subacute vs chronic; rigors (bacteraemia); night sweats (TB, lymphoma)
Localising SymptomsCough/pleurisy (resp), dysuria/flank pain (UTI), abdominal pain (IAI), headache/neck stiffness (CNS), joint pain (septic arthritis)
Travel & GeographyMalaria (endemic + <1y), dengue, typhoid, TB, rickettsial, schistosomiasis, viral haemorrhagic fevers
ExposuresAnimal contact (brucellosis, leptospirosis, Q fever), ticks (rickettsial, Lyme), freshwater (leptospira, schisto), sexual (STI, HIV), IVDU (endocarditis, abscesses)
Host FactorsImmunosuppression (steroids, chemo, transplant, HIV), diabetes, cirrhosis, asplenia, prosthetic devices, recent antibiotics/hospitalisation/surgery
Drug HistoryDrug fever (beta-lactams, sulfonamides, allopurinol, anticonvulsants, ATT, checkpoint inhibitors) — diagnosis of exclusion
Vaccination StatusCOVID, influenza, pneumococcal, meningococcal, Hib, typhoid, yellow fever

2. Examination — Systematic & Targeted

SystemKey Signs
GeneralTemperature (core), rigors, haemodynamic status, hydration, cachexia
SkinPetechiae/purpura (meningococcal, DIC), erythema migrans (Lyme), eschar (rickettsial, scrub typhus), necrotising fasciitis (pain↑, crepitus, bronzing), cellulitis, abscess, drug rash, Janeway/Osler nodes (endocarditis), splinter haemorrhages, Roth spots
EyesFundoscopy: Roth spots, papilloedema (ICP), CMV retinitis, endophthalmitis; conjunctival haemorrhage (meningococcal, leptospira)
ENTPharyngitis, peritonsillar abscess, sinus tenderness, otitis media
NeckStiffness (meningitis), lymphadenopathy (local/generalised), JVP
RespiratoryConsolidation, effusion, wheeze, cavity signs
CardiovascularMurmurs (new/regurgitant = endocarditis), pericardial rub, signs of heart failure
AbdomenHepatosplenomegaly, tenderness, guarding, masses, ascites, peritonitis
NeurologicalGCS, meningeal signs (Kernig, Brudzinski), focal deficits, cranial nerves
MusculoskeletalHot swollen joint (septic arthritis — emergency), osteomyelitis, discitis
GenitourinaryUrethral discharge, epididymo-orchitis, pelvic exam (PID)
DevicesLine sites (erythema, purulence), urinary catheter, drains, prostheses

3. Stepwise Investigations

TierTestsIndication
Tier 1 (All)FBC + diff, CRP, PCT, U&E, LFTs, Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺/PO₄, glucose, blood cultures ×2–3 sets (aerobic/anaerobic) BEFORE antibiotics, lactate, coagulation, urinalysis + culture, CXRBaseline for all
Tier 2 (Syndrome-driven)LP (CNS symptoms), echo (new murmur, IVDU, embolic phenomena), CT abdomen/pelvis (abdominal symptoms), joint aspiration (hot joint), sputum (cough), stool (diarrhoea), wound/line tip culturesTargeted
Tier 3 (PUO / Unresolved)HIV, ANA, ANCA, RF, ACE, ferritin, LDH, urine TB PCR/GeneXpert, CT TAP, PET-CT, bone marrow (if cytopenias), temporal artery biopsy (if >50), serology (brucella, leptospira, rickettsia, EBV, CMV, HIV, syphilis), IGRA/TSTPUO protocol
Tier 4 (Specialised)16S rRNA PCR (culture-negative), metagenomic sequencing, autoantibody panel, lymph node biopsy, liver biopsyRefractory

Syndrome-Based Empirical Approach

Clinical SyndromeLikely SourcesEmpirical Antibiotics (adjust local)
Septic shock / severe sepsisAny — see Sepsis noteSee Sepsis and Septic Shock note
Community-acquired pneumonia (severe)S. pneumoniae, atypicals, H. influenzaeCeftriaxone 2g IV 12h + Azithromycin 500mg IV/PO OD
Healthcare-associated / ventilator pneumoniaPseudomonas, MRSA, Gram-negativesPip-tazo 4.5g 6h OR Meropenem 1g 8h + Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg 6h
Meningitis / encephalitisSee Acute Bacterial Meningitis / Viral Encephalitis notesCeftriaxone + Vancomycin + Dexamethasone ± Aciclovir
Infective endocarditisSee IE noteSee IE note
Intra-abdominal sepsisEnterobacteriaceae, Bacteroides, EnterococcusPip-tazo 4.5g 6h OR Meropenem 1g 8h
Complicated UTI / PyelonephritisE. coli, Klebsiella, Enterococcus, PseudomonasCeftriaxone 2g 12h OR Pip-tazo 4.5g 6h
Skin/Soft tissue (severe/necrotising)MSSA/MRSA, streptococci, anaerobes, Gram-negPip-tazo + Vancomycin + Clindamycin
Septic arthritisS. aureus, streptococci, N. gonorrhoeae (young)Flucloxacillin 2g 4h + Ceftriaxone 2g 12h (add vancomycin if MRSA risk)
Febrile neutropeniaGram-negatives (Pseudomonas), Gram-positivesPip-tazo 4.5g 6h OR Meropenem 1g 8h (+ vancomycin if indicated)
Line sepsisCoNS, S. aureus, Gram-neg, CandidaVancomycin + Pip-tazo/Meropenem (remove line if S. aureus/Candida/BCx+ve)
Post-surgical fever (day 0–2)Atelectasis, drugs, transfusionUsually no antibiotics
Post-surgical fever (day 3–7)Wound infection, UTI, line, DVT/PETargeted by source
Post-surgical fever (>7d)Abscess, anastomotic leak, DVT/PE, drugCT abdomen, cultures

Red Flags — Urgent/Immediate Action

Red FlagImplicationAction
Hyperpyrexia ≥41.5°CHeat stroke, NMS, malignant hyperthermia, severe sepsisCooling, dantrolene (MH/NMS), ICU
Rigors + hypotensionBacteraemia, sepsisBlood cultures, sepsis bundle
Petechial/purpuric rash + feverMeningococcal sepsis, DIC, vasculitisSepsis bundle, ceftriaxone, ICU
New murmur + fever + embolic phenomenaInfective endocarditis3 sets blood cultures, TTE/TOE, empirical IE antibiotics
Hot swollen jointSeptic arthritisUrgent joint aspiration → antibiotics → orthopaedics
Neck stiffness + AMSMeningitis/encephalitisLP if safe, aciclovir + empirical meningitis abx
Focal neurological deficitBrain abscess, stroke, TB meningitisUrgent MRI/CT, LP if safe
Immunocompromised + feverNeutropenic sepsis, opportunisticBroad abx immediately, G-CSF, antifungal if persistent
Returning traveller + feverMalaria (falciparum = emergency), dengue, typhoid, VHFThick/thin films ×3 (or RDT), malaria PCR; isolate if VHF suspected

Non-Infectious Fever (Differential)

CategoryExamplesClues
Drug feverBeta-lactams, sulfonamides, allopurinol, anticonvulsants, ATT, checkpoint inhibitors, biologicsTemporal relation, rash, eosinophilia, resolves on stopping
MalignancyLymphoma (Pel-Ebstein), leukaemia, renal cell, hepatocellular, metastasesWeight loss, night sweats, lymphadenopathy, cytopenias
Autoimmune / AutoinflammatorySLE, Still disease, vasculitis (GCA, PAN), AOSD, FMF, TRAPS, CAPSRash, arthritis, serositis, high ferritin, rash evanescent (Still)
ThromboembolicPE, DVT, cerebral venous thrombosisHypoxia, pleuritic pain, Wells score, D-dimer
EndocrineThyrotoxicosis (thyroid storm), adrenal crisis, phaeochromocytomaTachycardia, tremor, hypotension, hyperglycaemia
FactitiousSelf-induced (thermometer manipulation, injection)Unexplained fever, healthcare access, psychiatric history
Post-proceduralPost-embolisation, post-ablation, post-chemoTemporal, self-limiting

Pyrexia of Unknown Origin (PUO) — Modern Approach

Definition

  • Classic: Fever ≥38.3°C on multiple occasions >3 weeks, uncertain after 1 week inpatient workup
  • Nosocomial: Fever >38.3°C on multiple occasions in hospitalised patient, uncertain after 3 days workup
  • Immunocompromised: Fever >38.3°C in neutropenic/immunosuppressed, uncertain after 3 days
  • HIV-associated: Fever >38.3°C for >4 weeks (outpatient) or >3 days (inpatient), CD4<200

Aetiology Distribution (Classic PUO)

Category%Examples
Infections30–40%TB (extrapulmonary), endocarditis, abscesses, EBV/CMV, HIV, brucellosis, typhoid, disseminated fungal
Malignancy20–25%Lymphoma, leukaemia, renal cell, hepatocellular, metastatic
Autoimmune / Inflammatory15–20%SLE, Still disease, GCA, PAN, sarcoidosis, AOSD
Miscellaneous / Undiagnosed10–15%Drug fever, factitious, thromboembolic, endocrine
Truly Unknown5–10%Resolves spontaneously

PUO Workup Protocol

⬇ Clinical Pathway
PUO: Fever >3w (opd) / >3d (ip immunocompromised) no dx after standard workup
↓
Repeat History + Exam daily
↓
Tier 1: FBC, CRP, PCT, U&E, LFTs, Ca, glucose, lactate, coag, urine Cx, blood Cx×3, CXR, HIV, TST/IGRA
↓
Tier 2: ANA, ANCA, RF, ACE, ferritin, LDH, ESR, urine TB PCR, stool Cx, serology (brucella, lepto, rickettsia, EBV, CMV, syphilis)
↓
Localising clues?
↓
Targeted imaging: CT TAP, PET-CT, echo, LP, joint aspiration, bone scan
Non-localised: CT TAP → PET-CT → Bone marrow biopsy (if cytopenias) → Temporal artery biopsy (if >50)
↓
Targeted biopsies: lymph node, liver, bone marrow, temporal artery
↓
Empirical therapeutic trials ONLY if life-threatening: ATT (TB), steroids (Still/GCA), doxycycline (rickettsial)

Special Populations

PopulationKey Considerations
ElderlyAtypical presentation (hypothermia, confusion, falls), polymorbidity, polypharmacy, blunted fever response
PregnancyPhysiological leukocytosis, reduced cell-mediated immunity, listeriosis, pyelonephritis, chorioamnionitis; avoid fluoroquinolones/doxycycline
Returning TravellerMalaria until proven otherwise — thick/thin films ×3 at 12–24h intervals or RDT+PCR; consider dengue, typhoid, rickettsial, schistosomiasis, VHF
Immunocompromised (HIV CD4<200, transplant, chemo, steroids)Broader differential: atypical TB, fungal (crypto, histoplasma, PCP), CMV, EBV, MAC, toxoplasma, nocardia, strongyloides; lower threshold for empirical broad abx + antifungal; G-CSF if neutropenic
Post-splenectomy / Functional HyposplenismOverwhelming post-splenectomy infection (OPSI): S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, N. meningitidis, Capnocytophaga; vaccinate + lifelong penicillin prophylaxis; urgent abx if fever

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Fever ≥38.3°C; hyperpyrexia ≥41.5°C = emergency; hyperthermia = antipyretics don't work
  • Structured approach: ABCDE → sepsis screen → history (travel, exposures, host factors, drugs) → targeted exam (skin, fundi, murmur, joints, devices) → stepwise investigations
  • Blood cultures BEFORE antibiotics (×2–3 sets) — do not delay abx >45min for cultures
  • Syndrome-based empirical therapy (see table) — de-escalate at 48–72h
  • Red flags: rigors+hypotension, petechial rash, new murmur+emboli, hot joint, neck stiffness+AMS, focal neuro deficit, immunocompromised, returning traveller
  • Non-infectious fever: drug fever (temporal, eosinophilia), malignancy (lymphoma), autoimmune (Still, GCA, vasculitis), PE/DVT, thyroid storm
  • PUO: Infections 30–40%, malignancy 20–25%, autoimmune 15–20%; workup: repeat Hx/Ex, Tier 1→2 labs, CT TAP→PET-CT→biopsy
  • Returning traveller: Malaria first — thick/thin films ×3 or RDT+PCR; falciparum = emergency
  • Immunocompromised: broader differential, lower threshold for broad abx+antifungal, consider PCP, CMV, MAC, TB, fungal
  • Drug fever: diagnosis of exclusion; temporal relation, eosinophilia, rash, resolves on stopping

Common Viva Questions

  1. What is the difference between fever and hyperthermia? Fever = hypothalamic set-point ↑ (antipyretics work); Hyperthermia = failed thermoregulation, set-point normal (antipyretics DON'T work) — heat stroke, NMS, MH, thyrotoxicosis.
  2. What are the tier 1 investigations for a patient with fever? FBC, CRP, PCT, U&E, LFTs, glucose, lactate, coag, blood cultures ×2–3, urinalysis+Cx, CXR.
  3. How do you approach a returning traveller with fever? Malaria first: thick/thin films ×3 at 12–24h intervals OR RDT+PCR. If negative, consider dengue, typhoid, rickettsial, VHF. Isolate if VHF suspected.
  4. What are the common causes of PUO? Infections (TB, endocarditis, abscesses) 30–40%, Malignancy (lymphoma) 20–25%, Autoimmune (Still, GCA, vasculitis) 15–20%.
  5. What is the workup for PUO? Repeat Hx/Ex daily; Tier 1 labs (FBC, CRP, blood cx×3, HIV, TST/IGRA, CXR); Tier 2 (autoimmune, serology, TB PCR); CT TAP → PET-CT → biopsies (lymph node, bone marrow, temporal artery if >50).
  6. When do you suspect drug fever? Temporal relation to drug start, rash, eosinophilia, fever resolves on stopping — diagnosis of exclusion.

Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
Treat fever with antipyretics routinelyTreat the CAUSE; antipyretics only for comfort/hyperpyrexia/underlying cardiac/respiratory compromise
Fever pattern diagnoses specific diseasePatterns have POOR specificity — do not rely
Single blood culture sufficient2–3 sets (aerobic/anaerobic) from DIFFERENT sites
Start antibiotics before cultures in all feverCultures BEFORE abx if stable; if septic/unstable → abx within 1h, cultures concurrent
PUO = just infectionsMalignancy 20–25%, Autoimmune 15–20% — must investigate all
Empirical steroids in PUOTherapeutic trials ONLY if life-threatening (TB meningitis, Still, GCA) — otherwise delays diagnosis
Returning traveller = just malariaDengue, typhoid, rickettsial, VHF, schisto — broad differential
Immunocompromised = same workupLower threshold for broad abx, antifungal, early imaging, bronchoscopy, LP
All post-op fever = infectionDay 0–2: atelectasis, drugs, transfusion; Day 3–7: wound, UTI, line, DVT; >7d: abscess, leak

Mnemonics

  • FEVER HISTORY: Fever pattern, Exposures (travel, animal, sexual), Vaccines, Exam focus, Rigors; Host factors, Immunosuppression, Surgery/hosp, Travel, Occupation, Recent abx, Year (seasonal)
  • EXAM FOCUS: Fundi (Roth, papilloedema), Otoscope/sinuses, Cardiac (murmur), Urine/genital, Skin (rash, petechiae, eschar, nodes, line sites), Joints (hot, swollen)
  • PUO CAUSES: Infections (TB, IE, abscess), Malignancy (lymphoma), Autoimmune (Still, GCA, vasculitis), Drug fever, Undiagnosed
  • TRAVEL FEVER: Malaria (films x3), Dengue, Typhoid, Rickettsial, VHF (isolate), Schisto, Leptospirosis

Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet

FEVER APPROACH: ABCDE→qSOFA→Red flags→Sepsis bundle. Hx: travel, exposures, host, drugs. Exam: skin, fundi, murmur, joints, devices. Tier 1: CBC, CRP, PCT, lactate, coag, Cx×3, urine Cx, CXR. Empirical: syndrome-based (CAP, meningitis, IE, IAI, UTI, SSTI, neutropenia). Non-infectious: drug, lymphoma, autoimmune, PE, thyroid storm. PUO: >3w opd/>3d ip; Inf 30-40%, Malig 20-25%, Autoimm 15-20%; Tier1→2, CT TAP→PET→Biopsy. Traveller: Malaria films×3 first.

Summary

Fever = core temperature ≥38.3°C; Hyperpyrexia ≥41.5°C (emergency); Hyperthermia = failed thermoregulation (heat stroke, NMS, malignant hyperthermia, thyrotoxicosis) — antipyretics INEFFECTIVE. Immediate: ABCDE, sepsis screen (qSOFA/SOFA); red flags (rigors+hypotension, petechial rash, new murmur+emboli, hot joint, neck stiffness+AMS, focal neuro deficit, immunocompromised, returning traveller) → sepsis bundle within 1 hour. Structured approach: History (onset, localising, travel, exposures, host factors, drug history) → Targeted examination (skin: rash/petechiae/eschar/nodes; fundi: Roth/papilloedema; cardiac murmur; joints; devices) → Stepwise investigations: Tier 1 (all: CBC, CRP, PCT, U&E, LFTs, lactate, coag, blood cultures ×3, urine culture, CXR, HIV, TST/IGRA) → Tier 2 (syndrome-driven: LP, echo, CT, aspirate) → Tier 3 (PUO: autoimmune, serology, TB PCR, CT TAP, PET-CT, biopsies). Syndrome-based empirical antibiotics (CAP, meningitis, IE, IAI, UTI, SSTI, febrile neutropenia, line sepsis) — de-escalate at 48–72h. Non-infectious fever: drug fever (temporal, eosinophilia, rash), malignancy (lymphoma), autoimmune (Still, GCA, vasculitis), thromboembolic (PE/DVT), endocrine (thyroid storm, adrenal crisis). PUO: Fever >3 weeks (OPD) or >3 days (IP immunocompromised) without diagnosis after standard workup; Infections 30–40% (TB, IE, abscesses), Malignancy 20–25% (lymphoma), Autoimmune 15–20% (Still, GCA, vasculitis); Workup: repeat Hx/Ex daily → Tier 1→2 → CT TAP → PET-CT → biopsies (lymph node, bone marrow, temporal artery if >50); empirical trials ONLY if life-threatening. Returning traveller: Malaria first (thick/thin films ×3 at 12–24h intervals OR RDT+PCR); P. falciparum = emergency; also consider dengue, typhoid, rickettsial, VHF (isolate).

Flashcards

  • Q: Fever definition

A: Core temp ≥38.3°C once or ≥38.0°C sustained 1h

  • Q: Hyperpyrexia

A: ≥41.5°C — emergency

  • Q: Hyperthermia vs Fever

A: Hyperthermia = set-point normal, thermoregulation fail (antipyretics DON'T work) — heat stroke, NMS, MH, thyroid storm

  • Q: Blood cultures

A: 2-3 sets (aerobic/anaerobic) from DIFFERENT sites BEFORE antibiotics

  • qSOFA

A: RR≥22, GCS<15, SBP≤100 (≥2 = high risk screen)

  • Q: Red flags for sepsis bundle

A: Rigors+hypotension, petechial rash, new murmur+emboli, hot joint, neck stiffness+AMS, focal neuro, immunocompromised, traveller

  • Q: PUO definition

A: Fever >3w opd / >3d ip immunocompromised, no dx after standard workup

  • Q: PUO aetiology

A: Infections 30-40%, Malignancy 20-25%, Autoimmune 15-20%

  • Q: PUO workup

A: Tier 1 (CBC, CRP, Cx×3, CXR, HIV, TST/IGRA) → Tier 2 (autoimmune, serology, TB PCR) → CT TAP → PET-CT → Biopsy

  • Q: Traveller fever

A: Malaria FIRST (films×3 or RDT+PCR); Falciparum = emergency

  • Q: Drug fever clues

A: Temporal relation, rash, eosinophilia, resolves on stopping

  • Q: Post-splenectomy

A: OPSI risk: S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, N. meningitidis, Capnocytophaga; vaccinate + lifelong penicillin prophylaxis

  • Q: Post-op fever day 0-2

A: Atelectasis, drugs, transfusion — usually NO antibiotics

  • Q: Empirical trials in PUO

A: ONLY life-threatening: TB meningitis, Still, GCA

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — Fever = hypothalamic set-point ↑ (prostaglandin-mediated), antipyretics (COX inhibitors) work. Hyperthermia = set-point normal, thermoregulation overwhelmed (heat stroke, NMS, MH, thyrotoxicosis), antipyretics ineffective.
  2. B — 2–3 sets (aerobic + anaerobic) from different venipuncture sites. Increases yield for bacteraemia and detects contaminants.
  3. B — Returning traveller from malaria-endemic area: malaria first. Thick/thin films ×3 at 12–24h intervals (gold standard) or RDT + PCR. P. falciparum can be fatal within 24–48h.
  4. B — Petechial/purpuric rash + fever + hypotension = meningococcal sepsis / DIC / vasculitis → immediate sepsis bundle.
  5. C — Infections (TB, endocarditis, abscesses, HIV, brucellosis) are the most common cause of PUO (30–40%).
  6. B — CT TAP is the standard next imaging step in PUO after basic labs; PET-CT if CT negative or for malignancy/inflammation localisation.
  7. B — Drug fever: temporal relationship to drug initiation, rash, eosinophilia, resolution on discontinuation. Diagnosis of exclusion.
  8. B — Post-splenectomy / hyposplenism: OPSI risk from encapsulated organisms — S. pneumoniae most common, then H. influenzae, N. meningitidis, Capnocytophaga canimorsus.
  9. B — Therapeutic trials in PUO only for life-threatening conditions where diagnostic delay causes irreversible harm: TB meningitis, Still disease (steroids), GCA (steroids — prevent blindness).
  10. C — qSOFA ≥2 = screening tool for high risk of in-hospital mortality/ICU referral; NOT diagnostic for sepsis.

SBAs

  1. B — Checkpoint inhibitors cause immune-related adverse events (hepatitis, colitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies) with fever; temporal relation, negative cultures, transaminitis = likely immune-related hepatitis/drug fever.
  2. B — SLE flare: high ESR, low complement, high anti-dsDNA, proteinuria; CRP typically only mildly elevated in SLE (unlike infection where CRP ↑↑). Discordant ESR/CRP = flare.
  3. B — Elderly often present atypically (confusion, falls, hypothermia); temperature 37.8°C may be significant; sepsis screen + cultures + CXR + urine culture before/with antibiotics.
  4. B — Splenic microabscesses with negative cultures → percutaneous aspiration for culture, PCR, 16S rRNA, fungal/TB PCR before empirical therapy.
  5. B — Drug fever: temporal relation (amoxicillin started before fever, continued through), negative cultures, mild eosinophilia possible, normal exam. Diagnosis of exclusion but highly likely.
  6. B — Dengue NS1 negative day 7 (sensitivity drops after day 5); dengue IgM/IgG or PCR next. Malaria films also indicated but dengue more likely with conjunctival injection + rash.
  7. B — Immunocompromised (transplant): broad antipseudomonal β-lactam (pip-tazo/meropenem) ± vancomycin; add antifungal (caspofungin/voriconazole) if persistent fever >4–5 days or clinical deterioration.
  8. B — Streptococcus gallolyticus (bovis) bacteraemia/endocarditis has strong association with colonic neoplasia (adenoma/carcinoma) — mandatory colonoscopy.
  9. C — Post-op day 0–2: atelectasis, drug fever, transfusion reaction most common; no antibiotics unless sepsis criteria met.
  10. B — AOSD: evanescent salmon-pink rash, very high ferritin (>10,000 = highly specific), sore throat, arthralgias, high WBC; exclusion diagnosis.
