Healthcare-Associated Infections (HAI) — CLABSI, CAUTI, SSI, VAP

Related: [[Antimicrobial Prescribing Principles]], [[Antimicrobial Stewardship]], [[Infection in Immunocompromised Host (Transplant, Biologics)]], [[Sepsis and Septic Shock]], [[Clostridioides difficile Infection (CDI)]], [[Infection in Pregnancy and Neonates]], [[Urinary Tract Infections (Complicated, CA-UTI, Pyelonephritis)]], [[Hospital-Acquired and Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia (HAP-VAP)]]

[!important]
HAI = infection acquired ≥48h after hospital admission (or ≤48h if healthcare-associated). CLABSI, CAUTI, VAP, SSI = ~80% of HAI. Prevention bundles = evidence-based, reduce HAI 30–70%. Surveillance = NHSN definitions; rate per 1000 device-days (CLABSI, CAUTI, VAP) or per 100 procedures (SSI). Antimicrobial stewardship + IPC = core strategies.

Learning Objectives

  • Define HAI and classify by type (CLABSI, CAUTI, VAP, SSI, CDI)
  • Apply diagnostic criteria (CDC/NHSN) for each HAI type
  • Implement evidence-based prevention bundles (CLABSI, CAUTI, VAP, SSI)
  • Select appropriate empirical therapy by HAI type and local epidemiology
  • Implement surveillance and outbreak investigation
  • Apply antimicrobial stewardship principles to HAI management

Definitions & Surveillance

HAI TypeDefinitionRate Denominator
CLABSICentral line-associated BSI — laboratory-confirmed BSI in patient with central line ≥48h, not secondary to another site1000 central line-days
CAUTICatheter-associated UTI — symptomatic UTI with indwelling catheter ≥48h1000 catheter-days
VAPVentilator-associated pneumonia — pneumonia in patient on invasive ventilation ≥48h1000 ventilator-days
SSISurgical site infection — infection at surgical site within 30d (90d if implant)100 procedures
CDIClostridioides difficile infection — see CDI note10,000 patient-days
[!key]
HAI = infection ≥48h after admission (not incubating at admission). CLABSI/CAUTI/VAP rates per 1000 device-days; SSI per 100 procedures.

Central Line-Associated BSI (CLABSI)

Diagnosis (CDC/NHSN)

CriterionDetails
Central lineIn place ≥48h before culture (or day of device removal)
Laboratory-confirmed BSI≥1 blood culture with recognised pathogen OR ≥2 blood cultures with common skin commensal (CoNS, Corynebacterium, Bacillus, Micrococcus) + clinical signs (fever, hypotension)
Not secondaryNo other site infection explaining BSI

Common Pathogens

Pathogen%Key Feature
Coagulase-negative Staphylococci (CoNS)30–40%Most common; often contamination but true CLABSI if ≥2 cultures
Staphylococcus aureus15–20%MSSA/MRSA; high mortality
Enterococcus spp.10–15%VRE increasing
Candida spp.10–15%C. albicans, C. glabrata, C. parapsilosis (biofilm)
Gram-negative (Klebsiella, E. coli, Pseudomonas, Enterobacter)15–20%ESBL/CRE concern
Other<5%Bacillus, Corynebacterium, Stenotrophomonas

CLABSI Prevention Bundle (IHI/CDC)

ComponentAction
Hand hygieneBefore/after line access; alcohol-based rub
Maximal barrier precautionsSterile gloves, gown, cap, mask, large drape for insertion
Chlorhexidine skin prep2% CHG in 70% alcohol (superior to povidone-iodine)
Optimal site selectionSubclavian > Jugular > Femoral (lowest infection risk)
Daily review of line necessityRemove as soon as no longer needed
Chlorhexidine dressingsCHG-impregnated sponge/dressing for lines >7d
Hub disinfectionScrub hub 15s with 2% CHG/70% alcohol before each access
Avoid routine replacementGuidewire exchange NOT recommended for CLABSI prevention
[!tip]
Subclavian site = lowest CLABSI risk. CHG skin prep + maximal barrier = strongest evidence. Daily line necessity review = most impactful. CHG dressings for lines >7d reduce CLABSI 50%.

CLABSI Empirical Therapy

ScenarioRegimen
No MDR risk, stableCeftriaxone 2g IV 24h + Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg 6h
MDR risk (prior abx, ICU, known colonisation)Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g 6h OR Meropenem 1g 8h (+ Vancomycin)
Suspected CandidaEchinocandin (caspofungin/micafungin/anidulafungin)
Line removalStrongly recommended for S. aureus, Candida, Pseudomonas, Bacillus, mycobacteria; consider salvage for CoNS

Catheter-Associated UTI (CAUTI)

Diagnosis (CDC/NHSN)

CriterionDetails
Indwelling catheter≥48h (or day of removal)
SymptomsFever (>38°C), suprapubic pain, CVA tenderness, altered mental status, rigors
Microbiology≥10³ CFU/mL of ≥1 organism from catheter specimen OR ≥10⁵ CFU/mL from voided specimen

Common Pathogens

Pathogen%
E. coli25–30%
Klebsiella15–20%
Pseudomonas10–15%
Enterococcus10–15%
Candida5–10%
Proteus, Morganella, Providencia10–15%

CAUTI Prevention Bundle

ComponentAction
Avoid unnecessary catheterisationUse alternatives (condom catheter, intermittent cath, nappies)
Aseptic insertionSterile technique, smallest appropriate catheter
Closed drainage systemMaintain unobstructed flow; no disconnections
Catheter securementPrevent traction/urethral trauma
Drainage bag below bladderPrevent reflux
Daily necessity reviewNurse-driven protocol: remove ASAP
Periurethral cleaningRoutine soap/water (no antiseptic benefit)
Antibiotic prophylaxis at insertionNOT routinely recommended
Catheter materialSilver-alloy or hydrogel-coated if prolonged (>7d) anticipated
[!key]
Daily review of catheter necessity with nurse-driven removal protocol = single most effective intervention. Avoid unnecessary catheterisation = primary prevention.

CAUTI Management

StepAction
1. Remove/replace catheterIf ≥2 days old; send tip for culture if removed
2. Empirical antibioticsAs per complicated UTI (see UTI note)
3. Duration7 days if catheter removed; 10–14 days if catheter must remain
4. Catheter changeReplace after 48–72h of antibiotics if catheter must stay

Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia (VAP)

Diagnosis (CDC/NHSN — Clinical + Microbiological)

CriterionDetails
VentilationInvasive ventilation ≥48h
Chest X-rayNew/progressive infiltrate
ClinicalFever/hypothermia, ↑ WBC, purulent secretions, ↑ O₂ requirement
MicrobiologyQualitative/semi-quantitative culture (endotracheal aspirate ≥10⁵ CFU/mL; BAL ≥10⁴ CFU/mL)
ExcludeNon-infectious causes (atelectasis, ARDS, pulmonary oedema)

Common Pathogens

TimeframePathogens
Early VAP (≤5 days)S. aureus (MSSA), S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, Enterobacteriaceae
Late VAP (>5 days)MRSA, Pseudomonas, Acinetobacter, Stenotrophomonas, Enterobacteriaceae (ESBL/CRE)

VAP Prevention Bundle (IHI/CDC)

ComponentAction
Head of bed elevation30–45° (unless contraindicated)
Daily sedation vacationDaily sedation interruption + spontaneous breathing trial
Peptic ulcer prophylaxisPPI/H2 blocker (balance with C. difficile risk)
DVT prophylaxisPharmacological (unless contraindicated)
Oral careChlorhexidine 0.12–2% oral rinse q6–12h
Subglottic secretion drainageEndotracheal tube with subglottic suction port
Early extubationDaily assessment for readiness to extubate
Hand hygiene + PPEStandard + contact for MDRO
[!tip]
Head of bed 30–45° + daily sedation vacation + SBT = strongest evidence. Subglottic secretion drainage reduces VAP 50%.

VAP Empirical Therapy

ScenarioRegimen
Early VAP, no MDR riskCeftriaxone 2g 24h +/− Vancomycin (if MRSA risk)
Late VAP / MDR riskPiperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g 6h OR Meropenem 1g 8h + Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg 6h
MRSA riskAdd Vancomycin (trough 15–20) OR Linezolid
Pseudomonas riskAnti-pseudomonal β-lactam (Pip-tazo, Meropenem, Cefepime, Ceftazidime) +/− Aminoglycoside
Duration7 days (short-course non-inferior to 14d); stop if culture negative

Surgical Site Infection (SSI)

Classification (CDC)

TypeDepthTimeframe
Superficial IncisionalSkin/subcutaneous≤30 days
Deep IncisionalFascia/muscle≤30 days (90 days if implant)
Organ/SpaceOrgan/space manipulated≤30 days (90 days if implant)

Risk Factors

PatientOperativePostoperative
Diabetes, obesity, smoking, immunosuppression, malnutrition, age >60, prolonged pre-op stayDuration >T, contaminated/dirty wound, emergency, implant, blood loss >1L, poor techniqueHypothermia, hyperglycaemia, hypoxaemia, haematoma, drain, transfusion

SSI Prevention Bundle (WHO/CDC)

PhaseIntervention
Pre-operativeShower with CHG night before + morning; No hair removal (or clippers if necessary); Antibiotic prophylaxis within 60 min before incision (cefazolin 2g IV; add metronidazole if colorectal); Glycaemic control (perioperative glucose <180); Normothermia (>36°C); Oxygenation (FiO₂ 80% intraop + 2h post)
Intra-operativeSkin prep: CHG 2% in alcohol; Sterile technique; Minimise OR traffic; Maintain normothermia; Glycaemic control
Post-operativeContinue antibiotic ≤24h (no benefit beyond); Glycaemic control (first 48h); Normothermia; Early mobilisation; Wound care (sterile dressing 48h)

SSI Empirical Therapy

ScenarioRegimen
Superficial, MSSA/StrepFlucloxacillin 2g IV 6h / Cephalexin 500mg 6h
Deep/Organ space, no MDROCeftriaxone 2g 24h + Metronidazole 500mg 8h
MRSA riskVancomycin 15–20mg/kg 6h +/− Ceftriaxone/Metronidazole
Gram-negative / MDROPiperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g 6h OR Meropenem 1g 8h
Duration5–7 days (superficial); 7–14 days (deep/organ space); source control critical (drainage, debridement)

Antimicrobial Stewardship in HAI

InterventionDescription
Formulary restrictionCarbapenems, newer agents (CAV, MV, IR, plazomicin) require ID approval
Prospective audit & feedbackID pharmacist/physician reviews at 48–72h; recommends de-escalation/stop
IV→oral switchProtocol-driven at 48–72h if clinical stability
Automatic stop ordersDefault durations (e.g., VAP 7d, CAUTI 7d, SSI 5–7d) with mandatory renewal
Biomarker guidancePCT-guided antibiotic discontinuation in sepsis/VAP
Penicillin allergy de-labellingAssessment + graded challenge → ↓ vancomycin/fluoroquinolone use
Diagnostic stewardshipDo NOT culture asymptomatic patients (catheter tips, urine, blood without clinical signs)
Device removalCLABSI: remove for S. aureus, Candida, Pseudomonas, Bacillus, mycobacteria; CAUTI: remove if possible; VAP: no routine line removal

Outbreak Investigation

StepAction
1. Confirm outbreakRate >expected baseline; molecular typing (WGS, PFGE)
2. Case definitionPerson, place, time, clinical criteria
3. Case findingActive surveillance; retrospective review
4. Descriptive epidemiologyEpidemic curve; person/place/time
5. Hypothesis generationCommon source vs person-to-person
6. Analytical studyCohort/case-control; environmental sampling
7. Control measuresIsolation, cohorting, enhanced cleaning, device removal, antibiotic restriction
8. CommunicationStaff, patients, public health, media
9. MonitoringContinue surveillance until baseline reached

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • HAI = ≥48h after admission; CLABSI, CAUTI, VAP, SSI = 80% of HAI
  • CLABSI: CoNS #1; subclavian > jugular > femoral; CHG prep + maximal barrier + daily review + CHG dressings
  • CAUTI: E. coli #1; daily necessity review = most effective prevention; remove ASAP
  • VAP: early (≤5d) = MSSA, H. influenzae; late (>5d) = MRSA, Pseudomonas, ESBL; HOB 30–45° + daily SBT + subglottic drainage
  • SSI: cefazolin prophylaxis ≤60min pre-incision; discontinue ≤24h post-op; glycaemic control + normothermia
  • CLABSI treatment: line removal for S. aureus, Candida, Pseudomonas, Bacillus; CoNS consider salvage
  • CAUTI: remove catheter if possible; 7d abx if removed, 10–14d if retained
  • VAP duration: 7 days (short-course non-inferior)
  • SSI prophylaxis: cefazolin 2g IV ≤60min pre-incision; stop ≤24h; glucose <180, normothermia
  • Stewardship: 48–72h audit, IV→oral switch, PCT-guided, automatic stop orders, de-labelling

Common Viva Questions

  1. CLABSI prevention bundle components? Hand hygiene, maximal barrier, CHG prep, subclavian site, daily review, CHG dressings, hub disinfection.
  2. CAUTI most effective prevention? Daily catheter necessity review with nurse-driven removal protocol.
  3. VAP prevention — strongest evidence? Head of bed 30–45° + daily sedation vacation + spontaneous breathing trial.
  4. SSI antibiotic prophylaxis timing? Cefazolin 2g IV within 60 minutes before incision; discontinue within 24h.
  5. CLABSI — when to remove line? S. aureus, Candida, Pseudomonas, Bacillus, mycobacteria; CoNS may salvage.
  6. VAP empirical therapy early vs late? Early (≤5d): ceftriaxone +/− vancomycin; Late (>5d): pip-tazo/meropenem + vancomycin.
  7. SSI prophylaxis duration? ≤24 hours post-op (no benefit beyond).
  8. VAP duration? 7 days (short-course non-inferior to 14d).
  9. CLABSI most common pathogen? Coagulase-negative Staphylococci (CoNS).
  10. Subglottic secretion drainage effect on VAP? Reduces VAP by ~50%.
  11. SSI glucose target perioperatively? <180 mg/dL (<10 mmol/L).

Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
Routine central line replacement prevents CLABSINo benefit; increases infection risk; guidewire exchange NOT recommended
CAUTI prophylaxis at insertionNOT routinely recommended
VAP = any pneumonia on ventilatorMust be ≥48h after intubation + new infiltrate + clinical + microbiological criteria
SSI prophylaxis continue >24hNo benefit; increases resistance/C. difficile
CLABSI = any BSI with lineMust be central line ≥48h + NOT secondary to another site
VAP duration 14–21 days7 days short-course non-inferior
Prophylactic antibiotics at catheter insertionReduces CAUTI but selects resistance; NOT recommended
CHG dressings for all central linesOnly for lines expected >7 days
Daily CHG bathing for all ICUReduces CLABSI, MRSA, VRE; but not standard for all
SSI = only superficialDeep incisional and organ/space also SSI (90d if implant)

Mnemonics

  • CLABSI BUNDLE: Hand hygiene, Maximal Barrier, CHG Prep, Subclavian Site, Daily Review, CHG Dressings, Hub Scrub
  • CAUTI PREVENTION: Avoid unnecessary, Aseptic insertion, Closed system, Secure catheter, Daily review, Remove ASAP = AACSDR
  • VAP BUNDLE: Head of Bed 30–45°, Daily Sedation Vacation + SBT, Subglottic Drainage, Oral CHG = HBDSSO
  • SSI PROPHYLAXIS: Cefazolin 60min pre-incision; Stop ≤24h; Glucose <180; Normothermia; Oxygen 80%
  • CLABSI REMOVE: Staph aureus, Candida, Pseudomonas, Bacillus, Mycobacteria = SCPBM
  • CAUTI: Remove catheter = Most Effective; 7d abx if removed, 10-14d if retained

Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • CLABSI bundle (7 components)
  • CAUTI: daily review + remove ASAP
  • VAP bundle (HOB, SBT, subglottic, oral CHG)
  • SSI prophylaxis timing (cefazolin ≤60min pre-incision; stop ≤24h)
  • CLABSI line removal criteria (S. aureus, Candida, Pseudomonas, Bacillus, mycobacteria)
  • VAP: early vs late pathogens; duration 7d
  • CAUTI: remove catheter if possible; 7d vs 10–14d
  • SSI prophylaxis: cefazolin ≤60min pre-incision; stop ≤24h; glucose <180
  • CLABSI most common pathogen: CoNS

Should Know

  • Subglottic secretion drainage (VAP reduction 50%)
  • CHG dressings for lines >7d
  • CAUTI catheter change at 48–72h if retained
  • VAP empirical therapy by timing/MDRO risk
  • SSI classification (superficial, deep, organ/space)
  • Diagnostic stewardship (don't culture asymptomatic)
  • CLABSI CoNS salvage vs removal
  • VAP subglottic drainage cost-effectiveness

Nice to Know

  • CHG bathing for ICU patients
  • Antimicrobial-impregnated catheters
  • SSI risk scores (NNIS, SIR)
  • VAP biomarkers (PCT, suPAR)
  • CAUTI new devices (condom cath, suprapubic)
  • Outbreak investigation methodology
  • Novel anti-biofilm strategies

  2. CLABSI (criteria, pathogens, bundle, treatment, line removal)
  3. CAUTI (criteria, pathogens, bundle, catheter removal, treatment)
  4. VAP (criteria, early vs late pathogens, bundle, treatment, duration)
  5. SSI (classification, risk factors, prophylaxis bundle, treatment)
  6. Antimicrobial stewardship in HAI (audit, IV→oral, PCT, de-labelling, diagnostic stewardship)
  7. Outbreak investigation steps

Short Note Skeleton

  • CLABSI: CoNS #1; Bundle (H, M, C, S, D, D, H); Rx: Pip-tazo/Mero+Vanco; Remove S.aureus/Candida/Pseudo
  • CAUTI: E.coli #1; Daily review→Remove; Rx: Remove→7d; Retain→10-14d+change cath
  • VAP: Early (≤5d) MSSA/H.inf; Late (>5d) MRSA/Pseudo; Bundle: HOB, SBT, Subglottic, Oral CHG; Rx: 7d
  • SSI: Cefazolin ≤60min pre-inc; Stop ≤24h; Glucose<180; Normothermia; Rx by depth/MDRO
  • Stewardship: 48-72h audit, IV→oral, PCT, Auto-stop, De-labelling, Diagnostic stewardship

Viva One-Liners

  • CLABSI: CoNS #1; Subclavian>Jugular>Femoral; CHG prep+Max barrier+Daily review
  • CAUTI: Daily review → Remove ASAP = most effective
  • VAP: HOB 30-45° + Daily SBT + Subglottic = strongest bundle
  • SSI: Cefazolin ≤60min pre-inc; Stop ≤24h; Glucose<180
  • CLABSI remove: S.aureus, Candida, Pseudomonas, Bacillus, Mycobacteria
  • VAP: 7d duration; Early Ceftriaxone±Vanco; Late Pip-tazo/Mero+Vanco
  • CAUTI: Remove→7d; Retain→10-14d+change cath 48-72h

Ward-Case Discussion Points

  • ICU patient, central line day 7, S. aureus bacteremia → CLABSI criteria met → line removal + pip-tazo + vancomycin
  • ICU patient, Foley day 5, fever, E. coli 10⁵ CFU/mL → CAUTI → remove catheter, ceftriaxone 7d, change if must retain
  • Ventilated patient day 6, new infiltrate, purulent secretions, MRSA on BAL → late VAP → pip-tazo + vancomycin 7d
  • Post-op day 5, abdominal wound purulent, S. aureus → deep SSI → ceftriaxone + metronidazole; source control (drainage)

CAUTI: E.coli #1; Daily review → Remove ASAP; Remove→7d abx; Retain→10-14d+change cath 48-72h. Stewardship: 48-72h audit, IV→oral, PCT, Auto-stop, De-labelling.

Summary

Healthcare-associated infections (HAI) are infections acquired ≥48 hours after hospital admission (healthcare-associated). Four major types account for ~80%: CLABSI, CAUTI, VAP, SSI. CLABSI: central line ≥48h + laboratory-confirmed BSI not secondary; pathogens: CoNS (30–40%), S. aureus (15–20%), Enterococcus (10–15%), Candida (10–15%), Gram-negatives (15–20%); Prevention bundle: hand hygiene, maximal barrier precautions, CHG 2% in 70% alcohol skin prep, subclavian site preferred, daily line necessity review, CHG-impregnated dressings for lines >7d, hub disinfection (CHG/alcohol 15s); Treatment: empirical piperacillin-tazobactam/meropenem + vancomycin; remove line for S. aureus, Candida, Pseudomonas, Bacillus, mycobacteria. CAUTI: indwelling catheter ≥48h + symptoms + ≥10³ CFU/mL; pathogens: E. coli (25–30%), Klebsiella, Pseudomonas, Enterococcus, Candida; Prevention: avoid unnecessary catheterisation, aseptic insertion, closed drainage system, securement, daily necessity review with nurse-driven removal = most effective, no routine antibiotic prophylaxis; Management: remove catheter if possible, empirical antibiotics per complicated UTI, 7 days if removed, 10–14 days if retained + change catheter at 48–72h. VAP: invasive ventilation ≥48h + new/progressive infiltrate + clinical signs + microbiological criteria; early (≤5d): MSSA, S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae; late (>5d): MRSA, Pseudomonas, Acinetobacter, ESBL/CRE; Prevention bundle: head of bed 30–45°, daily sedation vacation + spontaneous breathing trial, subglottic secretion drainage, chlorhexidine oral care, stress ulcer/DVT prophylaxis; Empirical therapy: early — ceftriaxone ± vancomycin; late — piperacillin-tazobactam/meropenem + vancomycin; duration 7 days. SSI: infection at surgical site within 30 days (90 days if implant); Prophylaxis: cefazolin 2g IV within 60 min before incision, discontinue ≤24 hours post-op; glucose <180 mg/dL, normothermia >36°C, FiO₂ 80% intraop + 2h post; Empirical treatment by depth and MDRO risk; source control critical (drainage, debridement). Antimicrobial stewardship in HAI: prospective audit at 48–72h with feedback, IV→oral switch protocols, PCT-guided duration, automatic stop orders, penicillin allergy de-labelling, diagnostic stewardship (no cultures without clinical indication), device removal over routine replacement. Outbreak investigation: confirm, define, find, describe, hypothesise, analyse, control, communicate, monitor.

  1. VAP prevention bundle component with strongest evidence:

A. Stress ulcer prophylaxis

B. Head of bed elevation 30–45° + daily sedation vacation + spontaneous breathing trial

C. Subglottic secretion drainage

D. Chlorhexidine oral care

A. Within 120 minutes before incision

B. Within 60 minutes before incision

C. At time of incision

D. After incision closure

E. 24 hours before incision

  1. CLABSI — central line removal is STRONGLY RECOMMENDED for all EXCEPT:

A. Staphylococcus aureus

B. Candida species

C. Pseudomonas aeruginosa

D. Coagulase-negative Staphylococci (CoNS)

E. Bacillus species

  1. VAP empirical therapy for late-onset (>5 days) with MDR risk:

A. Ceftriaxone + Vancomycin

B. Piperacillin-tazobactam/Meropenem + Vancomycin

C. Cefepime alone

D. Ciprofloxacin + Clindamycin

E. Ertapenem + Vancomycin

  1. Recommended duration of antibiotic therapy for VAP:

A. 14 days

B. 10 days

C. 7 days

D. 21 days

E. Until CRP normalises

  1. SSI prophylaxis — when to discontinue antibiotics:

A. 48 hours post-op

B. Within 24 hours post-op

C. 72 hours post-op

D. 5 days post-op

E. Until drains removed

  1. CAUTI management — if catheter removed, duration of antibiotics:

A. 3 days

B. 5 days

C. 7 days

D. 10 days

D. 14 days

  1. Most common pathogen in ventilator-associated pneumonia (early, ≤5 days):

A. MRSA

B. Pseudomonas aeruginosa

C. MSSA / H. influenzae / S. pneumoniae

D. Acinetobacter baumannii

E. Candida

SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 65-year-old man in ICU has a triple-lumen subclavian catheter for 7 days. He develops fever 39°C, hypotension. Blood cultures ×2 grow CoNS. Catheter tip culture: CoNS >15 CFU. Management?

A. Remove catheter immediately

B. Continue catheter; treat with vancomycin (CoNS salvage acceptable)

C. Remove catheter; treat with vancomycin

D. Change catheter over guidewire; treat with vancomycin

E. Remove catheter; treat with daptomycin

  1. A 70-year-old woman with indwelling urinary catheter for 5 days develops fever 38.5°C, suprapubic tenderness. Urine culture from catheter: E. coli >10⁵ CFU/mL. Catheter removed. Duration of antibiotic therapy?

A. 3 days

B. 5 days

C. 7 days

D. 10 days

E. 14 days

  1. A 55-year-old man on mechanical ventilation for 7 days develops new infiltrate, purulent secretions, fever. Late VAP suspected. Empirical antibiotics?

A. Ceftriaxone + Vancomycin

B. Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV 6h + Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg IV 6h

C. Cefepime + Metronidazole

D. Meropenem alone

E. Ciprofloxacin + Clindamycin

  1. A patient undergoes elective colorectal surgery. SSI prophylaxis — correct cefazolin dosing and timing?

A. 1g IV at induction

B. 2g IV within 60 minutes before incision; re-dose if >4h or blood loss >1.5L

C. 2g IV after wound closure

D. 1g IV 6-hourly ×24h

E. 2g IV 8-hourly ×48h

  1. A 40-year-old man with a central line develops S. aureus bacteremia. Catheter removed. Empirical therapy while awaiting susceptibilities?

A. Vancomycin alone

B. Vancomycin + Piperacillin-tazobactam (or Ceftriaxone if MSSA suspected)

C. Daptomycin alone

D. Ceftriaxone alone

E. Linezolid alone

  1. A ventilated patient develops VAP on day 3. CXR: new right lower lobe infiltrate. Purulent sputum. No prior antibiotics, no MDR risk factors. Empirical therapy?

A. Piperacillin-tazobactam + Vancomycin

B. Meropenem + Vancomycin

C. Ceftriaxone 2g IV 24h (± Vancomycin if MRSA risk)

D. Ciprofloxacin + Metronidazole

E. Cefepime alone

  1. SSI prophylaxis for clean-contaminated surgery — recommended duration:

A. 48 hours

B. ≤24 hours

C. 72 hours

D. 5 days

E. Until drains removed

  1. CLABSI prevention — chlorhexidine-impregnated dressings indicated for:

A. All central lines

B. Lines expected to remain >7 days

C. Only femoral lines

D. Only dialysis catheters

E. Lines with positive blood cultures

  1. Subglottic secretion drainage in VAP prevention:

A. No proven benefit

B. Reduces VAP incidence by ~50%

C. Increases VAP risk

D. Only for early VAP

E. Replaces oral chlorhexidine

  1. Antimicrobial stewardship in HAI — which intervention has the HIGHEST impact on reducing inappropriate antibiotic use?

A. Automatic stop orders

B. Prospective audit with feedback at 48–72 hours

C. IV→oral switch protocol

C. Penicillin allergy de-labelling

E. Diagnostic stewardship

Flashcards

  • Q: CLABSI most common pathogen

A: CoNS

  • Q: CLABSI best site

A: Subclavian > Jugular > Femoral

  • Q: CLABSI bundle

A: Hand hygiene, Max barrier, CHG prep, Subclavian, Daily review, CHG dressing, Hub scrub

  • Q: CLABSI remove line

A: S. aureus, Candida, Pseudomonas, Bacillus, Mycobacteria

  • Q: CAUTI prevention #1

A: Daily review → Remove catheter ASAP

  • Q: CAUTI duration

A: Removed → 7d; Retained → 10-14d + change cath 48-72h

  • Q: VAP early pathogens

A: MSSA, H. influenzae, S. pneumoniae

  • Q: VAP late pathogens

A: MRSA, Pseudomonas, Acinetobacter, ESBL

  • Q: VAP bundle

A: HOB 30-45°, Daily SBT, Subglottic drainage, Oral CHG

  • Q: VAP duration

A: 7 days

  • Q: SSI prophylaxis timing

A: Cefazolin ≤60min pre-incision; Stop ≤24h

  • Q: SSI glucose target

A: <180 mg/dL

  • Q: SSI prophylaxis duration

A: ≤24 hours

  • Q: VAP subglottic drainage

A: Reduces VAP ~50%

  • Q: CLABSI most common

A: CoNS

  • Q: CAUTI #1 prevention

A: Daily review + Remove

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — CoNS account for 30–40% of CLABSI, most common pathogen.
  2. C — Subclavian site has lowest CLABSI risk; femoral highest; jugular intermediate.
  3. C — Daily necessity review with nurse-driven removal is the single most effective CAUTI prevention strategy.
  4. B — Head of bed 30–45° + daily sedation vacation + spontaneous breathing trial has strongest evidence for VAP reduction.
  5. B — SSI prophylaxis cefazolin 2g IV within 60 min before incision; re-dose if prolonged surgery/blood loss.
  6. D — CoNS CLABSI may be managed with line salvage (treat through line + systemic); removal not mandatory.
  7. B — Late VAP with MDR risk: anti-pseudomonal β-lactam (pip-tazo/meropenem) + vancomycin.
  8. C — Multiple RCTs show 7 days non-inferior to 14 days for VAP.
  9. B — SSI prophylaxis should be discontinued within 24 hours post-operatively; no benefit beyond.
  10. C — CAUTI with catheter removed: 7 days antibiotics.
  11. C — Early VAP (≤5 days): community flora — MSSA, H. influenzae, S. pneumoniae.

SBAs

  1. B — CoNS CLABSI with stable patient: line salvage acceptable (treat through line + systemic vancomycin).
  2. C — CAUTI catheter removed: 7 days antibiotics.
  3. B — Late VAP (>5d) with MDR risk: pip-tazo/meropenem + vancomycin.
  4. B — Cefazolin 2g IV within 60 min pre-incision; re-dose if surgery >4h or blood loss >1.5L.
  5. B — S. aureus CLABSI: remove line + vancomycin + β-lactam (pip-tazo or ceftriaxone).
  6. C — Early VAP (day 3), no MDR risk: ceftriaxone ± vancomycin covers community pathogens.
  7. B — SSI prophylaxis: discontinue ≤24 hours post-op (no benefit beyond).
  8. B — CHG dressings recommended for lines expected to remain >7 days.
  9. B — Subglottic secretion drainage reduces VAP by ~50% (meta-analyses).
  10. B — Prospective audit with feedback at 48–72h has highest impact on reducing inappropriate antibiotic use (IDSA/SHEA guideline).

tags: [medicine, infectious-disease, davidson, chapter13, hap, vap, hospital-acquired, ventilator-associated, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_chapter: Chapter 13: Infectious disease

topic_category: Respiratory Infections Domain

status: full-fcps-mrcp-topic-note

Hospital-Acquired and Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia (HAP/VAP)

Related: [[Community-Acquired Pneumonia (CAP)]], [[Aspiration Pneumonia]], [[Sepsis and Septic Shock]], [[Clostridioides difficile Infection]], [[Antimicrobial Stewardship]]

[!important]
HAP = pneumonia ≥48h after hospital admission, not incubating at admission. VAP = pneumonia ≥48h after endotracheal intubation. Non-ventilator HAP (NV-HAP) = HAP without intubation. Diagnosis: clinical + radiographic + microbiological. Empirical antibiotics by local epidemiology, risk factors for MDR. De-escalate at 48–72h. Duration 7 days (shorter if clinical criteria met). VAP prevention bundle.

Learning Objectives

  • Define HAP, VAP, NV-HAP and distinguish from CAP
  • Apply clinical, radiological, and microbiological diagnostic criteria
  • Identify risk factors for MDR pathogens
  • Select empirical antibiotics by local antibiogram and MDR risk
  • Interpret quantitative vs qualitative cultures (VAP)
  • De-escalate therapy at 48–72h based on cultures
  • Apply 7-day treatment duration (shorter if criteria met)
  • Implement VAP prevention bundle

Definitions & Epidemiology

TermDefinitionTimingTypical Pathogens
HAPPneumonia ≥48h after admission, not incubating at admission≥48h post-admissionS. aureus, Pseudomonas, Enterobacteriaceae, Acinetobacter
VAPPneumonia ≥48h after endotracheal intubation≥48h post-intubationPseudomonas, S. aureus, Enterobacteriaceae, Acinetobacter
NV-HAPHAP without mechanical ventilation≥48h post-admissionSimilar to HAP but more S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae
Early-onset HAP/VAP≤4 days hospitalisation≤4 daysSimilar to CAP (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, MSSA)
Late-onset HAP/VAP≥5 days hospitalisation≥5 daysMDR pathogens (Pseudomonas, MRSA, Acinetobacter, ESBL)
[!tip]
HAP/VAP = 2nd most common nosocomial infection (after UTI). Mortality 20–50% (attributable ~10–15%). VAP increases ICU stay by 4–6 days, cost $40K+.

Risk Factors for MDR Pathogens

Risk FactorPathogens of Concern
Prior IV antibiotics (≤90 days)ESBL Enterobacteriaceae, MDR Pseudomonas, MRSA, Acinetobacter
Current hospitalisation ≥5 daysLate-onset flora (MDR Gram-neg, MRSA)
ICU admissionPseudomonas, Acinetobacter, Stenotrophomonas
Mechanical ventilationPseudomonas, Acinetobacter
Structural lung disease (bronchiectasis, COPD)Pseudomonas, S. aureus
ImmunosuppressionFungi, Pneumocystis, CMV, Nocardia, Mycobacteria
Recent travel (high MDR regions)NDM/ESBL Enterobacteriaceae, Acinetobacter
Known colonisationSame organism likely
[!key]
MDR risk factors: prior antibiotics, ≥5 days hospitalisation, ICU, ventilation, known colonisation. Local antibiogram essential for empirical choice.

Diagnostic Criteria

Clinical + Radiological (All Required for Possible HAP/VAP)

  1. New/progressive infiltrate on CXR/CT (or ≥2 serial CXRs for VAP)
  2. Plus ≥2 of:
  • Fever >38.3°C or hypothermia <36°C
  • Leukocytosis (>10×10⁹/L) or leukopenia (<4×10⁹/L)
  • Purulent respiratory secretions
  • Worsening gas exchange (↑FiO₂, ↓PaO₂/FiO₂, new/increased ventilator support)

Microbiological (For Probable/Confirmed)

Sample TypeThresholdSignificance
Endotracheal aspirate (ETA)≥10⁵ CFU/mLGood sensitivity, low specificity (colonisation)
Bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL)≥10⁴ CFU/mLBetter specificity
Protected specimen brush (PSB)≥10³ CFU/mLHigher specificity, more invasive
Mini-BAL≥10⁴ CFU/mLCompromise
Blood culturePositiveDefinitive (haematogenous spread rare)
Pleural fluidPositiveDefinitive if empyema
[!tip]
Quantitative cultures (BAL ≥10⁴, PSB ≥10³) > qualitative for VAP — reduce overtreatment. Clinical Pulmonary Infection Score (CPIS) ≥6 supports VAP but not diagnostic alone.

Empirical Antibiotic Therapy (Start IMMEDIATELY after cultures)

Strategy: Cover likely pathogens based on LOCAL ANTIBIOGRAM + MDR risk factors

Low MDR Risk (Early-onset, no risk factors, low local MDR prevalence)

RegimenDose
Piperacillin-tazobactam4.5g IV 6h
OR Cefepime2g IV 8h
OR Ceftriaxone2g IV 12h
OR Levofloxacin750mg IV OD
OR Meropenem1g IV 8h

High MDR Risk (≥1 risk factor) — MUST Cover: Pseudomonas, MRSA, ESBL

Regimen (Dual Pseudomonas + MRSA + ESBL coverage)Doses
Anti-pseudomonal β-lactam + Anti-MRSA agent
Pip-tazo 4.5g 6h OR Cefepime 2g 8h OR Meropenem 1g 8h OR Ceftazidime 2g 8h+
Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg IV 6h (trough 15–20) OR Linezolid 600mg IV 12h
[!warning]
If local Pseudomonas resistance to Pip-tazo >10–15% → use Meropenem/Cefepime as primary β-lactam. If MRSA prevalence >10–20% → ADD anti-MRSA agent empirically. If ESBL prevalence high → Carbapenem preferred.

Additional Coverage if Indicated

ScenarioAdd
Anaerobes suspected (aspiration, necrotising)Metronidazole 500mg IV 8h (if β-lactam not anaerobic-active)
Legionella (immunocompromised, water system outbreak)Azithromycin 500mg IV/PO OD
Fungal (prolonged ICU, immunosuppressed)Echinocandin (Caspofungin/Micafungin)

Culture Interpretation & De-escalation (At 48–72 Hours)

Culture ResultAction

| Negative (no growth)

  • Qualitative: Consider stopping antibiotics if clinical improvement
  • Quantitative (BAL <10⁴, PSB <10³): Stop antibiotics (high NPV) |
Single pathogen susceptibleDe-escalate to narrowest effective agent
MDR pathogen confirmedContinue/Adjust targeted therapy
PolymicrobialCover all; consider anaerobes if aspiration

De-escalation Principles

  1. Stop antibiotics if cultures negative + clinical improvement (especially quantitative)
  2. Narrow spectrum to targeted pathogen(s)
  3. Switch IV → oral if clinical stability (same criteria as CAP)
  4. Remove redundant agents (e.g., drop vancomycin if MSSA, drop second Gram-neg agent)

Treatment Duration

ScenarioDuration
Uncomplicated HAP/VAP7 days (IDSA/ATS 2016)
Non-fermenting Gram-neg (Pseudomonas, Acinetobacter)7 days (extend if slow response)
MRSA7 days
Clinical stability criteria at day 7Stop if: afebrile 48h, WBC normalising, improving gas exchange, tolerating oral
Slow response / immunocompromised / empyema / abscessExtend to 10–14 days
[!key]
7 days = standard for uncomplicated HAP/VAP. Shorter (5–7d) non-inferior in RCTs. Do NOT prolong "just in case" — drives resistance.

VAP Prevention Bundle (Institute for Healthcare Improvement)

ComponentAction
Head of bed elevation30–45° (unless contraindicated)
Daily sedation interruption + SBTDaily (spontaneous breathing trial)
Peptic ulcer prophylaxisPPI/H2 blocker (balance with C. diff risk)
DVT prophylaxisLMWH/UFH + mechanical
Oral care with chlorhexidine0.12–2% CHX q6–12h (controversial for mortality, reduces VAP)
Subglottic secretion drainageEndotracheal tube with subglottic suction port (reduces VAP ~50%)
Cuff pressure20–30 cmH₂O (prevents microaspiration)
Avoid unnecessary intubationNIV when appropriate
Early extubationDaily readiness assessment

Pathogen-Specific Targeted Therapy

PathogenPreferred Agent(s)AlternativesDuration
MSSAFlucloxacillin/Cefazolin 2g 4–6hPip-tazo, Meropenem7 days
MRSAVancomycin (trough 15–20) OR Linezolid 600mg 12hDaptomycin, Ceftaroline7 days
P. aeruginosa (susceptible)Pip-tazo/Cefepime/Meropenem/CeftazidimeCiprofloxacin/Levofloxacin7 days
MDR Pseudomonas (XDR)Ceftolozane-tazobactam, Ceftazidime-avibactam, Imipenem-relebactam, Polymyxin + high-dose meropenemAminoglycoside + β-lactam7–14 days
ESBL EnterobacteriaceaeMeropenem 1g 8h (preferred)Ertapenem (if no Pseudomonas risk)7 days
Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacteriaceae (CRE)Ceftazidime-avibactam, Meropenem-vaborbactam, Imipenem-relebactam, Plazomicin, Polymyxin-basedTigecycline (not for VAP — poor lung penetration)7–14 days
Acinetobacter baumanniiMeropenem/Sulbactam (if susceptible)Polymyxin, Tigecycline, Minocycline, Ampicillin-sulbactam7–14 days
Stenotrophomonas maltophiliaTMP-SMX 5mg/kg (TMP) 6hLevofloxacin, Minocycline, Ceftazidime-avibactam7–14 days

Special Situations

SituationAdjustment
ImmunocompromisedBroader empirical (add antifungal, PJP cover, antiviral); BAL preferred
Post-lung transplantCover Pseudomonas, Aspergillus, CMV, Nocardia; BAL mandatory
Structural lung disease (bronchiectasis, CF)Higher Pseudomonas risk; antipseudomonal β-lactam + fluoroquinolone/aminoglycoside
Penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis)Aztreonam + Fluoroquinolone + Vancomycin/Linezolid
Renal impairmentDose adjust all; avoid aminoglycosides if possible

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • HAP = ≥48h post-admission; VAP = ≥48h post-intubation; NV-HAP = HAP without vent
  • Early-onset (≤4d) = CAP-like flora; Late-onset (≥5d) = MDR flora
  • MDR risk factors: prior antibiotics (90d), ≥5d hospitalisation, ICU, vent, known colonisation
  • Diagnosis: new infiltrate + clinical signs + microbiological confirmation (quantitative cultures preferred for VAP)
  • Empirical: local antibiogram + MDR risk → anti-pseudomonal β-lactam ± anti-MRSA agent
  • Quantitative cultures: BAL ≥10⁴, PSB ≥10³ = positive; negative = stop antibiotics if improving
  • De-escalate at 48–72h: stop if negative, narrow if positive, switch IV→oral if stable
  • Duration: 7 days standard (shorter if criteria met)
  • VAP bundle: HOB 30–45°, daily sedation hold + SBT, subglottic suction, chlorhexidine oral care, cuff pressure 20–30
  • CRE: ceftazidime-avibactam/meropenem-vaborbactam/imipenem-relebactam; avoid tigecycline for VAP
  • Stenotrophomonas: TMP-SMX first-line

Common Viva Questions

  1. Difference between HAP, VAP, NV-HAP? HAP ≥48h post-admission; VAP ≥48h post-intubation; NV-HAP = HAP without ventilation.
  2. Early vs late onset HAP/VAP? Early ≤4d = CAP-like flora; Late ≥5d = MDR flora (Pseudomonas, MRSA, Acinetobacter).
  3. MDR risk factors for HAP/VAP? Prior IV antibiotics ≤90d, hospitalisation ≥5d, ICU, mechanical ventilation, known colonisation.
  4. VAP diagnostic criteria? New/progressive infiltrate + ≥2 clinical signs + quantitative culture (BAL ≥10⁴, PSB ≥10³).
  5. Empirical antibiotics for high MDR risk VAP? Anti-pseudomonal β-lactam (Pip-tazo/Meropenem/Cefepime) + anti-MRSA (Vancomycin/Linezolid).
  6. When to de-escalate? 48–72h: stop if cultures negative + improving; narrow to targeted pathogen; switch IV→oral if stable.
  7. Standard treatment duration? 7 days for uncomplicated HAP/VAP.
  8. VAP prevention bundle components? HOB 30–45°, daily sedation hold + SBT, subglottic suction, chlorhexidine oral care, cuff pressure 20–30 cmH₂O, DVT/PUD prophylaxis.
  9. CRE treatment options? Ceftazidime-avibactam, Meropenem-vaborbactam, Imipenem-relebactam, Plazomicin, Polymyxin-based.
  10. Stenotrophomonas maltophilia treatment? TMP-SMX first-line (5mg/kg TMP 6h).

Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
HCAP still existsHCAP retired — assess MDR risk factors within HAP/VAP
Qualitative cultures enough for VAPQuantitative (BAL/PSB) preferred — higher specificity, allows stopping if negative
Always need dual Gram-neg coverageDual only if high resistance prevalence or severe sepsis; monotherapy with appropriate β-lactam often sufficient
Vancomycin for all HAP/VAPOnly if MRSA risk factors (prevalence >10–20%, prior MRSA, necrotising)
14–21 days standard duration7 days standard; extend only if slow response / complications
Tigecycline for VAPPoor lung penetration — avoid for VAP
Colistin first-line for CRENewer β-lactam/β-lactamase inhibitors preferred (ceftazidime-avibactam etc.)
Chlorhexidine oral care reduces mortalityReduces VAP incidence but NOT mortality
Subglottic suction for all ventsOnly for expected >24–48h ventilation
Stop antibiotics if qualitative culture negativeQualitative negative ≠ no infection; quantitative negative (BAL<10⁴) has high NPV

Mnemonics

  • HAP/VAP DEFINITIONS: HAP = Hospital ≥48h; VAP = Vent ≥48h; NV-HAP = Non-Vent HAP
  • MDR RISK: Prior abx (90d), Hospital ≥5d, ICU, Vent, Known colonisation (PHI VK)
  • EARLY vs LATE: Early (≤4d) = Easy flora (CAP-like); Late (≥5d) = Lethal MDR flora
  • VAP CULTURE THRESHOLDS: BAL = 10⁴, PSB = 10³, ETA = 10⁵
  • VAP BUNDLE: Head Of Bed 30–45°, Sedation hold + SBT, Subglottic Suction, CHX oral care, Cuff Pressure
  • HAP/VAP EMPIRICAL: Low MDR risk = single β-lactam; High MDR risk = Anti-Pseudo β-lactam + Anti-MRSA
  • DURATION: 7 DAYS (stop if stable)
  • CRE Rx: CAV (Ceftazidime-Avibactam), MV (Meropenem-Vaborbactam), IR (Imipenem-Relebactam)
  • STENOTROPHOMONAS: TMP-SMX first-line

Mind Map

HAP / VAP
Definitions
HAP: ≥48h post-admission
VAP: ≥48h post-intubation
NV-HAP: non-vent HAP
Early ≤4d vs Late ≥5d
MDR Risk Factors
Prior abx ≤90d
Hospital ≥5d
ICU / Ventilation
Known colonisation
Diagnosis
New infiltrate + clinical signs
Quantitative cultures: BAL ≥10⁴, PSB ≥10³
CPIS ≥6 supportive
Empirical Rx
Low MDR: single β-lactam
High MDR: Anti-Pseudo β-lactam + Anti-MRSA
De-escalation (48-72h)
Negative quant → STOP
Positive → narrow targeted
IV→Oral if stable
Duration
7 days standard
VAP Bundle
HOB 30-45°, Sedation hold+SBT
Subglottic suction, CHX, Cuff pressure
Pathogen-Specific
MRSA: Vanco/Linezolid
Pseudomonas: Anti-Pseudo β-lactam
CRE: CAV/MV/IR/Plazomicin
Acinetobacter: Meropenem/Sulbactam/Polymyxin
Stenotrophomonas: TMP-SMX

Flowchart

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Suspected HAP/VAP
↓
CXR: New/Progressive Infiltrate
↓
Clinical Signs: Fever, WBC, Purulent secretions, Gas exchange
↓
Microbiological: Quantitative cultures (BAL/PSB preferred)
↓
MDR Risk Factors?
↓
Single β-lactam: Pip-tazo/Cefepime/Ceftriaxone/Levo/Mero
Anti-Pseudo β-lactam + Anti-MRSA agent
↓
Cultures at 48-72h
↓
Result?
↓
STOP antibiotics if improving
De-escalate to narrowest active agent
Continue/Adjust targeted therapy
Cover all ± anaerobes
↓
7 days total if uncomplicated
7-14 days per pathogen

Suggested Visuals / Image Notes

  • HAP/VAP diagnostic criteria algorithm
  • VAP bundle checklist
  • MDR risk factor assessment
  • De-escalation decision tree
  • CRE treatment algorithm
  • Antibiotic spectrum comparison

Suggested Video References

  • IDSA/ATS HAP/VAP guidelines 2016
  • VAP prevention bundle implementation
  • Quantitative vs qualitative cultures
  • CRE management
  • Antimicrobial stewardship in ICU

One-Page Revision Summary

TopicKey Points
DefinitionsHAP ≥48h admission; VAP ≥48h intubation; NV-HAP = non-vent HAP
OnsetEarly ≤4d = CAP-like; Late ≥5d = MDR
MDR RiskPrior abx 90d, hosp ≥5d, ICU, vent, colonisation
DiagnosisInfiltrate + clinical signs + quant culture (BAL ≥10⁴, PSB ≥10³)
Low MDR RxSingle β-lactam (Pip-tazo/Cefepime/Ceftriaxone/Levo/Mero)
High MDR RxAnti-Pseudo β-lactam + Anti-MRSA (Vanco/Linezolid)
De-escalation48-72h: neg quant → STOP; pos → narrow; IV→Oral if stable

VAP BundleHOB 30-45°, daily sedation hold+SBT, subglottic suction, CHX, cuff 20-30

24-Hour Recall Prompts

7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

My Weak Points

Self-Test Scorecard

[!tip]
Interpretation: <35 = weak topic, 35-44 = acceptable but insecure, 45+ = strong exam-ready topic.

Exam Answer Modes

Long Answer Skeleton

  1. Definitions (HAP, VAP, NV-HAP, early vs late onset)
  2. MDR risk factors
  3. Diagnostic criteria (clinical, radiological, microbiological)
  4. Quantitative vs qualitative cultures
  5. Empirical therapy by MDR risk (local antibiogram)
  6. De-escalation strategy at 48–72h
  7. Treatment duration
  8. VAP prevention bundle
  9. Pathogen-specific targeted therapy (MSSA, MRSA, Pseudomonas, ESBL, CRE, Acinetobacter, Stenotrophomonas)
  10. Special populations

Short Note Skeleton

  • HAP: ≥48h admission; VAP: ≥48h intubation; Early ≤4d CAP-like; Late ≥5d MDR
  • MDR risk: prior abx, hosp≥5d, ICU, vent, colonisation
  • Diagnosis: infiltrate + signs + quant culture (BAL≥10⁴, PSB≥10³)
  • Low MDR: single β-lactam; High MDR: anti-Pseudo β-lactam + anti-MRSA
  • De-escalate 48-72h: neg quant→STOP; pos→narrow; IV→Oral if stable
  • Duration: 7 days
  • VAP bundle: HOB 30-45°, sedation hold+SBT, subglottic suction, CHX, cuff pressure
  • CRE: CAV/MV/IR/Plazomicin; Stenotrophomonas: TMP-SMX

Viva One-Liners

  • HAP ≥48h admission; VAP ≥48h intubation
  • Early ≤4d = CAP flora; Late ≥5d = MDR flora
  • MDR risk: PHI VK (Prior abx, Hosp≥5d, ICU, Vent, Known colonisation)
  • VAP culture: BAL 10⁴, PSB 10³, ETA 10⁵
  • High MDR empirical: Anti-Pseudo β-lactam + Vanco/Linezolid
  • 7 days duration
  • VAP bundle: HOB, SBT, subglottic, CHX, cuff
  • CRE: CAV/MV/IR; Stenotrophomonas: TMP-SMX

Ward-Case Discussion Points

  • 65M, post-op day 6, vented, new infiltrate, purulent secretions. Prior pip-tazo 2w ago. MDR risk: YES (prior abx, ≥5d, vent). Empirical: Meropenem + Vancomycin. BAL at 48h: Pseudomonas susceptible → Meropenem monotherapy 7d.
  • 40F, ICU day 10, VAP. BAL: CRE (NDM+). Empirical: Meropenem + Vanco. Targeted: Ceftazidime-avibactam 2.5g 8h ×7-14d.
  • 55M, bronchiectasis, HAP day 4. CXR: new consolidation. Low MDR risk (early, no prior abx). Ceftriaxone + Azithromycin (atypical cover). Sputum: H. influenzae → Amoxicillin 7d.

Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet

HAP/VAP: HAP ≥48h admission; VAP ≥48h intubation. Early ≤4d = CAP flora; Late ≥5d = MDR flora. MDR risk: prior abx 90d, hosp≥5d, ICU, vent, colonisation. Diagnosis: infiltrate + signs + quant culture (BAL≥10⁴, PSB≥10³). Low MDR: single β-lactam. High MDR: anti-Pseudo β-lactam + anti-MRSA (Vanco/Linezolid). De-escalate 48-72h: neg quant→STOP; pos→narrow; IV→Oral if stable. 7 days duration. VAP bundle: HOB 30-45°, daily sedation hold+SBT, subglottic suction, CHX oral care, cuff 20-30. CRE: CAV/MV/IR/Plazomicin (avoid tigecycline VAP). Stenotrophomonas: TMP-SMX.

Summary

Hospital-acquired pneumonia (HAP) = pneumonia ≥48h after hospital admission, not incubating at admission. Ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) = pneumonia ≥48h after endotracheal intubation. Non-ventilator HAP (NV-HAP) = HAP without mechanical ventilation. Early-onset (≤4 days hospitalisation) = CAP-like flora (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, MSSA). Late-onset (≥5 days) = MDR flora (Pseudomonas, MRSA, Acinetobacter, ESBL Enterobacteriaceae). MDR risk factors: prior IV antibiotics ≤90 days, hospitalisation ≥5 days, ICU admission, mechanical ventilation, known colonisation. Diagnosis: new/progressive infiltrate + ≥2 clinical signs (fever, leukocytosis/leukopenia, purulent secretions, worsening gas exchange) + microbiological confirmation. Quantitative cultures preferred for VAP: BAL ≥10⁴ CFU/mL, PSB ≥10³ CFU/mL, ETA ≥10⁵ CFU/mL. Empirical antibiotics: guided by LOCAL antibiogram + MDR risk. Low MDR risk = single anti-pseudomonal β-lactam (Pip-tazo, Cefepime, Meropenem, Ceftriaxone, Levofloxacin). High MDR risk = anti-pseudomonal β-lactam + anti-MRSA agent (Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg 6h or Linezolid 600mg 12h). De-escalation at 48–72h: negative quantitative culture + improving → STOP; single pathogen → narrow to targeted agent; switch IV→oral if clinically stable. Duration: 7 days standard for uncomplicated HAP/VAP. VAP prevention bundle: HOB 30–45°, daily sedation interruption + SBT, subglottic secretion drainage, chlorhexidine oral care, cuff pressure 20–30 cmH₂O, DVT/PUD prophylaxis. CRE: ceftazidime-avibactam, meropenem-vaborbactam, imipenem-relebactam, plazomicin, polymyxin-based; avoid tigecycline for VAP (poor lung penetration). Stenotrophomonas maltophilia: TMP-SMX 5mg/kg (TMP) 6h first-line.

MCQs (10)

  1. VAP is defined as pneumonia developing:

A. ≥24h after intubation

B. ≥48h after intubation

C. ≥72h after intubation

D. ≥5 days after intubation

E. Any time after intubation

  1. Which is a risk factor for MDR pathogens in HAP/VAP?

A. Hospitalisation 2 days

B. Prior IV antibiotics within 90 days

C. Age >65

D. COPD without recent antibiotics

E. Single comorbidity

  1. Quantitative culture threshold for BAL in VAP:

A. ≥10³ CFU/mL

B. ≥10⁴ CFU/mL

C. ≥10⁵ CFU/mL

D. ≥10⁶ CFU/mL

E. Any growth

  1. Preferred empirical regimen for HIGH MDR risk VAP:

A. Ceftriaxone alone

B. Levofloxacin alone

C. Piperacillin-tazobactam + Vancomycin

D. Meropenem alone

E. Cefepime + Azithromycin

  1. Standard treatment duration for uncomplicated HAP/VAP:

A. 5 days

B. 7 days

C. 10 days

D. 14 days

E. 21 days

  1. Which VAP bundle component has the STRONGEST evidence for reducing VAP incidence?

A. Chlorhexidine oral care

B. Subglottic secretion drainage

C. HOB elevation 30–45°

D. Daily sedation hold

E. Peptic ulcer prophylaxis

  1. First-line treatment for Stenotrophomonas maltophilia pneumonia:

A. Meropenem

B. TMP-SMX

C. Ciprofloxacin

C. Ceftazidime-avibactam

E. Tigecycline

  1. Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacteriaceae (CRE) VAP — AVOID:

A. Ceftazidime-avibactam

B. Meropenem-vaborbactam

C. Tigecycline (poor lung penetration)

D. Imipenem-relebactam

E. Plazomicin

  1. De-escalation at 48–72h: quantitative BAL culture negative (<10⁴) + patient improving:

A. Continue empirical antibiotics for 7 days

B. Stop antibiotics

C. Switch to oral antibiotics

D. Repeat BAL in 24h

E. Add antifungal

  1. Early-onset HAP (day 3 post-admission) most likely pathogen:

A. Pseudomonas aeruginosa

B. S. pneumoniae

C. Acinetobacter baumannii

D. MRSA

E. ESBL Klebsiella

SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 70-year-old man, post-abdominal surgery day 6, mechanically ventilated 4 days. New infiltrate, purulent secretions, fever 39°C. Received piperacillin-tazobactam for intra-abdominal infection 3 weeks ago. MDR risk factors?

A. None

B. Prior antibiotics (within 90d) + hospitalisation ≥5d + mechanical ventilation

C. Only hospitalisation ≥5d

D. Only mechanical ventilation

E. Only prior antibiotics

  1. A patient with VAP. BAL quantitative culture: 5×10³ CFU/mL P. aeruginosa. Clinical status improving. Action?

A. Continue empirical broad-spectrum

B. De-escalate to targeted anti-pseudomonal monotherapy ×7 days total

C. Stop antibiotics (culture <10⁴)

D. Add second Gram-negative agent

E. Extend to 14 days

  1. A 60-year-old woman with VAP day 10. Known MRSA colonised (nares). Empirical regimen?

A. Cefepime alone

B. Meropenem + Vancomycin

C. Pip-tazo + Linezolid

D. Ceftriaxone + Vancomycin

E. Levofloxacin + Vancomycin

  1. A patient with VAP. Quantitative PSB culture: no growth. Patient afebrile, improving gas exchange, WBC normalising. Best action?

A. Continue antibiotics 7 days

B. Stop antibiotics

C. Switch to oral levofloxacin

D. Repeat PSB in 24h

E. Start antifungals

  1. CRE VAP (NDM-producing Klebsiella). Targeted regimen?

A. Tigecycline + Polymyxin B

B. Ceftazidime-avibactam 2.5g IV 8h

C. High-dose meropenem alone

D. Ertapenem + Polymyxin

E. Fosfomycin IV

  1. VAP prevention: subglottic secretion drainage endotracheal tube indicated for:

A. All intubated patients

B. Expected ventilation >24–48 hours

C. Only COPD patients

D. Only if VAP already diagnosed

E. Only immunocompromised

  1. A 55-year-old man with HAP day 5. No MDR risk factors. CXR: right lower lobe consolidation. Blood cultures negative. Sputum: H. influenzae (ampicillin-susceptible). Targeted therapy?

A. Meropenem ×7d

B. Amoxicillin/Amoxicillin-clavulanate ×7d

C. Levofloxacin ×7d

D. Ceftriaxone ×14d

E. Pip-tazo ×7d

  1. A patient with Acinetobacter baumannii VAP (carbapenem-susceptible). Preferred targeted therapy?

A. Tigecycline

B. Meropenem 1g IV 8h (or Ampicillin-sulbactam high-dose)

C. Polymyxin B

C. Ceftazidime-avibactam

E. Minocycline

  1. IV to oral switch in HAP/VAP — criteria same as CAP EXCEPT:

A. Afebrile 24h

B. HR ≤100

C. Chest X-ray cleared (NOT required)

D. SBP ≥90

E. Tolerating oral

  1. Duration of therapy for uncomplicated VAP with clinical stability at day 7:

A. Stop antibiotics (7 days total)

B. Continue to 10 days

C. Continue to 14 days

D. Switch to oral for 7 more days

E. Extend until CXR clear

Flashcards

  • Q: HAP definition

A: Pneumonia ≥48h after admission, not incubating at admission

  • Q: VAP definition

A: Pneumonia ≥48h after endotracheal intubation

  • Q: Early vs Late onset

A: Early ≤4d = CAP flora; Late ≥5d = MDR flora

  • Q: MDR risk factors

A: Prior abx 90d, hosp ≥5d, ICU, vent, colonisation

  • Q: VAP quant culture thresholds

A: BAL ≥10⁴, PSB ≥10³, ETA ≥10⁵

  • Q: High MDR empirical

A: Anti-Pseudo β-lactam + Anti-MRSA

  • Q: Low MDR empirical

A: Single β-lactam

  • Q: De-escalation 48-72h

A: Neg quant→STOP; Pos→narrow; IV→Oral if stable

  • Q: Duration

A: 7 days standard

  • Q: VAP bundle

A: HOB 30-45°, sedation hold+SBT, subglottic suction, CHX, cuff 20-30

  • Q: CRE treatment

A: CAV/MV/IR/Plazomicin/Polymyxin (not tigecycline for VAP)

  • Q: Stenotrophomonas

A: TMP-SMX first-line

  • Q: Acinetobacter

A: Meropenem/Sulbactam/Polymyxin

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — VAP = pneumonia ≥48h after endotracheal intubation.
  2. B — Prior IV antibiotics within 90 days is a major MDR risk factor. Hospitalisation ≥5d, ICU, ventilation, and known colonisation are the others.
  3. B — BAL quantitative threshold for VAP = ≥10⁴ CFU/mL. PSB = ≥10³, ETA = ≥10⁵.
  4. C — High MDR risk VAP: anti-pseudomonal β-lactam (Pip-tazo/Meropenem/Cefepime) + anti-MRSA agent (Vancomycin/Linezolid).
  5. B — 7 days is standard for uncomplicated HAP/VAP (IDSA/ATS 2016).
  6. B — Subglottic secretion drainage reduces VAP incidence by ~50% (strongest evidence). Chlorhexidine reduces VAP but not mortality.
  7. B — Stenotrophomonas maltophilia is intrinsically resistant to most β-lactams; TMP-SMX is first-line.
  8. C — Tigecycline has poor lung penetration (low ELF concentrations); avoid for VAP. Use newer β-lactam/β-lactamase inhibitors.
  9. B — Negative quantitative culture (BAL <10⁴) has high NPV; if patient improving, STOP antibiotics.
  10. B — Early-onset (≤4d) HAP/VAP has CAP-like flora; S. pneumoniae most common.

SBAs

  1. B — Prior antibiotics 3 weeks ago (within 90d) + hospitalisation day 6 (≥5d) + mechanical ventilation = 3 MDR risk factors.
  2. B — BAL 5×10³ = below threshold (10⁴) but P. aeruginosa grown; targeted anti-pseudomonal monotherapy for 7 days total. Not "stop" because pathogen identified.
  3. B — MRSA colonisation = MDR risk factor for MRSA; Meropenem (anti-pseudomonal) + Vancomycin (anti-MRSA) covers both. Cefepime less reliable for Pseudomonas in high-resistance settings.
  4. B — Negative quantitative PSB + clinical improvement = STOP antibiotics (high NPV).
  5. B — NDM CRE: Ceftazidime-avibactam preferred (active vs NDM). Meropenem-vaborbactam/Imipenem-relebactam NOT active vs NDM. Tigecycline avoid for VAP.
  6. B — Subglottic suction tubes indicated for expected ventilation >24–48h (cost-benefit).
  7. B — H. influenzae ampicillin-susceptible: narrow to amoxicillin/co-amoxiclav ×7 days. No need for broader agents.
  8. B — Carbapenem-susceptible Acinetobacter: Meropenem high-dose (or ampicillin-sulbactam high-dose) preferred. Tigecycline/polymyxin for resistant strains.
  9. C — CXR clearance NOT required for IV→oral switch; same clinical stability criteria as CAP.
  10. A — Clinical stability at day 7 = stop antibiotics (7 days total). Do not extend for CXR clearance.

topics.title: Infection In Immunocompromised Host Transplant Biologics

tags: [medicine, infectious-disease, davidson, chapter13, immunocompromised, transplant, biologics, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_chapter: Chapter 13: Infectious disease

topic_category: Immunocompromised Host Infections Domain

status: full-fcps-mrcp-topic-note

Infection in Immunocompromised Host (Transplant, Biologics, Chemotherapy)

Related: [[Febrile Neutropenia]], [[Invasive Candidiasis]], [[Mucormycosis]], [[Pneumocystis Pneumonia]], [[CMV Disease]], [[TB in HIV]], [[Healthcare-Associated Infections (CLABSI, CAUTI, SSI, VAP)]], [[Antimicrobial Prescribing Principles]]

[!important]
Immunocompromised host = defective innate/adaptive immunity → ↑ risk, atypical presentation, opportunistic pathogens. Risk stratification by deficit: neutropenia, T-cell deficiency, B-cell/humoral, complement, phagocyte, barrier. Transplant timeline: 0–1m (surgical, hospital flora), 1–6m (opportunistic: CMV, PCP, fungi), >6m (community + chronic rejection). Biologics: anti-TNF → TB, intracellular; anti-CD20 → HBV reactivation, PML; IL-17/23 → Candida, TB.

Learning Objectives

  • Classify immunodeficiency by immune deficit and risk profile
  • Apply transplant timeline for infection risk (0–1m, 1–6m, >6m)
  • Recognise biologic-specific infection risks (anti-TNF, anti-CD20, integrin inhibitors, JAK inhibitors)
  • Select appropriate prophylaxis (PCP, fungal, viral, TB)
  • Diagnose and manage key opportunistic infections (CMV, PCP, EBV-PTLD, BK virus, HHV-6)
  • Apply vaccination principles in immunocompromised (timing, live vs inactivated)

Classification by Immune Deficit

DeficitCausesTypical Infections
NeutropeniaChemo, HSCT, drugs (ganciclovir, valproate), aplastic anaemiaBacteria (Gram-neg, Staph), Candida, Aspergillus, Gram-neg sepsis
T-cell DeficiencyHIV, transplant (CNI, mTOR, steroids), biologics (anti-TNF, alemtuzumab), chemoViruses (CMV, EBV, BK, HHV-6, VZV), TB, fungi (Pneumocystis, Cryptococcus), Listeria, Nocardia, Toxoplasma
B-cell / HumoralAnti-CD20 (rituximab), BTK inhibitors, CLL, myeloma, hypogammaglobulinaemiaEncapsulated bacteria (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae), Giardia, enteroviruses, chronic sinopulmonary
ComplementC1q–C9 deficiency, eculizumab, C1-INH deficiencyEncapsulated bacteria (Neisseria, S. pneumoniae), immune complex disease
PhagocyteCGD, diabetes, steroids, dialysis, BurnsCatalase-positive organisms (S. aureus, Burkholderia, Nocardia, Aspergillus)
Barrier DefectBurns, surgery, lines, drains, mucositisSkin flora (Staph, Strep), Candida, Gram-neg

Transplant Timeline — Infection Risk by Period

PeriodTimeframeKey RisksTypical Pathogens
Early0–1 monthSurgical, hospital-acquiredWound infection, bacteremia (CoNS, Gram-neg), UTI, C. difficile, donor-derived
Intermediate1–6 monthsOPPORTUNISTIC (peak)CMV (peak 1–4m), PCP, invasive fungi (Aspergillus, Candida), EBV-PTLD, BK virus, HHV-6, adenovirus, TB, Nocardia, Listeria
Late>6 monthsCommunity-acquired + chronic rejectionCommunity viruses, encapsulated bacteria, chronic viral (BK, CMV), chronic rejection
All periodsAny timeNet state of immunosuppressionNet state = baseline risk + current immunosuppression + exposures
[!key]
CMV = MOST IMPORTANT opportunistic infection in transplant. Peak 1–4 months. PCP prophylaxis universal 6–12 months. Antifungal prophylaxis in high-risk (lung, liver, heart, re-transplant).

Biologic-Specific Infection Risks

Biologic ClassTargetKey Infection RisksScreening/Monitoring
Anti-TNF (infliximab, adalimumab, etanercept, certolizumab, golimumab)TNF-αTB (reactivation), intracellular bacteria (Listeria, Salmonella, Nocardia), histoplasmosis, coccidioidomycosis, hepatitis B reactivationTB screen (IGRA/TST) BEFORE; HBV screen; monitor for infection
Anti-IL-12/23 (ustekinumab)IL-12/IL-23TB, fungal, viral (similar to anti-TNF but lower risk)TB screen before
Anti-IL-17 (secukinumab, ixekizumab)IL-17ACandida (mucocutaneous), TB (rare)Monitor for Candida
Anti-IL-23 (guselkumab, risankizumab)IL-23TB, fungal (lower risk than anti-TNF)TB screen before
Anti-IL-6 (tocilizumab, sarilumab)IL-6RBacterial, TB, diverticulitis, hepatotoxicityTB screen; monitor LFTs, neutrophils
Anti-CD20 (rituximab, ocrelizumab, ofatumumab)CD20 (B-cells)HBV reactivation (HBsAg+ or anti-HBc+), PML (JCV), hypogammaglobulinaemia, encapsulated bacteria, VZVHBV screen (HBsAg, anti-HBc) BEFORE; IgG levels; JCV index if natalizumab hx
Anti-CD52 (alemtuzumab)CD52 (T/B/NK)Severe T-cell depletion → CMV, VZV, Listeria, Nocardia, TB, fungal, PMLIntensive monitoring; prophylaxis (PCP, viral, fungal)
Anti-integrin (natalizumab, vedolizumab)α4β1/α4β7 integrinPML (JCV) — natalizumab; vedolizumab gut-selective = lower PML riskJCV antibody index (natalizumab); monitor
JAK Inhibitors (tofacitinib, baricitinib, upadacitinib)JAK1/2/3Herpes zoster (VZV) ↑↑, TB, bacterial, fungal, CMV, EBV, lymphopeniaVZV vaccination before; TB screen; monitor lymphocytes
BTK Inhibitors (ibrutinib, acalabrutinib)BTKBleeding, atrial fibrillation, infections (sinopulmonary, fungal), PML riskMonitor IgG, infections
BCL-2 Inhibitor (venetoclax)BCL-2TLS, neutropenia, infectionsTLS prophylaxis; monitor counts
[!tip]
TB screen (IGRA/TST) REQUIRED BEFORE starting anti-TNF, anti-IL-12/23, anti-IL-17, anti-IL-23, JAK inhibitors, alemtuzumab. HBV screen (HBsAg, anti-HBc) REQUIRED BEFORE anti-CD20, anti-TNF, JAK inhibitors.

Key Opportunistic Infections in Transplant

CMV Disease

AspectDetails
RiskD+/R− (highest), lung/heart/intestinal transplant, lymphocyte-depleting induction
SyndromesViraemia, syndrome (fever, leucopenia, hepatitis), tissue-invasive (pneumonitis, colitis, retinitis, encephalitis)
DiagnosisQuantitative PCR (blood) — viral load guides pre-emptive therapy
Prophylaxis vs Pre-emptiveHigh-risk: prophylaxis (valganciclovir 900mg OD ×3–6m); Standard-risk: pre-emptive (weekly PCR ×3–6m)
TreatmentValganciclovir 900mg BD (oral) OR Ganciclovir 5mg/kg IV 12h ×2–3w → maintenance; Foscarnet/Cidofovir if resistant (UL97/UL54 mut)
ResistanceUL97 mutation = ganciclovir/valganciclovir resistance; UL54 = cidofovir/foscarnet resistance

PCP (Pneumocystis jirovecii Pneumonia)

AspectDetails
RiskAll transplant (T-cell suppression); HSCT; biologics (anti-TNF, alemtuzumab); HIV CD4<200
ProphylaxisTMP-SMX 960mg PO daily (or 3×/wk); alternatives: dapsone, atovaquone, inhaled pentamidine
Duration≥6–12 months post-transplant; lifelong if ongoing immunosuppression
TreatmentHigh-dose TMP-SMX 15–20mg/kg TMP IV/PO q6h ×21d + steroids if hypoxic

EBV-PTLD (Post-Transplant Lymphoproliferative Disorder)

AspectDetails
RiskEBV D+/R−, intense immunosuppression (OKT3, ATG), paediatric, intestinal/lung transplant
SpectrumEarly lesions → polymorphic PTLD → monomorphic PTLD (lymphoma)
DiagnosisEBV PCR (blood) — rising viral load; biopsy for histology
TreatmentReduce immunosuppression (RI) = cornerstone; Rituximab (anti-CD20); EBV-CTL; chemo for monomorphic

BK Virus Nephropathy

AspectDetails
RiskKidney transplant; immunosuppression (Tacrolimus, MMF); ureteric stent
DiagnosisQuantitative PCR (blood, urine) — decoy cells in urine; biopsy (SV40 staining)
TreatmentReduce immunosuppression (reduce/stop Tac/MMF); Leflunomide, Cidofovir, IVIG (adjunctive)

Fungal Infections in Immunocompromised

FungusHigh-Risk HostProphylaxisTreatment
AspergillusLung transplant, HSCT, prolonged neutropenia, steroidsPosaconazole (HSCT, AML), voriconazole (lung)Voriconazole 1st-line; isavuconazole; L-AmB
CandidaICU, abdominal surgery, neutropenia, TPN, broad antibioticsFluconazole (selective), echinocandin (high-risk)Echinocandin 1st-line; step-down fluconazole
MucormycosisDKA, neutropenia, deferoxamine, steroids, transplantNone standardL-AmB + urgent surgery
PneumocystisAll transplant, HIV CD4<200, biologicsTMP-SMX universalHigh-dose TMP-SMX + steroids
CryptococcusHIV CD4<100, transplant, steroidsFluconazole (targeted)L-AmB + flucytosine → fluconazole

Prophylaxis Protocols — Transplant (Example: Kidney)

PathogenAgentDurationHigh-Risk Indications
PCPTMP-SMX 960mg 3×/wk6–12 monthsAll (lifelong if ongoing immunosuppression)
CMVValganciclovir 900mg OD3–6 months (prophylaxis) OR pre-emptiveD+/R−, lung/heart, lymphocyte depletion
FungalFluconazole 200mg OD / Posaconazole1–3 monthsLung, liver, heart, re-transplant, prolonged ICU
VZVAcyclovir 400mg BD3–6 monthsSeropositive recipients
HBVEntecavir/TDFIndefiniteHBsAg+ recipients
UTITMP-SMX / Nitrofurantoin3–6 monthsRecurrent UTI, structural abnormality

Vaccination in Immunocompromised

VaccineTypeTimingNotes
Inactivated (Flu, Pneumococcal, Hep A/B, HPV, Tdap, COVID, Rabies, IPV, Hib, MenACWY)InactivatedAnytime; ideally ≥2 weeks BEFORE immunosuppressionMay have reduced response; give extra doses per schedule
Live (MMR, Varicella, Zoster live, Yellow Fever, BCG, Oral Typhoid, Rotavirus, LAIV)Live≥4 weeks BEFORE immunosuppression; CONTRAINDICATED duringExcept: Zoster recombinant (Shingrix) = safe during; MMR/Varicella if CD4>200 in HIV
Zoster Recombinant (Shingrix)Recombinant (non-live)Safe during immunosuppressionPreferred for immunocompromised ≥18y
Post-TransplantInactivated: 3–6m post; Live: ≥2 years post (if off immunosuppression)Check local guidelines
[!warning]
Live vaccines CONTRAINDICATED during immunosuppression. Exception: Shingrix (recombinant) safe. HIV CD4>200: MMR/Varicella may be given.

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Transplant timeline: 0–1m (surgical/hospital), 1–6m (opportunistic: CMV, PCP, fungi, EBV-PTLD), >6m (community)
  • CMV = most important opportunistic infection; D+/R− highest risk; prophylaxis vs pre-emptive; valganciclovir/ganciclovir
  • PCP prophylaxis: TMP-SMX universal 6–12m post-transplant; lifelong if ongoing immunosuppression
  • Anti-TNF: TB reactivation (screen IGRA/TST BEFORE); histoplasma, coccidioides, HBV reactivation
  • Anti-CD20 (rituximab): HBV reactivation (screen HBsAg/anti-HBc BEFORE); PML risk; hypogammaglobulinaemia
  • JAK inhibitors: VZV (shingles) ↑↑; TB; vaccinate VZV before; screen TB
  • Alemtuzumab: severe T-cell depletion; intense prophylaxis (PCP, viral, fungal)
  • PCP prophylaxis: TMP-SMX 6–12m post-transplant; lifelong if ongoing immunosuppression
  • Fungal prophylaxis: posaconazole/voriconazole in high-risk (lung, liver, heart, HSCT)
  • Vaccination: live vaccines contraindicated during immunosuppression; Shingrix safe; give inactivated ≥2w before

Common Viva Questions

  1. Transplant infection timeline? 0–1m: surgical/hospital; 1–6m: opportunistic (CMV, PCP, fungi, EBV); >6m: community.
  2. CMV prophylaxis vs pre-emptive? High-risk (D+/R−, lung/heart, lymphocyte depletion) = prophylaxis 3–6m; standard-risk = pre-emptive PCR-guided.
  3. Anti-TNF screening? TB (IGRA/TST), HBV BEFORE starting.
  4. Anti-CD20 (rituximab) screening? HBV (HBsAg, anti-HBc) BEFORE; monitor IgG, infections.
  5. JAK inhibitor infection risk? VZV (shingles) ↑↑, TB, bacterial, fungal; vaccinate VZV before; monitor lymphocytes.
  6. PCP prophylaxis post-transplant? TMP-SMX 960mg 3×/wk ×6–12m; lifelong if ongoing immunosuppression.
  7. CMV treatment? Valganciclovir 900mg BD or ganciclovir 5mg/kg IV 12h; foscarnet/cidofovir if resistant.
  8. BK virus nephropathy management? Reduce immunosuppression (Tac/MMF); leflunomide/cidofovir/IVIG adjunct.
  9. Anti-CD20 HBV reactivation? Screen HBsAg/anti-HBc BEFORE; prophylactic antivirals if HBsAg+ or anti-HBc+.
  10. JAK inhibitor VZV risk? Shingles ↑↑; vaccinate with Shingrix BEFORE starting.
  11. Alemtuzumab prophylaxis? PCP (TMP-SMX), viral (acyclovir/valganciclovir), fungal (posaconazole) — intensive.

Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
CMV prophylaxis for all transplantOnly high-risk (D+/R−, lung/heart, lymphocyte depletion); standard-risk = pre-emptive
PCP prophylaxis only 6 monthsContinue lifelong if ongoing immunosuppression
Anti-TNF safe if TB screen negativeScreen IGRA/TST BEFORE; but can still reactivate latent TB (false neg) — educate patient
Rituximab only affects B-cellsAlso causes hypogammaglobulinaemia → encapsulated bacteria; PML risk (JCV)
JAK inhibitors = just oral DMARDsSignificant VZV ↑↑, TB, infection risk; vaccinate Shingrix BEFORE
Live vaccines always contraindicatedShingrix (recombinant) = SAFE during immunosuppression
CMV treatment = valganciclovir onlyResistance common (UL97/UL54); foscarnet/cidofovir for resistance
PCP only in HIVTransplant, biologics, steroids, malignancy all at risk
Fungal prophylaxis for all transplantOnly high-risk (lung, liver, heart, re-tx, prolonged neutropenia, steroids)

Mnemonics

  • TRANSPLANT TIMELINE: 0-1m = Surgical/Hospital; 1-6m = Opportunistic (CMV, PCP, Fungi, EBV); >6m = Community
  • CMV RISK: Donor+/Recipient-Neg = Highest; Lung/Heart/Intestinal Tx; Lymphocyte depletion
  • ANTI-TNF SCREEN: TB (IGRA/TST) + HBV BEFORE
  • RITUXIMAB SCREEN: HBV (HBsAg, Anti-HBc) BEFORE
  • JAK INHIBITORS: VZV ↑↑, TB, Lymphopenia → Shingrix BEFORE
  • PCP PROPHYLAXIS: TMP-SMX 6-12m post-tx; Lifelong if Immunosuppressed
  • LIVE VACCINES: Contraindicated During Immunosuppression; Shingrix = Safe
  • ALEMTUZUMAB: Severe T-cell depletion → Intense Prophylaxis (PCP, Viral, Fungal)

Mind Map

Immunocompromised Host
Transplant Timeline
0-1m: Surgical, Hospital
1-6m: Opportunistic (CMV, PCP, Fungi, EBV-PTLD, BK)
>6m: Community + Chronic rejection
Biologics
Anti-TNF: TB, Histo, Cocci, HBV
Anti-CD20: HBV reactivation, PML, Hypogammaglobulinaemia
JAK Inhibitors: VZV↑↑, TB, Lymphopenia
Alemtuzumab: Severe T-cell depletion
Anti-integrin: PML (natalizumab)
Key OIs
CMV: D+/R- highest; Prophylaxis vs Pre-emptive; Valganciclovir
PCP: TMP-SMX 6-12m universal
EBV-PTLD: Reduce IS + Rituximab
BK Virus: Reduce IS
Fungal: Aspergillus (Vorico), Candida (Echinocandin), Mucor (L-AmB+Surg)
Prophylaxis
PCP: TMP-SMX 6-12m
CMV: Valganciclovir (high-risk)
Fungal: Posaconazole/Vorico (high-risk)
VZV: Acyclovir
Vaccines
Inactivated: OK (give before if possible)
Live: Contraindicated (except Shingrix)

Flowchart

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Immunocompromised Host
↓
Transplant?
Vaccination: Inactivated OK; Live CONTRAINDICATED (Shingrix OK)
↓
Timeline: 0-1m / 1-6m / >6m
Biologics?
↓
CMV Risk Stratification: D+/R- = Prophylaxis
PCP Prophylaxis: TMP-SMX 6-12m universal
Fungal Prophylaxis: High-risk only (Posaconazole/Vorico)
Screen TB + HBV BEFORE
Screen HBV BEFORE; Monitor IgG, PML
Shingrix BEFORE; Screen TB; Monitor Lymphocytes
Intense Prophylaxis: PCP + Viral + Fungal
JCV Index (Natalizumab)

  • CMV prophylaxis vs pre-emptive decision tree
  • Biologic screening table
  • Vaccination algorithm (live vs inactivated)
  • BK virus nephropathy algorithm

Suggested Video References

  • PJP prophylaxis guidelines

One-Page Revision Summary

TopicKey Points
Timeline0-1m: surgical/hospital; 1-6m: opportunistic (CMV, PCP, fungi, EBV, BK); >6m: community
CMVD+/R− highest; prophylaxis (high-risk) vs pre-emptive; valganciclovir 1st-line
PCPTMP-SMX 960mg 3×/wk ×6–12m (lifelong if ongoing IS)
Anti-TNFScreen TB (IGRA/TST) + HBV BEFORE
Anti-CD20Screen HBV (HBsAg, anti-HBc) BEFORE; PML risk; hypogammaglobulinaemia
JAK InhibitorsVZV ↑↑; TB; screen TB; Shingrix BEFORE
AlemtuzumabSevere T-cell depletion; intense prophylaxis (PCP + viral + fungal)
PCP ProphylaxisTMP-SMX 6–12m; lifelong if ongoing IS
Fungal ProphylaxisHigh-risk only (lung, liver, heart, HSCT, re-tx)
VaccinesInactivated OK (≥2w before); Live CONTRAINDICATED (Shingrix safe)
