tags: [medicine, davidson, infectious-disease, schistosomiasis, bilharzia, parasites, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_chapter: Chapter 11: Infectious disease

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: high

exam_relevance: "FCPS: High-yield | MRCP: Core | Schistosoma haematobium/mansoni/japonicum, acute (Katayama), chronic (hepatic/intestinal/urinary), praziquantel, egg detection, hepatosplenic disease"

see_also: "[[Soil-Transmitted Helminths & Strongyloides]], [[Liver Disease & Alcohol Macrocytosis]], [[Fever in Returned Traveller & FUO]], [[Hepatology]]"

created: 2025-06-17

modified: 2025-06-17

Schistosomiasis (Bilharzia)

[!info] Davidson Ch 11 Alignment: Infectious Disease → Specific Organism Groups → Parasites (Helminths) → Schistosomiasis
FCPS/MRCP Focus: S. haematobium (Urinary), S. mansoni/japonicum (Intestinal/Hepatic), acute Katayama fever, chronic hepatic/intestinal/urinary, praziquantel, egg detection, hepatosplenic disease

🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Identify Schistosome Species: S. haematobium (Urinary), S. mansoni (Intestinal/Hepatic), S. japonicum (Intestinal/Hepatic, Zoonotic), S. intercalatum, S. mekongi
  • [ ] Understand Life Cycle: Snail Intermediate Host → Cercariae → Skin Penetration → Schistosomula → Portal Venous System → Adult Worms → Egg Migration
  • [ ] Recognise Acute (Katayama Fever): Fever, Cough, Hepatosplenomegaly, Eosinophilia (Weeks Post-Exposure)
  • [ ] Recognise Chronic Intestinal (S. mansoni/japonicum): Abdominal Pain, Bloody Diarrhoea, Hepatosplenomegaly, Periportal Fibrosis (Pipestem Fibrosis), Portal Hypertension
  • [ ] Recognise Chronic Urinary (S. haematobium): Terminal Haematuria, Bladder Pathology, SCC Risk, Hydronephrosis, Renal Failure
  • [ ] Diagnose: Egg Detection (Urine/Stool), Serology (ELISA/IHA), PCR, Ultrasound (Liver/Spleen/Bladder), Cystoscopy
  • [ ] Manage: Praziquantel 40mg/kg Single Dose (1st Line All Species), Oxamniquine (S. mansoni Alternative), Corticosteroids (Acute Katayama)
  • [ ] Recognise Complications: Portal Hypertension, Variceal Bleeding, Splenomegaly, Bladder Cancer (SCC), Glomerulonephritis, Pulmonary Hypertension

📖 Life Cycle & Transmission

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Eggs in Water (Urine/Stool)
C
E
↓
Miracidium Hatches] --> C[Snail Intermediate Host (Specific per Species)
Sporocyst → Cercariae Released] --> E[Cercariae Penetrate Human Skin
Schistosomula → Lung → Liver → Portal Vein
↓
Adult Worms Mate in Mesenteric/Pelvic Veins
↓
& H2
S. mansoni/japonicum → Mesenteric Veins → Eggs in Stool
↓
Eggs → Tissue Trapping (Granulomas) OR Excreted in Urine/Stool
SpeciesAdult LocationEgg ExcretionGeographic Distribution
S. haematobiumVesical/Pelvic Venous PlexusUrine (Terminal Haematuria)Africa, Middle East
S. mansoniInferior Mesenteric VeinsStoolAfrica, Middle East, South America, Caribbean
S. japonicumSuperior Mesenteric VeinsStoolChina, Philippines, Philippines, Indonesia
S. intercalatumMesenteric VeinsStoolCentral/West Africa
S. mekongiMesenteric VeinsStoolMekong River Basin (Laos/Cambodia)
[!tip] S. haematobium = Urinary (Terminal Haematuria); S. mansoni/japonicum = Intestinal/Hepatic (Stool Eggs). Snail = Intermediate Host (Species-Specific).

📖 Clinical Syndromes

1. Acute Schistosomiasis (Katayama Fever)

FeatureDetails
Timing4-8 Weeks Post-Exposure (Coincides with Egg Laying)
SymptomsHigh Fever, Cough, Hepatosplenomegaly, Eosinophilia (Marked), Rash, Abdominal Pain, Diarrhoea
PathophysiologyImmune Complex Reaction to Egg Antigens (Type III Hypersensitivity)
SpeciesS. japonicum > S. mansoni > S. haematobium

2. Chronic Intestinal / Hepatic Schistosomiasis (S. mansoni, S. japonicum)

FeatureDetails
IntestinalAbdominal Pain, Bloody/Mucus Diarrhoea, Tenesmus, Rectal Prolapse (Heavy Infection)
Hepatic (Hepatosplenic)Periportal Fibrosis (Pipestem Fibrosis) → Portal Hypertension, Splenomegaly, Oesophageal Varices, Ascites, Thrombocytopenia
S. japonicumMore Severe, Higher Egg Output, More Hepatic Involvement, Zoonotic (Cattle/Water Buffalo)

3. Urinary Schistosomiasis (S. haematobium)

FeatureDetails
Cardinal SignTerminal Haematuria (Painless, End of Micturition)
Bladder PathologySandy Patches, Granulomas, Ulceration, Calcification, Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) Risk ↑
Upper TractUreteric Strictures, Hydronephrosis, Renal Failure
GenitalSeminal Vesicle/Prostate Involvement, Haematospermia, Infertility

🔬 Diagnosis

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Suspected Schistosomiasis
↓
**Species / Clinical Syndrome**
↓
**Urine Microscopy (Terminal Urine)**
**Stool Microscopy (Kato-Katz)**
**Serology (ELISA/IHA/IFA)**
**Imaging**
↓
**Eggs with Terminal Spine** (S. haematobium)
**Lateral Spine Egg** (S. mansoni) / **Lateral Knob** (S. japonicum)
**US Liver/Spleen** (Portal Hypertension, Bladder Wall Thickening)
**Cystoscopy** (Bladder Lesions)

Diagnostic Methods

MethodSampleSensitivity/SpecificityRole
Urine Microscopy (Terminal)Terminal UrineHigh (S. haematobium)1st Line (Urinary)
Stool Microscopy (Kato-Katz)StoolQuantitative (EPG)1st Line (Intestinal)
Urine FiltrationUrineConcentrates EggsField-Friendly
Serology (ELISA/IHA/IFA)SerumHigh Sens/SpecTravellers, Low Parasite Load
PCR (Urine/Stool/Blood)VariousHigh Sens/SpecLow Parasite Load, Species ID
Ultrasound (Liver/Spleen/Bladder)—Portal Hypertension, Bladder WallMorbidity Assessment
CystoscopyBladderVisualize LesionsBladder Pathology Assessment
[!tip] Urine Microscopy (Terminal) = S. haematobium. Stool Kato-Katz = S. mansoni/japonicum. Serology = Travellers/Low Burden. Ultrasound = Morbidity Assessment (Portal Hypertension, Bladder).

💊 Management

Praziquantel — Drug of Choice for ALL Species

SpeciesDoseSchedule
S. haematobium / S. mansoni / S. intercalatum40mg/kg Single DoseSingle Dose
S. japonicum / S. mekongi60mg/kg (Divided 2-3 Doses)Single Day
Acute Katayama FeverPraziquantel 40-60mg/kg + Prednisolone 1mg/kg (Anti-Inflammatory)Single Dose + Steroids 5-7 Days
[!warning] Praziquantel = Drug of Choice for ALL Schistosome Species. Single Dose (40-60mg/kg). Give with Food (↑ Absorption). Corticosteroids for Acute Katayama (Anti-Inflammatory).

Oxamniquine — Alternative for S. mansoni Only

IndicationDoseNotes
S. mansoni (Praziquantel Failure/Allergy)15mg/kg Single Dose (Up to 40mg/kg)Only for S. mansoni

Chronic Complications Management

ComplicationManagement
Portal Hypertension / VaricesBeta-Blockers, Endoscopic Band Ligation, TIPS, Splenectomy
Bladder Cancer (SCC)Cystoscopic Surveillance, TURBT, Radical Cystectomy
GlomerulonephritisACEi/ARB, Immunosuppression (If Nephrotic), Praziquantel
Pulmonary HypertensionAnticoagulation, PDE5 Inhibitors, Prostacyclin, Lung Transplant (End-Stage)
NeuroschistosomiasisPraziquantel + Corticosteroids (Dexamethasone)

🔬 Diagnostic Summary Table

SpeciesEgg MorphologySampleKey Feature
S. haematobiumTerminal SpineUrine (Terminal)Terminal Haematuria
S. mansoniLateral SpineStoolHepatosplenic Disease
S. japonicumLateral Knob / Small SpineStoolMore Severe, Zoonotic
S. intercalatumTerminal Spine (Longer)StoolMild Intestinal
S. mekongiSmall Lateral SpineStoolMekong Basin
[!tip] Egg Morphology = Species ID. Terminal Spine = S. haematobium (Urine). Lateral Spine = S. mansoni (Stool). Lateral Knob = S. japonicum (Stool).

🩺 Morbidity Assessment (WHO Ultrasound Grading)

OrganFindings
LiverPeriportal Fibrosis (Pipestem), Portal Vein Dilatation, Splenomegaly
SpleenSplenomegaly (Size, Texture)
BladderWall Thickening, Sandy Patches, Polyps, Masses (SCC), Calcification
KidneyHydronephrosis, Ureteric Dilatation

💡 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Life CycleSnail → Cercaria → Skin Penetration → Schistosomula → Portal Vein → Adult Worms → Eggs
S. haematobiumUrinary, Terminal Haematuria, Terminal Spine Egg, Bladder Cancer (SCC) Risk
S. mansoniIntestinal/Hepatic, Lateral Spine Egg, Pipestem Fibrosis, Portal HTN
S. japonicumIntestinal/Hepatic, More Severe, Zoonotic, Lateral Knob Egg
Acute (Katayama)Fever, Cough, Hepatosplenomegaly, Eosinophilia (Weeks Post-Exposure) → Praziquantel + Prednisolone
Chronic IntestinalAbdominal Pain, Bloody Diarrhoea, Hepatosplenomegaly, Portal HTN
Chronic UrinaryTerminal Haematuria, Bladder Pathology, SCC Risk
DiagnosisEgg Microscopy (Urine/Stool/Kato-Katz), Serology/PCR, Ultrasound (Morbidity)
TreatmentPraziquantel 40mg/kg Single Dose (haematobium/mansoni), 60mg/kg (japonicum)
Acute KatayamaPraziquantel + Prednisolone
ComplicationsPortal HTN, Variceal Bleeding, Bladder SCC, Glomerulonephrosis, Pulm HTN

❓ Viva Questions

  1. What is the drug of choice for all Schistosome species?
  • Praziquantel 40mg/kg Single Dose (60mg/kg for S. japonicum).
  1. How do you differentiate Schistosome species by egg morphology?
  • S. haematobium = Terminal Spine (Urine); S. mansoni = Lateral Spine (Stool); S. japonicum = Lateral Knob/Small Spine (Stool).
  1. What is Katayama Fever and how is it managed?
  • Acute Serum Sickness-Like Illness (Fever, Cough, Hepatosplenomegaly, Eosinophilia) 4-8 Weeks Post-Exposure → Praziquantel + Prednisolone 1mg/kg.
  1. What is the clinical presentation of S. haematobium infection?
  • Terminal Haematuria (Painless, End of Micturition), Bladder Pathology (Sandy Patches, Granulomas), SCC Risk.
  1. What is the pathology of chronic S. mansoni infection?
  • Periportal Fibrosis (Pipestem Fibrosis) → Portal Hypertension → Splenomegaly, Oesophageal Varices, Ascites.
  1. What is the egg morphology of S. haematobium and where is it found?
  • Terminal Spine, Found in Urine (Terminal Specimen Best).
  1. What are the complications of chronic S. haematobium infection?
  • Bladder Cancer (SCC), Ureteric Stricture → Hydronephrosis → Renal Failure, Glomerulonephritis.
  1. How do you differentiate S. mansoni from S. japonicum clinically?
  • S. japonicum = More Severe, Higher Egg Output, Zoonotic (Cattle/Water Buffalo), Lateral Knob Egg.
  1. What is the treatment for Katayama Fever?
  • Praziquantel 40-60mg/kg + Prednisolone 1mg/kg/day × 5-7 Days.
  1. What is the diagnostic method of choice for S. haematobium?
  • Urine Microscopy (Terminal Urine) for Terminal Spine Eggs; Filtration Concentrates Eggs.
  1. What is the treatment for S. japonicum infection?
  • Praziquantel 60mg/kg (Divided 2-3 Doses) Single Day.
  1. What is the "Pipestem Fibrosis" in Schistosomiasis?
  • Periportal Fibrosis radiating from Portal Vein → Portal Hypertension → Splenomegaly, Varices.

🧠 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
S. haematobium vs S. mansoni vs S. japonicumHaematobium = Urine + Terminal Spine; Mansoni = Stool + Lateral Spine; Japonicum = Stool + Lateral Knob
Katayama vs Acute SchistosomiasisSame — Acute Serum Sickness-Like Reaction ~4-8 Weeks Post-Infection
Praziquantel vs OxamniquinePraziquantel = All Species; Oxamniquine = S. mansoni Only
S. mansoni vs S. japonicum EggLateral Spine vs Lateral Knob
S. haematobium vs Bladder CancerChronic Infection → SCC Risk; Cystoscopy Surveillance
MnemonicMeaning
"Haematobium = Haematuria + Terminal Spine"Urinary Schisto
"Mansoni = Mansoni = Stool + Lateral Spine"Intestinal Schisto
"Japonicum = Japan/Asia + Lateral Knob + Severe"Japanese Schisto
"Praziquantel = 40mg/kg (All), 60mg/kg (Japonicum)"Dosing
"Katayama = Fever + Cough + Hepatosplenomegaly + Eosinophilia"Acute Schisto
"Pipestem Fibrosis = Portal Hypertension"Chronic Hepatic

topics.title: Sepsis And Septic Shock
topics.html:
tags: [medicine, infectious-disease, davidson, chapter13, sepsis, septic-shock, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_chapter: Chapter 13: Infectious disease

topic_category: General Principles / Sepsis Domain

status: full-fcps-mrcp-topic-note

Sepsis and Septic Shock

Related: [[Fever and Septic Syndrome Approach]], [[Acute Bacterial Meningitis]], [[Brain Abscess]], [[Infective Endocarditis]], [[Post-Transplant Infections]]

[!important]
Sepsis = life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by dysregulated host response to infection. Septic shock = sepsis + persisting hypotension requiring vasopressors to maintain MAP ≥65mmHg + lactate >2mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation. TIME IS TISSUE — sepsis bundle within 1 hour. Source control is as important as antibiotics.

Learning Objectives

  • Apply Sepsis-3 definitions (SOFA/qSOFA) and identify septic shock
  • Execute 1-hour sepsis bundle: lactate, blood cultures, broad antibiotics, fluid resuscitation, vasopressors
  • Select empirical antibiotics by suspected source and local epidemiology
  • Manage fluid resuscitation, vasopressor escalation (norepinephrine → vasopressin → adrenaline), and adjuncts
  • Recognise and treat lactic acidosis, coagulopathy, AKI, ARDS
  • Implement source control (drainage, debridement, device removal)
  • Understand corticosteroids in septic shock (hydrocortisone 200mg/day)
  • Monitor dynamic indices of fluid responsiveness

Definitions (Sepsis-3, 2016)

TermDefinition
InfectionSuspected/documented pathogen invasion
SepsisLife-threatening organ dysfunction caused by dysregulated host response to infection = SOFA score increase ≥2 points from baseline
Septic shockSubset of sepsis with circulatory, cellular, metabolic abnormalities substantially increasing mortality: vasopressor requirement to maintain MAP ≥65mmHg + serum lactate >2mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation
qSOFAQuick SOFA (screening tool, NOT diagnostic): RR ≥22, AMS (GCS<15), SBP ≤100mmHg — ≥2 points = high risk for poor outcome
[!tip]
SOFA ≥2 = sepsis diagnosis. qSOFA ≥2 = screen for ICU referral/early intervention. Do NOT use qSOFA to rule out sepsis.

SOFA Score (Sepsis-related Organ Failure Assessment)

System01234
Respiratory (PaO₂/FiO₂)≥400<400<300<200 (vent)<100 (vent)
Coagulation (Platelets)≥150<150<100<50<20
Liver (Bilirubin)<1.21.2–1.92.0–5.96.0–11.9>12.0
CardiovascularMAP≥70MAP<70Dopamine ≤5 or DobutamineDopamine >5 or Epi ≤0.1 or NorEpi ≤0.1Dopamine >15 or Epi >0.1 or NorEpi >0.1
CNS (GCS)1513–1410–126–9<6
Renal (Creatinine/UO)<1.21.2–1.92.0–3.43.5–4.9 / <500mL/d>5.0 / <200mL/d
[!tip]
Baseline SOFA assumed 0 if unknown. Increase ≥2 = sepsis. Cardiovascular SOFA drives septic shock definition.

Pathophysiology — Dysregulated Host Response

PhaseMechanism
HyperinflammatoryPAMPs/DAMPs → TLRs → NF-κB → cytokine storm (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6, IFN-γ) → vasodilation, capillary leak, myocardial depression
Immunosuppressive (CARS)Anti-inflammatory cytokines (IL-10, TGF-β), HLA-DR downregulation, lymphocyte apoptosis, endotoxin tolerance → secondary infections
CoagulopathyTissue factor → thrombin → fibrin deposition → DIC; Protein C/S depletion, antithrombin consumption
MetabolicMitochondrial dysfunction → aerobic glycolysis (Warburg effect) → lactate ↑ (not just hypoperfusion); cytopathic hypoxia

1-Hour Sepsis Bundle (Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

ActionTarget
1. Measure lactateInitial + repeat if >2mmol/L
2. Blood cultures (×2 sets, aerobic/anaerobic)BEFORE antibiotics (but do NOT delay abx >45min for cultures)
3. Broad-spectrum IV antibioticsWithin 1 HOUR of recognition
4. Fluid resuscitation30mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate ≥4mmol/L
5. VasopressorsIf hypotensive after fluids → Norepinephrine first-line to MAP ≥65mmHg
6. Source controlIdentify and intervene within 6–12 hours (drainage, debridement, device removal)

Sepsis-3 Septic Shock Criteria (Clinical)

ALL 3 must be present:

  1. Sepsis (SOFA ≥2 / suspected infection + organ dysfunction)
  2. Vasopressor requirement to maintain MAP ≥65mmHg
  3. Lactate >2mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation (30mL/kg crystalloid)
[!warning]
Mortality: Sepsis ~25–30%, Septic shock ~40–50%. Every hour of antibiotic delay ↑ mortality ~7–8%.

qSOFA (Bedside Screen — NOT for Diagnosis)

ParameterPoints
Respiratory rate ≥22/min1
Altered mental status (GCS <15)1
Systolic BP ≤100mmHg1
Total ≥2High risk for in-hospital mortality / ICU admission
[!tip]
qSOFA ≥2 → activate sepsis protocol, consider ICU, measure lactate, start bundle. qSOFA sensitivity ~60%, specificity ~80% for sepsis.

Empirical Antibiotic Selection — Source-Based

Suspected SourceEmpirical RegimenKey Coverage
Unknown source (community)Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV 6h OR Meropenem 1g IV 8h + Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg IV 6h (if MRSA risk)Gram-negatives (including Pseudomonas), anaerobes, Gram-positives
Pneumonia (CAP severe)Ceftriaxone 2g IV 12h + Azithromycin 500mg IV/PO OD OR Levofloxacin 750mg IV ODS. pneumoniae, atypicals
Pneumonia (HAP/VAP)Pip-tazo 4.5g 6h OR Meropenem 1g 8h OR Cefepime 2g 8h + Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg 6h (+/- Aztreonam if Pseudomonas)Pseudomonas, MRSA, Gram-negatives
Intra-abdominalPip-tazo 4.5g 6h OR Meropenem 1g 8h + Vancomycin if MRSA riskEnterobacteriaceae, anaerobes (Bacteroides), Enterococcus
UTI / PyelonephritisCeftriaxone 2g 12h OR Pip-tazo 4.5g 6h + Vancomycin if enterococcal/MRSA riskE. coli, Klebsiella, Enterococcus, Pseudomonas
Skin/Soft tissue (necrotising)Pip-tazo 4.5g 6h + Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg 6h + Clindamycin 900mg 8hMSSA/MRSA, streptococci, anaerobes, Gram-neg; clindamycin = toxin suppression
CNS (meningitis)See Acute Bacterial Meningitis note
Endocarditis (native valve)See Infective Endocarditis note
Febrile neutropeniaPip-tazo 4.5g 6h OR Meropenem 1g 8h (+ Vancomycin if catheter/HSV/mucositis)Pseudomonas, Gram-negatives
[!important]
Vancomycin 15–20mg/kg IV 6h (trough 15–20) for MRSA coverage. Add if: MRSA risk factors (colonisation, prior MRSA, skin/soft tissue, prosthesis, IVDU, hospital exposure). De-escalate at 48–72h based on cultures.

Fluid Resuscitation

PrincipleDetail
Fluid typeBalanced crystalloid (Plasma-Lyte, Ringer's lactate) preferred over 0.9% saline (less hyperchloraemic acidosis, less AKI)
Initial bolus30mL/kg over 1–3 hours (e.g., 2–3L for 70kg)
ReassessmentDynamic indices after each bolus: passive leg raise (PLR), stroke volume variation (SVV), pulse pressure variation (PPV), echocardiographic IVC variation
Stopping pointNo fluid responsiveness (ΔSV <10–15% with PLR/500mL bolus) OR signs of fluid overload (pulmonary oedema, rising CVP/JVP)
AlbuminConsider if large-volume resuscitation (>4–5L crystalloid) or persistent hypoalbuminaemia
AvoidHydroxyethyl starch (HES) — ↑ AKI, mortality; gelatin — similar

Vasopressor Escalation

AgentDoseRoleKey Points
Norepinephrine0.01–3μg/kg/minFirst-lineα₁ > β₁; ↑ MAP, ↑ SVR, mild ↑ CO; start peripherally if central line delayed
Vasopressin0.03U/min (fixed)Second-line (add-on)V₁ receptors; spares catecholamines; ↓ norepinephrine dose; do NOT titrate
Adrenaline (Epinephrine)0.01–3μg/kg/minThird-line / alternativeα₁+β₁+β₂; ↑ CO, ↑ HR, ↑ lactate (β₂), arrhythmia risk
Dopamine2–20μg/kg/minAvoid (arrhythmia, ↑ mortality)β₁ > α₁ at low dose; α₁ at high; not recommended
Phenylephrine0.5–10μg/kg/minAlternative if tachycardia/arrhythmiaPure α₁; ↓ CO, ↓ splanchnic perfusion
[!tip]
MAP target ≥65mmHg (individualise: chronic HTN may need 70–75mmHg). Norepinephrine first, add vasopressin 0.03U/min to reduce NE dose, then adrenaline if needed.

Adjunctive Therapies

TherapyIndicationDose / Detail
HydrocortisoneSeptic shock requiring vasopressors (NE >0.25–0.5μg/kg/min)200mg/day IV continuous infusion OR 50mg IV 6h ×7–10d taper; do NOT give bolus
Vitamin C / Thiamine / Hydrocortisone (HAT)Not routinely recommendedEvidence inconsistent (VITAMINS, VICTAS trials negative); not standard
IVIGNot routineConsider in streptococcal/staphylococcal toxic shock, necrotising fasciitis
AnticoagulationDIC with thrombosis / VTE prophylaxisLMWH prophylactic dose; therapeutic if VTE; no routine heparin for DIC
Renal replacementAKI stage 2–3 (KDIGO)CRRT preferred (haemodynamic stability); early initiation debated
Mechanical ventilationARDS (Berlin criteria)Low tidal volume 6mL/kg PBW, PEEP table, prone >12h if PaO₂/FiO₂ <150

Source Control — TIME-CRITICAL

SourceInterventionTimeline
Abscess / empyemaPercutaneous drainage / surgical drainageWithin 6–12h
Necrotising fasciitisUrgent surgical debridement (often multiple)IMMEDIATE
CholangitisERCP ± sphincterotomy / stentWithin 24h (urgent if septic)
Infected device (line, prosthesis, pacemaker)Remove deviceAs soon as possible
Peritonitis (perforated viscus)Laparotomy / laparoscopy + washoutUrgent
Infected vascular graftSurgical excision + extra-anatomic bypassUrgent

Monitoring & Targets

ParameterTarget
MAP≥65mmHg (individualise)
LactateClearance ≥10–20% per 2h (or normalisation)
Urine output≥0.5mL/kg/h
ScvO₂ / SvO₂≥70% / ≥65% (if measured)
Capillary refill time<3 seconds (peripheral perfusion)
Skin mottlingResolution
Dynamic fluid responsivenessPLR ΔSV >10–15% = fluid responsive

Complications

ComplicationManagement
ARDSLow Vt, PEEP, prone, neuromuscular blockade (early), conservative fluids
AKIAvoid nephrotoxins, optimise perfusion, CRRT if indicated
DICTreat underlying sepsis; platelets if <10×10⁹/L or bleeding; cryo if fibrinogen <1.5g/L
Myocardial depressionEcho-guided inotropes (dobutamine 2.5–20μg/kg/min) if low CO despite NE
Immune paralysisSecondary infections (fungal, viral, resistant bacteria); HLA-DR monitoring
Critical illness polyneuropathy/myopathyEarly mobilisation, glycaemic control, avoid prolonged paralysis

Special Situations

SituationAdjustment
PregnancyCeftriaxone + azithromycin (avoid fluoroquinolones, doxycycline); norepinephrine first-line; hydrocortisone OK
ImmunocompromisedBroader coverage (antifungal, antiviral, PJP); earlier source control; lower threshold for ICU
CirrhosisAlbumin 1g/kg day 1 + 1g/kg day 3 (if SBP); terlipressin for HRS; avoid NSAIDs
BurnsParkland formula + sepsis bundle; Pseudomonas/MRSA coverage
Post-cardiac arrestSepsis mimic; therapeutic hypothermia; procalcitonin-guided antibiotics

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Sepsis-3: SOFA ≥2 = sepsis; Septic shock = vasopressors + lactate >2 despite 30mL/kg fluids
  • 1-hour bundle: lactate, blood cultures, antibiotics, 30mL/kg crystalloid, vasopressors if needed
  • Antibiotics within 1 HOUR — every hour delay ↑ mortality ~7–8%
  • Fluid: balanced crystalloid (Plasma-Lyte/RL) 30mL/kg; reassess with dynamic indices (PLR, SVV)
  • Vasopressors: Norepinephrine first → add Vasopressin 0.03U/min → Adrenaline third-line
  • Hydrocortisone 200mg/day IV infusion if NE >0.25–0.5μg/kg/min (not bolus)
  • Source control within 6–12h (drainage, debridement, device removal)
  • Lactate clearance ≥10–20%/2h = resuscitation adequacy
  • De-escalate antibiotics at 48–72h based on cultures
  • qSOFA ≥2 = screen for ICU referral (RR≥22, GCS<15, SBP≤100)

Common Viva Questions

  1. What is the Sepsis-3 definition of septic shock? Vasopressor requirement to maintain MAP ≥65mmHg + lactate >2mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation (30mL/kg).
  2. What is the 1-hour sepsis bundle? Lactate, blood cultures (before abx), broad IV antibiotics within 1h, 30mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension/lactate≥4, vasopressors if still hypotensive.
  3. First-line vasopressor in septic shock? Norepinephrine.
  4. When do you add vasopressin? As second-line to reduce norepinephrine dose (fixed 0.03U/min).
  5. When do you give hydrocortisone in septic shock? Vasopressor-dependent shock (NE >0.25–0.5μg/kg/min); 200mg/day IV infusion ×7–10d taper.
  6. What fluid for resuscitation? Balanced crystalloid (Plasma-Lyte, Ringer's lactate) preferred over 0.9% saline.
  7. How do you assess fluid responsiveness? Passive leg raise (PLR) with cardiac output monitoring, SVV/PPV (if ventilated, regular rhythm), echo IVC variation.
  8. When do you de-escalate antibiotics? At 48–72h based on culture results and clinical response.

Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
qSOFA diagnoses sepsisqSOFA screens risk; SOFA ≥2 diagnoses sepsis
Lactate >4 = septic shockSeptic shock = vasopressors + lactate >2 AFTER fluids
Give 0.9% saline for resuscitationBalanced crystalloid preferred (less AKI, less hyperchloraemic acidosis)
Fluid bolus until CVP 8–12Static CVP unreliable; use dynamic indices (PLR, SVV)
Hydrocortisone as bolusContinuous infusion 200mg/day (or 50mg 6h) — bolus not recommended
Dopamine as first-lineNorepinephrine first-line; dopamine ↑ arrhythmia, mortality
Vasopressin titratedFixed dose 0.03U/min — do NOT titrate
IVIG routine in sepsisOnly streptococcal/staphylococcal TSS, necrotising fasciitis
Procalcitonin guides antibiotics startPCT supports de-escalation/stop; do NOT delay antibiotics for PCT
All septic shock needs central lineStart norepinephrine peripherally if central access delayed

Mnemonics

  • SEPSIS BUNDLE: Serum lactate, Exercise blood cultures, Prompt antibiotics (1h), Saline 30mL/kg, Initiate vasopressors, Source control (6-12h)
  • VASOPRESSORS: Norepinephrine 1st, Vasopressin 2nd (0.03U fix), Adrenaline 3rd, Dopamine avoid
  • SEPTIC SHOCK: SOFA≥2 + Vasopressors + Lactate>2 after fluids = SVL
  • FLUID RESPONSE: PLR (gold standard), SVV/PPV (ventilated), Echo IVC, CVP NOT reliable

Mind Map

Sepsis / Septic Shock
Definitions
Sepsis-3: SOFA ≥2
Septic shock: Vasopressors + Lactate>2 after fluids
qSOFA: screen only (RR, GCS, SBP)
Pathophysiology
Hyperinflammatory -> CARS
Coagulopathy (DIC)
Metabolic (lactate, mitochondrial)
1-Hour Bundle
Lactate, Blood Cx, Abx 1h, Fluids 30mL/kg, Vasopressors, Source control
Fluids
Balanced crystalloid
Dynamic assessment (PLR, SVV)
Vasopressors
NE 1st -> Vasopressin 2nd -> Adrenaline 3rd
Adjuncts
Hydrocortisone 200mg/day infusion
No routine IVIG, Vit C, anticoagulation
Source Control
Drainage, Debridement, Device removal
Monitoring
MAP≥65, Lactate clearance, UO, ScvO₂, CRT

Flowchart

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Suspected infection + organ dysfunction
↓
Measure lactate, SOFA score
↓
SOFA ≥2?
↓
SEPSIS: Activate 1-hour bundle
Monitor, qSOFA if indicated
↓
Lactate, Blood cultures x2
↓
IV Broad antibiotics WITHIN 1h
↓
30mL/kg balanced crystalloid if hypotensive/lactate≥4
↓
MAP≥65 after fluids?
↓
Norepinephrine titration
Monitor lactate clearance, UO, dynamic indices
↓
MAP≥65 on NE?
Source control within 6-12h
↓
Add Vasopressin 0.03U/min
De-escalate abx at 48-72h
↓
Still hypotensive?
↓
Add Adrenaline
↓
Hydrocortisone 200mg/day if NE>0.25-0.5

Suggested Visuals / Image Notes

  • Sepsis-3 definitions diagram
  • SOFA score table
  • Vasopressor escalation algorithm
  • Passive leg raise technique
  • Source control timeline

Suggested Video References

  • Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021 guidelines
  • SSC hour-1 bundle implementation
  • Dynamic fluid responsiveness (PLR, SVV)
  • Vasopressor pharmacology
  • Hydrocortisone in septic shock (ADRENAL, APROCCHSS trials)

One-Page Revision Summary

TopicKey Points
Sepsis-3SOFA ≥2 = sepsis; Septic shock = vasopressors + lactate>2 after 30mL/kg fluids
qSOFARR≥22, GCS<15, SBP≤100 — ≥2 = high risk (screen, NOT diagnose)
1-Hour BundleLactate, Blood Cx, Abx 1h, 30mL/kg crystalloid, Vasopressors, Source control
FluidsBalanced crystalloid 30mL/kg; dynamic assessment (PLR, SVV) — NOT CVP
VasopressorsNE 1st (0.01–3μg/kg/min) → Vasopressin 0.03U/min 2nd → Adrenaline 3rd
Hydrocortisone200mg/day IV infusion if NE >0.25–0.5; 7–10d taper
AntibioticsSource-based, within 1h, de-escalate 48–72h
Source ControlDrain/debride/remove device within 6–12h
MonitoringMAP≥65, Lactate clearance ≥10–20%/2h, UO≥0.5mL/kg/h, CRT<3s

24-Hour Recall Prompts

  • Write the 1-hour sepsis bundle.
  • Sepsis-3 septic shock definition (3 components).
  • Vasopressor escalation order.
  • Hydrocortisone indication and dose.
  • Fluid type and volume for initial resuscitation.

7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

  • [ ] Day 1 completed
  • [ ] 24-hour recall completed
  • [ ] Day 7 revision completed
  • [ ] Day 15 revision completed
  • [ ] Day 30 revision completed

Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Sepsis-3 definitions (SOFA, septic shock, qSOFA)
  • 1-hour bundle (lactate, cultures, abx 1h, fluids 30mL/kg, vasopressors)
  • Balanced crystalloid, dynamic fluid assessment
  • NE → Vasopressin → Adrenaline
  • Hydrocortisone 200mg/day infusion for vasopressor-dependent shock
  • Source control 6–12h
  • Antibiotic de-escalation 48–72h
  • Lactate clearance monitoring

Should Know

  • SOFA scoring details
  • Pathophysiology phases (hyperinflammatory → CARS)
  • Cardiovascular SOFA (vasopressor doses)
  • Adjuncts: IVIG indications, DIC management, AKI/ARDS
  • Special populations (pregnancy, cirrhosis, immunocompromised)
  • Procalcitonin-guided de-escalation

Nice to Know

  • HAT therapy trial data (VITAMINS, VICTAS)
  • HLA-DR monitoring for immune paralysis
  • Mitochondrial dysfunction in sepsis
  • Long-term outcomes (PICS)
  • Sepsis biomarkers beyond lactate/PCT

My Weak Points

  • [ ] Exact NE dose equivalents for cardiovascular SOFA
  • [ ] Hydrocortisone taper schedule
  • [ ] Procalcitonin cut-offs for de-escalation
  • [ ] Dynamic indices thresholds (PLR ΔSV, SVV%)

Self-Test Scorecard

  • Understanding: /10
  • Recall: /10
  • MCQ Performance: /10
  • SBA Performance: /10
  • Viva Confidence: /10
  • Total: /50
[!tip]
Interpretation: <35 = weak topic, 35-44 = acceptable but insecure, 45+ = strong exam-ready topic.

Exam Answer Modes

Long Answer Skeleton

  1. Definitions: Sepsis-3 (SOFA ≥2), septic shock (vasopressors + lactate>2 after fluids), qSOFA (screening)
  2. Pathophysiology: hyperinflammatory → immunosuppressive (CARS), coagulopathy, metabolic
  3. 1-hour bundle: lactate, blood cultures, antibiotics 1h, 30mL/kg crystalloid, vasopressors, source control
  4. Fluid resuscitation: balanced crystalloid, dynamic assessment (PLR, SVV), stopping criteria
  5. Vasopressor escalation: NE first, vasopressin second (fixed), adrenaline third, avoid dopamine
  6. Adjuncts: hydrocortisone 200mg/day infusion (indication, dose, taper), IVIG (selective), DIC management
  7. Source control: drainage, debridement, device removal timelines
  8. Monitoring: MAP, lactate clearance, UO, ScvO₂, CRT, mottling
  9. Complications: ARDS, AKI, DIC, myocardial depression, immune paralysis
  10. Special situations: pregnancy, cirrhosis, immunocompromised, burns

Short Note Skeleton

  • Sepsis = SOFA≥2; Septic shock = vasopressors + lactate>2 after 30mL/kg
  • qSOFA: RR≥22, GCS<15, SBP≤100 (≥2 = screen for ICU)
  • 1h bundle: lactate, Cx, abx, 30mL/kg crystalloid, vasopressors, source control
  • Fluids: balanced crystalloid, PLR/SVV dynamic assessment
  • Vasopressors: NE 1st → Vasopressin 0.03U/min 2nd → Adrenaline 3rd
  • Hydrocortisone 200mg/day infusion if NE>0.25-0.5
  • Source control 6-12h; de-escalate abx 48-72h
  • Monitor: MAP≥65, lactate clearance ≥10-20%/2h, UO≥0.5, CRT<3s

Viva One-Liners

  • Sepsis-3: SOFA≥2; Septic shock: vasopressors + lactate>2 after fluids
  • 1h bundle: lactate, cultures, abx 1h, 30mL/kg crystalloid, vasopressors, source control
  • NE first, vasopressin 0.03U/min fixed second, adrenaline third
  • Hydrocortisone 200mg/day infusion for NE-dependent shock
  • Balanced crystalloid > saline; PLR > CVP for fluid responsiveness
  • Source control 6-12h; abx de-escalate 48-72h

Ward-Case Discussion Points

  • 65M, pneumonia, BP 80/45, lactate 5.2, UO 15mL/h → lactate, Cx, pip-tazo+azithro, 2L RL, NE titration, MAP≥65, lactate clearance, source control (none needed for CAP)
  • 40F, necrotising fasciitis, septic shock → lactate, Cx, pip-tazo+vanco+clinda, 30mL/kg RL, NE, urgent surgical debridement (source control), hydrocortisone if NE>0.5
  • 70M, cholangitis, septic shock → lactate, Cx, pip-tazo, 30mL/kg RL, NE, ERCP within 24h (urgent if septic)

Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet

SEPSIS: SOFA≥2. SEPTIC SHOCK: Vasopressors + lactate>2 after 30mL/kg fluids. qSOFA: RR≥22, GCS<15, SBP≤100 (≥2=ICU screen). 1H BUNDLE: Lactate, Cx, Abx 1h, 30mL/kg balanced crystalloid, Vasopressors, Source control. FLUIDS: RL/Plasma-Lyte, dynamic (PLR, SVV). VASOPRESSORS: NE 1st → Vasso 0.03U/min 2nd → Adrenaline 3rd. STEROIDS: Hydrocortisone 200mg/day infusion if NE>0.25-0.5. SOURCE CONTROL: 6-12h. DE-ESCALATE: 48-72h.

Summary

Sepsis = life-threatening organ dysfunction from dysregulated host response to infection = SOFA score increase ≥2. Septic shock = sepsis subset with vasopressor requirement to maintain MAP ≥65mmHg + lactate >2mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation (30mL/kg crystalloid). Mortality: sepsis ~25–30%, septic shock ~40–50%. 1-Hour Bundle: (1) Measure lactate, (2) Blood cultures ×2 BEFORE antibiotics, (3) Broad IV antibiotics WITHIN 1 HOUR, (4) 30mL/kg balanced crystalloid (Plasma-Lyte/RL) for hypotension/lactate≥4, (5) Vasopressors if still hypotensive, (6) Source control within 6–12h. Vasopressor escalation: Norepinephrine first-line (0.01–3μg/kg/min) → add Vasopressin 0.03U/min fixed → Adrenaline third-line; avoid dopamine. Hydrocortisone 200mg/day IV continuous infusion (or 50mg 6h) if norepinephrine >0.25–0.5μg/kg/min; taper 7–10d. Fluid responsiveness: passive leg raise (gold standard), SVV/PPV (ventilated), echo IVC — NOT static CVP. De-escalate antibiotics at 48–72h based on cultures. qSOFA ≥2 (RR≥22, GCS<15, SBP≤100) = screen for ICU referral/early intervention.

MCQs (10)

  1. According to Sepsis-3, septic shock is defined by which combination?

A. SOFA ≥2 + lactate >4mmol/L

B. Vasopressor requirement for MAP ≥65mmHg + lactate >2mmol/L despite 30mL/kg crystalloid

C. qSOFA ≥2 + hypotension + lactate >2mmol/L

D. SBP <90mmHg + lactate >4mmol/L + oliguria

E. MAP <65mmHg on fluids + lactate >4mmol/L

  1. What is the recommended initial fluid bolus for sepsis-induced hypotension or lactate ≥4mmol/L?

A. 10mL/kg 0.9% saline

B. 30mL/kg balanced crystalloid (Plasma-Lyte / Ringer's lactate)

C. 20mL/kg albumin 5%

D. 500mL 0.9% saline bolus, repeat to CVP 8–12

E. 30mL/kg 0.9% saline

  1. First-line vasopressor for septic shock:

A. Vasopressin 0.03U/min

B. Norepinephrine 0.01–3μg/kg/min

C. Adrenaline 0.01–3μg/kg/min

D. Dopamine 5–20μg/kg/min

E. Phenylephrine 0.5–10μg/kg/min

  1. Hydrocortisone in septic shock: correct indication and dose?

A. All septic shock: 100mg IV bolus 6h

B. Vasopressor-dependent shock (NE >0.25–0.5μg/kg/min): 200mg/day IV continuous infusion ×7–10d taper

C. Lactate >4mmol/L: 50mg IV 6h

D. Refractory shock on 3 vasopressors: 300mg/day infusion

E. Not recommended in any septic shock

  1. Best method to assess fluid responsiveness in a mechanically ventilated patient on norepinephrine?

A. CVP target 8–12mmHg

B. Stroke volume variation (SVV) or pulse pressure variation (PPV)

C. Urine output >0.5mL/kg/h

D. MAP response to 250mL bolus

E. Lactate clearance

  1. When should broad-spectrum IV antibiotics be administered in the sepsis bundle?

A. Within 3 hours

B. Within 1 hour of recognition

C. After blood cultures are drawn (no time limit)

D. After CT scan identifies source

E. Within 6 hours

  1. Which fluid is preferred for sepsis resuscitation?

A. 0.9% saline

B. Balanced crystalloid (Plasma-Lyte / Ringer's lactate)

C. Albumin 5%

D. Hydroxyethyl starch 6%

E. Gelatin

  1. Vasopressin in septic shock: correct dosing?

A. 0.01–0.04U/min, titrate to MAP

B. Fixed 0.03U/min, do NOT titrate

C. 0.06U/min bolus then 0.03U/min

D. 0.04U/min, increase by 0.01U/min q15min

E. 2U IV bolus then 0.03U/min

  1. Source control in sepsis should be achieved within:

A. 1 hour

B. 6–12 hours

C. 24 hours

D. 48 hours

E. Before antibiotics

  1. qSOFA ≥2 indicates:

A. Definite sepsis

B. Septic shock

C. High risk for poor outcome / need for ICU referral

D. Need for immediate vasopressors

E. Lactate >4mmol/L

SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 68-year-old man with community-acquired pneumonia presents with BP 85/50, RR 28, GCS 14, lactate 4.8mmol/L. He receives 30mL/kg Ringer's lactate. Repeat BP 88/52, lactate 4.2. Next step?

A. Another 30mL/kg fluid bolus

B. Start norepinephrine infusion targeting MAP ≥65mmHg

C. Add vasopressin 0.03U/min

D. Hydrocortisone 200mg/day infusion

E. Dobutamine for low cardiac output

  1. A 45-year-old woman with necrotising fasciitis is in septic shock on norepinephrine 0.6μg/kg/min, vasopressin 0.03U/min, MAP 62mmHg. Lactate 3.5mmol/L. Best next intervention?

A. Increase norepinephrine to 1.0μg/kg/min

B. Add adrenaline infusion

C. Hydrocortisone 200mg/day IV continuous infusion

D. IVIG 1g/kg

E. Transfuse FFP for coagulopathy

  1. In a patient with septic shock from cholangitis, which source control intervention has the strongest evidence for timing?

A. Percutaneous drainage within 1 hour

B. ERCP within 24 hours (urgent if septic shock)

C. Laparoscopic cholecystectomy within 6 hours

D. IV antibiotics alone for 48 hours before drainage

E. Endoscopic sphincterotomy within 1 hour

  1. A mechanically ventilated patient on norepinephrine 0.3μg/kg/min receives a 500mL fluid bolus. SVV decreases from 18% to 8%. Interpretation?

A. Not fluid responsive

B. Fluid responsive (ΔSVV >10–15% or absolute <10–13%)

C. Needs vasopressin

D. Needs dobutamine

E. SVV unreliable on norepinephrine

  1. Procalcitonin (PCT) use in sepsis: which statement is CORRECT?

A. PCT <0.25 rules out sepsis — withhold antibiotics

B. PCT guides initial antibiotic selection

C. PCT supports de-escalation/discontinuation (e.g., drop >80% or <0.25)

D. PCT >10 confirms septic shock

E. PCT replaces lactate for resuscitation monitoring

  1. A 30-year-old pregnant woman (28 weeks) develops septic shock from pyelonephritis. Vasopressor choice?

A. Dopamine (safe in pregnancy)

B. Norepinephrine (first-line in pregnancy)

C. Vasopressin (preferred in pregnancy)

D. Adrenaline (first-line in pregnancy)

E. Phenylephrine only

  1. Which patient with sepsis should receive empirical antifungal coverage?

A. All septic shock patients

B. Immunocompromised (prolonged neutropenia, transplant, high-dose steroids) with risk factors

C. Community-acquired pneumonia with sepsis

D. Post-operative sepsis day 2

E. UTI sepsis with diabetes

  1. Hydrocortisone taper in septic shock (standard 7–10 day course):

A. Stop abruptly after shock resolves

B. 50mg 6h ×2d → 25mg 6h ×2d → 25mg 12h ×2d → 25mg 24h ×2d → stop

C. 100mg 6h ×3d → 50mg 6h ×3d → stop

D. 200mg/day infusion ×7d → stop abruptly

E. No taper needed if <7 days

  1. A patient with septic shock has lactate 8mmol/L initially. After 2 hours of resuscitation (fluids, NE, source control), lactate 6mmol/L. Assessment?

A. Inadequate resuscitation (clearance <10%)

B. Adequate (clearance 25% >10–20% target)

C. Need more fluids

D. Add vasopressin

E. Start CRRT

  1. In febrile neutropenia (absolute neutrophil count <500), empirical antibiotic regimen?

A. Ceftriaxone + Azithromycin

B. Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV 6h OR Meropenem 1g IV 8h (+/- Vancomycin if catheter/mucositis)

C. Vancomycin + Meropenem

D. Ciprofloxacin + Clindamycin

E. Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole

Flashcards

  • Q: Sepsis-3 sepsis definition

A: SOFA score increase ≥2 points from baseline

  • Q: Sepsis-3 septic shock definition

A: Vasopressors for MAP≥65 + lactate>2 despite 30mL/kg fluids

  • Q: qSOFA components

A: RR≥22, GCS<15, SBP≤100 (≥2 = high risk screen)

  • Q: 1-hour bundle

A: Lactate, Blood Cx, Abx 1h, 30mL/kg balanced crystalloid, Vasopressors, Source control

  • Q: Fluid type

A: Balanced crystalloid (Plasma-Lyte/RL) > 0.9% saline

  • Q: Fluid responsiveness

A: PLR (gold), SVV/PPV (ventilated), echo IVC — NOT CVP

  • Q: Vasopressor order

A: NE 1st → Vasopressin 0.03U/min fixed 2nd → Adrenaline 3rd

  • Q: Hydrocortisone

A: 200mg/day infusion if NE>0.25-0.5; 7-10d taper

  • Q: Source control timing

A: 6-12h (immediate for necrotising fasciitis)

  • Q: Antibiotic de-escalation

A: 48-72h based on cultures

  • Q: Lactate clearance target

A: ≥10-20% per 2h

  • Q: PCT role

A: De-escalation/discontinuation (drop >80% or <0.25)

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — Sepsis-3 septic shock: vasopressor requirement for MAP≥65 + lactate>2mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation (30mL/kg crystalloid).
  2. B — 30mL/kg balanced crystalloid (Plasma-Lyte/RL) preferred over 0.9% saline (less hyperchloraemic acidosis, less AKI).
  3. B — Norepinephrine is first-line vasopressor for septic shock.
  4. B — Hydrocortisone 200mg/day IV continuous infusion (or 50mg 6h) if vasopressor-dependent (NE >0.25–0.5μg/kg/min); taper 7–10d.
  5. B — Dynamic indices: SVV/PPV (if ventilated, regular rhythm, tidal volume ≥8mL/kg) or PLR with CO monitoring. CVP static = unreliable.
  6. B — Antibiotics within 1 HOUR of sepsis recognition. Every hour delay increases mortality ~7–8%.
  7. B — Balanced crystalloid preferred over 0.9% saline (less AKI, less hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis).
  8. B — Vasopressin fixed dose 0.03U/min, do NOT titrate. Added to reduce norepinephrine dose.
  9. B — Source control (drainage, debridement, device removal) within 6–12 hours. Necrotising fasciitis = immediate.
  10. C — qSOFA ≥2 = screening tool for high risk of poor outcome/ICU referral; NOT diagnostic for sepsis.

SBAs

  1. B — After 30mL/kg fluids, still hypotensive (MAP<65) → start norepinephrine. Lactate not cleared adequately.
  2. C — NE 0.6μg/kg/min (>0.5) = vasopressor-dependent shock → hydrocortisone 200mg/day infusion indicated.
  3. B — Cholangitis with septic shock: ERCP within 24h (urgent if septic). Tokyo guidelines.
  4. B — SVV decreased from 18% to 8% = fluid responsive (SVV <10–13% or Δ>10–15%).
  5. C — PCT supports de-escalation/discontinuation (e.g., >80% drop or <0.25). Do NOT use to withhold initial antibiotics.
  6. B — Norepinephrine first-line in pregnancy (and all adults). Dopamine not preferred (arrhythmia).
  7. B — Empirical antifungals for immunocompromised with risk factors (neutropenia, transplant, steroids), not routine.
  8. B — Standard taper: 50mg 6h ×2d → 25mg 6h ×2d → 25mg 12h ×2d → 25mg 24h ×2d → stop (7–10d total).
  9. B — Lactate clearance = (8-6)/8 = 25% > 10–20% target = adequate resuscitation.
  10. B — Febrile neutropenia: Pip-tazo or Meropenem monotherapy (antipseudomonal β-lactam); add vancomycin if catheter/mucositis/hemodynamically unstable.

davidson_chapter: Chapter 13: Infectious disease

topic_category: STI Domain

status: full-fcps-mrcp-topic-note

Sexually Transmitted Infections (STIs)

Related: [[HIV Infection and AIDS]], [[Pelvic Inflammatory Disease]], [[Epididymo-orchitis]], [[Reactive Arthritis]], [[Dermatology (Genital Ulcers)]]

[!important]
STI = infection transmitted predominantly through sexual contact. Syndromic management: urethral discharge, vaginal discharge, genital ulcer, inguinal bubo, scrotal swelling, neonatal conjunctivitis. Partner notification and treatment essential. Screening: chlamydia/gonorrhoea (NAAT), syphilis (serology), HIV, HBV, HCV. Expedited partner therapy (EPT) for chlamydia/gonorrhoea.

Learning Objectives

  • Diagnose common STIs using clinical syndromes and laboratory tests
  • Apply syndromic management algorithms
  • Select appropriate antimicrobial therapy for each STI
  • Manage complications (PID, epididymo-orchitis, neonatal infection)
  • Conduct partner notification and expedited partner therapy
  • Screen appropriately (chlamydia, gonorrhoea, syphilis, HIV, HBV, HCV)
  • Recognise antimicrobial resistance (gonorrhoea, mycoplasma genitalium)

Syndromic Classification & Common Aetiology

SyndromeCommon Pathogens
Urethral discharge (male)Chlamydia trachomatis (40–50%), Neisseria gonorrhoeae (30–40%), M. genitalium (15–25%), Ureaplasma, Trichomonas
Vaginal discharge (female)C. trachomatis, N. gonorrhoeae, T. vaginalis, Candida, BV, M. genitalium
Genital ulcerHSV-1/2 (most common), Treponema pallidum (syphilis), Haemophilus ducreyi (chancroid), Klebsiella granulomatis (donovanosis), LGV
Inguinal buboLGV (C. trachomatis L1–L3), chancroid, syphilis
Scrotal swellingC. trachomatis, N. gonorrhoeae (epididymo-orchitis)
Neonatal conjunctivitisC. trachomatis (5–14 days), N. gonorrhoeae (2–5 days), HSV

Key STIs — Clinical Features, Diagnosis, Treatment

1. Chlamydia trachomatis

AspectDetails
ClinicalOften asymptomatic (70% women, 50% men); urethritis, cervicitis, PID, epididymitis, LGV (L1–L3), neonatal conjunctivitis/pneumonia
DiagnosisNAAT (urine, swab) — gold standard; culture rarely used
Treatment (Uncomplicated)Doxycycline 100mg PO 12h ×7 days (1st line)
AlternativeAzithromycin 1g PO single dose (if adherence concern, pregnancy)
LGVDoxycycline 100mg PO 12h ×21 days
PregnancyAzithromycin 1g PO single dose (or Amoxicillin 500mg PO 8h ×7d)
Partner notificationAll partners last 6 months (or last partner if >6m)

2. Neisseria gonorrhoeae (Gonorrhoea)

AspectDetails
ClinicalUrethritis, cervicitis, pharyngitis, proctitis, PID, DGI (arthritis-dermatitis), neonatal ophthalmia
DiagnosisNAAT (urine, swab); Culture + AST essential (resistance monitoring)
Treatment (Uncomplicated)Ceftriaxone 500mg IM single dose (if <150kg) / 1g IM if ≥150kg
PLUSDoxycycline 100mg PO 12h ×7d (if chlamydia not excluded) OR Azithromycin 1g PO (if doxy contraindicated)
PharyngealCeftriaxone 1g IM single dose (higher failure at pharynx)
DGICeftriaxone 1g IV/IM 24h ×7d (switch oral after 24–48h improvement)
ResistanceRising MICs to ceftriaxone, azithromycin; ceftriaxone 1g IM if decreased susceptibility suspected
PregnancyCeftriaxone 500mg/1g IM + Azithromycin 1g PO

3. Syphilis (Treponema pallidum)

StageClinical FeaturesTreatment
PrimaryChancre: painless, clean-based, indurated ulcer at inoculation site (3–90d); regional lymphadenopathyBenzathine penicillin G 2.4MU IM single dose
SecondaryRash (palms/soles, trunk), condyloma lata (moist, infectious), mucous patches, generalised lymphadenopathy, fever, malaise (4–10w)Benzathine penicillin G 2.4MU IM single dose
Early Latent<1 year post-infection; seropositive, no symptomsBenzathine penicillin G 2.4MU IM single dose
Late Latent>1 year or unknown duration; seropositiveBenzathine penicillin G 2.4MU IM weekly ×3 doses
TertiaryGummatous (skin, bone, liver), Cardiovascular (aortitis, aneurysm), NeurosyphilisSee Neurosyphilis
NeurosyphilisMeningitis, stroke, tabes dorsalis, general paresis, ocular/otic syphilisPenicillin G 18–24MU/day IV ÷4h ×14d (or Ceftriaxone 2g IV 12h ×14d)
Ocular/Otic SyphilisUveitis, optic neuritis, hearing lossTreat as neurosyphilis (IV penicillin ×14d)
Special SituationsTreatment
Penicillin allergy (non-pregnant)Doxycycline 100mg PO 12h ×14d (early) / ×28d (late); Ceftriaxone 1g IM/IV daily ×10–14d
PregnancyPenicillin ONLY (desensitise if allergic); Benzathine penicillin G per stage
HIV coinfectionSame regimens; closer follow-up (RPR titres); consider neurosyphilis if CD4<350
Jarisch-HerxheimerFever, rigors, hypotension, worsening rash 2–12h post-treatment; self-limiting; prednisolone not routine
SerologyInterpretation
Non-treponemal (RPR, VDRL)Quantitative; correlates with disease activity; ↓4-fold = treatment response; can be negative in early primary/late tertiary
Treponemal (TPHA, TPPA, EIA, FTA-ABS)Qualitative; remains positive for life; confirms exposure
AlgorithmTraditional: Screen treponemal → confirm non-treponemal; Reverse: Screen non-treponemal → confirm treponemal

4. Genital Herpes (HSV-1/2)

AspectDetails
ClinicalPrimary: painful vesicles/ulcers, inguinal lymphadenopathy, systemic symptoms (fever, malaise); Recurrent: milder, shorter, prodrome (tingling, pain)
DiagnosisNAAT (swab from vesicle/ulcer) — gold standard; Tzanck smear (multinucleated giant cells) — low sensitivity; Type-specific serology (HSV-1/2 IgG) — for counselling, asymptomatic
Treatment (Episodic)Acyclovir 400mg PO 12h ×5d (or 800mg 12h ×5d); Valacyclovir 500mg 12h ×5d; Famciclovir 250mg 12h ×5d
Treatment (Suppressive)Acyclovir 400mg PO 12h; Valacyclovir 500mg PO OD; Famciclovir 250mg PO 12h — if ≥6 recurrences/year or severe psychosocial impact
Severe/ImmunocompromisedAcyclovir 5mg/kg IV 8h ×5–7d → oral step-down
PregnancySuppressive from 36 weeks (Acyclovir 400mg 12h or Valacyclovir 500mg OD) to reduce neonatal HSV; C-section if active lesions at delivery
Neonatal HSVAcyclovir 20mg/kg IV 8h ×14d (SEM) / ×21d (CNS/Disseminated)

5. Mycoplasma genitalium

AspectDetails
ClinicalUrethritis (often non-gonococcal, non-chlamydial), cervicitis, PID; increasing macrolide resistance
DiagnosisNAAT (with macrolide resistance detection if available)
Treatment (Resistance unknown / sensitive)Doxycycline 100mg PO 12h ×7d → Azithromycin 1g PO day 1, then 500mg daily ×3d (total 2.5g)
Treatment (Macrolide-resistant)Moxifloxacin 400mg PO OD ×7–14d (after doxycycline lead-in)
Resistance testingRecommended before treatment if available; guides macrolide vs fluoroquinolone choice

6. Trichomonas vaginalis

AspectDetails
ClinicalVaginitis (frothy yellow-green discharge, odour, pruritus, strawberry cervix); urethritis (often asymptomatic in men)
DiagnosisNAAT (gold standard); wet mount (motile trichomonads, sensitivity ~60%); culture
TreatmentMetronidazole 2g PO single dose (or 400mg 12h ×7d); Tinidazole 2g single dose
Partner treatmentSimultaneous (single dose metronidazole)
PregnancyMetronidazole 2g single dose safe after 1st trimester

7. Lymphogranuloma Venereum (LGV)

AspectDetails
AetiologyC. trachomatis serovars L1, L2, L3
ClinicalProctocolitis (MSM): rectal pain, discharge, bleeding, tenesmus, ulceration; Inguinal syndrome: bubo, fistulae; Genital ulcer (rare)
DiagnosisNAAT + genotyping (L1–L3) or rectal swab for LGV PCR
TreatmentDoxycycline 100mg PO 12h ×21 days
AlternativeErythromycin 500mg PO 6h ×21d (pregnancy)

8. Chancroid (Haemophilus ducreyi)

AspectDetails
ClinicalPainful, soft, purulent ulcer with ragged edges; painful inguinal lymphadenopathy (bubo)
DiagnosisClinical + PCR (specialised); culture difficult
TreatmentAzithromycin 1g PO single dose OR Ceftriaxone 250mg IM single dose OR Ciprofloxacin 500mg PO 12h ×3d OR Erythromycin 500mg PO 6h ×7d

9. Donovanosis (Klebsiella granulomatis)

AspectDetails
ClinicalProgressive, painless, beefy-red, granulomatous ulcers (pseudobuboes); genital, perineal, perianal
DiagnosisTissue smear (Donovan bodies — intracytoplasmic rods in macrophages); PCR
TreatmentAzithromycin 1g PO weekly ×3 weeks OR Doxycycline 100mg 12h ×3w OR TMP-SMX DS 12h ×3w

10. Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID)

AspectDetails
ClinicalLower abdominal pain, cervical motion tenderness, adnexal tenderness, fever, abnormal discharge, irregular bleeding
DiagnosisClinical (high index of suspicion); empirical treatment if criteria met; laparoscopy gold standard
Treatment (Outpatient, mild-moderate)Ceftriaxone 500mg IM single dose + Doxycycline 100mg PO 12h ×14d + Metronidazole 400mg PO 12h ×14d
Treatment (Inpatient, severe)Ceftriaxone 2g IV 24h + Doxycycline 100mg PO/IV 12h + Metronidazole 500mg IV 8h → step-down oral ×14d total
Alternative (if cephalosporin allergy)Ofloxacin 400mg PO 12h + Metronidazole 400mg PO 12h ×14d (NOT if gonorrhoea suspected)

11. Epididymo-orchitis

AspectDetails
<35 years / STI riskCeftriaxone 500mg IM single dose + Doxycycline 100mg PO 12h ×10–14d (covers C. trachomatis, N. gonorrhoeae)
>35 years / low STI risk / urinary tractCiprofloxacin 500mg PO 12h ×10–14d OR Ofloxacin 200mg PO 12h ×10–14d (covers Enterobacteriaceae)
Mumps orchitisSupportive (scrotal elevation, NSAIDs); no antibiotics

Partner Notification & Expedited Partner Therapy (EPT)

PrincipleDetails
Partner notificationAll sexual partners in relevant lookback period
Lookback periodsChlamydia: 6 months; Gonorrhoea: 2 months (or last partner); Syphilis: primary/secondary/early latent = 3m/6m/12m; HIV: all partners since last negative test
EPTPatient-delivered partner therapy for chlamydia (azithromycin 1g) and gonorrhoea (ceftriaxone 500mg IM + azithromycin 1g) where legal/approved
AbstinenceUntil both treated + 7 days after completion

STI Screening Recommendations

PopulationScreen For
All sexually active <25Annual Chlamydia (NAAT urine/swab); Gonorrhoea if risk factors
MSM3-site testing: pharyngeal, rectal, urethral for CT/NG; Syphilis (serology); HIV; HBV/HCV
PregnancySyphilis (booking, 28w, delivery if risk); HIV (booking); HBV (booking); Chlamydia/Gonorrhoea if <25 or risk; HCV if risk
New partner / symptomaticFull screen (CT, NG, Syphilis, HIV, HBV, HCV)
PrEP users3-monthly: HIV, Syphilis, CT/NG (3-site MSM), Renal function

Antimicrobial Resistance Concerns

PathogenResistance Issue
N. gonorrhoeaeRising ceftriaxone MICs (ceftriaxone 1g IM if decreased susceptibility); azithromycin resistance; MDR/XDR strains reported
M. genitaliumHigh macrolide resistance (mutations in 23S rRNA); moxifloxacin resistance emerging
C. trachomatisRare resistance to doxycycline/azithromycin
T. pallidumNo documented penicillin resistance

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Urethral discharge: CT (doxy 7d) → NG (ceftriaxone IM) → Mgen (doxy + azithro/moxi)
  • Vaginal discharge: CT/NG/T trichomonas/Candida/BV
  • Genital ulcer: HSV (NAAT, acyclovir) → Syphilis (RPR/TPPA, penicillin) → Chancroid/LGV/Donovanosis
  • Syphilis stages: Primary/Secondary/Early Latent = 2.4MU BPG ×1; Late Latent/Tertiary = 2.4MU BPG ×3 weekly; Neurosyphilis = Pen G IV 18-24MU/day ×14d
  • Pregnancy syphilis: Penicillin ONLY (desensitise if allergic)
  • Gonorrhoea: Ceftriaxone 500mg/1g IM + Doxy 7d (or azithro 1g if doxy contraindicated)
  • Chlamydia: Doxycycline 100mg 12h ×7d (1st line); Azithro 1g single if pregnancy/adherence
  • LGV: Doxycycline ×21d
  • M. genitalium: Doxy lead-in + Azithro (if sensitive) OR Moxifloxacin (if resistant)
  • PID: Ceftriaxone + Doxy + Metro ×14d
  • Epididymo-orchitis <35y: Ceftriaxone + Doxy; >35y: Cipro/Ofloxacin
  • Partner notification: Chlamydia 6m, Gonorrhoea 2m, Syphilis by stage
  • Screening: <25y annual CT; MSM 3-site CT/NG + Syphilis + HIV; Pregnancy full screen

Common Viva Questions

  1. First-line treatment for uncomplicated chlamydia? Doxycycline 100mg PO 12h ×7 days.
  2. Treatment for uncomplicated gonorrhoea? Ceftriaxone 500mg IM single dose (1g if ≥150kg) + Doxycycline 100mg 12h ×7d.
  3. Syphilis treatment by stage? Primary/Secondary/Early Latent: BPG 2.4MU IM ×1; Late Latent/Tertiary: BPG 2.4MU IM weekly ×3; Neurosyphilis: Pen G 18–24MU/day IV ×14d.
  4. Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction? Fever, rigors, hypotension, worsening rash 2–12h post-penicillin; self-limiting.
  5. LGV treatment? Doxycycline 100mg 12h ×21 days.
  6. M. genitalium macrolide-resistant treatment? Doxycycline 100mg 12h ×7d → Moxifloxacin 400mg OD ×7–14d.
  7. PID empirical regimen? Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Doxycycline 100mg 12h ×14d + Metronidazole 400mg 12h ×14d.
  8. Epididymo-orchitis in 30-year-old? Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Doxycycline 100mg 12h ×10–14d.
  9. Partner notification lookback for chlamydia? 6 months (or last partner if >6m).
  10. Neonatal HSV treatment? Acyclovir 20mg/kg IV 8h ×14d (SEM) / ×21d (CNS/Disseminated).

Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
Azithromycin 1st line for chlamydiaDoxycycline 7d = 1st line (higher cure, less resistance); Azithro = alternative
Ceftriaxone IV for uncomplicated gonorrhoeaCeftriaxone IM single dose (IV only for DGI, pharyngeal fallback)
Single dose BPG for late latent syphilisLate latent = BPG 2.4MU weekly ×3 doses
Doxycycline in pregnancyCONTRAINDICATED (fetal bone/teeth); use Azithro (chlamydia), Amoxicillin, Erythromycin
Fluconazole for trichomonasMetronidazole/Tinidazole ONLY
Moxifloxacin 1st line for MgenDoxy + Azithro 1st line; Moxifloxacin ONLY if macrolide-resistant
EPT for syphilisEPT ONLY for chlamydia/gonorrhoea (not syphilis — needs staging, penicillin)
Asymptomatic partner = no treatmentAsymptomatic partners MUST be treated (especially chlamydia/gonorrhoea/syphilis)
HIV PrEP = STI protectionPrEP protects against HIV ONLY, not other STIs

Mnemonics

  • URETHRAL DISCHARGE: CT (Doxy 7d) → GC (Ceftriaxone IM) → Mgen (Doxy+Azithro/Moxi)
  • GENITAL ULCER: HSV (NAAT, Acyclovir) → Syphilis (RPR/TPPA, Penicillin) → Chancroid/LGV/Donovanosis
  • SYPHILIS STAGES: Primary/Secondary/Early Latent = 1 dose BPG; Late Latent/Tertiary = 3 doses BPG; Neurosyphilis = IV Pen G ×14d
  • GONORRHOEA: Ceftriaxone IM + Doxy 7d
  • CHLAMYDIA: Doxy 7 days
  • LGV: Doxy 21 days
  • M GENITALIUM: DOXY lead-in → Azithro (sensitive) / Moxi (resistant)
  • PID: Ceftriaxone + DOXY + METRO 14 days
  • EPIDIDYMO-ORCHITIS: <35y = Ceftriaxone + Doxy; >35y = Cipro/Ofloxacin
  • PARTNER LOOKBACK: CT 6m, GC 2m, Syph by stage
  • NEONATAL HSV: Acyclovir 20mg/kg IV ×14d (SEM) / ×21d (CNS/Dissem)

Summary

Sexually Transmitted Infections (STIs) are infections transmitted predominantly through sexual contact. Syndromic presentation: urethral discharge, vaginal discharge, genital ulcer, inguinal bubo, scrotal swelling, neonatal conjunctivitis. Chlamydia trachomatis: commonest bacterial STI; often asymptomatic; NAAT diagnosis; 1st line: Doxycycline 100mg PO 12h ×7d; alternative azithromycin 1g single dose; LGV (L1–L3) → doxycycline ×21d. Neisseria gonorrhoeae: NAAT + culture for AST; Ceftriaxone 500mg IM (1g if ≥150kg) + Doxycycline 12h ×7d; pharyngeal → ceftriaxone 1g IM; DGI → ceftriaxone 1g IV ×7d; rising ceftriaxone MICs. Syphilis (T. pallidum): stages — primary (chancre), secondary (rash, condyloma lata), early latent (<1y), late latent (>1y), tertiary (gumma, CV, neuro), neurosyphilis, ocular/otic. Serology: non-treponemal (RPR/VDRL, quantitative) + treponemal (TPPA/EIA, qualitative). Treatment: Primary/Secondary/Early Latent → Benzathine penicillin G 2.4MU IM single dose; Late Latent/Tertiary → BPG 2.4MU IM weekly ×3; Neurosyphilis/Ocular/Otic → Penicillin G 18–24MU/day IV ÷4h ×14d (or Ceftriaxone 2g IV 12h ×14d). Pregnancy: penicillin ONLY (desensitise if allergic). Jarisch-Herxheimer: fever, rigors, hypotension 2–12h post-treatment. Herpes (HSV-1/2): NAAT diagnosis; episodic acyclovir 400mg 12h ×5d; suppressive if ≥6/year acyclovir 400mg 12h. Pregnancy: suppressive from 36w; C-section if active lesions. Neonatal HSV: acyclovir 20mg/kg IV 8h ×14d (SEM) / ×21d (CNS/Disseminated). M. genitalium: NAAT with resistance testing; sensitive → doxycycline 7d → azithromycin 2.5g total; resistant → doxycycline 7d → moxifloxacin 400mg OD ×7–14d. Trichomonas: metronidazole 2g single dose. LGV: doxycycline ×21d. Chancroid: azithromycin 1g single dose. Donovanosis: azithromycin 1g weekly ×3. PID: ceftriaxone 500mg IM + doxycycline 100mg 12h ×14d + metronidazole 400mg 12h ×14d. Epididymo-orchitis: <35y → ceftriaxone + doxycycline 10–14d; >35y → ciprofloxacin/ofloxacin 10–14d. Partner notification: chlamydia 6 months, gonorrhoea 2 months, syphilis by stage; EPT for chlamydia/gonorrhoea. Screening: <25y annual chlamydia; MSM 3-site CT/NG + syphilis + HIV; pregnancy full screen. Resistance: gonorrhoea (ceftriaxone MICs, azithromycin), M. genitalium (macrolide resistance).

D. Ceftriaxone 1g IV daily ×7d

E. No antibiotics (viral)

  1. A patient treated for gonorrhoea (ceftriaxone + doxycycline) returns at 2 weeks with persistent urethritis. NAAT still positive for GC. Ceftriaxone MIC elevated. Next step?

A. Repeat ceftriaxone 500mg IM

B. Ceftriaxone 1g IM + consider test of cure + resistance testing

C. Switch to ciprofloxacin

D. Add azithromycin 2g

E. Refer for urethroscopy

  1. A 22-year-old woman on PrEP (tenofovir/emtricitabine) presents for 3-monthly screen. MSM. Which tests?

A. HIV only

B. HIV + Syphilis only

C. HIV + Syphilis + 3-site CT/NG (pharyngeal, rectal, urethral) + Renal function

D. HIV + CT/NG urine only

E. HIV + Syphilis + CT/NG urine + HBV/HCV

  1. A neonate born to mother with active genital herpes at delivery (lesions present). Infant management?

A. Observe only

B. Acyclovir 20mg/kg IV 8h ×14d (SEM) / ×21d (CNS/Disseminated)

C. Acyclovir PO ×7d

D. Valacyclovir PO ×14d

E. No treatment unless symptoms

  1. A 40-year-old man with HIV (CD4 350, VL <50) presents with secondary syphilis (RPR 1:64). Treatment?

A. Benzathine penicillin G 2.4MU IM single dose (same as HIV-negative)

B. Benzathine penicillin G 2.4MU IM weekly ×3

C. Penicillin G IV ×14d

D. Doxycycline 100mg 12h ×28d

E. Ceftriaxone 1g IM weekly ×3

  1. Expedited Partner Therapy (EPT) is recommended for which STIs?

A. Syphilis and HIV

B. Chlamydia and Gonorrhoea

C. Herpes and Syphilis

D. Trichomonas and BV

E. All STIs

Flashcards

  • Q: CT 1st line

A: Doxycycline 100mg 12h ×7d

  • Q: GC treatment

A: Ceftriaxone 500mg/1g IM + Doxy 7d

  • Q: Syphilis 1°/2°/E.Lat

A: BPG 2.4MU IM ×1

  • Q: Syphilis Late Latent

A: BPG 2.4MU IM weekly ×3

  • Q: Neurosyphilis

A: PenG 18-24MU/day IV ÷4h ×14d

  • Q: HSV episodic

A: Acyclovir 400mg 12h ×5d

  • Q: HSV suppressive

A: Acyclovir 400mg 12h (if ≥6/yr)

  • Q: Mgen sensitive

A: Doxy 7d → Azithro 2.5g total

  • Q: Mgen resistant

A: Doxy 7d → Moxi 400mg 7-14d

  • Q: LGV

A: Doxy 21d

  • Q: Trichomonas

A: Metro 2g single

  • Q: PID

A: Ceftriaxone + Doxy + Metro 14d

  • Q: Epididymo-orchitis <35

A: Ceftriaxone + Doxy 10-14d

  • Q: Epididymo-orchitis >35

A: Cipro/Ofloxacin 10-14d

  • Q: Partner lookback CT

A: 6 months

  • Q: Partner lookback GC

A: 2 months

  • Q: EPT

A: CT + GC

  • Q: Pregnant Syphilis

A: Penicillin ONLY (desensitise)

  • Q: Neo HSV

A: Acyclovir 20mg/kg IV ×14d/21d

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — Doxycycline 100mg PO 12h ×7d is 1st line for chlamydia (IDSA/BASHH). Azithromycin single dose is alternative.
  2. C — Ceftriaxone 500mg IM (1g if ≥150kg) + Doxycycline 100mg 12h ×7d for uncomplicated gonorrhoea. Dual therapy recommended.
  3. B — Late latent (>1y): Benzathine penicillin G 2.4MU IM weekly ×3 doses.
  4. C — Neurosyphilis: Penicillin G 18–24MU/day IV divided 4-hourly ×14 days (or Ceftriaxone 2g IV 12h ×14d).
  5. C — LGV: Doxycycline 100mg PO 12h ×21 days.
  6. B — Macrolide-resistant M. genitalium: Doxycycline lead-in 7d → Moxifloxacin 400mg OD ×7–14d.
  7. C — PID: Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Doxycycline 100mg 12h ×14d + Metronidazole 400mg 12h ×14d.
  8. B — <35y with STI risk: Ceftriaxone 500mg IM + Doxycycline 100mg 12h ×10–14d (covers CT/GC).
  9. C — Chlamydia partner lookback: 6 months (or last partner if >6 months).
  10. C — Syphilis in pregnancy: Penicillin ONLY. If allergic → desensitisation then benzathine penicillin per stage.

SBAs

  1. B — Chlamydia monotherapy: Doxycycline 7d 1st line.
  2. B — Trichomonas: Metronidazole 2g PO single dose (or 400mg 12h ×7d).
  3. C — LGV proctitis: Doxycycline 100mg 12h ×21 days.
  4. C — Syphilis in pregnancy: Penicillin only. Desensitise if anaphylaxis. Doxy/azithro/ceftriaxone not alternatives.
  5. B — >35y epididymo-orchitis: enteric Gram-neg (E. coli etc.) → fluoroquinolone (ciprofloxacin/ofloxacin) 10–14d.
  6. B — GC treatment failure with elevated ceftriaxone MIC: ceftriaxone 1g IM + resistance testing + test of cure. Dual therapy essential.
  7. C — PrEP monitoring MSM: 3-monthly HIV, syphilis, 3-site CT/NG, renal function.
  8. B — Neonate exposed to active HSV at delivery: acyclovir 20mg/kg IV 8h ×14d (SEM) or ×21d (CNS/disseminated).
  9. A — HIV+ with secondary syphilis (CD4>200, VL suppressed): same treatment as HIV-negative — BPG 2.4MU IM single dose. Closer serological follow-up.
  10. B — EPT: patient-delivered therapy for chlamydia (azithromycin) and gonorrhoea (ceftriaxone + azithromycin) where authorised.
