topics.title: Tuberculosis Pulmonary And Extrapulmonary
topics.html:
tags: [medicine, infectious-disease, davidson, chapter13, tb, tuberculosis, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_chapter: Chapter 13: Infectious disease

topic_category: Mycobacterial Infections Domain

status: full-fcps-mrcp-topic-note

Tuberculosis (Pulmonary and Extrapulmonary)

Related: [[Tuberculous Meningitis]], [[Lymph Node Tuberculosis]], [[Pleural Tuberculosis]], [[Abdominal Tuberculosis]], [[Genitourinary Tuberculosis]], [[Bone and Joint Tuberculosis]], [[HIV-Associated Opportunistic Infections]], [[Drug-Resistant TB]]

[!important]
TB = Mycobacterium tuberculosis complex. Pulmonary TB = most common (80%). Diagnosis: GeneXpert MTB/RIF Ultra (rapid, sensitivity ~85–95% smear+, 50–70% smear-), culture gold standard. Treatment: 2HRZE/4HR (6 months total) for drug-susceptible. MDR-TB: BPaLM (6 months) or 9–12 month regimen. TB/HIV: ART timing by CD4. Latent TB: treat high-risk.

Learning Objectives

  • Diagnose pulmonary TB: symptoms, CXR, GeneXpert, culture, LPA, sequencing
  • Diagnose extrapulmonary TB: site-specific approaches, GeneXpert on non-respiratory samples
  • Initiate standard 6-month regimen (2HRZE/4HR) with correct doses and monitoring
  • Manage drug-resistant TB (RR/MDR/XDR): BPaLM, longer regimens, monitoring
  • Manage TB/HIV coinfection: ART timing, IRIS, drug interactions
  • Diagnose and treat latent TB infection (LTBI)
  • Recognise and manage adverse drug reactions (hepatotoxicity, neuropathy, optic neuritis, hyperuricaemia)
  • Apply infection control (airborne precautions, UV, ventilation)

Epidemiology & Transmission

AspectDetail
Global burden~10.6M incident cases/year, 1.3M deaths (2022); leading infectious killer
TransmissionAirborne droplet nuclei (1–5μm) from sputum-positive pulmonary TB
InfectivitySmear-positive > smear-negative > extrapulmonary (non-pulmonary rarely infectious)
Risk factorsHIV (20–30×), diabetes (2–3×), malnutrition, silicosis, TNF-α inhibitors, steroids, transplant, CKD, alcohol, smoking, prisoners, healthcare workers
Latent TB (LTBI)~25% world population infected; 5–10% lifetime reactivation risk (↑ with HIV, TNF-α inhibitors)

Pathogenesis

  1. Inhalation → alveolar macrophages
  2. Primary infection → Ghon focus (subpleural) + hilar lymphadenopathy = primary complex
  3. Containment → caseous granuloma (latent TB) OR progression → primary progressive TB
  4. Reactivation (post-primary) → apical/posterior upper lobe / superior lower lobe (high O₂ tension)
  5. Haematogenous spread → miliary TB, extrapulmonary sites (meninges, bone, kidney, etc.)

Clinical Features

Pulmonary TB

SymptomFrequency
Cough >2 weeks90%+
Fever (often low-grade, evening)70–80%
Night sweats50–70%
Weight loss / anorexia70–90%
Haemoptysis20–30%
Chest pain (pleuritic)20–30%
DyspnoeaIf extensive disease/effusion

Extrapulmonary TB (EPTB) — Common Sites

SiteFeatures
Lymph nodes (cervical)Painless, matted, firm, rubbery; may suppurate (cold abscess)
PleuralExudative effusion, lymphocytic, high ADA, often paucibacillary
Meninges (TBM)Subacute, basilar exudates, cranial nerves, hydrocephalus — see TBM note
AbdominalAscites (high protein, lymphocytic), peritoneal nodules, ileocaecal mass, strictures
GenitourinarySterile pyuria, renal calculi, strictures, epididymal masses, infertility
Bone/Joint (Pott's spine)Thoracic/lumbar spine, gibbus, paraspinal abscess, kyphosis, neuro deficit
PericardialEffusion, constriction, tamponade
MiliaryDiffuse 1–3mm nodules, high fever, hepatosplenomegaly, pancytopenia, choroidal tubercles

Diagnosis

Pulmonary TB

TestSensitivitySpecificityRole
GeneXpert MTB/RIF Ultra (sputum)85–95% smear+; 50–70% smear->98%First-line rapid test; detects RIF resistance
Smear microscopy (ZN/fluorescence)40–60% (culture+)HighRapid, cheap; cannot distinguish MTBC/NTM
Culture (MGIT liquid)Gold standard (100%)100%DST, species ID; 2–4 weeks
LPA (Line Probe Assay)~95% on culture; ~85% on smear+ sputumHighRapid DST (RIF, INH, FQ, SLI)
Whole Genome SequencingDefinitiveDefinitiveComprehensive DST, transmission mapping
CXRVariableLowScreening, supportive; upper lobe cavitation, miliary, effusion
LAM Ag (urine)Low (CD4<100)ModerateHIV advanced disease only

Extrapulmonary TB

SiteBest Diagnostic Approach
Lymph nodeFNAC (granulomas, AFB), GeneXpert on aspirate, excisional biopsy
Pleural effusionADA >40 U/L (exudate, lymphocytic); GeneXpert on fluid (low yield); biopsy
MeningesCSF GeneXpert, AFB, culture, ADA — see TBM note
AbdominalAscitic ADA, GeneXpert, laparoscopy/biopsy, CT
GenitourinaryUrine GeneXpert ×3 (early morning), IVU/CT, biopsy
Bone/JointMRI (gold standard), CT-guided biopsy, GeneXpert on pus
PericardialEcho, pericardial fluid ADA/GeneXpert, biopsy
MiliaryCXR (miliary pattern), CXR, blood culture, liver biopsy, choroidal tubercles
[!tip]
GeneXpert on non-respiratory samples: sensitivity lower than sputum but still valuable. Always send for culture + DST. ADA supportive in lymphocytic exudates (pleural, pericardial, ascitic).

Standard Treatment — Drug-Susceptible TB (6 Months)

Regimen

PhaseDurationDrugsDoses (Daily)
Intensive2 monthsHRZEH 5mg/kg (4–6), R 10mg/kg (8–12), Z 25mg/kg (20–30), E 15mg/kg (15–20)
Continuation4 monthsHRSame H, R doses
[!important]
Total 6 months (2HRZE/4HR). Daily dosing preferred. Intermittent (3×/week) ONLY in continuation phase if DOT assured. Pyridoxine 25–50mg/day with INH. Ethambutol: baseline visual acuity + colour vision; monthly monitoring.

Drug Doses (Daily, Weight-Based)

DrugDose (mg/kg)Max DoseKey Toxicity
Isoniazid (H)5 (4–6)300mgPeripheral neuropathy, hepatotoxicity
Rifampicin (R)10 (8–12)600mgHepatotoxicity, orange secretions, drug interactions (CYP450 induction)
Pyrazinamide (Z)25 (20–30)2gHepatotoxicity, hyperuricaemia/gout, GI upset
Ethambutol (E)15 (15–20)1.2gOptic neuritis (colour vision, acuity)
Pyridoxine—25–50mg/dayPrevents INH neuropathy

Monitoring

ParameterSchedule
Sputum smear/cultureEnd of intensive (2m), end of treatment (6m); if +ve at 2m → extend intensive to 3m
LFTs (ALT, AST, bilirubin)Baseline, 2w, 4w, 8w, then monthly (more if abnormal)
Visual acuity + colour vision (E)Baseline, monthly
Uric acid (Z)Baseline, if symptomatic
Neuropathy screen (H)Each visit
WeightMonthly

Treatment Response Criteria

TimepointExpected ResponseAction if Not Met
2 monthsSmear/culture negativeExtend intensive phase to 3 months; review adherence, DST
5 monthsCulture negativeExtend continuation phase to 7 months (total 9m)
6 monthsClinical/radiological cureDeclare treatment success
[!key]
Sputum conversion at 2 months = best predictor of cure. Failure = smear/culture +ve at 5m or later.

Drug-Resistant TB

Definitions

TypeDefinition
RR-TBRifampicin-resistant (with or without other resistance)
MDR-TBResistant to Rifampicin + Isoniazid
Pre-XDR-TBMDR + resistant to any fluoroquinolone
XDR-TBMDR + resistant to fluoroquinolone + at least one Group A drug (bedaquiline, linezolid)

WHO 2022 Regimens (Preferred)

RegimenCompositionDurationIndication
BPaLMBedaquiline + Pretomanid + Linezolid + Moxifloxacin6 monthsPreferred for RR/MDR-TB (no FQ resistance)
BPaLBedaquiline + Pretomanid + Linezolid6 monthsMDR-TB + FQ resistance (or intolerance)
9–12 month all-oralBedaquiline + Linezolid + Levofloxacin/Moxifloxacin + Clofazimine + Cycloserine/Terizidone + Pyrazinamide + Ethambutol9–12 monthsIf BPaLM contraindicated
[!important]
BPaLM = 6 months, all-oral, high success (~90%). Linezolid 600mg/day (reduce to 300mg if toxicity); monitor CBC, neuropathy, optic neuritis. Bedaquiline: ECG monitoring (QTc). Pretomanid: teratogenic (contraception mandatory).

TB / HIV Co-infection

CD4 CountART Timing
<50 cells/μLWithin 2 weeks of TB treatment start
50–100 cells/μLWithin 2–8 weeks
>100 cells/μLWithin 8 weeks (can delay up to 12w if logistical)
[!important]
IRIS risk: highest with low CD4, early ART. Paradoxical TB-IRIS: worsening TB symptoms/signs 2–12w post-ART; Prednisolone 1.5mg/kg/day taper 2w if moderate-severe.
Rifampicin + ART interactions: Dolutegravir 50mg BD (double dose); Efavirenz 600mg OK; Avoid PIs (rifampicin reduces levels); Nevirapine avoid.

TB/HIV Key Points

  • TB treatment duration SAME (6 months) for drug-susceptible TB
  • Cotrimoxazole prophylaxis if CD4<350 or WHO stage 3/4
  • Pyridoxine mandatory (INH neuropathy ↑ with HIV)
  • GeneXpert sensitivity higher in HIV (higher bacillary load)

Latent TB Infection (LTBI)

Who to Test & Treat

High-Risk GroupsTestTreat if Positive
HIV+IGRA/TSTYes (regardless of BCG)
TNF-α inhibitor candidatesIGRA/TSTYes
Transplant candidates/recipientsIGRA/TSTYes
SilicosisIGRA/TSTYes
Close contacts of smear+ TBIGRA/TSTYes (especially <5y, HIV+)
Healthcare workers (high TB burden)Serial IGRA/TSTConsider
Prisoners, homeless, IVDUIGRA/TSTConsider

LTBI Regimens

RegimenDurationNotes
3HP (Isoniazid 15mg/kg + Rifapentine 15mg/kg) weekly ×12 weeks3 monthsPreferred (high completion, less hepatotoxicity); DOT
3HR (Isoniazid + Rifampicin daily)3 monthsAlternative
4R (Rifampicin daily)4 monthsPreferred if INH contraindicated
6H/9H (Isoniazid daily)6/9 monthsOlder regimens; 9H if high risk
[!tip]
Exclude active TB BEFORE LTBI treatment (symptom screen, CXR). IGRA preferred over TST if BCG vaccinated.

Adverse Drug Reactions — Major

DrugReactionManagement
IsoniazidPeripheral neuropathyPyridoxine 25–50mg/day prophylaxis; if established → pyridoxine 100mg/day
HepatotoxicitySee hepatotoxicity algorithm
Seizures (rare)Pyridoxine IV
RifampicinHepatotoxicitySee hepatotoxicity algorithm
Drug interactions (CYP450 induction)Adjust co-medications (ART, anticoagulants, contraceptives, etc.)
Flu-like syndrome, thrombocytopeniaUsually transient
PyrazinamideHepatotoxicitySee hepatotoxicity algorithm
Hyperuricaemia / goutAllopurinol if symptomatic; usually asymptomatic
GI intoleranceTake with food
EthambutolOptic neuritis (reversible if caught early)Baseline + monthly acuity/colour vision; STOP if deterioration
BedaquilineQTc prolongationECG baseline, 2w, 4w, 8w, 12w; avoid with other QTc drugs
LinezolidMyelosuppression (anaemia, thrombocytopenia)CBC weekly ×4, then 2w; hold if Hb<8, plt<50
Peripheral/optic neuropathyMonitor; reduce to 300mg/day if toxicity
ClofazimineSkin discolouration (pink-brown), GI intoleranceReversible; counseling

Hepatotoxicity Algorithm (ANTI-TB Drug-Induced Liver Injury - ATB-DILI)

ALT/ASTBilirubinAction
<3×ULNNormalContinue, monitor weekly
3–5×ULNNormalContinue, monitor 2×/week; if symptomatic → hold
>5×ULNOR >3×ULN + symptomsHOLD all hepatotoxic drugs (H, R, Z); continue E + streptomycin/fluoroquinolone if needed
ReintroductionAfter ALT <2×ULNSequential: R → H → Z (2–3 day intervals); monitor LFTs
[!warning]
Rifampicin induces CYP450 → reduces levels of ART (PIs, NNRTIs), warfarin, OCP, steroids, etc. Dolutegravir 50mg BD with RIF. Bedaquiline + linezolid + clofazimine + cycloserine for MDR if needed.

Extrapulmonary TB — Treatment Duration

SiteDurationNotes
Pulmonary6 months (2HRZE/4HR)Standard
Lymph node6 monthsSame; longer if extensive/slow response
Pleural6 monthsSame; thoracentesis if large effusion
Meninges (TBM)12 months (2HRZE/10HR)Dexamethasone — see TBM note
Abdominal6–9 months9 months if extensive/slow response
Genitourinary6 monthsSame; surgery for strictures/calculi
Bone/Joint (Pott's spine)9–12 monthsSurgery for instability/neuro deficit
Pericardial6 months + prednisolone taper (prevent constriction)
Miliary6 months (9–12m if meningeal involvement)
Disseminated (HIV)6 monthsSame regimen; ART timing critical

Infection Control

MeasureDetail
Airborne precautionsNegative pressure room, N95/FFP2 for staff, surgical mask for patient
Ventilation≥12 air changes/hour, UVGI upper room
Sputum collectionOutdoors or well-ventilated area
Treatment adherenceDOT (directly observed therapy) — standard of care
Contact tracingHousehold contacts: symptom screen, CXR, IGRA/TST; LTBI treatment if indicated

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • TB = airborne, M. tuberculosis complex
  • Diagnosis: GeneXpert MTB/RIF Ultra first-line (rapid, RIF resistance); culture gold standard
  • Standard regimen: 2HRZE/4HR (6 months total); daily dosing; pyridoxine with INH
  • Monitoring: sputum at 2m (conversion), LFTs, vision (E), uric acid (Z), neuropathy (H)
  • MDR-TB: BPaLM (6 months) preferred; BPaL if FQ resistance; 9–12m all-oral alternative
  • TB/HIV: ART timing by CD4 (<50→2w, 50–100→2–8w, >100→8w); DTG 50mg BD with RIF
  • IRIS: prednisolone 1.5mg/kg taper if moderate-severe
  • TBM: 12 months (2HRZE/10HR) + dexamethasone
  • Pott's spine: 9–12 months; surgery for instability/neuro deficit
  • LTBI: 3HP (weekly INH+RPT ×12w) preferred; exclude active TB first
  • Hepatotoxicity: hold H/R/Z if ALT>5×ULN or >3×ULN+symptoms; reintroduce R→H→Z
  • Ethambutol: baseline + monthly vision; stop if deterioration
  • Rifampicin: CYP450 inducer — major drug interactions (DTG 50mg BD, avoid PIs)
  • Contact tracing: household contacts screen + LTBI treatment

Common Viva Questions

  1. What is the standard treatment regimen for drug-susceptible pulmonary TB? 2 months HRZE + 4 months HR = 6 months total.
  2. What is the first-line diagnostic test for TB? GeneXpert MTB/RIF Ultra (rapid, detects RIF resistance).
  3. What is the duration of TB meningitis treatment? 12 months (2HRZE/10HR) + dexamethasone.
  4. How do you monitor ethambutol toxicity? Baseline and monthly visual acuity and colour vision; stop if deterioration.
  5. When do you start ART in TB/HIV co-infection? CD4<50 → within 2 weeks; CD4 50–100 → 2–8 weeks; CD4>100 → within 8 weeks.
  6. What is the preferred regimen for MDR-TB? BPaLM (Bedaquiline + Pretomanid + Linezolid + Moxifloxacin) for 6 months.
  7. How do you manage ATB-DILI? Hold H/R/Z if ALT>5×ULN or >3×ULN+symptoms; reintroduce sequentially R→H→Z after ALT<2×ULN.
  8. What is the regimen for latent TB? 3HP (Isoniazid + Rifapentine weekly ×12 weeks) preferred.
  9. Rifampicin drug interactions? CYP450 induction — reduces ART (PIs, NNRTIs), warfarin, OCP, steroids; DTG 50mg BD with RIF.
  10. Pott's spine treatment duration? 9–12 months; surgery for instability/neurological deficit.

Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
TB treatment = 9 monthsDrug-susceptible = 6 months (2HRZE/4HR)
Ethambutol in continuation phase onlyEthambutol in INTENSIVE phase (2 months) — good CSF/penetration
Rifampicin dose 600mg fixedWeight-based 10mg/kg (max 600mg)
Pyridoxine only if neuropathyProphylactic 25–50mg/day with INH for ALL
GeneXpert replaces cultureCulture still gold standard for DST, species ID, confirmatory
MDR-TB = 20 monthsBPaLM = 6 months all-oral (WHO 2022)
ART timing same for all TB/HIVBy CD4: <50→2w, 50–100→2–8w, >100→8w
TST preferred over IGRAIGRA preferred if BCG vaccinated (no cross-reactivity)
LTBI treatment without excluding active TBMUST exclude active TB first (symptoms, CXR)
Bedaquiline safe in pregnancyPretomanid teratogenic; bedaquiline limited data — avoid BPaLM in pregnancy

Mnemonics

  • TB REGIMEN: 2 HRZE / 4 HR = 6 months
  • TB DRUGS TOXICITY: Hepato (H,R,Z), Neuro (H,E), Optic (E), Uric acid (Z), Drug interactions (R)
  • HIV TB ART: CD4 <50 = 2 WEEKS, 50–100 = 2–8 WEEKS, >100 = 8 WEEKS
  • MDR REGIMEN: BPaLM = Bedaquiline, Pretomanid, Linezolid, Moxifloxacin (6 months)
  • HEPATOTOXICITY: ALT>5×ULN or >3×ULN+symptoms → STOP H,R,Z → Reintroduce R→H→Z
  • TBM: 12 MONTHS (2HRZE/10HR) + DEXAMETHASONE
  • LATENT TB: 3HP (weekly INH+RPT ×12w) PREFERRED

Mind Map

Tuberculosis
Transmission
Airborne droplet nuclei
Smear+ most infectious
Pathogenesis
Primary -> Latent -> Reactivation (apices)
Haematogenous -> Miliary, EPTB
Diagnosis
GeneXpert Ultra (1st line, RIF resistance)
Culture (gold, DST, 2-4w)
LPA, WGS
CXR (supportive)
Pulmonary Rx
2HRZE/4HR (6m)
Daily dosing
Pyridoxine with H
Monitor: sputum 2m, LFTs, vision, uric acid
MDR-TB
BPaLM (6m) preferred
BPaL if FQ resistance
9-12m all-oral alternative
TB/HIV
ART by CD4: <50=2w, 50-100=2-8w, >100=8w
DTG 50mg BD with RIF
IRIS: prednisolone taper
EPTB Duration
TBM: 12m + Dex
Pott's: 9-12m
Pericardial: 6m + prednisolone
Others: 6m
LTBI
3HP (INH+RPT weekly x12w) preferred
Exclude active TB first
Adverse Effects
Hepato: H,R,Z (ALT>5x stop, reintroduce R->H->Z)
Optic: E (monthly vision)
Neuro: H (pyridoxine), Lzd
QTc: Bdq
Myelo: Lzd

Flowchart

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Suspected TB
↓
GeneXpert Ultra on sputum ×1-2
↓
MTB detected?
↓
Standard 2HRZE/4HR
MDR-TB workup: LPA/WGS, culture DST
Culture, LPA, clinical diagnosis
↓
Pyridoxine 25-50mg/day
BPaLM 6m (if no FQ resistance)
Empirical 2HRZE/4HR if high suspicion
↓
Monitor: sputum 2m, LFTs, vision, uric acid
ECG for Bdq, CBC for Lzd, contraception for Pa
↓
Sputum -ve at 2m?
↓
Continue HR 4m
Extend intensive to 3m, check DST

Suggested Visuals / Image Notes

  • GeneXpert cartridge and result interpretation
  • TB treatment monitoring schedule

Suggested Video References

One-Page Revision Summary

Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Standard regimen: 2HRZE/4HR (6 months)
  • GeneXpert Ultra first-line
  • MDR-TB: BPaLM 6 months
  • TB/HIV ART timing by CD4
  • TBM: 12 months + dexamethasone
  • Pyridoxine with INH
  • Ethambutol vision monitoring
  • Hepatotoxicity management (stop H/R/Z, reintroduce R→H→Z)
  • Rifampicin interactions (DTG 50mg BD)

  • Host-directed therapies (aspirin, statins, vitamin D)

My Weak Points

Interpretation: <35 = weak topic, 35-44 = acceptable but insecure, 45+ = strong exam-ready topic.

Exam Answer Modes

Long Answer Skeleton

  1. Epidemiology, transmission, pathogenesis (primary → latent → reactivation)
  2. Clinical features (pulmonary, extrapulmonary sites)
  3. Diagnosis (GeneXpert, culture, LPA, WGS, CXR, site-specific for EPTB)
  4. Standard treatment (2HRZE/4HR, doses, pyridoxine, monitoring)
  5. Drug-resistant TB (definitions, BPaLM, BPaL, 9–12m regimens, monitoring)
  6. TB/HIV (ART timing by CD4, DTG dosing, IRIS, cotrimoxazole)
  7. Extrapulmonary TB durations (TBM, Pott's, pericardial, others)
  8. Latent TB (risk groups, 3HP, exclusion of active TB)
  9. Adverse effects (hepatotoxicity algorithm, neuropathy, optic neuritis, QTc, myelosuppression)
  10. Infection control, contact tracing

Short Note Skeleton

  • TB: airborne, GeneXpert Ultra 1st line, culture gold
  • Rx: 2HRZE/4HR (6m), pyridoxine, daily dosing
  • Monitor: sputum 2m, LFTs, E-vision, uric acid
  • MDR: BPaLM 6m (Bdq+Pa+Lzd+Mfx); BPaL if FQ-R
  • TB/HIV: ART <50=2w, 50-100=2-8w, >100=8w; DTG 50mg BD; IRIS=pred taper
  • TBM: 12m (2HRZE/10HR) + Dex; Pott's: 9-12m
  • LTBI: 3HP weekly x12w preferred
  • Hepato: ALT>5x or >3x+symptoms → stop H,R,Z → R→H→Z reintro
  • RIF interactions: DTG 50mg BD, avoid PIs

Viva One-Liners

  • TB Rx: 2HRZE/4HR = 6 months
  • GeneXpert Ultra: rapid, RIF resistance detection
  • MDR-TB: BPaLM 6 months all-oral
  • TB/HIV ART: CD4<50=2w, 50-100=2-8w, >100=8w
  • TBM: 12 months + dexamethasone
  • Ethambutol: monthly vision, stop if deterioration
  • Hepatotoxicity: stop H/R/Z if ALT>5x or >3x+symptoms
  • LTBI: 3HP (INH+RPT weekly x12w)
  • Rifampicin: DTG 50mg BD, CYP450 inducer

Ward-Case Discussion Points

  • 35M, cough 3w, fever, weight loss, GeneXpert MTB+ RIF-susceptible → 2HRZE/4HR + pyridoxine, sputum at 2m, LFTs, vision
  • 28F HIV+ CD4 30, TB diagnosed → start TB Rx, ART within 2 weeks, DTG 50mg BD, cotrimoxazole, pyridoxine
  • 45M MDR-TB (RIF+INH resistant), no FQ resistance → BPaLM 6m, ECG baseline/2w/4w, CBC weekly, contraception
  • 60M, back pain, gibbus, Pott's spine → MRI, CT-guided biopsy, GeneXpert, 9-12m HRZE/HR, neurosurgical review

Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet

TB: Airborne. GeneXpert Ultra 1st line. Rx: 2HRZE/4HR (6m). Pyridoxine. Sputum 2m, LFTs, E-vision monthly. MDR: BPaLM 6m (Bdq+Pa+Lzd+Mfx). TB/HIV: ART CD4<50=2w, 50-100=2-8w, >100=8w; DTG 50mg BD w/ RIF. IRIS=pred taper. TBM: 12m (2HRZE/10HR) + Dex. Pott's: 9-12m. LTBI: 3HP weekly x12w. Hepato: ALT>5x or >3x+symptoms → stop H,R,Z → reintroduce R→H→Z. RIF: DTG 50mg BD, avoids PIs.

Summary

Tuberculosis (TB) = Mycobacterium tuberculosis complex, airborne transmission. Pulmonary TB (80%): cough >2w, fever, night sweats, weight loss, haemoptysis. Diagnosis: GeneXpert MTB/RIF Ultra (first-line, sensitivity 85–95% smear+, 50–70% smear−; detects RIF resistance), culture (MGIT) gold standard (2–4w, DST), LPA/WGS for rapid DST. Standard treatment (drug-susceptible): 2HRZE/4HR = 6 months total (daily dosing preferred). Doses: H 5mg/kg, R 10mg/kg, Z 25mg/kg, E 15mg/kg. Pyridoxine 25–50mg/day with INH. Monitoring: sputum conversion at 2m (extend intensive to 3m if +ve), LFTs (baseline, 2w, 4w, 8w, monthly), ethambutol vision monthly, uric acid (Z), neuropathy screen (H). MDR-TB (RR/MDR): BPaLM (Bedaquiline + Pretomanid + Linezolid + Moxifloxacin) 6 months preferred (if no FQ resistance); BPaL if FQ resistance; 9–12 month all-oral alternative. TB/HIV: ART timing by CD4 — <50: 2 weeks, 50–100: 2–8 weeks, >100: 8 weeks; DTG 50mg BD with rifampicin; avoid PIs; IRIS → prednisolone 1.5mg/kg taper. EPTB durations: TBM 12 months (2HRZE/10HR) + dexamethasone; Pott's spine 9–12 months; pericardial 6m + prednisolone; others 6m. LTBI: 3HP (Isoniazid + Rifapentine weekly ×12 weeks) preferred; exclude active TB first. Hepatotoxicity: ALT >5×ULN or >3×ULN + symptoms → hold H, R, Z; reintroduce sequentially R → H → Z after ALT <2×ULN. Rifampicin: potent CYP450 inducer — DTG 50mg BD, avoid PIs, adjust warfarin/OCP/steroids.

MCQs (10)

  1. Standard treatment duration for drug-susceptible pulmonary TB:

A. 4 months

B. 6 months (2HRZE/4HR)

C. 8 months

D. 9 months

E. 12 months

  1. GeneXpert MTB/RIF Ultra sensitivity for smear-positive pulmonary TB:

A. 50–60%

B. 85–95%

C. 95–99%

D. 40–50%

E. 70–80%

  1. Preferred regimen for RR/MDR-TB without fluoroquinolone resistance (WHO 2022):

A. 9–12 month all-oral

B. BPaLM (Bedaquiline + Pretomanid + Linezolid + Moxifloxacin) 6 months

C. BPaL 6 months

D. 18–20 month regimen with injectable

E. Standard 2HRZE/4HR

  1. ART initiation timing in TB/HIV with CD4 <50 cells/μL:

A. Within 8 weeks

B. Within 4 weeks

C. Within 2 weeks

D. After TB treatment completion

E. After intensive phase (2 months)

  1. Ethambutol toxicity monitoring:

A. Monthly LFTs

B. Monthly uric acid

C. Monthly visual acuity and colour vision

D. Monthly creatinine

E. Baseline only

  1. TB meningitis treatment duration:

A. 6 months

B. 9 months

C. 12 months (2HRZE/10HR)

D. 18 months

E. 24 months

  1. Hepatotoxicity threshold for holding ALL hepatotoxic drugs (H, R, Z):

A. ALT >2×ULN

B. ALT >5×ULN OR >3×ULN with symptoms

C. ALT >10×ULN

D. Any ALT elevation with symptoms

E. Bilirubin >2×ULN

  1. Reintroduction sequence after ATB-DILI:

A. H → R → Z

B. Z → H → R

C. R → H → Z (2–3 day intervals)

D. All three simultaneously

E. R → Z → H

  1. Rifampicin + Dolutegravir interaction management:

A. Standard DTG 50mg OD

B. DTG 50mg BD (double dose)

C. Switch to EFV

D. Switch to PI/r

E. Reduce rifampicin dose

  1. Preferred regimen for latent TB infection (LTBI):

A. 9H (Isoniazid daily ×9 months)

B. 3HP (Isoniazid + Rifapentine weekly ×12 weeks)

C. 6H (Isoniazid daily ×6 months)

D. 4R (Rifampicin daily ×4 months)

E. 3HR (Isoniazid + Rifampicin daily ×3 months)

SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 40-year-old man presents with 3 weeks cough, fever, weight loss. GeneXpert Ultra: MTB detected, RIF susceptible. HIV negative. Treatment?

A. 2HRZE/2HR (4 months)

B. 2HRZE/4HR (6 months) + Pyridoxine 25mg/day

C. 2HRZE/7HR (9 months)

D. 2HRZE/4HR without pyridoxine

E. 2HRZE/4HR + Streptomycin 2 months

  1. A 30-year-old woman with HIV (CD4 80) diagnosed with pulmonary TB (GeneXpert RIF-susceptible). When to start ART?

A. Immediately with TB treatment

B. After 2 weeks of TB treatment

C. After intensive phase (2 months)

D. After continuation phase (6 months)

E. Only if CD4 drops <50

  1. A patient on 2HRZE/4HR develops ALT 380 (ULN 40), bilirubin 30, nausea on week 3. Management?

A. Stop all drugs permanently

B. Hold H, R, Z; continue E; monitor LFTs 2×/week; reintroduce R→H→Z when ALT <2×ULN

C. Stop Z only, continue HRE

D. Reduce all doses by 50%

E. Switch to fluoroquinolone-based regimen

  1. A patient with MDR-TB (RIF+INH resistant, FQ susceptible). Preferred regimen?

A. BPaL 6 months

B. BPaLM 6 months

C. 9-month all-oral

D. 18-month regimen with amikacin

E. 2HRZE/4HR + Bedaquiline

  1. A 50-year-old man on TB treatment (2HRZE/4HR) develops blurred vision and red-green colour desaturation on month 2. Most likely cause?

A. Isoniazid

B. Rifampicin

C. Pyrazinamide

D. Ethambutol

E. Pyridoxine deficiency

  1. A healthcare worker with positive IGRA, normal CXR, no symptoms. BCG vaccinated in childhood. Next step?

A. Start 3HP immediately

B. Exclude active TB (symptom screen, CXR) → then 3HP

C. Start 4R

D. Repeat IGRA in 3 months

E. No treatment needed (BCG false positive)

  1. A 25-year-old woman with Pott's disease (T10-T11), paraspinal abscess, mild lower limb weakness. Treatment duration?

A. 6 months

B. 9–12 months

C. 12 months + dexamethasone

D. 18 months

E. 6 months + surgery

  1. A TB patient on rifampicin requires warfarin for atrial fibrillation. INR drops from 2.5 to 1.2 after starting TB treatment. Why?

A. Warfarin absorption reduced

B. Rifampicin induces CYP2C9/CYP3A4 → increases warfarin metabolism

C. Rifampicin displaces warfarin from protein binding

D. Pyrazinamide effect

E. Isoniazid effect

  1. A patient with TB pericarditis develops constrictive physiology on echo. Adjunct to anti-TB therapy?

A. Dexamethasone 0.4mg/kg/day ×4–6w

B. Prednisolone taper (60mg→0 over 6–8w) to prevent constriction

C. No steroids needed

D. Colchicine

E. Pericardiocentesis only

  1. BPaLM regimen monitoring:

A. ECG only

B. ECG (QTc) for bedaquiline, CBC for linezolid, contraception for pretomanid, LFTs for all

B. LFTs only

C. CBC only

D. Vision only

Flashcards

  • Q: Standard TB regimen

A: 2HRZE/4HR (6 months)

  • Q: GeneXpert Ultra sensitivity

A: 85-95% smear+, 50-70% smear-

  • Q: MDR-TB preferred regimen

A: BPaLM 6 months (Bdq+Pa+Lzd+Mfx)

  • Q: TB/HIV ART timing

A: CD4<50=2w, 50-100=2-8w, >100=8w; DTG 50mg BD

  • Q: TBM duration

A: 12 months (2HRZE/10HR) + Dexamethasone

  • Q: Pott's spine duration

A: 9-12 months

  • Q: LTBI preferred

A: 3HP (INH+RPT weekly x12w)

  • Q: Hepatotoxicity threshold

A: ALT>5x or >3x+symptoms → stop H,R,Z → reintroduce R→H→Z

  • Q: Ethambutol monitoring

A: Monthly visual acuity + colour vision

  • Q: Rifampicin interactions

A: CYP450 inducer → DTG 50mg BD, avoid PIs, adjust warfarin/OCP

  • Q: BPaLM monitoring

A: ECG (Bdq), CBC (Lzd), contraception (Pa), LFTs

  • Q: Pyridoxine

A: 25-50mg/day with INH for all

  • Q: Sputum conversion 2m

A: Best predictor of cure; if +ve extend intensive to 3m

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — Drug-susceptible pulmonary TB: 2 months intensive (HRZE) + 4 months continuation (HR) = 6 months total.
  2. B — GeneXpert MTB/RIF Ultra sensitivity: 85–95% for smear-positive, 50–70% for smear-negative pulmonary TB.
  3. B — WHO 2022: BPaLM (6 months) is preferred for RR/MDR-TB without fluoroquinolone resistance. BPaL for FQ resistance.
  4. C — CD4<50: ART within 2 weeks. CD4 50–100: 2–8 weeks. CD4>100: within 8 weeks.
  5. C — Ethambutol causes optic neuritis (reversible if caught early); monthly visual acuity and colour vision mandatory.
  6. C — TBM: 12 months total (2 months HRZE + 10 months HR) + adjunctive dexamethasone.
  7. B — ATB-DILI: hold H/R/Z if ALT >5×ULN OR ALT >3×ULN with symptoms (nausea, vomiting, jaundice).
  8. C — Reintroduce sequentially: Rifampicin → Isoniazid → Pyrazinamide (2–3 day intervals) after ALT <2×ULN.
  9. B — Rifampicin induces CYP3A4/CYP2C9 → reduces DTG levels; DTG 50mg BD (double dose) compensates.
  10. B — 3HP (Isoniazid 15mg/kg + Rifapentine 15mg/kg weekly ×12 weeks) = preferred LTBI regimen (high completion, less hepatotoxicity).

SBAs

  1. B — Standard 6-month regimen (2HRZE/4HR) + pyridoxine 25–50mg/day for all patients on INH.
  2. B — TB/HIV CD4 80: start ART after 2 weeks of TB treatment (CD4 50–100 = 2–8 weeks).
  3. B — ALT 380 = 9.5×ULN (>5×ULN); hold H/R/Z, continue E; reintroduce R→H→Z after ALT<2×ULN.
  4. B — MDR-TB with FQ susceptibility: BPaLM 6 months preferred.
  5. D — Ethambutol = optic neuritis (blurred vision, colour desaturation, central scotoma). Stop immediately if detected.
  6. B — LTBI: MUST exclude active TB first (symptoms, CXR), then treat. 3HP preferred. IGRA not false positive from BCG.
  7. B — Pott's spine (bone/joint TB): 9–12 months treatment. Surgery for instability/neurological deficit.
  8. B — Rifampicin induces CYP2C9 (warfarin metabolism) → INR drops; increase warfarin dose, monitor INR closely.
  9. B — TB pericarditis: prednisolone taper (60mg→0 over 6–8 weeks) reduces risk of constrictive pericarditis.
  10. B — BPaLM: ECG for bedaquiline (QTc), CBC for linezolid (myelosuppression), contraception for pretomanid (teratogenic), LFTs for all.

davidson_chapter: Chapter 13: Infectious disease

topic_category: Central Nervous System Infections Domain

status: full-fcps-mrcp-topic-note

Tuberculous Meningitis

Related: [[Tuberculosis (Pulmonary and Extrapulmonary)]], [[Acute Bacterial Meningitis]], [[Viral Encephalitis and Meningitis]], [[HIV-Associated Opportunistic Infections]]

[!important]
Tuberculous meningitis (TBM) is the most severe form of TB — mortality 20–40% even with treatment. Subacute onset (1–4 weeks), basilar exudates, cranial nerve palsies, hydrocephalus. Dexamethasone reduces mortality. GeneXpert on CSF has sensitivity ~50–80%. Treat 12 months (2HRZE/10HR).

Learning Objectives

  • Recognise subacute clinical presentation: headache, fever, AMS, cranial nerve palsies
  • Interpret CSF: lymphocytic pleocytosis, very low glucose, high protein, opening pressure ↑
  • Use GeneXpert MTB/RIF Ultra on CSF (rapid, moderate sensitivity)
  • Initiate empirical anti-TB + dexamethasone immediately if high suspicion
  • Manage hydrocephalus (VP shunt), stroke (vasculitis), raised ICP
  • Understand TB/HIV co-management and IRIS in TBM

Definition

Tuberculous meningitis = Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection of the meninges. Subacute (days–weeks). Basilar exudates → cranial nerve palsies, hydrocephalus, vasculitis → stroke.

Pathogenesis

  1. Primary TB → haematogenous spread → Rich focus (subpial/ependymal granuloma in brain/spine)
  2. Rupture of Rich focus into subarachnoid space → meningeal inflammation
  3. Basilar exudate (thick, gelatinous) → entrapment of cranial nerves (III, VI, VII), vessels (vasculitis), CSF pathways (hydrocephalus)

Clinical Features

StageFeatures
Prodrome (1–3w)Low-grade fever, malaise, headache, personality change, irritability
Meningeal (1–2w)Severe headache, vomiting, neck stiffness, photophobia, fever
Neurological (late)Cranial nerve palsies (III → ptosis, dilated pupil; VI → diplopia; VII → facial weakness), AMS (confusion → coma), focal deficits (hemiparesis from vasculitis), seizures
ComplicationsHydrocephalus (communicating → non-communicating), stroke (MCA territory, basal ganglia), SIADH, hyponatraemia

Normal Values / Important Cut-offs

ParameterTBM CSFBacterial MeningitisViral Meningitis
Opening pressure↑↑ (often >30 cmH₂O)↑↑Normal/mild ↑
WBC50–500 (lymphocytes)Neutrophils ↑↑↑Lymphocytes 10–500
Protein↑↑ (100–500 mg/dL, often >200)↑↑Mild ↑
Glucose (CSF/serum)↓↓ (<0.5, often <2.2 mmol/L or <40 mg/dL)↓↓ (<0.4)Normal (>0.6)
GeneXpert MTB/RIF Ultra50–80% sensitivityN/AN/A
AFB smear10–20%N/AN/A
Culture50–70% (4–8 weeks)N/AN/A
ADA>10 U/L (supportive)N/AN/A
[!tip]
CSF glucose <2.2 mmol/L (40 mg/dL) or ratio <0.5 = TBM / bacterial / fungal. Lymphocytic pleocytosis + very low glucose + high protein + high pressure = classic TBM triad.

Approach / Algorithm

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Subacute meningitis: headache 1-4w + fever + AMS/cranial nerve palsies
↓
LP if safe (contraindications: papilloedema, GCS<10, focal deficit)
↓
CSF: opening pressure, cell count, protein, glucose, AFB, GeneXpert, culture
↓
CT/MRI brain with contrast: basal exudates, hydrocephalus, infarcts, enhancement
↓
Empirical anti-TB + Dexamethasone IMMEDIATELY if high suspicion
↓
GeneXpert +ve → confirm; GeneXpert -ve but high suspicion → continue Rx, await culture
↓
Monitor: ICP, hydrocephalus (serial imaging), stroke, hyponatraemia, drug toxicity
↓
Duration: 12 months total (2HRZE/10HR)

Investigations

TestUtility
CSF GeneXpert MTB/RIF UltraRapid (2h), sensitivity 50–80%, specificity >98%; detects RIF resistance
CSF AFB smearLow sensitivity (10–20%); >1 smear increases yield
CSF culture (MGIT)Gold standard (50–70%); 2–4 weeks; allows DST
CSF LPA / WGSRapid DST if culture +ve
CSF ADA>10 U/L supportive (not diagnostic); high in lymphocytic exudates
CT/MRI brain (contrast)Basilar exudates (meningeal enhancement), hydrocephalus, infarcts (vasculitis), tuberculomas
Chest X-ray / CTActive pulmonary TB in 30–50%
IGRA / TSTSupportive; not diagnostic for active TBM
HIV testMandatory (co-infection changes Rx duration, ART timing, IRIS risk)

Treatment

Standard Regimen (12 Months Total)

PhaseDurationDrugsDoses
Intensive2 monthsHRZE (Rifampicin, Isoniazid, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol)R 10mg/kg, H 5mg/kg, Z 25mg/kg, E 15mg/kg
Continuation10 monthsHR (Rifampicin, Isoniazid)Same doses
[!important]
Total 12 months (not 6 months like pulmonary TB). Ethambutol essential in intensive phase (good CSF penetration). Pyridoxine 25mg/day mandatory with INH.

Adjunctive Dexamethasone — MORTALITY BENEFIT

PopulationDoseDuration
AdultsDexamethasone 0.3–0.4mg/kg/day IV (max 24mg/day)4–6 weeks taper (reduce every 1–2w)
ChildrenDexamethasone 0.6mg/kg/day IV (max 30mg/day)4–6 weeks taper
[!warning]
Start dexamethasone WITH or BEFORE first anti-TB dose. Reduces mortality by ~30%. Monitor glucose, GI bleed, psychiatric effects. Taper slowly to avoid rebound inflammation.

Drug Penetration into CSF (Inflamed Meninges)

DrugCSF PenetrationNotes
IsoniazidExcellent (100%)Core drug
PyrazinamideExcellent (100%)Core drug
EthambutolGood (50–70%)Include in intensive phase
RifampicinPoor (10–20%)High dose (10mg/kg) compensates; protein binding
StreptomycinPoorAvoid (otalgia, no CSF penetration)
Fluoroquinolones (Mfx/Lfx)Good (50–70%)MDR-TBM regimens
BedaquilinePoorNot for TBM
LinezolidGood (>80%)MDR-TBM if needed

HIV Co-infection

AspectManagement
ART timingDelay 8–12 weeks after starting TB Rx (IRIS risk high in TBM)
DexamethasoneStill indicated; monitor closely
Rifampicin + ARTDolutegravir 50mg BD (double dose); EFV 600mg OK; avoid PIs
IRISParadoxical worsening 2–12w post-ART; prednisolone 1.5mg/kg taper if moderate-severe

Complications & Management

ComplicationManagement
HydrocephalusVP shunt if symptomatic (GCS declining, papilloedema, imaging progression); EVD temporising
Vasculitis / StrokeAspirin 75–150mg/day (some evidence); anticoagulation controversial; continue anti-TB + dex
Raised ICPHead elevation, mannitol/hypertonic saline, hyperventilation (temp), dexamethasone
SIADH / HyponatraemiaFluid restriction, hypertonic saline if symptomatic; check cortisol (adrenal TB)
Cranial nerve palsiesUsually improve with treatment; III/VI most common
TuberculomasSame anti-TB regimen; surgery if mass effect/refractory
Drug-induced hepatotoxicityLFTs baseline, 2w, monthly; hold/reintroduce per guidelines

Red Flags / Emergencies

  • GCS declining → hydrocephalus, stroke, raised ICP → urgent imaging, VP shunt/EVD
  • Focal deficit / seizure → stroke (vasculitis) → aspirin, continue dex, imaging
  • Severe hyponatraemia (Na⁺<120) → hypertonic saline, fluid restriction, check cortisol
  • Drug-induced liver injury (ALT>5×ULN or >3×ULN+symptoms) → hold hepatotoxic drugs
  • Adrenal insufficiency (TB adrenalitis) → hydrocortisone replacement + fludrocortisone

Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Bacterial meningitisAcute (hours–days), neutrophilic CSF, very low glucose, Gram stain +ve
Viral meningitisAcute, normal glucose, self-limiting, no cranial nerves/hydrocephalus
Fungal (Cryptococcal)Immunocompromised, ↑↑ opening pressure, India ink/CrAg +ve
Carcinomatous meningitisMalignancy known; CSF malignant cells, low glucose, high protein
Sarcoid / Behçet / autoimmuneSystemic features; CSF lymphocytic, low glucose possible; no AFB/GeneXpert
Partially treated bacterialHistory of antibiotics; mixed CSF picture

Special Situations

SituationAdjustment
PregnancyStandard 2HRZE/10HR safe; avoid streptomycin, fluoroquinolones, bedaquiline; pyridoxine mandatory; dexamethasone OK
Renal impairmentH, R, Z dose adjust; E contraindicated if CrCl<30; moxifloxacin/levofloxacin OK
Hepatic impairmentAvoid Z if severe; monitor LFTs weekly; R/H/E caution
MDR-TBMExpert consultation; fluoroquinolone + linezolid + clofazimine + cycloserine + bedaquiline (poor CNS) + high-dose INH; duration 18–24m
ChildrenWeight-based dosing; dexamethasone 0.6mg/kg/day; VP shunt threshold lower; monitor growth, development

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • TBM = subacute (1–4w), basilar exudates, cranial nerves III/VI/VII, hydrocephalus, vasculitis → stroke
  • CSF: lymphocytic, very low glucose (<2.2 mmol/L, ratio <0.5), high protein (>200), high pressure
  • GeneXpert on CSF: sensitivity 50–80%, specificity >98%, detects RIF resistance in 2h
  • Treatment: 12 months (2HRZE/10HR) — NOT 6 months
  • Dexamethasone 0.3–0.4mg/kg/day ×4–6w taper: MORTALITY BENEFIT — start WITH/BEFORE anti-TB
  • Ethambutol essential in intensive phase (good CSF penetration); pyridoxine mandatory
  • HIV: delay ART 8–12w (IRIS risk high); DTG 50mg BD with rifampicin
  • Hydrocephalus = VP shunt if symptomatic; stroke = aspirin consideration
  • MDR-TBM: prolonged regimen (18–24m), fluoroquinolone + linezolid + clofazimine + cycloserine

Common Viva Questions

  1. What is the duration of treatment for tuberculous meningitis? 12 months (2 months HRZE + 10 months HR).
  2. What is the role of dexamethasone in TBM? Reduces mortality by ~30%; 0.3–0.4mg/kg/day ×4–6w taper; start with/ before anti-TB.
  3. What is the CSF profile in TBM? Lymphocytic pleocytosis (50–500), very low glucose (<2.2 mmol/L, ratio <0.5), high protein (>100, often >200), high opening pressure.
  4. What is the sensitivity of GeneXpert on CSF for TBM? 50–80% (moderate); specificity >98%.
  5. When do you start ART in HIV/TBM co-infection? Delay 8–12 weeks after starting TB treatment (high IRIS risk).
  6. Which anti-TB drugs have good CSF penetration? INH, PZA, EMB, fluoroquinolones, linezolid. Rifampicin has poor penetration (compensated by high dose).
  7. How do you manage hydrocephalus in TBM? VP shunt if symptomatic (declining GCS, papilloedema, progressive imaging).

Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
TBM duration = 6 months like pulmonary TB12 months (2HRZE/10HR) — continuation phase 10 months
Dexamethasone optional in TBMStrong mortality benefit — standard of care
GeneXpert negative = rule out TBMSensitivity only 50–80% — clinical suspicion + culture if -ve
Rifampicin penetrates CSF wellPoor (10–20%) — high dose 10mg/kg compensates
Streptomycin for TBMNo CSF penetration + ototoxicity — contraindicated
ART timing same as pulmonary TB/HIVTBM: delay 8–12w (pulmonary: CD4<50 → 2w)
VP shunt never neededSymptomatic hydrocephalus → VP shunt (EVD temporising)
Aspirin contraindicated in TBM strokeAspirin 75–150mg/day may reduce infarcts (some evidence)

Mnemonics

  • TBM CSF: Lymphocytes, Over 200 protein, Water (glucose) low, Pressure high = LOW P
  • TBM RX: 12 MONTHS = 2 HRZE / 10 HR (not 6 months)
  • DEX TBM: 0.3–0.4mg/kg/day IV ×4–6w taper — START WITH ANTI-TB
  • TBM COMPLICATIONS: Hydrocephalus (VP shunt), Stroke (vasculitis, aspirin), Cranial nerves (III, VI, VII), SIADH, ICPs raised
  • HIV TBM ART: DELAY 8–12 WEEKS (IRIS risk)

Mind Map

Tuberculous Meningitis

Rich focus rupture
Basilar exudate
Clinical

Headache, fever, AMS
Cranial nerves (III, VI, VII)

Flowchart

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Subacute meningitis + cranial nerves + hydrocephalus
↓
LP: lymphocytic, low glucose, high protein, high pressure
↓
GeneXpert CSF + CT/MRI brain
↓
Empirical 2HRZE/10HR + Dexamethasone 0.3-0.4mg/kg/day IMMEDIATELY

Suggested Visuals / Image Notes

  • MRI: basal exudates (meningeal enhancement), hydrocephalus, infarcts (basal ganglia, MCA), tuberculomas
  • CSF findings comparison table
  • Dexamethasone taper schedule
  • VP shunt indication algorithm

Suggested Video References

  • WHO TB meningitis guidelines
  • TBM case presentations (Lancet Neurology)
  • Hydrocephalus management in TBM
  • IRIS in TB/HIV

One-Page Revision Summary

24-Hour Recall Prompts

7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • TBM = subacute, basilar exudates, cranial nerves, hydrocephalus
  • CSF: lymphocytic, very low glucose, high protein, high pressure
  • GeneXpert CSF sensitivity 50–80%
  • Treatment 12 months (2HRZE/10HR)
  • Dexamethasone 0.3–0.4mg/kg/day ×4–6w taper WITH/BEFORE anti-TB
  • HIV: delay ART 8–12w
  • Hydrocephalus → VP shunt if symptomatic

Should Know

  • Drug CSF penetration differences
  • Rifampicin poor penetration → high dose
  • Stroke/vasculitis → aspirin consideration
  • MDR-TBM prolonged regimen
  • Adrenal insufficiency check if hyponatraemia
  • Pyridoxine mandatory

Nice to Know

  • Tuberculomas management
  • Pediatric dosing differences
  • Newer MDR-TBM regimens (bedaquiline poor CNS)
  • Role of host-directed therapies (aspirin, statins)

My Weak Points

  • [ ] Aspirin evidence in TBM vasculitis

  • [ ] Pediatric dexamethasone dose

Self-Test Scorecard

  • Understanding: /10
  • Recall: /10
  • MCQ Performance: /10
  • SBA Performance: /10
  • Viva Confidence: /10
  • Total: /50
[!tip]
Interpretation: <35 = weak topic, 35-44 = acceptable but insecure, 45+ = strong exam-ready topic.

Exam Answer Modes

Long Answer Skeleton

  1. Pathogenesis: haematogenous spread, Rich focus, basilar exudate
  2. Clinical: subacute stages, cranial nerves, hydrocephalus, stroke
  3. CSF: lymphocytic, low glucose, high protein, high pressure; GeneXpert, culture
  4. Imaging: basal exudates, hydrocephalus, infarcts, tuberculomas
  5. Treatment: 12 months (2HRZE/10HR), drug CSF penetration
  6. Dexamethasone: dose, timing, taper, mortality benefit
  7. HIV co-infection: ART delay 8–12w, DTG BD, IRIS
  8. Complications: hydrocephalus (VP shunt), stroke (aspirin), hyponatraemia, hepatotoxicity
  9. MDR-TBM: principles, prolonged duration

Short Note Skeleton

  • Subacute 1-4w: headache, fever, AMS, CN palsies (III, VI, VII), hydrocephalus
  • CSF: lymphocytic, glucose↓↓ (<2.2, ratio<0.5), protein↑↑ (>200), pressure↑
  • GeneXpert CSF: 50-80% sens, >98% spec
  • Rx: 2HRZE/10HR (12m); Dex 0.3-0.4mg/kg/day IV ×4-6w taper WITH/BEFORE anti-TB
  • HIV: ART delay 8-12w; DTG 50mg BD w/ RIF
  • Complications: Hydrocephalus→VP shunt; Stroke→aspirin; SIADH; Hepatotoxicity

Viva One-Liners

  • TBM = subacute, basilar exudates, CN III/VI/VII, hydrocephalus
  • CSF: lymphocytic, very low glucose, high protein, high pressure
  • GeneXpert CSF: 50-80% sensitivity
  • Rx: 12 months (2HRZE/10HR) — NOT 6 months
  • Dexamethasone: 0.3-0.4mg/kg/day ×4-6w taper, START WITH anti-TB
  • HIV/TBM: ART delay 8-12w (IRIS risk)
  • Hydrocephalus symptomatic → VP shunt

Ward-Case Discussion Points

  • 30M, 3w headache, fever, confusion, CN VI palsy → CSF lymphocytic/glucose 1.5/protein 300 → GeneXpert +ve → 2HRZE/10HR + dex 0.4mg/kg taper
  • HIV+ TBM, CD4 50 → start anti-TB + dex, delay ART 8-12w, DTG 50mg BD
  • TBM day 10, GCS dropping, imaging shows progressive hydrocephalus → VP shunt

Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet

TBM: Subacute 1-4w. CSF: lymphocytic, glucose↓↓ (<2.2, ratio<0.5), protein↑↑, pressure↑. GeneXpert CSF 50-80% sens. Rx: 12m (2HRZE/10HR). Dex 0.3-0.4mg/kg/day ×4-6w taper WITH/BEFORE anti-TB. HIV: ART delay 8-12w. Hydrocephalus symptomatic→VP shunt. Stroke→aspirin. Rifampicin poor CSF penetration (high dose compensates).

Summary

Tuberculous meningitis is the most severe form of TB with subacute onset (1–4 weeks), basilar exudates causing cranial nerve palsies (III, VI, VII), hydrocephalus, and vasculitic stroke. CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis (50–500), very low glucose (<2.2 mmol/L, ratio <0.5), high protein (>200), and high opening pressure. GeneXpert MTB/RIF Ultra on CSF has 50–80% sensitivity, >98% specificity. Treatment is 12 months: 2 months HRZE (intensive) → 10 months HR (continuation) — NOT 6 months. Adjunctive dexamethasone 0.3–0.4mg/kg/day IV ×4–6 weeks taper STARTED WITH or BEFORE first anti-TB dose reduces mortality by ~30%. Pyridoxine mandatory. HIV co-infection: delay ART 8–12 weeks (high IRIS risk); dolutegravir 50mg BD with rifampicin. Complications: symptomatic hydrocephalus → VP shunt; vasculitic stroke → aspirin consideration; SIADH/hyponatraemia; drug-induced hepatotoxicity. MDR-TBM requires prolonged 18–24 month regimens with fluoroquinolone, linezolid, clofazimine, cycloserine.

MCQs (10)

  1. What is the recommended total duration of treatment for tuberculous meningitis?

A. 6 months

B. 9 months

C. 12 months

D. 18 months

E. 24 months

  1. TBM CSF typically shows:

A. Neutrophils, normal glucose, normal protein

B. Lymphocytes, very low glucose (<2.2 mmol/L), high protein (>200), high pressure

C. Lymphocytes, normal glucose, low protein

D. Neutrophils, low glucose, high protein

E. Acellular, normal glucose, normal protein

  1. GeneXpert MTB/RIF Ultra on CSF for TBM has a sensitivity of approximately:

A. 90–95%

B. 50–80%

C. 30–40%

D. 10–20%

E. >95%

  1. Adjunctive dexamethasone in TBM: correct dosing in adults?

A. Dexamethasone 0.1mg/kg/day ×2w

B. Dexamethasone 0.3–0.4mg/kg/day IV ×4–6w taper

C. Dexamethasone 0.6mg/kg/day ×8w

D. Prednisolone 1mg/kg/day ×4w

E. No dexamethasone recommended

  1. When should dexamethasone be started in TBM?

A. After 2 weeks of anti-TB therapy

B. After culture confirmation

C. WITH or BEFORE the first anti-TB dose

D. Only if GCS <10

E. Only if hydrocephalus present

  1. Which anti-TB drug has POOR CSF penetration but is given at high dose to compensate?

A. Isoniazid

B. Pyrazinamide

C. Ethambutol

D. Rifampicin

E. Moxifloxacin

  1. In HIV/TBM co-infection, ART should be delayed for:

A. 2 weeks

B. 2–8 weeks

C. 8–12 weeks

D. 12–16 weeks

E. Until TB treatment completion

  1. Symptomatic hydrocephalus in TBM (declining GCS, papilloedema, progressive imaging) is managed by:

A. Increased dexamethasone

B. Mannitol only

C. VP shunt (EVD temporising)

D. Lumbar puncture drainage

E. Acetazolamide

  1. Aspirin in TBM is considered for:

A. All patients routinely

B. Vasculitic stroke / infarct prevention

C. Hydrocephalus

D. Cranial nerve palsies

E. SIADH

  1. Ethambutol in TBM intensive phase:

A. Contraindicated

B. Optional

C. Essential (good CSF penetration)

D. Only if drug resistance suspected

E. Replaced by streptomycin

SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 35-year-old man presents with 3 weeks of headache, fever, confusion, and right CN VI palsy. CSF: opening pressure 35 cmH₂O, lymphocytes 200, protein 350 mg/dL, glucose 1.2 mmol/L (serum 5.0). GeneXpert CSF positive for MTB, RIF susceptible. He is HIV-negative. Best initial treatment?

A. 2HRZE/4HR (6 months)

B. 2HRZE/10HR (12 months) + Dexamethasone 0.4mg/kg/day IV ×4–6w taper

C. 2HRZE/4HR + Dexamethasone 0.1mg/kg/day ×2w

D. 2HRZE/10HR alone (no dexamethasone)

E. 2HRZE/4HR + high-dose dexamethasone 1mg/kg/day

  1. A 28-year-old woman with HIV (CD4 80) on no ART presents with TBM (GeneXpert +ve). She starts anti-TB + dexamethasone. When should ART be initiated?

A. Immediately with anti-TB

B. After 2 weeks

C. After 8–12 weeks (delayed due to high IRIS risk in TBM)

D. After intensive phase (2 months)

E. After continuation phase (12 months)

  1. A patient on TBM treatment (2HRZE/10HR + dexamethasone) develops ALT 380 (ULN 40), bilirubin 35, asymptomatic at week 3. Best management?

A. Stop all 4 drugs permanently

B. Hold R, H, Z; continue E; monitor LFTs; reintroduce sequentially once ALT <2×ULN

C. Stop Z only, continue RHE

D. Switch to fluoroquinolone-based regimen

E. Reduce all doses by 50%

  1. Which anti-TB drug has the BEST CSF penetration (in inflamed meninges)?

A. Rifampicin

B. Isoniazid / Pyrazinamide (both ~100%)

C. Ethambutol

D. Streptomycin

E. Bedaquiline

  1. A 45-year-old man with TBM develops acute right hemiparesis on day 14 of treatment. MRI shows left MCA territory infarct. CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis, low glucose. Best addition to regimen?

A. Increase dexamethasone dose

B. Aspirin 75–150mg/day

C. Therapeutic anticoagulation

D. Add fluoroquinolone

E. Stop anti-TB drugs

  1. In MDR-TBM (RIF+INH resistant), which drug is NOT typically included in the intensive phase regimen?

A. Levofloxacin / Moxifloxacin

B. Linezolid

C. Clofazimine

D. Cycloserine / Terizidone

E. Bedaquiline (poor CNS penetration)

  1. A patient with TBM develops hyponatraemia (Na⁺ 122 mmol/L) on week 2. CSF shows no new abnormality. What is the most likely cause?

A. Cerebral salt wasting

B. SIADH (common in TBM)

C. Adrenal insufficiency (TB adrenalitis)

D. Dexamethasone effect

E. Overhydration from IV fluids

  1. Dexamethasone taper in TBM (adults): standard schedule?

A. 0.4mg/kg/day ×2w, then 0.3mg/kg/day ×2w, then 0.2mg/kg/day ×2w, then 0.1mg/kg/day ×2w, then stop

B. 0.4mg/kg/day ×1w, then 0.3mg/kg/day ×1w, then 0.2mg/kg/day ×1w, then 0.1mg/kg/day ×1w, then stop

C. 0.4mg/kg/day week 1–2, 0.3mg/kg/day week 3–4, 0.2mg/kg/day week 5, 0.1mg/kg/day week 6, then stop

D. 0.4mg/kg/day ×4w, then stop abruptly

E. 0.4mg/kg/day ×6w, then stop

  1. Which cranial nerve palsy is MOST COMMON in TBM?

A. CN I (olfactory)

B. CN II (optic)

C. CN VI (abducens) — also CN III, CN VII

D. CN VIII (vestibulocochlear)

E. CN XII (hypoglossal)

  1. Rifampicin dose in TBM intensive phase:

A. 5mg/kg

B. 10mg/kg (high dose compensates for poor CSF penetration)

C. 15mg/kg

D. 20mg/kg

E. 600mg fixed dose regardless of weight

Flashcards

  • Q: TBM treatment duration

A: 12 months (2HRZE/10HR)

  • Q: TBM CSF profile

A: Lymphocytes 50-500, glucose very low (<2.2 mmol/L, ratio<0.5), protein >200, pressure high

  • Q: GeneXpert CSF sensitivity for TBM

A: 50-80%

  • Q: Dexamethasone TBM dose

A: 0.3-0.4mg/kg/day IV ×4-6w taper; START WITH/BEFORE anti-TB

  • Q: HIV/TBM ART timing

A: Delay 8-12 weeks (high IRIS risk)

  • Q: Rifampicin CSF penetration

A: Poor (10-20%); high dose 10mg/kg compensates

  • Q: Hydrocephalus TBM management

A: VP shunt if symptomatic (GCS↓, papilloedema, progressive imaging)

  • Q: Stroke TBM management

A: Aspirin 75-150mg/day consideration; continue anti-TB + dex

  • Q: Best CSF penetration drugs

A: INH, PZA, EMB, Mfx, Lzd (good); Rifampicin (poor)

  • Q: SIADH in TBM

A: Fluid restrict, hypertonic saline if symptomatic; check cortisol (adrenal TB)

  • Q: MDR-TBM regimen

A: 18-24m; fluoroquinolone + linezolid + clofazimine + cycloserine (+ bedaquiline poor CNS)

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. C — TBM requires 12 months total (2 months intensive HRZE + 10 months continuation HR). Pulmonary TB is 6 months.
  2. B — Classic TBM CSF: lymphocytic pleocytosis, very low glucose (<2.2 mmol/L, ratio <0.5), high protein (often >200 mg/dL), high opening pressure.
  3. B — GeneXpert Ultra on CSF: sensitivity 50–80% (moderate), specificity >98%. Lower than pulmonary specimens due to paucibacillary CSF.
  4. B — Adults: dexamethasone 0.3–0.4mg/kg/day IV ×4–6 weeks taper. Children: 0.6mg/kg/day.
  5. C — Dexamethasone MUST be started WITH or BEFORE first anti-TB dose for mortality benefit.
  6. D — Rifampicin has poor CSF penetration (10–20% of serum); high dose 10mg/kg compensates. INH, PZA, EMB, fluoroquinolones, linezolid have good penetration.
  7. C — TBM/HIV: ART delayed 8–12 weeks (vs pulmonary TB: CD4<50 → 2w). High IRIS risk in TBM.
  8. C — Symptomatic hydrocephalus → VP shunt (EVD temporising). Medical management alone insufficient.
  9. B — Vasculitic stroke in TBM → aspirin 75–150mg/day may reduce infarcts (some evidence).
  10. C — Ethambutol has good CSF penetration (50–70%) and is essential in intensive phase for TBM.

SBAs

  1. B — TBM: 12 months (2HRZE/10HR) + dexamethasone 0.3–0.4mg/kg/day ×4–6w taper started with anti-TB.
  2. C — TBM/HIV: ART delayed 8–12 weeks due to high IRIS risk (basilar exudates, hydrocephalus).
  3. B — Drug-induced liver injury (ALT >5×ULN or >3×ULN + symptoms) → hold R/H/Z, continue E, reintroduce sequentially.
  4. B — INH and PZA have ~100% CSF penetration in inflamed meninges. Rifampicin poor.
  5. B — Vasculitic stroke in TBM → aspirin 75–150mg/day. Therapeutic anticoagulation not standard.
  6. E — Bedaquiline has poor CNS penetration; not standard in MDR-TBM regimens. Fluoroquinolone, linezolid, clofazimine, cycloserine are core.
  7. B — SIADH is common in TBM (basilar exudates → hypothalamic irritation). Adrenal insufficiency also possible — check cortisol.
  8. C — Standard dexamethasone taper: week 1–2: 0.4mg/kg/day; week 3–4: 0.3mg/kg/day; week 5: 0.2mg/kg/day; week 6: 0.1mg/kg/day; then stop.
  9. C — CN VI (abducens) most common, followed by CN III (oculomotor) and CN VII (facial). CN II (optic) from chiasmal compression.
  10. B — Rifampicin 10mg/kg (high dose) used in TBM to compensate for poor CSF penetration.

tags: [medicine, infectious-disease, davidson, chapter13, typhoid, paratyphoid, salmonella, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_chapter: Chapter 13: Infectious disease

topic_category: Enteric Fever Domain

status: full-fcps-mrcp-topic-note

Typhoid and Paratyphoid Fever (Enteric Fever)

Related: [[Fever in Returned Traveller and FUO]], [[Infective Diarrhoea and Food Poisoning]], [[Travel Medicine and Pre-Travel Advice]], [[Sepsis and Septic Shock]], [[Healthcare-Associated Infections (CLABSI, CAUTI, SSI, VAP)]]

[!important]
Enteric Fever = Typhoid (S. Typhi) + Paratyphoid A/B/C (S. Paratyphi). Faecal-oral transmission; chronic carriers (gallbladder). Step-ladder fever, relative bradycardia, rose spots, hepatosplenomegaly. Blood culture = gold standard (first week); Widal test = supportive. Ceftriaxone = 1st-line; Azithromycin for resistant/children; Ciprofloxacin (resistance common). Carriers: ciprofloxacin ×4–6w + cholecystectomy if persistent.

Learning Objectives

  • Recognise clinical features of enteric fever (step-ladder fever, relative bradycardia, rose spots)
  • Apply diagnostic approach (blood culture, Widal test, PCR)
  • Select empirical therapy by resistance patterns and patient factors
  • Manage complications (intestinal perforation, haemorrhage, relapse, carrier state)
  • Apply vaccination and public health measures

Epidemiology & Transmission

AspectDetails
AgentsS. Typhi (typhoid); S. Paratyphi A, B, C (paratyphoid) — S. Paratyphi A most common paratyphoid
TransmissionFaecal-oral (contaminated food/water, chronic carriers); shellfish, raw vegetables, milk
ReservoirHumans only (acute cases, convalescent carriers, chronic carriers)
Incubation7–14 days (range 3–60 days); shorter for paratyphoid
RiskTravellers to endemic areas (South Asia, SE Asia, Africa, Latin America); poor sanitation; household contacts
[!tip]
Chronic carriers: 1–4% of untreated cases; gallbladder colonisation (biofilm on gallstones); shedding in stool >1 year; "Typhoid Mary" phenomenon.

Clinical Features

PhaseFeatures
Week 1Step-ladder fever (rising daily), headache, malaise, anorexia, relative bradycardia (pulse-temperature dissociation), cough, epistaxis, abdominal discomfort
Week 2Fever plateau (39–40°C), rose spots (faint salmon macules on trunk, 2–4mm, 10–20%), hepatosplenomegaly, abdominal distension, constipation > diarrhoea, relative bradycardia
Week 3Complications (if untreated): intestinal perforation, GI haemorrhage, encephalopathy, myocarditis, abscesses
Week 4Gradual defervescence (if survived); relapse in 10–20% (1–2 weeks after stopping antibiotics)

Key Clinical Signs

SignSignificance
Step-ladder feverTemp rises incrementally each day (classic but not always present)
Relative bradycardiaPulse not commensurate with fever (Faget's sign)
Rose spotsFaint salmon macules on trunk (pathognomonic but only 10–30%); blanch on pressure
HepatosplenomegalyCommon by week 2
Abdominal tendernessRight lower quadrant / diffuse

Diagnosis

TestTimingSensitivityUtility
Blood CultureWeek 1–2 (best first week)40–80% (increases with volume/repeats)Gold standard; allows AST
Bone Marrow CultureAny time80–95%Higher yield if prior antibiotics; invasive
Stool CultureWeek 2–330–40%Late; carrier detection
Urine CultureWeek 2–3LowAncillary
Widal TestWeek 2+VariableSupportive only; O ≥1:160, H ≥1:320 suggestive; false +/− common
Typhoid PCRWeek 1–280–95%Rapid; blood/stool/urine; not universally available
Typhidot (IgM/IgG)Week 1+70–90%Rapid; false +/− in endemic areas
[!key]
Blood culture = gold standard; collect 10–20mL before antibiotics. Widal = supportive only (not diagnostic alone). Bone marrow = highest yield if antibiotics given.

Complications

ComplicationFrequencyManagement
Intestinal Perforation1–3% (ileum, terminal ileum)Emergency laparotomy + broad antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam/meropenem)
GI Haemorrhage1–5%Blood transfusion, endoscopy, supportive; surgery if massive
Relapse10–20% (1–4w post-treatment)Repeat full course antibiotics (different class if resistance)
Chronic Carrier1–4% (if untreated/partial treatment)Ciprofloxacin 750mg BD ×4–6w + cholecystectomy if gallstones
Typhoid Encephalopathy<1%ICU support; high-dose steroids controversial; antibiotics
AbscessesRareLiver, spleen, lung, bone, brain; drainage + antibiotics

Treatment

Uncomplicated Enteric Fever

DrugDoseDurationNotes
Ceftriaxone2g IV/IM OD (children 50–75mg/kg)7–10 days1st-line (incl. resistant strains); IV preferred severe
Azithromycin500mg PO OD (children 15mg/kg)7 days1st-line oral (esp. children, ciprofloxacin-resistant); safe pregnancy
Ciprofloxacin500mg PO 12h (750mg if severe)7–10 daysOnly if susceptible (resistance common: South Asia >90%); avoid children/pregnancy
Cefixime20mg/kg PO 12h (max 400mg)7–14 daysAlternative oral; less reliable for resistant strains
[!tip]
Ceftriaxone = 1st-line (IV/IM); Azithromycin = 1st-line oral. Ciprofloxacin resistance >90% in South Asia — avoid empirical use there. Azithromycin preferred for children/pregnancy.

Severe/Complicated Enteric Fever

ScenarioRegimen
Severe sepsis / shockMeropenem 1g IV 8h +/− Azithromycin
Intestinal perforationEmergency laparotomy + Meropenem 1g IV 8h
Ciprofloxacin-resistantCeftriaxone 2g IV OD OR Azithromycin 500mg OD
RelapseFull course with different class (e.g., azithromycin if ceftriaxone used initially)

Chronic Carrier

RegimenDuration
Ciprofloxacin 750mg PO 12h4–6 weeks
+ CholecystectomyIf gallstones present (90% carriers have gallstones)

Vaccination

VaccineTypeScheduleEfficacyDuration
Typhoid Conjugate Vaccine (TCV)Vi polysaccharide-protein conjugateSingle dose IM80–85%3–5 years
Vi Polysaccharide (ViPS)Vi capsular polysaccharideSingle dose IM/SC60–70%2–3 years

[!tip]
TCV = preferred (single dose, higher efficacy, longer duration, usable <2y). Ty21a: live, contraindicated immunocompromised/pregnancy. Booster every 3 years if ongoing risk.

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Enteric fever: S. Typhi (typhoid) + S. Paratyphi A/B/C; faecal-oral; human reservoir
  • Step-ladder fever, relative bradycardia, rose spots, hepatosplenomegaly
  • Blood culture = gold standard (week 1); bone marrow = highest yield
  • Widal test = supportive only (O ≥1:160, H ≥1:320)
  • Ceftriaxone 2g IV OD = 1st-line; Azithromycin 500mg OD = 1st-line oral
  • Ciprofloxacin resistance >90% South Asia — avoid empirical
  • Complications: perforation, haemorrhage, relapse (10–20%), carrier state
  • Carrier: ciprofloxacin ×4–6w + cholecystectomy if gallstones
  • Vaccine: TCV single dose (preferred); boosters 3-yearly

Common Viva Questions

  1. Gold standard diagnosis of typhoid? Blood culture (first week); bone marrow if antibiotics given.
  2. Step-ladder fever pattern? Temperature rises incrementally each day during first week.
  3. Relative bradycardia (Faget's sign)? Pulse rate disproportionately low for degree of fever.
  4. Rose spots?* Faint salmon macules on trunk, 2–4mm, blanch on pressure; 10–30% of cases.
  5. First-line treatment? Ceftriaxone 2g IV OD (7–10d); Azithromycin 500mg PO OD (7d) if oral needed.
  6. Why avoid ciprofloxacin empirically in South Asia? >90% resistance.
  7. Complications of typhoid? Intestinal perforation, GI haemorrhage, relapse (10–20%), carrier state.
  8. Chronic carrier management? Ciprofloxacin 750mg BD ×4–6w + cholecystectomy if gallstones.
  9. Best typhoid vaccine? Typhoid Conjugate Vaccine (TCV) — single dose, 80–85% efficacy, from 6 months age.
  10. Relapse vs reinfection? Relapse = same strain, 1–4w post-treatment; reinfection = new exposure.

Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
Widal test = diagnosticSupportive only; not diagnostic alone
Ciprofloxacin 1st-line everywhereResistance >90% South Asia — use ceftriaxone/azithromycin
Rose spots = pathognomonicOnly 10–30%; blanch on pressure; not always present
Relapse = treatment failureRelapse = same strain 1–4w post-treatment; reinfection = new exposure
Carrier = always symptomaticAsymptomatic; excluded from food handling
Widal positive = typhoidFalse + in malaria, typhus, other infections; false - early
Cholecystectomy always needed for carrierOnly if gallstones present (90% have them); try antibiotics first
Azithromycin not for severe typhoidIV azithromycin available; ceftriaxone preferred severe
Typhoid vaccine 100% effectiveTCV 80–85%; hygiene still essential
Paratyphoid = milder typhoidSimilar severity; S. Paratyphi A most common paratyphoid

Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet

TYPHOID: S. Typhi/Paratyphi; faecal-oral. Clinical: Step-ladder fever, relative bradycardia, rose spots, hepatosplenomegaly. Dx: Blood culture wk1 (gold); Widal supportive (O≥160, H≥320). Rx: Ceftriaxone 2g IV OD (7-10d) or Azithromycin 500mg OD (7d). Cipro resistance >90% S Asia. Complications: perforation, bleed, relapse 10-20%, carrier. Carrier: Cipro 4-6w + cholecystectomy. Vaccine: TCV single dose preferred.

Summary

Enteric fever encompasses typhoid fever (Salmonella Typhi) and paratyphoid fever (S. Paratyphi A, B, C). Transmission: faecal-oral (contaminated food/water); human reservoir (acute cases, convalescent carriers, chronic carriers with gallbladder colonisation). Incubation: 7–14 days (3–60 days). Clinical: Week 1 — step-ladder fever, headache, relative bradycardia (Faget's sign), cough; Week 2 — fever plateau, rose spots (faint salmon macules on trunk, 10–30%), hepatosplenomegaly, relative bradycardia, abdominal distension; Week 3 — complications: intestinal perforation (terminal ileum), GI haemorrhage, encephalopathy; Week 4 — defervescence; relapse 10–20% at 1–4 weeks post-treatment. Diagnosis: Blood culture (gold standard, 40–80%, best first week, 10–20mL); bone marrow culture (80–95%, if prior antibiotics); Widal test (supportive: O titre ≥1:160, H ≥1:320); PCR (rapid). Treatment: Ceftriaxone 2g IV/IM OD ×7–10d (1st-line); Azithromycin 500mg PO OD ×7d (1st-line oral); Ciprofloxacin 500mg 12h ×7–10d ONLY if susceptible (resistance >90% in South Asia). Complications: intestinal perforation (1–3%, terminal ileum, emergency laparotomy + meropenem), GI haemorrhage (1–5%), relapse (10–20%), chronic carrier (1–4%, gallbladder). Chronic carrier: ciprofloxacin 750mg BD ×4–6w + cholecystectomy if gallstones (90% have stones). Vaccination: Typhoid Conjugate Vaccine (TCV) single dose (preferred, 80–85% efficacy, from 6 months age); Vi polysaccharide (3y); Ty21a oral live (3–5y). Public health: notification, contact tracing, carrier exclusion from food handling, water/food safety.

  5. B — Children <8 years: fluoroquinolones contraindicated; azithromycin 15mg/kg OD ×7d is preferred.
  6. A — XDR typhoid (resistant to ciprofloxacin, ceftriaxone, azithromycin): carbapenem (meropenem) is only reliable option.
  7. B — Persistent stool positivity despite cipro + gallstones → cholecystectomy + continue antibiotics.
  8. B — Pregnancy = ceftriaxone IV or azithromycin PO safe; avoid ciprofloxacin, chloramphenicol, TMP-SMX.
  9. B — TCV = single dose IM, 6m+, 80–85% efficacy; preferred over ViPS (3y) and Ty21a (oral live).
  10. B — Chronic carrier with gallstones persistent after 6 weeks cipro → cholecystectomy + continue antibiotics.
