tags: [medicine, infectious-disease, davidson, chapter13, uti, urinary-tract-infection, catheter-associated, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_chapter: Chapter 13: Infectious disease

topic_category: Urinary Tract Infections Domain

status: full-fcps-mrcp-topic-note

Urinary Tract Infections (Complicated, Catheter-Associated, Pyelonephritis)

Related: [[Sepsis and Septic Shock]], [[Antimicrobial Stewardship]], [[Prosthetic Joint Infection]], [[Renal Transplant Infections]]

[!important]
UTI = significant bacteriuria + urinary symptoms. Uncomplicated cystitis: healthy non-pregnant women. Complicated UTI: structural/functional abnormality, male, pregnant, immunocompromised, catheter, recent instrumentation. CA-UTI: catheter ≥2 days + symptoms/signs. Pyelonephritis: flank pain, fever, CVA tenderness + bacteriuria. Empirical antibiotics by local resistance, severity, risk factors. Duration: cystitis 3–5d, pyelonephritis 7–14d, CA-UTI 7d (remove catheter if possible). Asymptomatic bacteriuria: treat ONLY in pregnancy, pre-urologic procedure, renal transplant (early).

Learning Objectives

  • Classify UTI: uncomplicated vs complicated, cystitis vs pyelonephritis, CA-UTI
  • Diagnose using symptoms, urinalysis, urine culture thresholds
  • Select empirical antibiotics by syndrome, local resistance, MDR risk
  • Determine appropriate treatment duration
  • Manage asymptomatic bacteriuria (when to treat vs not)
  • Recognise and manage complications: emphysematous pyelonephritis, renal abscess, sepsis
  • Apply CA-UTI prevention strategies

Classification

CategoryDefinition
Uncomplicated cystitisHealthy, non-pregnant, premenopausal women; no structural/functional abnormality
Complicated UTIANY of: male, pregnant, postmenopausal, immunocompromised, diabetes, structural abnormality (stones, obstruction, reflux), indwelling catheter, recent instrumentation, renal transplant, neurogenic bladder
PyelonephritisUTI + fever/flank pain/CVA tenderness (upper tract involvement)
CA-UTICatheter ≥2 days + symptoms/signs (fever, suprapubic pain, costovertebral angle tenderness, delirium) + ≥10³ CFU/mL from catheter specimen
Asymptomatic bacteriuria (ASB)≥10⁵ CFU/mL (clean-catch) or ≥10² CFU/mL (catheter) in ASYMPTOMATIC patient

Aetiology

SyndromeCommon Pathogens
Uncomplicated cystitisE. coli (75–90%), S. saprophyticus (5–15%), Klebsiella, Proteus
Complicated UTI / PyelonephritisE. coli (50–60%), Klebsiella, Proteus, Pseudomonas, Enterococcus, S. aureus
CA-UTIE. coli, Klebsiella, Pseudomonas, Enterococcus, Candida, Proteus, Morganella, Providencia (often polymicrobial)
Healthcare-associated / MDR riskESBL Enterobacteriaceae, Pseudomonas, Acinetobacter, Enterococcus (VRE), Serratia
[!tip]
Proteus → urease → struvite stones, alkaline urine. Pseudomonas → biofilm on catheters. Candida in catheterised/ICU patients — treat only if symptomatic + pyuria.

Diagnosis

Urinalysis

FindingSignificance
Nitrite +Specific for Enterobacteriaceae (reduces nitrate)
Leukocyte esterase +Pyuria (WBC in urine)
WBC >10/HPFPyuria
Bacteria on microscopy~10⁵ CFU/mL if ≥1 bacterium/HPF
[!warning]
Pyuria + symptoms = UTI. Pyuria alone (no symptoms) = ASB (do not treat except specific indications). Elderly: delirium may be ONLY symptom.

Urine Culture Thresholds (Significant Bacteriuria)

SpecimenThresholdNotes
Clean-catch midstream (female)≥10⁵ CFU/mL≥10² if symptomatic (high pre-test probability)
Clean-catch midstream (male)≥10⁴ CFU/mL
Straight catheter≥10² CFU/mL
Suprapubic aspiration≥10¹ CFU/mLGold standard
Indwelling catheter≥10³ CFU/mLFor CA-UTI diagnosis

Empirical Antibiotic Therapy

Uncomplicated Cystitis (Women)

AgentDoseDurationNotes
Nitrofurantoin100mg PO 12h (MR) or 50mg 6h3 days (MR: 5 days)1st line; avoid if CrCl <30, G6PD, pregnancy term
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX)160/800mg PO 12h3 days1st line if local E. coli resistance <20%
Fosfomycin3g PO single doseSingle doseAlternative; lower cure rate
Pivmecillinam400mg PO 8h3–5 daysAlternative (not in all countries)
Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin)Cipro 250mg 12h / Levo 250mg OD3 daysAvoid 1st line (resistance, tendinopathy, C. diff, aortic dissection risk)

Complicated UTI / Pyelonephritis (Outpatient / Mild Inpatient)

AgentDoseDuration
Ciprofloxacin500mg PO 12h (or 750mg XR OD)7 days (pyelo) / 5–7 days (complicated)
Levofloxacin750mg PO OD5–7 days
TMP-SMX160/800mg PO 12h7–14 days (if susceptible)
Cefpodoxime / Cefdinir200mg PO 12h / 300mg PO 12h7–14 days
IV → Oral switchCeftriaxone 1–2g IV 24h → oral fluoroquinolone/TMP-SMXWhen clinically stable

Complicated UTI / Pyelonephritis (Inpatient / Severe / MDR Risk)

ScenarioEmpirical RegimenDuration
No MDR risk, stableCeftriaxone 1–2g IV 24h7–14 days (IV→oral at stability)
MDR risk (prior abx, healthcare, travel, known colonisation)Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV 6h OR Meropenem 1g IV 8h OR Cefepime 2g IV 8h7–14 days
+ Enterococcus risk (catheter, instrumentation, abscess)Add Amoxicillin 2g IV 4h (if E. faecalis) OR Vancomycin (if VRE risk)
+ Pseudomonas riskAnti-pseudomonal β-lactam above
Sepsis / ShockMeropenem 1g IV 8h (or Pip-tazo) ± Vancomycin ± Aminoglycoside7–14 days

CA-UTI

StepAction
1. Remove/replace catheter if ≥2 days (send tip for culture if removed)
2. Empirical antibiotics (if symptomatic): Same as complicated UTI by severity
3. Duration7 days (if catheter removed); 10–14 days if catheter MUST remain
4. Catheter changeReplace catheter after 48–72h of antibiotics if remaining
[!key]
CA-UTI: REMOVE catheter if possible. If catheter must stay, change after 48–72h antibiotics. Duration 7d if removed, 10–14d if retained.

Asymptomatic Bacteriuria (ASB) — When to TREAT

PopulationTreat?
Pregnant womenYES (screen at 12–16 weeks; treat to prevent pyelonephritis)
Pre-urologic procedure with mucosal bleedingYES (pre-op)
Renal transplant (first 1–3 months)YES (some guidelines)
Neutropenic patientsControversial (some treat)
Healthy non-pregnant womenNO
Elderly / LTCF residentsNO
DiabeticsNO
Catheterised patients (chronic)NO
Spinal cord injury / neurogenic bladderNO
Pre-non-urologic surgeryNO
[!critical]
ASB treatment in non-indicated populations → ↑ antibiotic resistance, ↑ C. diff, NO clinical benefit. Pyuria in ASB does NOT indicate treatment.

Treatment Duration Summary

SyndromeDuration
Uncomplicated cystitis (women)3 days (Nitrofurantoin, TMP-SMX); Single dose (Fosfomycin)
Complicated cystitis5–7 days
Pyelonephritis (outpatient/mild)7 days (fluoroquinolone) / 10–14 days (β-lactam/TMP-SMX)
Pyelonephritis (severe/inpatient)10–14 days (IV→oral at stability)
CA-UTI (catheter removed)7 days
CA-UTI (catheter retained)10–14 days (change catheter at 48–72h)
Prostatitis (acute bacterial)4–6 weeks
Prostatitis (chronic bacterial)6–12 weeks

Complications

ComplicationFeaturesManagement
Emphysematous pyelonephritisGas in renal parenchyme/collecting system (diabetics); high mortalityEmergency nephrectomy + broad antibiotics (Pip-tazo/Meropenem)
Renal / Perinephric abscessFlank mass, persistent fever, failure to respondCT-guided drainage + antibiotics
Papillary necrosisDiabetics, sickle cell, analgesic abuse; flank pain, haematuriaSupportive, antibiotics, urology
Xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritisChronic, staghorn stones, Proteus; mass mimics tumourNephrectomy
Sepsis / Septic shockSee Sepsis noteSepsis bundle + source control
Prostatitis (acute)Fever, perineal pain, dysuria, tender prostateFluoroquinolone 4–6 weeks (prostate penetration)

MDR Pathogens in UTI

PathogenPreferred TreatmentAlternatives
ESBL EnterobacteriaceaeMeropenem 1g IV 8h (or Ertapenem 1g IV 24h if no Pseudomonas risk)Fosfomycin IV, Ceftazidime-avibactam, Plazomicin
CRECeftazidime-avibactam, Meropenem-vaborbactam, Imipenem-relebactam, Plazomicin, PolymyxinFosfomycin, Tigecycline (poor urine levels)
MDR PseudomonasCeftolozane-tazobactam, Ceftazidime-avibactam, Imipenem-relebactam, Polymyxin + high-dose meropenemAminoglycoside + β-lactam
VRE (E. faecium)Linezolid 600mg IV/PO 12h OR Daptomycin 8–10mg/kg IV OD (NOT for urine — inactivated by surfactant)Tigecycline (poor urine levels)
Enterococcus faecalis (ampicillin-R)Vancomycin OR LinezolidDaptomycin
Candida (symptomatic + pyuria)Fluconazole 200–400mg PO/IV OD (if susceptible)Echinocandin (caspofungin/micafungin) — poor urine levels except micafungin
[!tip]
Fosfomycin IV excellent for MDR UTI (broad Gram-neg + Enterococcus). Daptomycin inactivated in urine — avoid for UTI. Tigecycline poor urine levels — avoid for UTI. Echinocandins poor urine levels (except micafungin) — fluconazole preferred for Candida UTI.

CA-UTI Prevention Bundle

ComponentAction
Avoid unnecessary catheterisationUse alternatives (condom catheter, intermittent catheterisation, nappies)
Aseptic insertionSterile technique, smallest appropriate catheter
Closed drainage systemMaintain unobstructed flow; no disconnections
Catheter securementPrevent traction/urethral trauma
Maintain drainage bag below bladderPrevent reflux
Daily review of necessityRemove as soon as possible (nurse-driven protocols)
Periurethral cleaningRoutine soap/water (no antiseptic benefit)
Antibiotic prophylaxis at insertionNOT routinely recommended
Catheter materialSilver-alloy or hydrogel-coated if prolonged (>7d) anticipated

Special Situations

SituationManagement
PregnancyScreen for ASB at 12–16w; treat positive. Cystitis: Nitrofurantoin (avoid term), Cephalexin, Fosfomycin. Pyelonephritis: IV Ceftriaxone → oral cephalexin. Avoid fluoroquinolones, TMP-SMX (1st tri), Nitrofurantoin (term).
Renal transplantASB treat early (1–3m); UTI: adjust for immunosuppression interactions (fluconazole ↑ tacrolimus, TMP-SMX ↑ creatinine).
Neurogenic bladder / Spinal cordAsymptomatic bacteriuria universal — do NOT treat. Treat only if symptomatic (fever, autonomic dysreflexia, new incontinence).
ProstatitisAcute: fluoroquinolone 4–6w (prostate penetration). Chronic: fluoroquinolone/TMP-SMX 6–12w.
Urosepsis with obstructionURGENT decompression (nephrostomy/stent) + antibiotics — source control critical.

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • UTI classification: uncomplicated cystitis (healthy women) vs complicated (ALL others)
  • Diagnosis: symptoms + pyuria + culture (≥10⁵ clean-catch, ≥10² catheter, ≥10³ indwelling catheter)
  • Uncomplicated cystitis: Nitrofurantoin 3d (1st line), TMP-SMX 3d (if resistance <20%), Fosfomycin single dose
  • Complicated UTI/Pyelonephritis: Fluoroquinolone 7d (outpatient) or IV Ceftriaxone → oral
  • MDR risk: prior abx, healthcare, travel, colonisation → Pip-tazo/Meropenem/Cefepime
  • CA-UTI: catheter ≥2d + symptoms + ≥10³ CFU/mL → REMOVE catheter; 7d antibiotics if removed
  • Asymptomatic bacteriuria: TREAT only in pregnancy, pre-urologic procedure with bleeding, early renal transplant
  • Duration: cystitis 3d, pyelo 7–14d, CA-UTI 7d (removed) / 10–14d (retained)
  • ESBL: carbapenem (meropenem/ertapenem); CRE: newer β-lactamase inhibitors; VRE: linezolid (daptomycin fails in urine)
  • Emphysematous pyelonephritis: diabetics, gas on imaging → emergency nephrectomy + antibiotics
  • Prostatitis: fluoroquinolone 4–6w (penetration)

Common Viva Questions

  1. What is the urine culture threshold for CA-UTI? ≥10³ CFU/mL from indwelling catheter specimen.
  2. When do you treat asymptomatic bacteriuria? Pregnancy, pre-urologic procedure with mucosal bleeding, renal transplant (early).
  3. First-line for uncomplicated cystitis? Nitrofurantoin 100mg PO 12h ×3 days (or TMP-SMX if resistance <20%).
  4. Duration for pyelonephritis? 7 days (fluoroquinolone) or 10–14 days (β-lactam/TMP-SMX).
  5. CA-UTI management? Remove catheter if possible; antibiotics 7d if removed, 10–14d if retained; change catheter at 48–72h.
  6. ESBL UTI treatment? Carbapenem (meropenem/ertapenem).
  7. Why avoid daptomycin for VRE UTI? Inactivated by pulmonary surfactant in urine (actually — inactivated by calcium/surfactant; poor urinary penetration).
  8. Emphysematous pyelonephritis management? Emergency nephrectomy + broad antibiotics.
  9. Asymptomatic bacteriuria in elderly LTCF resident — treat? NO.
  10. Acute prostatitis treatment and duration? Fluoroquinolone 4–6 weeks (prostate penetration).

Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
Treat all asymptomatic bacteriuriaTreat ONLY: pregnancy, pre-urologic bleeding, early renal transplant
Pyuria = treatPyuria without symptoms = ASB (do not treat)
Fosfomycin 1st line for cystitisNitrofurantoin/TMP-SMX 1st line; Fosfomycin alternative (lower cure)
Fluoroquinolone 1st line for cystitisAvoid 1st line (resistance, tendinopathy, C. diff, aortic risk)
CA-UTI: treat without removing catheterREMOVE catheter if possible; change at 48–72h if retained
Daptomycin for VRE UTIDaptomycin inactivated in urine — use Linezolid
Tigecycline for MDR UTIPoor urine levels — avoid
Echinocandin for Candida UTIPoor urine levels (except micafungin) — Fluconazole 1st line
Nitrofurantoin in renal impairmentAvoid if CrCl <30 (ineffective, toxicity)
ASB in catheterised = treatChronic catheter = ASB universal; do NOT treat

Mnemonics

  • UTI CLASSIFICATION: Uncomplicated = healthy women; Complicated = Everyone Else (male, pregnant, immunocompromised, catheter, structural, instrumentation)
  • CULTURE THRESHOLDS: Clean-catch Female 10⁵; Clean-catch Male 10⁴; Straight Cath 10²; Indwelling Cath 10³; Suprabic Asp 10¹
  • CYSTITIS 1ST LINE: Nitro Furantoin 3 days; TMP-SMX 3d (if R<20%)
  • PYELO DURATION: Fluoroquinolone 7d; β-lactam/TMP-SMX 10-14d
  • ASB TREAT: Pregnancy, Pre-urologic bleeding, Renal transplant early
  • CA-UTI: Remove catheter → 7d abx; Retain → 10-14d + change at 48-72h
  • ESBL UTI: Carbapenem (Mero/Erta)
  • VRE UTI: Linezolid (Daptomycin fails in urine)
  • PROSTATITIS: Fluoroquinolone 4-6 weeks

Mind Map

Urinary Tract Infections
Classification
Uncomplicated cystitis (healthy women)
Complicated (male, pregnant, immunocompromised, catheter, structural)
Pyelonephritis (fever, flank pain, CVA tenderness)
CA-UTI (cath ≥2d + symptoms + ≥10³ CFU/mL)
ASB (asymptomatic + significant bacteriuria)
Diagnosis
Symptoms + Pyuria (nitrite, leukocyte esterase, WBC>10/HPF)
Culture thresholds by specimen type
Treatment
Uncomplicated cystitis: Nitro 3d, TMP-SMX 3d, Fosfo single
Complicated/Pyelo: FQ 7d or Ceftriaxone→oral 7-14d
MDR risk: Pip-tazo/Mero/Cefepime
CA-UTI: Remove cath → 7d; Retain → 10-14d + change cath
ASB: Treat ONLY pregnancy, pre-urologic bleeding, renal transplant early
Complications
Emphysematous pyelo (diabetics) → nephrectomy
Renal abscess → drainage + abx
Prostatitis → FQ 4-6w
MDR
ESBL → Carbapenem
CRE → CAV/MV/IR/Plazomicin/Polymyxin
VRE → Linezolid (Dapto fails)
Candida → Fluconazole (Echino poor urine)
Prevention
CA-UTI bundle: avoid, aseptic, closed, secure, daily review, remove ASAP

Flowchart

⬇ Clinical Pathway
UTI Suspected
↓
Uncomplicated Cystitis?
ASB?
↓
Nitrofurantoin 3d or TMP-SMX 3d or Fosfo single
Complicated / Pyelo?
TREAT
DO NOT TREAT
↓
FQ 7d OR Ceftriaxone IV→oral 7-14d
Pip-tazo/Mero/Cefepime IV 7-14d
REMOVE catheter if possible
↓
Removed?
↓
7d antibiotics
10-14d + change catheter at 48-72h

Suggested Visuals / Image Notes

  • UTI classification algorithm
  • Urine culture thresholds by specimen
  • CA-UTI management algorithm
  • ASB treatment indications
  • MDR UTI treatment pathways
  • Emphysematous pyelonephritis CT

Suggested Video References

  • IDSA UTI guidelines
  • CA-UTI prevention
  • MDR UTI management
  • Asymptomatic bacteriuria stewardship
  • Prostatitis diagnosis and treatment

One-Page Revision Summary

TopicKey Points
Uncomplicated cystitisHealthy women; Nitro 3d, TMP-SMX 3d, Fosfo single
Complicated UTIMale, pregnant, immunocompromised, catheter, structural, recent instrumentation
PyelonephritisFever + flank/CVA pain + bacteriuria
CA-UTICath ≥2d + symptoms + ≥10³ CFU/mL → REMOVE cath
Culture thresholdsClean-catch F 10⁵, M 10⁴; Straight cath 10²; Indwelling 10³
ASB treatPregnancy, pre-urologic bleeding, early renal transplant ONLY
DurationCystitis 3d; Pyelo 7d (FQ) / 10-14d (β-lactam); CA-UTI 7d removed / 10-14d retained
MDRESBL → Carbapenem; CRE → CAV/MV/IR; VRE → Linezolid; Candida → Fluconazole
ComplicationsEmphysematous pyelo → nephrectomy; Abscess → drainage; Prostatitis → FQ 4-6w

24-Hour Recall Prompts

  • UTI classification criteria.
  • Culture thresholds by specimen type.
  • Uncomplicated cystitis 1st line and duration.
  • Pyelonephritis outpatient vs inpatient regimen.
  • CA-UTI: remove catheter, duration.
  • ASB treatment indications (3).
  • ESBL, CRE, VRE UTI treatments.
  • Emphysematous pyelonephritis management.
  • Prostatitis duration.

7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

  • [ ] Day 1 completed
  • [ ] 24-hour recall completed
  • [ ] Day 7 revision completed
  • [ ] Day 15 revision completed
  • [ ] Day 30 revision completed

Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Uncomplicated vs complicated UTI classification
  • Culture thresholds by specimen
  • Nitrofurantoin 3d / TMP-SMX 3d = 1st line cystitis
  • Pyelonephritis: FQ 7d or Ceftriaxone→oral 7-14d
  • CA-UTI: remove catheter, 7d if removed
  • ASB: treat ONLY pregnancy, pre-urologic bleeding, renal transplant early
  • ESBL → carbapenem; VRE → linezolid
  • No daptomycin/tigecycline/echinocandin for UTI

Should Know

  • MDR risk factors for complicated UTI
  • CA-UTI prevention bundle
  • Emphysematous pyelonephritis (diabetics)
  • Prostatitis: fluoroquinolone 4–6w
  • Renal transplant UTI nuances
  • Pregnancy UTI adjustments
  • Fosfomycin IV for MDR UTI

Nice to Know

  • Novel agents (cefiderocol, sulbactam-durlobactam)
  • Vaccine prevention (ExPEC vaccines)
  • Urosepsis with obstruction algorithm
  • Chronic bacterial prostatitis workup
  • Interstitial cystitis vs UTI differentiation

My Weak Points

  • [ ] Exact fosfomycin IV dosing for MDR UTI
  • [ ] Renal transplant UTI prophylaxis guidelines
  • [ ] Cefiderocol indications
  • [ ] Asymptomatic bacteriuria in spinal cord injury
  • [ ] Novel vaginal microbiome therapies

Self-Test Scorecard

  • Understanding: /10
  • Recall: /10
  • MCQ Performance: /10
  • SBA Performance: /10
  • Viva Confidence: /10
  • Total: /50
[!tip]
Interpretation: <35 = weak topic, 35-44 = acceptable but insecure, 45+ = strong exam-ready topic.

Exam Answer Modes

Long Answer Skeleton

  1. Classification (uncomplicated, complicated, pyelonephritis, CA-UTI, ASB)
  2. Aetiology by syndrome
  3. Diagnosis (symptoms, urinalysis, culture thresholds)
  4. Empirical antibiotics by syndrome and MDR risk
  5. Treatment duration by syndrome
  6. Asymptomatic bacteriuria management
  7. CA-UTI specific management and prevention
  8. Complications (emphysematous pyelo, abscess, prostatitis)
  9. MDR pathogen management
  10. Special populations (pregnancy, transplant, neurogenic bladder)

Short Note Skeleton

  • Uncomplicated cystitis: healthy women; Nitro 3d/TMP-SMX 3d/Fosfo single
  • Complicated: everyone else; Pyelo: fever+flank/CVA
  • CA-UTI: cath≥2d+symptoms+≥10³ → remove cath
  • Thresholds: clean F 10⁵, M 10⁴; straight 10²; indwelling 10³
  • ASB treat: pregnancy, pre-urologic bleed, renal transplant early ONLY
  • Duration: cystitis 3d, pyelo 7-14d, CA-UTI 7d/10-14d
  • MDR: ESBL→Carbapenem; CRE→CAV/MV/IR; VRE→Linezolid; Candida→Fluconazole
  • No dapto/tigecycline/echinocandin for UTI

Viva One-Liners

  • Nitro 3d = 1st line cystitis
  • Pyelo: FQ 7d or Ceftriaxone→oral 10-14d
  • CA-UTI: remove cath, 7d abx
  • ASB: treat pregnancy, pre-urologic bleed, renal transplant early
  • ESBL UTI: carbapenem
  • VRE UTI: linezolid (dapto fails)
  • Prostatitis: FQ 4-6w
  • Emphysematous pyelo: nephrectomy

Ward-Case Discussion Points

  • 25F, dysuria, frequency, no fever, no comorbidities → Nitrofurantoin 100mg 12h ×3d
  • 65M, diabetes, fever 39°C, flank pain, CVA tenderness → Ceftriaxone 2g IV 24h ×2d → Ciprofloxacin 500mg 12h ×7d total
  • 80F, chronic catheter, fever, delirium, catheter 2weeks → CA-UTI: remove catheter, send tip, Pip-tazo IV → oral step-down ×7d
  • 32F pregnant 20w, routine screen: E. coli 10⁵, asymptomatic → Treat (pregnancy) — Cephalexin 500mg 12h ×7d

Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet

UTI: Uncomplicated cystitis = healthy women → Nitro 3d / TMP-SMX 3d / Fosfo single. Complicated = everyone else. Pyelonephritis = fever + flank/CVA pain. CA-UTI = cath≥2d + symptoms + ≥10³ → REMOVE cath. Thresholds: Clean F 10⁵, M 10⁴; Straight 10²; Indwelling 10³. ASB treat ONLY: pregnancy, pre-urologic bleed, renal transplant early. Duration: cystitis 3d; pyelo 7d (FQ) / 10-14d (β-lactam); CA-UTI 7d removed / 10-14d retained. MDR: ESBL→Carbapenem; CRE→CAV/MV/IR; VRE→Linezolid; Candida→Fluconazole. Emphysematous pyelo (diabetics) → nephrectomy. Prostatitis → FQ 4-6w.

Summary

Urinary tract infections (UTIs) classified by host factors and anatomy. Uncomplicated cystitis = healthy, non-pregnant, premenopausal women. Complicated UTI = male, pregnant, postmenopausal, immunocompromised, diabetes, structural abnormality, indwelling catheter, recent instrumentation, renal transplant, neurogenic bladder. Pyelonephritis = UTI + fever/flank pain/CVA tenderness. CA-UTI = catheter ≥2 days + symptoms/signs + ≥10³ CFU/mL from catheter specimen. Asymptomatic bacteriuria (ASB) = significant bacteriuria without symptoms. Diagnosis: symptoms + pyuria (nitrite, leukocyte esterase, WBC>10/HPF) + urine culture. Thresholds: clean-catch female ≥10⁵, male ≥10⁴; straight catheter ≥10²; indwelling catheter ≥10³; suprapubic aspiration ≥10¹. Treatment: Uncomplicated cystitis: Nitrofurantoin 100mg PO 12h ×3d (1st line), TMP-SMX 160/800mg 12h ×3d (if resistance <20%), Fosfomycin 3g single dose. Complicated cystitis/Pyelonephritis outpatient: Fluoroquinolone 7d (ciprofloxacin 500mg 12h / levofloxacin 750mg OD) or Ceftriaxone 1–2g IV 24h → oral step-down 7–14d. Inpatient/severe/MDR risk: Pip-tazo 4.5g 6h / Meropenem 1g 8h / Cefepime 2g 8h ± vancomycin. CA-UTI: Remove catheter if possible; antibiotics 7d if removed, 10–14d if retained; change catheter at 48–72h. ASB: TREAT ONLY in pregnancy (screen 12–16w), pre-urologic procedure with mucosal bleeding, renal transplant (early). ESBL: carbapenem (meropenem/ertapenem). CRE: ceftazidime-avibactam, meropenem-vaborbactam, imipenem-relebactam, plazomicin, polymyxin. VRE: linezolid (daptomycin inactivated in urine). Candida UTI: fluconazole (echinocandins poor urine levels). Emphysematous pyelonephritis (diabetics, gas on imaging): emergency nephrectomy + broad antibiotics. Acute prostatitis: fluoroquinolone 4–6 weeks (prostate penetration).

MCQs (10)

  1. Urine culture threshold for significant bacteriuria in a clean-catch specimen from a woman:

A. ≥10² CFU/mL

B. ≥10³ CFU/mL

C. ≥10⁵ CFU/mL

D. ≥10⁴ CFU/mL

E. Any growth

  1. First-line treatment for uncomplicated cystitis in a non-pregnant woman:

A. Ciprofloxacin 250mg PO 12h ×3d

B. Nitrofurantoin 100mg PO 12h ×3d

C. Fosfomycin 3g PO single dose

D. TMP-SMX 160/800mg PO 12h ×3d (regardless of resistance)

E. Cephalexin 500mg PO 12h ×3d

  1. Asymptomatic bacteriuria should be treated in ALL EXCEPT:

A. Pregnant women

B. Elderly nursing home resident

C. Pre-urologic procedure with mucosal bleeding

D. Renal transplant recipient (first 3 months)

E. None of the above (treat all)

  1. CA-UTI diagnosis requires catheter in place for:

A. ≥24 hours

B. ≥48 hours (≥2 days)

C. ≥72 hours

D. ≥7 days

E. Any duration

  1. Preferred treatment for ESBL-producing E. coli pyelonephritis:

A. Ciprofloxacin

B. TMP-SMX

C. Meropenem 1g IV 8h (or Ertapenem 1g IV 24h)

D. Ceftriaxone

E. Fosfomycin IV

  1. Why is daptomycin NOT used for VRE urinary tract infection?

A. Nephrotoxic

B. Inactivated in urine (poor urinary penetration/activity)

C. Not active against Enterococcus

D. Causes rhabdomyolysis

E. Expensive

  1. Duration of antibiotics for CA-UTI if catheter is REMOVED:

A. 3 days

B. 5 days

C. 7 days

D. 10–14 days

E. 14–21 days

  1. Emphysematous pyelonephritis is most strongly associated with:

A. Hypertension

B. Diabetes mellitus

C. Renal stones

D. Pregnancy

E. Sickle cell disease

  1. Acute bacterial prostatitis — recommended antibiotic class and duration:

A. β-lactam ×2 weeks

B. Fluoroquinolone ×4–6 weeks

C. TMP-SMX ×2 weeks

C. Nitrofurantoin ×4 weeks

E. Fosfomycin ×single dose

  1. Fluconazole is preferred over echinocandins for Candida UTI because:

A. Echinocandins are not antifungal

B. Echinocandins have poor urinary penetration (except micafungin)

C. Fluconazole is cheaper

D. Echinocandins cause hepatotoxicity

E. Fluconazole covers moulds

SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 28-year-old woman presents with dysuria, frequency, urgency ×2 days. Afebrile, no flank pain, no comorbidities. Urinalysis: nitrite+, leukocyte esterase+, WBC>10/HPF. Best treatment?

A. Ciprofloxacin 250mg PO 12h ×3d

B. Nitrofurantoin 100mg PO 12h ×3d

C. Fosfomycin 3g PO single dose

D. TMP-SMX 160/800mg PO 12h ×7d

E. Ceftriaxone 1g IV 24h

  1. A 65-year-old man with diabetes presents with fever 39°C, left flank pain, CVA tenderness. Urinalysis: pyuria, bacteriuria. Blood cultures pending. No recent antibiotics, no healthcare exposure. Empirical outpatient treatment?

A. Nitrofurantoin 100mg PO 12h ×3d

B. TMP-SMX 160/800mg PO 12h ×3d

C. Ciprofloxacin 500mg PO 12h ×7d (or Levofloxacin 750mg OD ×7d)

D. Fosfomycin 3g PO single dose

E. Cephalexin 500mg PO 12h ×7d

  1. A 75-year-old woman with indwelling Foley catheter for 10 days develops fever 38.5°C and suprapubic tenderness. Urine culture from catheter: E. coli >10⁵ CFU/mL. Catheter removed. Duration of antibiotics?

A. 3 days

B. 5 days

C. 7 days

D. 10–14 days

E. 14 days

  1. A 40-year-old renal transplant recipient (2 months post-transplant) has asymptomatic bacteriuria on routine surveillance: Klebsiella 10⁵ CFU/mL. Management?

A. No treatment (asymptomatic)

B. Treat (early post-transplant ASB indication)

C. Treat only if pyuria

D. Treat only if creatinine rises

E. Repeat culture in 1 week

  1. A patient with pyelonephritis has Pseudomonas aeruginosa (susceptible to pip-tazo, meropenem, cefepime, ciprofloxacin). Oral step-down option?

A. Ciprofloxacin (or Levofloxacin)

B. TMP-SMX

C. Nitrofurantoin

C. Fosfomycin

E. Cephalexin

  1. A 70-year-old man with recurrent UTIs, prior Pip-tazo 2 weeks ago, now has febrile UTI. Urine culture: E. coli ESBL+. Empirical IV regimen while awaiting susceptibilities?

A. Ceftriaxone 2g IV 24h

B. Pip-tazo 4.5g IV 6h

C. Meropenem 1g IV 8h

D. Ciprofloxacin 400mg IV 12h

E. Cefepime 2g IV 8h

  1. A patient with VRE (E. faecium) UTI. Best treatment?

A. Daptomycin 8mg/kg IV OD

B. Linezolid 600mg PO/IV 12h

C. Tigecycline 100mg IV then 50mg 12h

D. Vancomycin 1g IV 12h

E. Fosfomycin IV

  1. A 30-year-old pregnant woman at 16 weeks gestation has asymptomatic bacteriuria on screening: E. coli 10⁵ CFU/mL. Treatment?

A. Nitrofurantoin 100mg PO 12h ×3d

B. Cephalexin 500mg PO 12h ×7d (or Amoxicillin if susceptible)

C. Ciprofloxacin 250mg PO 12h ×3d

D. TMP-SMX 160/800mg PO 12h ×3d

E. No treatment (asymptomatic)

  1. A 55-year-old diabetic man presents with fever, flank pain, and CT showing gas in the renal parenchyma and collecting system. Diagnosis and management?

A. Renal abscess → CT drainage + antibiotics

B. Emphysematous pyelonephritis → Emergency nephrectomy + broad antibiotics (Pip-tazo/Meropenem)

C. Xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis → Nephrectomy

D. Complicated pyelonephritis → IV antibiotics only

E. Papillary necrosis → Supportive

  1. CA-UTI prevention — which is NOT recommended?

A. Avoid unnecessary catheterisation

B. Aseptic insertion

C. Routine antibiotic prophylaxis at insertion

D. Closed drainage system

E. Daily review of necessity with prompt removal

Flashcards

  • Q: Uncomplicated cystitis 1st line

A: Nitrofurantoin 100mg 12h ×3d (TMP-SMX 3d alt)

  • Q: CA-UTI threshold

A: Cath ≥2d + symptoms + ≥10³ CFU/mL

  • Q: ASB treat indications

A: Pregnancy, pre-urologic bleeding, renal transplant early

  • Q: Pyelo outpatient

A: FQ 7d or Ceftriaxone→oral 7-14d

  • Q: CA-UTI duration

A: Removed cath → 7d; Retained → 10-14d + change cath at 48-72h

  • Q: ESBL UTI

A: Carbapenem (Mero/Erta)

  • Q: CRE UTI

A: CAV/MV/IR/Plazomicin/Polymyxin

  • Q: VRE UTI

A: Linezolid (Dapto fails in urine)

  • Q: Candida UTI

A: Fluconazole (Echino poor urine)

  • Q: Emphysematous pyelo

A: Diabetics, gas on CT → nephrectomy + abx

  • Q: Prostatitis

A: FQ 4-6w

  • Q: Culture thresholds

A: Clean F 10⁵, M 10⁴; Straight 10²; Indwelling 10³

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. C — Clean-catch midstream female: ≥10⁵ CFU/mL. Male: ≥10⁴. Straight catheter: ≥10². Indwelling catheter: ≥10³.
  2. B — Nitrofurantoin 100mg PO 12h ×3d is 1st line (IDSA/ESCMID). TMP-SMX if resistance <20%. Fluoroquinolones avoided 1st line. Fosfomycin alternative.
  3. B — ASB treatment ONLY: pregnancy, pre-urologic procedure with mucosal bleeding, renal transplant early. Elderly LTCF = do NOT treat.
  4. B — CA-UTI definition: indwelling catheter ≥2 days (≥48h) + symptoms/signs + ≥10³ CFU/mL.
  5. C — ESBL Enterobacteriaceae: carbapenem (meropenem/ertapenem) is treatment of choice.
  6. B — Daptomycin is inactivated by calcium and surfactant in urine; poor urinary activity. Linezolid preferred for VRE UTI.
  7. C — CA-UTI with catheter removed: 7 days antibiotics. If catheter retained: 10–14 days + change catheter at 48–72h.
  8. B — Emphysematous pyelonephritis: >90% in diabetics; gas in renal parenchyma/collecting system; emergency nephrectomy + antibiotics.
  9. B — Acute bacterial prostatitis: fluoroquinolone 4–6 weeks (achieves prostatic penetration). β-lactams/TMP-SMX less reliable.
  10. B — Echinocandins have poor urinary penetration (except micafungin); fluconazole achieves high urinary concentrations.

SBAs

  1. B — Uncomplicated cystitis in healthy woman: Nitrofurantoin 3d 1st line.
  2. C — Pyelonephritis (fever + flank pain) in diabetic (complicated): Fluoroquinolone 7d preferred outpatient. Ceftriaxone→oral also acceptable.
  3. C — CA-UTI with catheter removed: 7 days antibiotics.
  4. B — Early renal transplant (≤3 months): ASB treated (some guidelines). Controversial but commonly tested.
  5. A — Pseudomonas UTI: oral fluoroquinolone (ciprofloxacin/levofloxacin) only oral with reliable anti-pseudomonal activity.
  6. C — Prior recent antibiotics + ESBL risk → empirical carbapenem (meropenem). Ceftriaxone/pip-tazo unreliable for ESBL.
  7. B — VRE UTI: Linezolid. Daptomycin inactivated in urine. Tigecycline poor urine levels.
  8. B — Pregnancy ASB: treat. Nitrofurantoin avoid term (1st tri OK but many avoid); TMP-SMX avoid 1st tri (folate antagonist); Fluoroquinolones contraindicated. Cephalexin/amoxicillin safe.
  9. B — Diabetic + gas in renal parenchyma/collecting system = emphysematous pyelonephritis → emergency nephrectomy + broad antibiotics.
  10. C — Antibiotic prophylaxis at catheter insertion NOT recommended (promotes resistance). Daily review and prompt removal IS recommended.

tags: [medicine, infectious-disease, davidson, chapter13, cns, encephalitis, meningitis, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_chapter: Chapter 13: Infectious disease

topic_category: Central Nervous System Infections Domain

status: full-fcps-mrcp-topic-note

Viral Encephalitis and Meningitis

Related: [[Acute Bacterial Meningitis]], [[Tuberculous Meningitis]], [[HIV-Associated Opportunistic Infections]], [[Fever and Septic Syndrome Approach]]

[!important]
Viral encephalitis = brain parenchymal inflammation (AMS, focal signs, seizures) — HSV-1 is the most common identifiable cause and the ONLY treatable one with specific antiviral (aciclovir). Viral meningitis = meningeal inflammation without parenchymal involvement — usually self-limiting (enteroviruses). Start aciclovir immediately if encephalitis suspected — do not wait for PCR.

Learning Objectives

  • Distinguish viral meningitis (self-limiting) from viral encephalitis (emergency)
  • Recognise HSV encephalitis clinical-radiological signature (temporal lobe, personality change, seizures)
  • Initiate empirical aciclovir within 1 hour for suspected encephalitis
  • Apply CSF interpretation: lymphocytic pleocytosis, normal glucose, normal/mildly ↑ protein
  • Know indications for MRI, EEG, PCR panel, autoantibody panels
  • Manage complications: raised ICP, seizures, long-term neurocognitive sequelae

Definition

SyndromeDefinitionKey Feature
Viral meningitisInflammation of leptomeninges by virusNo parenchymal involvement; AMS rare; self-limiting
Viral encephalitisInflammation of brain parenchyma by virusAMS, focal deficits, seizures, personality change; high morbidity/mortality
MeningoencephalitisCombined meningeal + parenchymal involvementOverlap features

Core Microbiology — Aetiology

CategoryPathogensKey Features
Herpesviruses (treatable)HSV-1 (adult encephalitis #1), HSV-2 (neonatal, genital, Mollaret's), VZV (shingles, Ramsay Hunt), EBV, CMV, HHV-6Aciclovir effective; HSV-1 = temporal lobe; VZV = vasculopathy
EnterovirusesCoxsackie, Echo, Enterovirus D68, PoliovirusMost common viral meningitis; summer/autumn; hand-foot-mouth, herpangina, acute flaccid myelitis (EV-D68)
Arboviruses (vector-borne)West Nile, Japanese encephalitis, St. Louis, La Crosse, dengue neurotropic, Zika, chikungunya, tick-borne encephalitisGeographic; Culex/Aedes mosquitoes; CSF IgM +ve
Respiratory virusesInfluenza, RSV, hMPV, parainfluenza, adenovirus, SARS-CoV-2Post-viral / para-infectious; acute necrotising encephalopathy (ANEC)
ZoonoticRabies (fatal), Nipah, Hendra, Lymphocytic choriomeningitis virus (LCMV)Animal exposure; rabies = hydrophobia, agitation, fatal
OtherMumps (pre-vaccine era), measles (SSPE), HIV (acute seroconversion, chronic)Vaccine-preventable; SSPE = subacute sclerosing panencephalitis

Normal Values / Important Cut-offs

ParameterViral MeningitisViral EncephalitisBacterial Meningitis
Opening pressureNormal or mildly ↑ (10–25)Normal/mildly ↑↑↑ (>25)
WBC count10–500 (lymphocytes)10–500 (lymphocytes)↑↑↑ neutrophils
ProteinNormal/mildly ↑ (45–150)Mildly ↑ (50–200)↑↑ (100–500)
Glucose (CSF/serum)Normal (>0.6)Normal (>0.6)↓↓ (<0.4)
Gram stainNegativeNegativePositive 60–80%
PCR panelEnterovirus, HSV, VZV, parechovirusHSV-1/2, VZV, enterovirus, arbovirusesBacterial targets
CSF IgM (arboviruses)—Positive (diagnostic)—
Autoantibody panel—Anti-NMDA, LGI1, CASPR2, etc.—
[!tip]
CSF glucose NORMAL = viral (or TB/fungal if low); CSF glucose LOW = bacterial/TB/fungal. Normal glucose essentially rules out typical bacterial meningitis.

Clinical Differentiation

FeatureViral MeningitisViral Encephalitis
OnsetAcute (hours–days)Subacute (days–weeks)
FeverYesYes
HeadacheSevereModerate-severe
Neck stiffnessProminentVariable (may be absent)
PhotophobiaCommonVariable
Altered mental statusAbsent/mildProminent (confusion, agitation, coma)
Focal neurological deficitsAbsentPresent (aphasia, hemiparesis, cranial nerves)
SeizuresRareCommon (30–50%)
Personality/behaviour changeAbsentProminent (HSV: temporal lobe → personality, memory)
OutcomeFull recovery (days–2w)Mortality 10–30%; sequelae 30–50%

Approach / Algorithm

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Suspected CNS infection: fever + headache + neck stiffness +/- AMS/seizures
↓
Encephalitis features? AMS, focal signs, seizures, personality change
↓
EMERGENCY: Start IV Aciclovir 10mg/kg 8h IMMEDIATELY
Supportive care; LP if no contraindications
↓
LP if safe (same contraindications as bacterial)
↓
CSF: cell count, protein, glucose, PCR panel (HSV, VZV, enterovirus), HSV PCR specific
↓
MRI brain with contrast (DWI, FLAIR, T2) — temporal lobe = HSV
↓
EEG if seizures suspected — temporal periodic complexes = HSV
↓
Autoantibody panel if PCR negative (anti-NMDA, LGI1, CASPR2, etc.)
↓
Continue aciclovir 14–21d if HSV PCR +ve; stop if -ve and alternative dx

Investigations

TestIndicationInterpretation
CSF HSV PCRAll suspected encephalitisGold standard for HSV; sensitivity 95–98%, specificity >99%; may be -ve <72h
Multiplex PCR panel (FilmArray)All CNS infectionsRapid (1h): HSV-1/2, VZV, enterovirus, parechovirus, HHV-6, bacteria
CSF VZV PCRShingles + CNS, Ramsay HuntSensitivity 70–90%
CSF enterovirus PCRSuspected viral meningitisHigh sensitivity; seasonal
Arbovirus IgM (CSF/serum)Travel, geographic exposureWest Nile, JE, dengue, Zika, TBE; IgM in CSF = CNS infection
MRI brain (contrast, DWI, FLAIR)All encephalitis; meningitis if atypicalHSV: T2/FLAIR hyperintensity medial temporal lobes ± insula ± cingulate; DWI restricted
EEGSeizures, encephalopathyHSV: periodic lateralised epileptiform discharges (PLEDs) / temporal periodic complexes
Autoantibody panel (CSF/serum)PCR-negative encephalitisAnti-NMDAR, LGI1, CASPR2, AMPAR, GABA-B, DPPX — treatable immune encephalitis
Serology (paired acute/convalescent)Arboviruses, specific viruses4-fold rise = acute infection
Blood PCR / NAATHIV, CMV, EBV, enterovirusSupplementary

Treatment

Specific Antivirals

VirusDrugDose (Adult)DurationNotes
HSV-1/2IV Aciclovir10mg/kg IV 8h (adjust renal)14–21 daysStart IMMEDIATELY — do not wait for PCR; renal adjust essential
VZVIV Aciclovir10–15mg/kg IV 8h10–14 daysHigher dose for encephalitis/vasculopathy
CMVGanciclovir / Valganciclovir5mg/kg IV 12h / 900mg PO 12h2–3w inductionImmunocompromised; monitor neutrophils
HHV-6Ganciclovir / Foscarnet5mg/kg IV 12h / 90mg/kg IV 8h2–3wPost-transplant encephalitis
EnterovirusPleconaril (investigational)——No licensed antiviral; supportive; IVIG for severe
ArbovirusesSupportive——No specific antiviral; IVIG sometimes used (West Nile)
RabiesPost-exposure prophylaxis ONLYRabies vaccine + HRIG—No treatment once symptomatic — fatal

Adjunctive / Supportive

MeasureIndication
Seizure controlLevetiracetam preferred (minimal interactions); phenytoin/valproate alternatives
Raised ICPHead elevation, mannitol/hypertonic saline, hyperventilation (temp), ICP monitoring
IVIGPost-infectious encephalomyelitis (ADEM), anti-NMDAR encephalitis, severe enterovirus
CorticosteroidsAutoimmune encephalitis (high-dose methylprednisolone); VZV vasculopathy; ADEM; NOT routine for viral
Plasma exchangeSevere autoimmune encephalitis, ADEM
RituximabRefractory anti-NMDAR, LGI1, CASPR2 encephalitis

Herpes Simplex Encephalitis (HSE) — THE MUST-KNOW

FeatureDetail
PathogenHSV-1 (>90% adult); HSV-2 (neonatal, immunocompromised)
PathogenesisReactivation from trigeminal ganglion → olfactory/temporal lobes
Peak ageBimodal: <3y and >50y
Classic triadFever, personality/behaviour change, seizures (focal temporal)
Other featuresAnosmia, aphasia, memory loss, hallucinations, psychosis
CSFLymphocytic pleocytosis (50–500), normal glucose, ↑ protein, RBCs common (haemorrhagic necrosis), HSV PCR +ve
MRIT2/FLAIR hyperintensity medial temporal lobes ± insula ± cingulate; haemorrhage on GRE/SWI; DWI restriction
EEGPeriodic lateralised epileptiform discharges (PLEDs) / temporal periodic complexes (specific but not sensitive)
TreatmentIV Aciclovir 10mg/kg 8h ×14–21d (renal adjust: CrCl 10–25 → 10mg/kg 12h; CrCl <10 → 10mg/kg 24h; HD → 10mg/kg post-dialysis)
MortalityUntreated 70%; treated 15–20%
SequelaeMemory impairment, personality change, epilepsy (30–50%)
Relapse10–25% at 1–3m (immune-mediated) — repeat MRI/LP, consider steroids

Autoimmune Encephalitis — Mimics Viral

SyndromeAntibodyClinical CluesTreatment
Anti-NMDAR encephalitisAnti-NMDARYoung women; psychiatric prodrome → seizures, dyskinesias, autonomic instability, catatonia; ovarian teratomaIVIG + methylprednisolone + rituximab; tumour removal
LGI1 encephalitisAnti-LGI1Older men; faciobrachial dystonic seizures (FBDS), hyponatraemia, limbic encephalitisIVIG + steroids; good response
CASPR2 encephalitisAnti-CASPR2Morvan syndrome (neuromyotonia, insomnia, autonomic), limbic encephalitisIVIG + steroids
Anti-GABA-BAnti-GABA-BLimbic encephalitis, seizures; SCLC associationTumour treatment + immunotherapy
ADEMPost-infectious / post-vaccinalChildren; multifocal demyelination; encephalopathy + polyfocal deficitsHigh-dose methylprednisolone + IVIG/plasma exchange

Red Flags / Emergencies

  • Suspected HSV encephalitis → Aciclovir within 1h — delay increases mortality
  • Raised ICP / herniation → mannitol, hypertonic saline, hyperventilation, neurosurgery
  • Status epilepticus → benzodiazepines → levetiracetam/phenytoin/phenobarbital → ICU
  • Autonomic instability (anti-NMDAR) → ICU monitoring
  • Rabies exposure → immediate PEP — once symptomatic, 100% fatal

Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Bacterial meningitisCSF: neutrophils, low glucose, high protein; Gram stain +ve; rapid progression
TB meningitisSubacute >1w; CSF: lymphocytes, low glucose, high protein; basal exudates; GeneXpert
Fungal (Cryptococcal)Immunocompromised; India ink/CrAg +ve; ↑ opening pressure
Autoimmune encephalitisSubacute; psychiatric features; autoantibodies +ve; MRI may show limbic T2 hyperintensity
NeurosyphilisArgyll Robertson pupil, tabes dorsalis; CSF VDRL +ve; serology +ve
Malignancy (paraneoplastic, lymphoma)Subacute; systemic cancer signs; autoantibodies (Hu, Yo, Ma2); CSF cytology
Stroke (limbic infarct)Acute; DWI restriction territorial; no fever/pleocytosis
Metabolic / toxicHyponatraemia, hepatic encephalopathy, alcohol withdrawal, drugs

Special Situations

SituationManagement
PregnancyAciclovir safe (category B); HSV encephalitis = treat aggressively; VZV = treat
NeonatesHSV-2 encephalitis: aciclovir 20mg/kg IV 8h ×21d; CSF PCR, liver function, eye exam
ImmunocompromisedBroader differential: CMV, HHV-6, EBV, JC virus (PML), fungi, TB, toxoplasma; aciclovir still first-line for HSV
Renal impairmentAciclovir renal adjust critical — crystalluria, AKI risk; dose by CrCl
Post-HSCTHHV-6 encephalitis (ganciclovir/foscarnet); CMV; adenovirus

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • Encephalitis vs meningitis: AMS + focal signs + seizures = encephalitis (emergency); meningitis = meningeal signs only, no parenchymal involvement
  • HSV-1 = most common identifiable cause of sporadic encephalitis — temporal lobe, personality change, seizures
  • Start IV aciclovir 10mg/kg 8h IMMEDIATELY — do NOT wait for PCR/LP/MRI
  • Aciclovir renal adjustment: CrCl 10–25 → 10mg/kg 12h; CrCl <10 → 10mg/kg 24h; HD → 10mg/kg post-HD
  • HSV PCR sensitivity 95–98%; may be negative <72h — repeat at 48–72h if high suspicion
  • MRI hallmark: bilateral medial temporal lobe T2/FLAIR hyperintensity ± haemorrhage ± DWI restriction
  • EEG hallmark: PLEDs / temporal periodic complexes
  • Autoimmune encephalitis mimics viral: anti-NMDAR (young women, psychiatric, ovarian teratoma), LGI1 (FBDS, hyponatraemia), CASPR2, ADEM
  • Enterovirus = most common viral meningitis (summer/autumn); self-limiting, supportive
  • Arboviruses: West Nile (acute flaccid paralysis), JE (rural Asia), dengue/Zika/chikungunya (travel)
  • Rabies: fatal once symptomatic; PEP = vaccine + HRIG (pre-exposure: vaccine only)

Common Viva Questions

  1. What is the most common cause of sporadic viral encephalitis? HSV-1.
  2. When do you start aciclovir in suspected encephalitis? Immediately — within 1 hour, do not wait for LP/MRI/PCR.
  3. What is the classic MRI finding in HSV encephalitis? Bilateral medial temporal lobe T2/FLAIR hyperintensity ± insula ± cingulate; haemorrhage common.
  4. How do you distinguish viral meningitis from encephalitis clinically? Encephalitis = AMS, focal deficits, seizures, personality change; meningitis = meningeal signs only, no parenchymal involvement.
  5. What is the aciclovir dose for HSV encephalitis and renal adjustment? 10mg/kg IV 8h ×14–21d; CrCl 10–25: 10mg/kg 12h; CrCl <10: 10mg/kg 24h; HD: 10mg/kg post-dialysis.
  6. Name three autoimmune encephalitis syndromes that mimic viral encephalitis. Anti-NMDAR, LGI1 (FBDS), CASPR2, ADEM.
  7. What is the most common cause of viral meningitis? Enteroviruses (coxsackie, echo) — summer/autumn.
  8. When do you suspect anti-NMDAR encephalitis? Young woman, psychiatric prodrome → seizures, dyskinesias, autonomic instability, catatonia; check for ovarian teratoma.

Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
Wait for HSV PCR before starting aciclovirNEVER — start aciclovir immediately; delay = mortality
Viral meningitis has low CSF glucoseNormal glucose in viral; low glucose = bacterial/TB/fungal
Aciclovir dose same for all indicationsEncephalitis: 10mg/kg 8h; herpes zoster: 10mg/kg 8h (or 800mg PO 5×/d); neonatal: 20mg/kg 8h
MRI normal rules out HSV encephalitisEarly MRI may be normal (first 24–48h); repeat at 48–72h; PCR is more sensitive early
All encephalitis is viralAutoimmune encephalitis common mimic — antibody panel if PCR negative
Arboviruses only in tropicsWest Nile in US/Europe; TBE in Europe/Asia; travel history key
IVIG for all viral encephalitisOnly for specific indications: anti-NMDAR, ADEM, post-infectious, severe enterovirus
Rabies treatable once symptomatic100% fatal — only PEP works

Mnemonics

  • HSV ENCEPHALITIS: HSV-1, Start aciclovir Very fast, ENCEPHALITIS = Emergency; Medial Temporal on MRI; PLEDs on EEG; ACiclovir 10mg/kg 8h; Lymphocytic CSF; Immediate start; Treat 14–21d; ICU if needed; Sequelae memory/seizures
  • AUTOIMMUNE ENCEPH: Anti-NMDAR (young F, teratoma), Unresponsive? FBDS = LGI1, Tumour check, Older M = LGI1/CASPR2, IVIG + steroids + rituximab, Memory limbic, Morvan = CASPR2, Unusual abs, NMDA receptor, EEG extreme delta brush
  • VIRAL MENINGITIS: Enterovirus #1, Normal glucose, Treat supportive, Enterovirus PCR, Recovery full, Mild AMS only

Mind Map

Viral CNS Infections
Viral Meningitis
Enteroviruses (most common)
HSV-2 (Mollaret's)
VZV
Arboviruses
Self-limiting
Viral Encephalitis
HSV-1 (most common identifiable)
VZV (vasculopathy)
Arboviruses (West Nile, JE, dengue)
Enterovirus (rare)
Rabies (fatal)
Post-viral (ADEM, ANEC)
Treatable
HSV → Aciclovir (IMMEDIATE)
VZV → Aciclovir (higher dose)
CMV/HHV-6 → Ganciclovir/Foscarnet
Autoimmune → Immunotherapy
Diagnostics
CSF PCR (HSV gold standard)
Multiplex panel
MRI (temporal lobe = HSV)
EEG (PLEDs = HSV)
Autoantibody panel
Arbovirus IgM

Flowchart

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Fever + headache + neuro symptoms
↓
AMS / focal signs / seizures / personality change?
↓
IV Aciclovir 10mg/kg 8h IMMEDIATELY
Supportive; LP if safe
↓
LP if safe: CSF PCR panel (HSV, VZV, enterovirus), cell count, protein, glucose
Enterovirus PCR +ve → supportive; recovery days-2w
↓
MRI brain with contrast (DWI, FLAIR)
↓
EEG if seizures
↓
HSV PCR +ve?
↓
Continue aciclovir 14-21d; renal adjust
Autoantibody panel; consider VZV, arboviruses, autoimmune; stop aciclovir if alternative dx

Suggested Visuals / Image Notes

  • MRI HSV encephalitis: bilateral medial temporal T2/FLAIR hyperintensity, haemorrhage on GRE, DWI restriction
  • EEG PLEDs / temporal periodic complexes
  • Anti-NMDAR encephalitis clinical course
  • Arbovirus geographic distribution maps
  • CSF algorithm: bacterial vs viral vs TB vs autoimmune

Suggested Video References

  • IDSA encephalitis guidelines
  • HSV encephalitis case presentations
  • Autoimmune encephalitis (anti-NMDAR, LGI1) - Neurology journals
  • Arbovirus neuroinvasive disease
  • Aciclovir pharmacokinetics and renal dosing

One-Page Revision Summary

TopicKey Points
Encephalitis vs MeningitisEncephalitis = AMS, focal signs, seizures; Meningitis = meningeal signs only
HSV-1#1 sporadic encephalitis; temporal lobe; personality change; seizures
Aciclovir10mg/kg IV 8h IMMEDIATELY; 14–21d; renal adjust critical
HSV MRIMedial temporal T2/FLAIR hyperintensity ± insula/cingulate; haemorrhage; DWI restriction
HSV EEGPLEDs / temporal periodic complexes
HSV PCRGold standard; 95–98% sens; may be -ve <72h — repeat
Autoimmune mimicsAnti-NMDAR (young F, teratoma), LGI1 (FBDS, hyponatraemia), CASPR2, ADEM
Viral meningitisEnterovirus #1; lymphocytic, normal glucose; supportive; full recovery
ArbovirusesWest Nile (flaccid paralysis), JE (Asia), dengue/Zika/chik (travel); CSF IgM
RabiesFatal once symptomatic; PEP only

24-Hour Recall Prompts

  • What is the #1 cause of sporadic viral encephalitis?
  • Write the aciclovir dose for HSV encephalitis with renal adjustments.
  • Describe the classic MRI finding in HSV encephalitis.
  • How do you clinically distinguish encephalitis from meningitis?
  • Name 3 autoimmune encephalitis mimics.

7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

  • [ ] Day 1 completed
  • [ ] 24-hour recall completed
  • [ ] Day 7 revision completed
  • [ ] Day 15 revision completed
  • [ ] Day 30 revision completed

Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • Encephalitis = AMS/focal/seizures = emergency; meningitis = meningeal only
  • HSV-1 = most common identifiable encephalitis
  • Aciclovir 10mg/kg 8h IMMEDIATELY; 14–21d; renal adjust
  • HSV MRI: bilateral medial temporal T2/FLAIR ↑
  • Autoimmune encephalitis mimics: anti-NMDAR, LGI1, CASPR2
  • Enterovirus = most common viral meningitis; normal CSF glucose

Should Know

  • VZV encephalitis/vasculopathy: higher dose aciclovir
  • Arboviruses: West Nile, JE, dengue, Zika, TBE; CSF IgM diagnostic
  • Aciclovir renal dosing table
  • Anti-NMDAR: young women, psychiatric, ovarian teratoma, immunotherapy
  • LGI1: FBDS, hyponatraemia, older men
  • ADEM: post-infectious, children, multifocal demyelination
  • Rabies: PEP only; fatal when symptomatic

Nice to Know

  • Acute necrotising encephalopathy (ANEC) - influenza, RanBP2 mutation
  • HHV-6 post-transplant encephalitis
  • Measles SSPE
  • Enterovirus D68 acute flaccid myelitis
  • Novel autoantibodies (DPPX, GFAP, etc.)
  • CSF metagenomic sequencing

My Weak Points

  • [ ] Memorise exact aciclovir renal dosing for all CrCl ranges
  • [ ] Anti-NMDAR immunotherapy sequence
  • [ ] Arbovirus geographic distributions
  • [ ] Differentiating ADEM from MS on first presentation

Self-Test Scorecard

  • Understanding: /10
  • Recall: /10
  • MCQ Performance: /10
  • SBA Performance: /10
  • Viva Confidence: /10
  • Total: /50
[!tip]
Interpretation: <35 = weak topic, 35-44 = acceptable but insecure, 45+ = strong exam-ready topic.

Exam Answer Modes

Long Answer Skeleton

  1. Classification: meningitis vs encephalitis vs meningoencephalitis
  2. Aetiology: viruses by category (herpes, enteroviruses, arboviruses, respiratory, zoonotic)
  3. Clinical differentiation: encephalitis vs meningitis features
  4. HSV encephalitis: epidemiology, pathogenesis, clinical features, CSF, MRI, EEG, PCR, treatment
  5. Other specific viral encephalitides: VZV, arboviruses, rabies, post-viral
  6. Autoimmune encephalitis mimics: anti-NMDAR, LGI1, CASPR2, ADEM
  7. Diagnostics: CSF PCR panel, MRI, EEG, autoantibodies, serology
  8. Treatment: specific antivirals, immunomodulation for autoimmune, supportive
  9. Complications and sequelae

Short Note Skeleton

  • Encephalitis (AMS/focal/seizures) vs Meningitis (meningeal only)
  • HSV-1: temporal lobe, personality, seizures → aciclovir 10mg/kg 8h IMMEDIATE ×14-21d
  • MRI: medial temporal T2/FLAIR ↑; EEG: PLEDs; PCR: gold standard
  • Autoimmune mimics: anti-NMDAR (F, teratoma), LGI1 (FBDS, Na↓), CASPR2, ADEM
  • Viral meningitis: Enterovirus #1; lymphocytic, normal glucose; supportive
  • Arboviruses: West Nile (flaccid paralysis), JE, dengue/Zika; CSF IgM
  • Rabies: fatal; PEP only

Viva One-Liners

  • Encephalitis = AMS + focal + seizures = emergency
  • HSV-1 = #1 sporadic encephalitis
  • Aciclovir: start NOW, 10mg/kg 8h, renal adjust
  • MRI HSV: medial temporal lobes
  • Autoimmune encephalitis = mimic; anti-NMDAR, LGI1, CASPR2
  • Viral meningitis = enterovirus, normal glucose, self-limiting

Ward-Case Discussion Points

  • 45M, fever, confusion, temporal lobe seizures → aciclovir STAT → MRI temporal T2 ↑ → HSV PCR +ve → continue 21d
  • 22F, psychiatric → dyskinesias → autonomic instability → anti-NMDAR +ve → IVIG + steroids + rituximab + teratoma removal
  • 30M post-travel to SE Asia, fever, flaccid paralysis → West Nile / JE suspected → CSF IgM, supportive

Last-Night-Before-Exam Sheet

VIRAL ENCEPHALITIS: AMS/focal/seizures = emergency. HSV-1 #1 cause. Aciclovir 10mg/kg 8h IMMEDIATE ×14-21d (renal adj). MRI: medial temporal T2/FLAIR↑. EEG: PLEDs. PCR gold. Autoimmune mimics: anti-NMDAR (F, teratoma), LGI1 (FBDS, Na↓), CASPR2, ADEM. VIRAL MENINGITIS: Enterovirus #1; lymphocytic, normal glucose; supportive. Arboviruses: West Nile (flaccid paralysis), JE, dengue/Zika; CSF IgM. Rabies: fatal; PEP only.

Summary

Viral encephalitis (brain parenchymal involvement: AMS, focal signs, seizures, personality change) is a neurological emergency distinct from viral meningitis (meningeal signs only, self-limiting). HSV-1 is the most common identifiable cause — hallmark: temporal lobe involvement, personality change, seizures. Start IV aciclovir 10mg/kg 8h IMMEDIATELY — do NOT wait for LP/MRI/PCR. Dose: 14–21 days; renal adjustment mandatory (CrCl 10–25: 10mg/kg 12h; <10: 10mg/kg 24h; HD: post-dialysis). Diagnosis: CSF HSV PCR (gold standard, 95–98% sens, may be -ve <72h), MRI (bilateral medial temporal T2/FLAIR hyperintensity ± haemorrhage ± DWI restriction), EEG (PLEDs/temporal periodic complexes). Autoimmune encephalitis mimics viral: anti-NMDAR (young women, psychiatric prodrome, ovarian teratoma), LGI1 (faciobrachial dystonic seizures, hyponatraemia), CASPR2, ADEM — treat with immunotherapy. Viral meningitis: Enteroviruses #1 (summer/autumn); CSF lymphocytic, normal glucose, self-limiting. Arboviruses: West Nile (flaccid paralysis), JE, dengue, Zika, TBE — CSF IgM diagnostic. Rabies: 100% fatal once symptomatic; PEP only.

MCQs (10)

  1. What is the single most important action in suspected viral encephalitis?

A. Lumbar puncture for HSV PCR

B. MRI brain with contrast

C. Start IV aciclovir 10mg/kg 8h immediately

D. EEG for PLEDs

E. Autoantibody panel

  1. Classic MRI finding in HSV-1 encephalitis:

A. Basal ganglia hyperintensity

B. Bilateral medial temporal lobe T2/FLAIR hyperintensity ± insula ± cingulate

C. Brainstem hyperintensity

D. Diffuse white matter lesions

E. Normal MRI (rules out HSV)

  1. Aciclovir dose for HSV encephalitis in adult with normal renal function:

A. 5mg/kg IV 8h

B. 10mg/kg IV 8h

C. 15mg/kg IV 8h

D. 20mg/kg IV 8h

E. 800mg PO 5×/day

  1. CSF profile in viral meningitis:

A. Neutrophils ↑↑, glucose ↓↓, protein ↑↑

B. Lymphocytes 10–500, glucose normal, protein normal/mildly ↑

C. Lymphocytes ↑, glucose ↓↓, protein ↑↑

D. Neutrophils ↑, glucose normal, protein normal

E. Acellular, glucose normal, protein normal

  1. Which autoimmune encephalitis is classically associated with ovarian teratoma in young women?

A. LGI1 encephalitis

B. CASPR2 encephalitis

C. Anti-NMDAR encephalitis

D. Anti-GABA-B encephalitis

E. ADEM

  1. Faciobrachial dystonic seizures (FBDS) and hyponatraemia are characteristic of:

A. Anti-NMDAR encephalitis

B. LGI1 encephalitis

C. CASPR2 encephalitis

D. HSV encephalitis

E. West Nile encephalitis

  1. A 35-year-old man returns from rural India with fever, altered sensorium, and flaccid paralysis. Most likely arbovirus?

A. West Nile virus

B. Japanese encephalitis virus

C. Dengue virus

D. Zika virus

E. Chikungunya virus

  1. Aciclovir renal adjustment for CrCl <10 mL/min (not on dialysis):

A. 10mg/kg IV 8h

B. 10mg/kg IV 12h

C. 10mg/kg IV 24h

D. 5mg/kg IV 12h

E. Contraindicated

  1. Enterovirus meningitis: which statement is TRUE?

A. CSF glucose is low

B. Requires IV aciclovir

C. Most common cause of viral meningitis; summer/autumn peak; self-limiting

D. Causes significant mortality in immunocompetent adults

E. MRI shows temporal lobe hyperintensity

  1. Rabies post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) for a previously unvaccinated person with category III exposure (bite):

A. Rabies vaccine only (4 doses)

B. Rabies vaccine (4 doses: day 0, 3, 7, 14) + Human Rabies Immunoglobulin (HRIG) 20 IU/kg infiltrated around wound

C. HRIG only

D. Vaccine + HRIG only if bat exposure

E. No PEP needed if wound washed

SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 50-year-old man presents with 5 days of fever, personality change, and focal seizures. MRI shows bilateral medial temporal lobe T2 hyperintensity with haemorrhage. CSF: lymphocytes 150, protein 80, glucose 3.5 (serum 5.0). HSV PCR pending. Best immediate management?

A. Wait for HSV PCR result

B. Start IV aciclovir 10mg/kg 8h immediately

C. Start IV ceftriaxone + vancomycin

D. Start IV methylprednisolone 1g daily

E. Repeat MRI in 48 hours

  1. A 24-year-old woman develops acute psychosis, catatonia, orofacial dyskinesias, and autonomic instability over 2 weeks. MRI normal. CSF: lymphocytes 30, protein 55, glucose normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A. HSV encephalitis

B. Anti-NMDAR encephalitis

C. LGI1 encephalitis

D. Viral meningitis

E. Acute psychotic disorder

  1. An 80-year-old man with hypertension presents with frequent brief unilateral arm dystonia with facial involvement (FBDS), hyponatraemia (Na⁺ 128), and memory impairment. MRI: unilateral medial temporal T2 hyperintensity. Most likely antibody?

A. Anti-NMDAR

B. Anti-LGI1

C. Anti-CASPR2

D. Anti-GABA-B

E. Anti-AMPAR

  1. A patient with HSV encephalitis on IV aciclovir 10mg/kg 8h develops acute kidney injury (CrCl 15 mL/min) on day 3. Correct dose adjustment?

A. Continue 10mg/kg 8h with hydration

B. 10mg/kg IV 12h

C. 10mg/kg IV 24h

D. 5mg/kg IV 8h

D. Stop aciclovir, switch to foscarnet

  1. Which arbovirus is most strongly associated with acute flaccid paralysis (AFP) resembling poliomyelitis?

A. Dengue

B. Zika

C. Chikungunya

D. West Nile virus

E. Japanese encephalitis

  1. In anti-NMDAR encephalitis, first-line immunotherapy is:

A. Rituximab alone

B. IVIG + high-dose methylprednisolone (+/- plasma exchange)

C. IV aciclovir

D. IV cyclophosphamide alone

E. Supportive care only

  1. A 6-week-old infant presents with fever, lethargy, and seizures. Mother had primary genital herpes at delivery. CSF: lymphocytes 200, protein 120, glucose 2.0 (serum 4.5), RBCs 5000. Best empirical treatment?

A. Aciclovir 10mg/kg IV 8h

B. Aciclovir 20mg/kg IV 8h

C. Ceftriaxone + vancomycin + aciclovir 10mg/kg 8h

D. Ganciclovir 5mg/kg IV 12h

E. Ceftriaxone + vancomycin

  1. Post-infectious encephalomyelitis (ADEM) typically occurs:

A. During acute viral infection

B. 1–4 weeks after viral infection or vaccination

C. 6–12 months after infection

D. Only after live vaccines

E. Only in adults

  1. Which statement about VZV encephalitis is CORRECT?

A. Only occurs with active zoster rash

B. Can cause vasculopathy (stroke) months after zoster; aciclovir 10–15mg/kg IV 8h

C. Treated with valaciclovir PO only

D. MRI shows basal ganglia lesions only

E. CSF VZV PCR sensitivity >95%

  1. A patient with suspected encephalitis has negative HSV PCR at 24h. High clinical suspicion persists. Next step?

A. Stop aciclovir

B. Repeat HSV PCR at 48–72h; continue aciclovir; MRI, EEG, autoantibody panel

C. Switch to ganciclovir

D. Start high-dose steroids

E. Brain biopsy

Flashcards

  • Q: Encephalitis vs meningitis distinction

A: Encephalitis = AMS, focal signs, seizures, personality change (parenchymal); Meningitis = meningeal signs only, no parenchymal involvement

  • Q: #1 cause sporadic viral encephalitis

A: HSV-1

  • Q: Aciclovir dose HSV encephalitis

A: 10mg/kg IV 8h ×14–21d (START IMMEDIATELY)

  • Q: Aciclovir renal adjust

A: CrCl 10–25: 10mg/kg 12h; CrCl <10: 10mg/kg 24h; HD: 10mg/kg post-HD

  • Q: HSV MRI hallmark

A: Bilateral medial temporal T2/FLAIR hyperintensity ± insula/cingulate; haemorrhage on GRE; DWI restriction

  • Q: HSV EEG hallmark

A: PLEDs / temporal periodic complexes

  • Q: HSV PCR sensitivity

A: 95–98%; may be negative <72h — repeat at 48–72h

  • Q: Anti-NMDAR encephalitis

A: Young women; psychiatric → seizures, dyskinesias, autonomic instability, catatonia; ovarian teratoma; IVIG + steroids + rituximab

  • Q: LGI1 encephalitis

A: Older men; FBDS (faciobrachial dystonic seizures), hyponatraemia, limbic encephalitis; IVIG + steroids

  • Q: CASPR2 encephalitis

A: Morvan syndrome (neuromyotonia, insomnia, autonomic) + limbic encephalitis

  • Q: ADEM

A: Post-infectious/vaccinal (1–4w); children; multifocal demyelination; encephalopathy + polyfocal deficits; steroids + IVIG/PLEX

  • Q: Viral meningitis CSF

A: Lymphocytic, normal glucose, normal/mild ↑ protein; enterovirus #1

  • Q: Enterovirus meningitis

A: Most common viral meningitis; summer/autumn; supportive; full recovery

  • Q: West Nile virus

A: Acute flaccid paralysis; CSF IgM; supportive; IVIG sometimes

  • Q: Japanese encephalitis

A: Rural Asia; Culex mosquito; pig/water bird reservoirs; vaccine available

  • Q: Rabies

A: Fatal once symptomatic; hydrophobia, agitation; PEP = vaccine + HRIG

  • Q: Aciclovir for VZV encephalitis

A: 10–15mg/kg IV 8h ×10–14d (higher dose than HSV)

  • Q: Aciclovir neonatal HSV

A: 20mg/kg IV 8h ×21d

  • Q: HSV PCR negative early

A: Repeat at 48–72h; continue aciclovir if high suspicion

Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. C — Start aciclovir IMMEDIATELY (within 1h). Delay increases mortality. All other investigations follow.
  2. B — Bilateral medial temporal lobes ± insula ± cingulate is the classic HSV encephalitis MRI signature. Haemorrhage and DWI restriction common.
  3. B — IV aciclovir 10mg/kg 8h for encephalitis. 5mg/kg 8h = VZV (some guidelines); 20mg/kg 8h = neonatal HSV; PO = non-CNS herpes.
  4. B — Viral meningitis: lymphocytic pleocytosis (10–500), normal glucose, normal/mildly elevated protein. A = bacterial. C = TB/fungal. D/E = not typical.
  5. C — Anti-NMDAR encephalitis: young women, psychiatric prodrome, ovarian teratoma (50%).
  6. B — FBDS + hyponatraemia = LGI1 encephalitis (classic). Anti-NMDAR = dyskinesias, psychiatric. CASPR2 = Morvan.
  7. B — Rural India + encephalitis + flaccid paralysis = Japanese encephalitis (Culex, pigs/water birds). West Nile = US/Europe/Mediterranean. Dengue/Zika/chikungunya = less AFP.
  8. C — CrCl <10 (non-dialysis): aciclovir 10mg/kg IV 24h. CrCl 10–25: 12h. HD: post-dialysis.
  9. C — Enterovirus = most common viral meningitis; summer/autumn; self-limiting; CSF normal glucose; no specific antiviral.
  10. B — Category III exposure (bite): vaccine days 0, 3, 7, 14 + HRIG 20 IU/kg infiltrated around wound (rest IM distant). Previously vaccinated: vaccine only (days 0, 3).

SBAs

  1. B — Classic HSV encephalitis clinical-radiological picture → start aciclovir immediately. Do NOT wait for PCR.
  2. B — Young woman, psychiatric prodrome → dyskinesias/autonomic instability/catatonia = anti-NMDAR encephalitis.
  3. B — FBDS + hyponatraemia + temporal T2 hyperintensity in older man = LGI1 encephalitis.
  4. B — CrCl 15 → aciclovir 10mg/kg IV 12h. Continue treatment, adjust dose.
  5. D — West Nile virus = acute flaccid paralysis (anterior horn cell involvement). JE = encephalitis, less AFP. Dengue/Zika/chikungunya = not classic for AFP.
  6. B — First-line anti-NMDAR: IVIG + high-dose methylprednisolone ± plasma exchange. Rituximab second-line.
  7. B — Neonatal HSV (mother primary genital herpes at delivery) → aciclovir 20mg/kg IV 8h ×21d. Higher dose than adult. Also needs ceftriaxone/vancomycin empirically until bacterial ruled out.
  8. B — ADEM = post-infectious/post-vaccinal, 1–4 weeks latency, monophasic.
  9. B — VZV encephalitis/vasculopathy: can occur months after zoster, without active rash; aciclovir 10–15mg/kg IV 8h; PCR sensitivity ~70–90%.
  10. B — HSV PCR may be negative <72h. Repeat at 48–72h; continue aciclovir; pursue MRI, EEG, autoantibody panel.

tags: [medicine, infectious-disease, davidson, chapter13, VHF, viral-haemorrhagic-fevers, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_chapter: Chapter 13: Infectious disease

topic_category: Viral Haemorrhagic Fevers Domain

status: full-fcps-mrcp-topic-note

Viral Haemorrhagic Fevers (VHF)

Related: [[Fever in Returned Traveller and FUO]], [[Travel Medicine and Pre-Travel Advice]], [[Sepsis and Septic Shock]], [[Infection in Immunocompromised Host (Transplant, Biologics)]], [[Infection Control]]

[!important]
VHF = group of viral infections causing fever + bleeding diathesis + multi-organ failure. Ebola, Marburg, Lassa, CCHF, Dengue (severe), Yellow Fever. High case fatality (25-90%). Transmission: contact with infected body fluids (Ebola, Marburg), aerosols (Lassa), vectors (CCHF, Dengue, Yellow Fever). ISOLATE IMMEDIATELY on suspicion. PPE mandatory. Public health notification.

Learning Objectives

  • Classify VHF by virus family, vector, geography
  • Recognise clinical syndrome: fever + bleeding + multi-organ involvement
  • Apply immediate isolation and infection control measures
  • Initiate supportive management and specific antivirals where available
  • Apply contact tracing and infection control measures
  • Recognise when to suspect VHF in returned traveller

Classification

Virus FamilyVirusesVector/ReservoirGeography
FiloviridaeEbola virus (EBOV)Fruit bats; human-to-humanCentral/West Africa (DRC, Guinea, Sierra Leone, Liberia)
Marburg virus (MARV)Fruit bats; human-to-humanCentral/East/Southern Africa
ArenaviridaeLassa virus (LASV)Multimammate rat (Mastomys); aerosol/excretaWest Africa (Nigeria, Sierra Leone, Liberia, Guinea)
Lujo virusUnknown rodentSouthern Africa
Bunyaviridae (Nairoviridae)Crimean-Congo Haemorrhagic Fever (CCHF) virusHyalomma ticks; livestock; human-to-humanAfrica, Middle East, Asia, Eastern Europe
Rift Valley Fever (RVF) virusMosquitoes; livestockAfrica, Arabian Peninsula
FlaviviridaeDengue virus (DENV) — severe dengue/DHF/DSSAedes mosquitoesTropical/subtropical worldwide
Yellow Fever (YFV)Aedes/Haemagogus mosquitoesAfrica, South America
Kyasanur Forest Disease (KFD) virusTicks; monkeysIndia (Western Ghats)
Omsk Haemorrhagic FeverTicks; rodentsRussia (Western Siberia)
Alkhurma Haemorrhagic FeverTicks; camelsSaudi Arabia, Egypt

Clinical Syndrome

FeatureTypical Presentation
Incubation2–21 days (Ebola 2–21; Lassa 6–21; CCHF 1–13; Dengue 4–10)
ProdromeFever, myalgia, headache, fatigue, anorexia, nausea/vomiting
Haemorrhagic ManifestationsPetechiae, ecchymoses, mucosal bleeding (gums, nose), GI bleeding, haematuria, vaginal bleeding, injection site bleeding
Organ InvolvementHepatitis (↑ AST/ALT), renal failure, encephalopathy, myocarditis, pancreatitis, ARDS, DIC, shock
LaboratoryThrombocytopenia, leukopenia (Ebola, Lassa), ↑ AST/ALT, ↑ creatinine, prolonged PT/aPTT, fibrinogen ↓, D-dimer ↑

Key Viruses — Clinical Pearls

Ebola Virus Disease (EVD)

AspectDetails
TransmissionDirect contact with blood/body fluids of symptomatic patient; fomites; bushmeat; sexual (persistent in semen)
ClinicalSudden fever, severe headache, myalgia → vomiting, diarrhoea, abdominal pain → haemorrhage (day 5–7), multi-organ failure
CFR50–90% (Zaire ebolavirus); Sudan ebolavirus ~50%
TreatmentInmazeb (atoltivimab/maftivimab/odesivimab) OR Ebanga (ansuvimab) — monoclonal antibodies; supportive care
VaccinerVSV-ZEBOV (Ervebo) — single dose, ring vaccination

Marburg Virus Disease (MVD)

AspectDetails
TransmissionSimilar to Ebola; bat exposure (caves/mines)
ClinicalIndistinguishable from Ebola; severe MACULOPAPULAR RASH (day 5)
CFR24–88%
TreatmentSupportive; no licenced antiviral/vaccine (experimental monoclonal)

Lassa Fever

AspectDetails
TransmissionAerosol/excreta of Mastomys rats; person-to-person (body fluids); nosocomial
ClinicalInsidious onset; fever, pharyngitis, retrosternal pain, facial oedema, pleural effusions, sensorineural hearing loss (common sequela), encephalopathy
CFR1–15% (hospitalised); higher in pregnancy (3rd trimester)
TreatmentRibavirin IV (early = better outcome); supportive; convalescent plasma (experimental)
VaccineNone licenced (candidates in development)

Crimean-Congo Haemorrhagic Fever (CCHF)

AspectDetails
TransmissionHyalomma tick bite; contact with infected livestock blood/tissues; nosocomial
ClinicalSudden fever, myalgia, headache, dizziness → haemorrhage (day 3–5), hepatomegaly, confusion, multiorgan failure
CFR10–40%
TreatmentRibavirin IV (early = better outcome); supportive
VaccineNone licenced (inactivated vaccine in limited use)

Dengue Haemorrhagic Fever / Dengue Shock Syndrome (DHF/DSS)

AspectDetails
TransmissionAedes aegypti/albopictus mosquito
ClinicalDHF: fever, thrombocytopenia (<100k), haemorrhagic manifestations, plasma leakage (pleural effusion, ascites); DSS: shock, narrow pulse pressure
CFR<1% with proper fluid management; >20% untreated
TreatmentFluid management (crystalloids, colloids; avoid overload); no antiviral
VaccineDengvaxia — ONLY for seropositive individuals (9–45y)

Diagnosis

TestUtility
RT-PCR (blood)Gold standard; rapid (hours); available for Ebola, Marburg, Lassa, CCHF, Dengue, YF
IgM/IgG ELISAAcute/convalescent; Lassa, CCHF, Dengue, YF
Antigen Detection (Rapid Test)Dengue NS1; Ebola VP40 (field use)
Virus IsolationBSL-4; definitive; not for acute management
Routine LabsThrombocytopenia, ↑ AST/ALT, prolonged PT, DIC screen
[!critical]
VHF SUSPICION → IMMEDIATE ISOLATION + PPE + PUBLIC HEALTH NOTIFICATION. Do NOT send samples routine lab — use dedicated VHF pathway.

Management

Immediate (Hour 0)

ActionDetails
1. ISOLATENegative pressure room; PPE: double gloves, impermeable gown, N95/FFP3, face shield, hood, boots; trained buddy system for donning/doffing
2. NOTIFYPublic health authorities immediately (statutory notification); hospital IPC team
3. SUPPORTIVEFluid resuscitation (crystalloids, avoid overload), blood products (platelets, FFP, cryoprecipitate for DIC), vasopressors, renal replacement, mechanical ventilation as needed
4. SPECIFIC ANTIVIRALSEbola: Inmazeb (atoltivimab/maftivimab/odesivimab) OR Ebanga (ansuvimab); Lassa/CCHF: Ribavirin IV (30mg/kg load → 16mg/kg q6h ×4d → 8mg/kg q8h ×6d); Dengue: Fluid management only; Marburg: Supportive only

Infection Control (IPC)

MeasureStandard
IsolationNegative pressure room; anteroom; dedicated equipment
PPEDouble gloves; impermeable gown; N95/FFP3; face shield; hood; boots; buddy system
WasteCategory A infectious; autoclave/incinerate
DecontaminationHypochlorite 10,000ppm (1%) for surfaces; 0.5% chlorine for skin
Contact Tracing21 days (Ebola/Marburg); 17 days (Lassa); 14 days (CCHF)
Dead Body ManagementTrained team; minimal handling; sealed body bag; cremation/deep burial

Post-Exposure Prophylaxis (PEP)

VirusPEP
EbolaMonoclonal antibodies (Inmazeb/Ebanga) ± rVSV-ZEBOV vaccine (ring vaccination)
Lassa/CCHFRibavirin (controversial; limited evidence)
DengueNone (no PEPreventative vaccine for seronegative)
MarburgNone licenced

VHF in Returned Traveller

ScenarioAction
Fever + travel to VHF-endemic area + bleeding/contact historyISOLATE IMMEDIATELY; notify IPC/public health; send blood for RT-PCR (Ebola, Marburg, Lassa, CCHF, Dengue, YF)
HCW needlestick from VHF patientImmediate washing; baseline labs; PEP per virus (Ebola: vaccine ± mAb; Lassa/CCHF: ribavirin controversial); monitor 21 days

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • VHF = fever + bleeding + multi-organ failure; high CFR
  • Ebola/Marburg = Filoviridae; contact fluids; CFR 50-90%; monoclonal antibodies (Inmazeb/Ebanga); rVSV-ZEBOV vaccine
  • Lassa = Arenaviridae; Mastomys rat; aerosol; facial oedema, hearing loss; Ribavirin IV early
  • CCHF = Nairoviridae; Hyalomma tick; livestock; Ribavirin IV early
  • Dengue DHF/DSS = plasma leakage, thrombocytopenia; fluid management; Dengvaxia seropositive only
  • ISOLATE IMMEDIATELY on suspicion; PPE; negative pressure; public health notification
  • RT-PCR = gold standard; send via dedicated VHF pathway
  • Ribavirin for Lassa/CCHF (early = better)
  • Ebola: Inmazeb/Ebanga monoclonals; rVSV-ZEBOV vaccine
  • No person-to-person for CCHF except nosocomial; Ebola/Marburg = body fluids; Lassa = aerosol/fluid

Common Viva Questions

  1. First action on suspecting VHF? Isolate immediately (negative pressure), PPE, notify public health.
  2. Which VHF has specific antiviral? Lassa & CCHF = Ribavirin; Ebola = monoclonal antibodies (Inmazeb/Ebanga).
  3. Lassa fever characteristic features? Facial oedema, pharyngitis, retrosternal pain, sensorineural hearing loss.
  4. CCHF transmission? Hyalomma tick bite; livestock contact; nosocomial.
  5. Ebola treatment? Inmazeb or Ebanga (monoclonal antibodies); supportive care; rVSV-ZEBOV vaccine for prevention.
  6. Dengue DHF vs DSS? DHF = plasma leakage + haemorrhage; DSS = shock (narrow pulse pressure).
  7. First action on VHF suspicion? ISOLATE (negative pressure), PPE, notify public health.
  8. Ribavirin use? Lassa fever & CCHF (early = better outcome).
  9. Ebola vaccine? rVSV-ZEBOV (Ervebo) single dose.
  10. VHF in pregnancy? Lassa severe in 3rd trimester; Ebola high mortality; Ribavirin teratogenic (avoid in pregnancy).

Common Confusions / Exam Traps

ConfusionClarification
Ribavirin for all VHFOnly Lassa & CCHF (early); NOT for Ebola/Marburg/Dengue
Ebola = airborneContact with body fluids; NOT airborne (except lab aerosols)
Dengue vaccine for allDengvaxia ONLY seropositive (9-45y); seronegative = ↑ severe dengue
Ribavirin safe in pregnancyTeratogenic (Category X) — avoid in pregnancy; Lassa in pregnancy = high mortality
Ebola = always haemorrhageOnly 50% develop overt bleeding; fever + GI symptoms common presentation
Marburg = same as EbolaClinically similar but distinct virus; maculopapular rash more prominent in Marburg
CCHF = only AfricaAlso Middle East, Asia, Eastern Europe
Lassa = only ruralUrban transmission occurs (rat infestation); nosocomial outbreaks common
Vaccine for all VHFOnly Ebola (rVSV-ZEBOV), YF, Dengue (seropositive only) have licenced vaccines
VHF = always fatalSupportive care + early specific therapy ↓ mortality significantly

Mnemonics

  • VHF FIRST: Isolate Negative Pressure; PPE Buddy; Notify Public Health
  • EBOLA: Ebolavirus; Body fluids; Outbreak (Africa); Lethal (50-90%); Antiviral mAbs
  • LASSA: Lassa; Arenavirus; Sensorineural Hearing Loss; Aerosol (rat); Ribavirin
  • CCHF: Crimean-Congo; Hyalomma Tick; Ribavirin; Haemorrhage
  • DENGUE: Dengue; Epidemic; Ng Glucose Uea Electrolytes; Aedes mosquito; Haemorrhagic Fever (DHF) → Shock (DSS)
  • RIBAVIRIN: Lassa + CCHF (early) = Ribavirin; NOT Ebola/Marburg/Dengue
  • VHF ISOLATE: Immediate Single Occupancy Legative Pressure Anteroom Trained Expert

Mind Map

Viral Haemorrhagic Fevers
Filoviridae
Ebola: Contact fluids, CFR 50-90%, mAbs (Inmazeb/Ebanga), rVSV-ZEBOV vaccine
Marburg: Bats/caves, CFR 24-88%, maculopapular rash, supportive only
Arenaviridae
Lassa: Mastomys rat, aerosol, facial oedema, hearing loss, Ribavirin
Lujo: Rare, Southern Africa
Nairoviridae
CCHF: Hyalomma tick, livestock, Ribavirin
RVF: Mosquitoes, livestock
Flaviviridae
Dengue: Aedes mosquito, DHF/DSS, plasma leakage, fluid management, Dengvaxia
Yellow Fever: Aedes, vaccines, lifelong immunity
KFD/Omsk/Alkhurma: Ticks
Management
ISOLATE + PPE + Notify
Supportive: Fluids, Blood products, Vasopressors
Specific: Ebola mAbs, Lassa/CCHF Ribavirin, Dengue fluids
IPC: Negative pressure, PPE, Contact tracing 21d

Flowchart

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Suspected VHF
↓

↓

↓

↓
Virus Identified
↓
Inmazeb OR Ebanga + Supportive
Supportive only
Ribavirin IV EARLY + Supportive
Ribavirin IV EARLY + Supportive
Fluid Management (DHF/DSS); NO antiviral
Supportive; Experimental mAbs
↓
Contact Tracing 21d + Ring Vaccination rVSV-ZEBOV
Contact Tracing 17d
Contact Tracing 14d

One-Page Revision Summary

VirusFamilyVector/ReservoirKey FeatureTreatmentVaccine
EbolaFiloviridaeBats; body fluidsHigh CFR 50-90%Inmazeb/Ebanga mAbsrVSV-ZEBOV (Ervebo)
MarburgFiloviridaeBats; body fluidsMaculopapular rashSupportiveNone

Yellow FeverFlaviviridaeAedes mosquitoHepatorenal failureSupportiverVSV-YF (lifelong)
General——ISOLATE + PPE + NotifySupportive + SpecificEbola, YF, Dengue

24-Hour Recall Prompts

  • Ebola: mAbs, vaccine, CFR
  • Lassa: facial oedema, hearing loss, Ribavirin early
  • CCHF: tick, livestock, Ribavirin early
  • Dengue DHF/DSS: plasma leakage, fluid management
  • Ebola: mAbs (Inmazeb/Ebanga), rVSV-ZEBOV vaccine
  • First action VHF: ISOLATE + PPE + Notify

  • Dengue vaccine: seropositive only
  • Contact tracing: Ebola 21d, Lassa 17d, CCHF 14d

7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

Must Know

  • ISOLATE + PPE + Notify = first actions
  • Ebola: mAbs (Inmazeb/Ebanga), vaccine (rVSV-ZEBOV)
  • Lassa: facial oedema, hearing loss, Ribavirin early
  • CCHF: Hyalomma tick, Ribavirin early
  • Dengue: fluid management for DHF/DSS
  • Ribavirin for Lassa/CCHF (NOT Ebola/Marburg/Dengue)
  • RT-PCR gold standard; isolate first

Should Know

  • Marburg: maculopapular rash, supportive only
  • Rift Valley Fever: livestock, mosquito
  • Yellow Fever: lifelong vaccine
  • Ebola ring vaccination
  • Post-exposure prophylaxis per virus
  • Contact tracing durations

Nice to Know

  • Experimental therapies (favipiravir, remdesivir for Ebola)
  • New vaccines (MVA-BN for Mpox/Ebola)
